videos.title: Weel 3-Lines in the Lands: Meaning, Memory, and Membership
videos.description:
Recorded May 28, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski
What makes a piece of land part of the Land of Israel? And who gets to decide? For thousands of years, Jewish thinkers have debated how Israel is defined: by geography, by history, by law, or by something else entirely. In this three-part course, Rabbi Uri Pilichowsky guides us through surprising and often overlooked Jewish texts that raise real-world questions. Where do Israel’s borders begin and end? What gives the land its unique status? And why might simply being in Israel change how a person is judged or understood? Each session focuses on one key issue:

1. What Creates the Sanctity of the Land of Israel? Was the land made holy by divine promise, military conquest, or active Jewish presence? Ancient sources offer radically different views, and the debate continues into modern times. This session explores how Jewish legal thinkers have defined — and redefined — what makes the land special.
2. Can You Find Israel on a Map? The borders of Israel have never been fixed: not in the Bible, not in the Talmud, and not today. What do those changing lines tell us about the evolving relationship between the Jewish people and the land itself? We’ll explore maps, texts, and the meanings behind the boundaries.
3. Does Living in Israel Change How We See Ourselves? Some Jewish legal principles apply differently inside the Land of Israel than outside it. Why would that be? This session explores how being in the land might shift not only what we do, but how we think, from private individuals to part of a national story.

Whether you’re drawn to geography, law, identity, or big Jewish questions, this course offers a fresh and thought-provoking look at how the Land of Israel has been understood across time.

videos.title: Ask the Rabbis: What’s Next for North American Judaism with Rabbi Sari Laufer
videos.description:
Recorded May 27, 2026
Instructors: Rabbi Sari Laufer and Rabbi Rachel Bovitz
What does meaningful Jewish engagement look like today, and how is it changing? As North American Jewish life continues to evolve, communities are rethinking not only what they offer but also how they connect.

In this ongoing Melton+ series, Rabbi Rachel Bovitz sits down with leading rabbinic voices for candid, wide-ranging conversations about the state of Jewish life today. In this session, she is joined by Rabbi Sari Laufer, Chief Engagement Officer at Stephen Wise Temple in Los Angeles, whose work focuses on building relationships, fostering belonging, and reimagining how individuals and families connect to Jewish community. Together, they explore the challenges and opportunities shaping contemporary Jewish life, from new models of engagement and participation to the evolving role of synagogue leadership. Drawing on Rabbi Laufer’s experience, this conversation offers an inside look at how communities are adapting and what it might mean for your own Jewish journey. Part of an ongoing series of conversations with rabbinic leaders across North America, this session invites you to listen in, reflect, and consider how these insights might shape your own connection to Jewish life.

videos.title: Week 3-From Sinai to Seinfeld: Jews and Their Jokes
videos.description:
Recorded May 27, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Alan Freedman
Humor is part of the Jewish soul. It gives us the ability to laugh at our tragedies, question authority, and make meaning out of the challenges we face. In fact, when asked what it means to be Jewish, more people chose “a sense of humor” than religious observance or communal belonging. What is it about Jewish life that has made humor so central?

In this course, we treat jokes as texts, reading them as we would Torah, midrash, or modern literature. Across ten sessions, we journey through Jewish history from the Bible and the Talmud to the Yiddish world, the Borscht Belt, and contemporary comedy. Along the way, we encounter figures like Sholem Aleichem, Fanny Brice, Lenny Bruce, Mel Brooks, Jerry Seinfeld, and Sarah Silverman, asking what their humor reveals about the times and communities they reflect.

As Jewish life changes, so does Jewish humor. Sometimes rooted in deep textual knowledge, sometimes shaped by the experience of being outsiders, Jewish comedy has long transformed hardship into insight and laughter into connection. By exploring the jokes Jews tell and retell, we uncover not only what makes us laugh, but what binds us together across generations.

Table of Contents
Lesson 1 - How to ‘read’ a Jewish joke
Lesson 2 - A lack of graves
Lesson 3 - The goyim annoy’em
Lesson 4 - If you’re so smart...
Lesson 5 - The joy of text
Lesson 6 - Take my wife, please
Lesson 7 - “You can change your noses but not your Moses”
Lesson 8 - Dig, I’m Jewish
Lesson 9 - Mothers and others
Lesson 10 - Seriously funny

videos.title: Week 1-With Dignity and Compassion: Jewish Wisdom for the End of Life
videos.description:
Recorded May 26, 2026
Instructors: David Zinner, Rick Light, Rabbi Stuart Kelman, and Dan Fendel of The Jewish Association for Death Education 
What does Jewish tradition teach us about how to approach the end of life with dignity, compassion, and care? Across centuries, Jewish texts and practices have created a framework that honors both the individual and the community, offering guidance in moments that can feel uncertain or overwhelming.

In partnership with the Jewish Association for Death Education, this three-part course invites learners into a thoughtful exploration of Jewish end-of-life practices. Through close reading of classical texts and liturgy, we will uncover how Jewish tradition responds to the emotional, spiritual, and communal needs that arise at the end of life, and how these practices continue to shape Jewish life today.

In this three-part series, we will explore:

Session 1: Jewish Traditions at the End of Life
An introduction to the core values and historical development of Jewish end-of-life practices, from Tanach through contemporary expressions. We will explore key texts and moments, including the role of the Chevrah Kadisha and the foundations of communal care.
Session 2: The Power of Taharah Liturgy
A close reading of the prayers recited during taharah, the ritual preparation of the body. We will ask what these words do, what needs they respond to, and how their structure and language reflect deeper spiritual and emotional truths.
Session 3: Jewish End-of-Life Practices in Moed Katan
A study of foundational rabbinic teachings on burial and mourning. Through selected texts, we will explore how Jewish law understands grief, ritual, and community, and what the connection between mourning and festival life reveals about Jewish tradition. 
This course is generously sponsored by the Poretzky family.

videos.title: From Privilege to Purpose: Edmond de Rothschild and Zion
videos.description:
Recorded May 26, 2026
Instructor: Dr. James McAuley
What draws a person from comfort into commitment? What leads someone born into extraordinary privilege to take up a cause that will reshape history? In this session, author James McAuley joins us to discuss his new biography of Edmond de Rothschild, one of the most consequential and often overlooked figures in the early history of Zionism. Born into immense wealth and refinement, Rothschild might have remained a patron of art and culture. Instead, he became a central force behind the first Jewish agricultural settlements in Ottoman Palestine.

Drawing on newly uncovered materials, including Rothschild’s own memoir, McAuley traces the personal transformation of a reserved French aristocrat who found purpose in supporting a bold and uncertain vision of a Jewish homeland. Together, we will explore what motivated Rothschild, what his legacy reveals about the early Zionist project, and how his choices continue to shape Israel's story today.

Part of our partnership with the Jewish Lives series, this session offers the opportunity to hear directly from the author and engage with a pivotal moment in modern Jewish history. Participants will receive a discount code to purchase the book upon its release.

videos.title: Week 2-Caring for Life: Judaism, Ethics, and Modern Medicine
videos.description:
Recorded May 19, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Richard Address
Advances in modern medicine have transformed how we live and how we face illness, aging, and the end of life. Along with these advances come difficult and deeply personal questions. How should we respond when treatment prolongs life but cannot relieve suffering? Who bears responsibility for ensuring that people receive the care they need?

In this two-part conversation, Rabbi Richard Address, a leading voice in Jewish conversations about aging and medical ethics, guides us through two pressing issues: medical aid in dying and the challenge of paying for and accessing health care. Drawing on classical Jewish sources and contemporary rabbinic responses, we will explore how Jewish tradition balances the sanctity of life with compassion, dignity, and communal responsibility. How do ancient texts help us think about medical realities their authors could never have imagined? This series invites thoughtful reflection on how Jewish ethics can guide individuals, families, and communities as we navigate some of the most complex decisions surrounding care, responsibility, and the value of life itself.

videos.title: Jewish Angels: Messengers, Meaning, and Mystery
videos.description:
Recorded May 18, 2026
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
When we think about angels today, certain images come to mind: winged figures, harps and halos, symbols of comfort or protection. But the angels of Jewish tradition are something quite different. In this engaging session, Sandra Lilienthal invites us to explore where angels appear in the Tanach and how they function within those texts. What roles do they actually play? What do they reveal about how Judaism understands divine presence and action in the world? Through accessible text study, we will challenge familiar assumptions and uncover a richer, more nuanced understanding of angels in Jewish thought.

videos.title: Mothers and Midrash: Motherhood in Jewish Text and Tradition
videos.description:
Recorded May 13, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Grossblatt Pessah
What makes someone a mother? The answer may seem obvious, but Jewish texts tell a far more complicated story. In the days following Mother’s Day, when motherhood is often framed in simple and celebratory terms, this session takes a different approach. Rabbi Amy Grossblatt Pessah invites us into a close reading of biblical and rabbinic texts that challenge, expand, and complicate our assumptions about what motherhood is and what it demands.

Through encounters with figures like Hagar, Hannah, and Rachel, we will explore strikingly different models of motherhood: motherhood without power, motherhood defined by longing, and motherhood that extends beyond a lifetime. Along the way, we will ask how these texts construct motherhood as a biological reality, an emotional experience, and a moral or spiritual role, and where those definitions come into tension with one another. Through careful textual study, this session offers more than a single definition of motherhood: it invites us into an ongoing conversation that reflects the complexity of Jewish tradition and the lived experience of those who engage with it.

videos.title: Week 3-Ballots and Power: How Israel Chooses Its Government
videos.description:
Recorded May 11, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Danny Schiff
How does a country without a constitution govern itself? And what really happens when Israelis head to the polls? Israel’s political system is both familiar and entirely unique, shaped by history, ideology, and the ongoing challenge of defining a Jewish and democratic state.

In this three-part course, Rabbi Danny Schiff guides us through the structure, strategy, and stakes of Israeli elections. From the foundational question of how Israel governs without a formal constitution to the complex process of voting and coalition-building, we will explore how power is organized and exercised. We will also turn to the key issues shaping the 2026 elections, asking what matters most to Israeli voters and what those choices mean for Israel’s future.

In this three-part series, we will explore:

Session 1: A Nation Without a Constitution: Why does Israel have no formal constitution? We will examine the development of the Basic Laws, the structure of the Israeli government, and the systems of checks and balances that shape political power.
Session 2: Electing a New Knesset: How do Israeli elections actually work? We will explore the voting system, the strategic decisions voters face, and the complex process of coalition-building that determines who ultimately governs.
Session 3: The Issues at Stake: What will be on the minds of Israeli voters in 2026? We will examine the central issues shaping the election and consider what they reveal about Israel’s identity and future as a Jewish state.

videos.title: Through the Jaffa Gate: A Portal into the Eternal City
videos.description:
Recorded May 11, 2026
Instructor: Shifra Dimbert
What can a single gate tell us about the history of a city? At Jaffa Gate, one of Jerusalem’s most iconic entry points, centuries of history unfold in stone.

Just days after Yom Yerushalayim, we turn to one of the city’s most storied landmarks to explore how Jerusalem has been built, reshaped, and reimagined across time. In this session, Shifra Dimbert, a guide at the Tower of David Jerusalem Museum, takes us on a journey through the history of Jaffa Gate from the Ottoman period to the present day. Beginning with Sultan Suleiman’s construction of the Old City walls, we will trace how this gateway has been shaped and reshaped by empires, armies, and modern development. As we follow the changes to the gate and its surroundings, from imperial design, to colonial intervention, to contemporary transformation, we will uncover how each era has left its mark on the physical and symbolic landscape of Jerusalem. Through this focused lens, the story of Jaffa Gate becomes a portal into the broader history of Jerusalem itself.

videos.title: Week 2-The Empowered Caregiver: Chesed and Kavod in Dementia Care
videos.description:
Recorded May 7, 2026
Instructors: Jane Michaels Grauman, LCSW and Yael Wyte, MSW
Caring for someone living with dementia can change the rhythms of daily life, and it can also change the way you speak, listen, and respond to the people you love most. This two-part Empowered Caregiver series, offered in partnership with the Alzheimer’s Association, brings practical caregiving tools into a Melton context shaped by the values that so often carry caregivers through: kavod (dignity), chesed (lovingkindness), and the steady work of showing up, again and again.
 
Led by Janie Michaels Grauman, LCSW, an Alzheimer’s Association Community Educator, these sessions offer clear guidance, usable strategies, and space for real questions. Alzheimer's Association Program & Education Manager Yael Wyte will help frame each class so the skills don’t just feel clinical but are connected to meaning, resilience, and relationships.

Class 1: Communicating Effectively
Learn how dementia affects communication and explore concrete tips for communicating with greater clarity and less stress, whether you’re speaking with family and friends or advocating with health care professionals.
Class 2: Responding to Dementia-Related Behaviors
Understand common behavior changes as a form of communication, learn non-medical approaches to responding, and recognize when it may be time to seek additional support.

videos.title: Week 10-Israeli Literature as a Window into a Diverse and Vibrant Society
videos.description:
Recorded May 4, 2026
Instructor: Aviva Laskov
What does it mean to long for a homeland, to wrestle with identity, or to seek peace in a land shaped by conflict and hope? In this 10-week course, you’ll explore the richness and complexity of Israeli society through its literature: raw, poetic, provocative, and deeply human.

Designed and taught by Israeli educator Aviva Laskov, this course is a rare opportunity to learn with the teacher who created this popular Melton Signature Course, now offered online via Melton+ for the first time. You’ll encounter the voices of Israel’s most celebrated writers—including Leah Goldberg, Yehuda Amichai, Dalia Ravikovitch, and Amos Oz—alongside powerful works by immigrants, Mizrahi poets, and others outside the cultural mainstream. From questions of language and land to war, peace, and personal memory, each session offers a new literary lens into the Israeli experience.

Whether you’re a seasoned reader or just beginning to explore Israeli literature, this journey promises fresh insights, layered discussions, and a deeper understanding of a proud and complex society.

Table of Contents: 

1. If I Forget Thee... Longing For Zion
2. Our Words:  Speak Hebrew!
3. Tel Aviv: The First Hebrew City
4. A Poet and His City: Amichai’s Jerusalem
5. The Trauma of the Shoah
6. From the Four Corners: Immigrants Write Themselves into Israeli Society
7. The Clash Between Land and Identities
8. Pro Patria Mori: War and Its Impact
9. Yearning For Peace
10. On a Personal Note

videos.title: Week 1-The Empowered Caregiver: Chesed and Kavod in Dementia Care
videos.description:
Recorded April 30, 2026
Instructors: Janie Michaels Grauman, LCSW and Yael Wyte, MSW

videos.title: Listening at Scale: Testimony, Technology, and Memory
videos.description:
Recorded April 30, 2026
Instructor: Professor Renana Keydar
From the courtroom to the cloud, how can new technologies deepen the way we bear witness to atrocity? Join Professor Renana Keydar of The Hebrew University of Jerusalem, an internationally recognized scholar at the intersection of law, digital humanities, and Holocaust studies, as she traces four defining moments in the evolution of testimony: the Nuremberg Tribunal, the Eichmann Trial, the rise of Holocaust archives in the 1990s, and today’s urgent efforts to document October 7 through the Edut 710 initiative.

Blending legal insight, historical depth, and cutting-edge computational analysis, Dr. Keydar invites us to consider how algorithms and AI can expand rather than replace the human act of listening. As these tools open new ways to engage vast archives of survivor testimony, this course asks a profound question: what does it mean to remember at scale, and how might technology help carry these voices into the future?

videos.title: The Scourge and the Surge: American Jews After October 7
videos.description:
Recorded April 28, 2026
Instructors: Professor Jonathan D. Sarna and Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz
Since the October 7 attacks, American Jewish life has been shaped by two powerful and seemingly contradictory forces: a rise in antisemitism and a renewed surge in Jewish engagement. How should we understand this moment in the longer story of American Jewish history?

In this special Melton+ conversation, renowned historian Dr. Jonathan Sarna joins Melton International Director Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz to reflect on how American Jews have responded to the events of October 7 and their aftermath. Drawing on his recent work, Sarna will explore how moments of crisis have often produced both fear and renewal within the Jewish community. Looking back to earlier periods of rising antisemitism in the United States—including the late nineteenth-century experiences of leaders such as Cyrus Adler and Henrietta Szold—we'll place the current moment in historical perspective. What patterns from the past can help us understand the present? And what might they suggest about the future of American Jewish life? This deep conversation invites us to step back from the headlines and consider how American Jews have responded to challenge before... and how those responses have shaped the community we know today.

videos.title: The Braid - On Beig Jewish Now (Class)
videos.description:
Recorded April 23, 20206
Instructor: Rabbi Rachel Bovitz
True stories. Tough times. Jewish values in action.

Melton+ is thrilled to partner with The Braid, the go-to Jewish story company, to bring their 2026 season of signature storytelling theatre to our community of learners. Each professionally acted production includes about a dozen true stories, and each show will be followed by a Melton+ learning session that delves into the issues with rigor, discussion, and Jewish depth only Melton can provide.

The season began with Do the Right Thing in February and continues with On Being Jewish Now, a dramatic retelling of selections from Zibby Owens's intimate and hopeful collection of meaningful and inspiring essays from Artists Against Antisemitism about how life has changed since the attacks on October 7th, 2023. Accompanying this performance on a separate date, Melton Executive Director Rabbi Rachel Bovitz will lead an interactive Melton+ discussion addressing the issues raised by the performance. Together, we will explore the common threads that bind us together in a post-October 7 world.

About On Being Jewish Now...

As featured in The Hollywood Reporter, this landmark adaptation of Zibby Owens' bestseller On Being Jewish Now is among the first theatrical works to explore the American Jewish experience in a post-October 7 world. Presented in The Braid’s signature style, professional actors bring to life true stories of humor, hope, heartbreak, and resilience from an array of writers, thinkers, and cultural voices. You’ll be gripped by noted author Joanna Rakoff’s (My Salinger Year) tale of visiting a hidden underground synagogue in Spain. Laugh as famed actor Mark Feuerstein (Royal Pains) shares about being a 50-year-old man who breakdances. Feel the suspense as esteemed Rabbi Sharon Brous and her family encounter people in Vietnam demanding to know if they’re Jews. And be uplifted by Zibby Owens’s own powerful self-reflection about the need to stand up and make one’s voice heard, plus other stories.

videos.title: Antisemitism in America: Old Hatred, Newly Urgent
videos.description:
Recorded April 23, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Jeffrey K. Salkin
Antisemitism is on the rise in the United States, and the moment demands more than outrage: it demands understanding. In this session, Rabbi Jeffrey K. Salkin traces antisemitism in American history, showing how older patterns from Europe and elsewhere took root here, and how they helped shape a distinctly American form of Jew-hatred. Along the way, he’ll highlight the recurring themes that keep reappearing, even when the headlines change.

A widely read writer and sought-after commentator, Rabbi Salkin brings clarity, candor, and story-driven teaching to a subject that can feel overwhelming. Drawing on major episodes and cultural flashpoints, he’ll help you recognize these patterns, sharpen how you talk about antisemitism, and leave with language for engaging the conversation with greater precision and moral confidence today.

videos.title: From Siren to Celebration: Yom Hazikaron and Yom Ha'atzmaut in Israel
videos.description:
Recorded April 22, 2026
Instructor: Avi Ben-Hur
In Israel, sacrifice and achievement are inseparable. As the sirens of Yom HaZikaron honor Israel’s fallen soldiers and victims of terror, the country transitions almost immediately into the celebrations of Yom HaAtzmaut. How does a society move from mourning to independence in a single day? Join longtime Melton educator Avi Ben-Hur to explore the meaning behind these powerful national rituals, from the ceremonies on Mount Herzl to the idea of a civic culture that binds a diverse society together.

videos.title: A Sneak Peek at Argentina and Uruguay
videos.description:
Recorded April 20, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Ernesto Yatta

Curious what Jewish life looks like in Argentina and Uruguay? This preview class offers a first look at an upcoming Melton World Travel journey that weaves together vibrant communities, powerful sites of memory, and the rich cultural life of South America. Together, we will explore the places and experiences that shape this upcoming trip. From the Jewish neighborhoods of Buenos Aires to the community life of Montevideo, from the colorful streets of La Boca to the solemn memorials of the AMIA and the Holocaust monument, this session introduces the stories, settings, and encounters that define the experience. We will also look ahead to moments of connection, including Shabbat with local communities, learning at the Seminario Rabínico Latinoamericano, and the art, architecture, and music that bring these cities to life. Join us for this sneak peek into a journey of memory, community, and discovery across Argentina and Uruguay.

videos.title: Week 8-Israeli Literature as a Window into a Diverse and Vibrant Society
videos.description:
Recorded April 20, 2026
Instructor: Aviva Laskov
What does it mean to long for a homeland, to wrestle with identity, or to seek peace in a land shaped by conflict and hope? In this 10-week course, you’ll explore the richness and complexity of Israeli society through its literature: raw, poetic, provocative, and deeply human.

Designed and taught by Israeli educator Aviva Laskov, this course is a rare opportunity to learn with the teacher who created this popular Melton Signature Course, now offered online via Melton+ for the first time. You’ll encounter the voices of Israel’s most celebrated writers—including Leah Goldberg, Yehuda Amichai, Dalia Ravikovitch, and Amos Oz—alongside powerful works by immigrants, Mizrahi poets, and others outside the cultural mainstream. From questions of language and land to war, peace, and personal memory, each session offers a new literary lens into the Israeli experience.

Whether you’re a seasoned reader or just beginning to explore Israeli literature, this journey promises fresh insights, layered discussions, and a deeper understanding of a proud and complex society.

Table of Contents: 
1. If I Forget Thee... Longing For Zion
2. Our Words:  Speak Hebrew!
3. Tel Aviv: The First Hebrew City
4. A Poet and His City: Amichai’s Jerusalem
5. The Trauma of the Shoah
6. From the Four Corners: Immigrants Write Themselves into Israeli Society
7. The Clash Between Land and Identities
8. Pro Patria Mori: War and Its Impact
9. Yearning For Peace
10. On a Personal Note

videos.title: Antisemitism Now: Left, Right, and Online
videos.description:
Recorded April 14, 2026
Instructor: Amy Frake
Antisemitism today crosses political lines, and it rarely arrives with a warning label. In this session, Amy Frake, Senior Associate Director of Education at Holocaust Museum Houston, helps us trace the narratives about Jews and Israel that circulate across the contemporary left and right, and then surge online through memes, slogans, and viral “hot takes.” We’ll examine how these ideas frame Jews and Zionism, what emotions they tap, and why they spread so quickly.

Offered in partnership with Holocaust Museum Houston, this program brings an educator’s lens to one of the most urgent challenges of our moment: recognizing antisemitism when it’s dressed up as activism, ideology, or “just asking questions,” and responding without losing moral clarity. Along the way, we’ll consider a range of response models, from the ADL to public voices such as David Baddiel and Bret Stephens, and leave with language and tools you can use in real conversations, on campus, at work, and online.

videos.title: A Haggadah for This Year: Passover Texts, Persistent Questions
videos.description:
Recorded March 26, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Wallk
At the Seder table, we don’t just retell an ancient story. We use the Haggadah to locate ourselves inside it. In this 90-minute session, Rabbi Amy Wallk guides us through the traditional text to ask what it can help us see about Jewish life right now, including the questions and tensions many of us carry about Israel. Bring your own Haggadah so you can follow the passages we’re reading together and notice how familiar words can sound newly urgent. Together, we’ll move through four powerful stopping points in the Haggadah: the Four Children and the different ways we ask, argue, and stay silent; Arami oved avi and the meaning of sovereignty after generations of vulnerability; B’chol dor vador and the challenge of seeing ourselves, and our people, in the Exodus story this year; and V’hi she’amdah and Chad Gadya, which confront threat, resilience, and the strength that faith can offer. Join us for a text-rich, honest conversation that helps make the Seder not only a remembrance of the past, but a lens for the present.

videos.title: Hallel at the Seder: Redemption in Two Parts
videos.description:
Recorded March 25, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Jay Stein
At our Passover Seder, we sing Hallel, but we do something unusual. We begin the song before the meal and complete it much later, after we eat the Afikomen. In this session, Rabbi Jay Stein explores what this structure suggests about the Jewish perspective on redemption and gratitude. He will examine why Hallel is divided at the Seder, why it is sometimes recited in full and sometimes only partially, and how the structure of the Seder itself carries theological meaning. Drawing on classical sources and modern voices, we will consider how Jewish tradition understands redemption not as a single triumphant moment, but as a process that unfolds. The song reflects the journey: narrated, embodied, and only then completed. Hallel becomes more than familiar words from the Seder table. It becomes a guide for how we move from constriction to expansiveness, from memory to gratitude, and from story to lived experience. Join us for a deeper look at the song that shapes our celebration of freedom.

videos.title: The Whole Megillah: Mordechai, Esther, and the Tension Between the Lines
videos.description:
Recorded March 24, 2026
Instructor: Dr. Ziva Hassenfeld
They are two of the most iconic heroes in Jewish history — but what kind of family did they form? The Book of Esther hints at a deep, complicated relationship between Mordecai and Esther. Guardian and dependent, insider and upstander, they both act with courage and coordination to save their people. Yet the text also hints at emotional strain, silence, and moral disagreement. What do we make of Esther’s hesitation...or Mordechai’s insistence? What happens when obligation and autonomy collide? Guided by Dr. Ziva R. Hassenfeld, a scholar of Bible pedagogy and reader interpretation, this class will slow down to notice what the text doesn’t say out loud but still asks us to imagine. Drawing on her deep expertise in how people make meaning of sacred stories, Dr. Ziva invites learners into the emotional complexity and ethical ambiguity of the Megillah, treating conflict not as failure but as a reflection of real-life relationships and moral growth.

videos.title: Week 3-Clickable Wisdom: Uncover Everyday Ethics with Sefaria
videos.description:
Recorded March 18, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Benjamin Kramer
How does a Jewish idea take shape, grow, and evolve over generations? Judaism’s ethical vocabulary—privacy, competition, responsibility—did not emerge fully formed. These values were debated, refined, and reimagined by Jews across time, creating a living conversation that continues into our own lives. Clickable Wisdom invites learners into that conversation through a three-part exploration of the everyday ethical questions that define human interaction.

Developed in partnership with Sefaria, a dynamic living library of Jewish texts online, this course uses the power of technology to bring our tradition’s ongoing dialogue to life. In each session, learners will use Sefaria’s platform to trace Jewish ideas from their biblical roots through rabbinic interpretation and into contemporary application.

Together, we will explore:

The right and responsibility of privacy, and how Jewish sources imagine personal boundaries;
The ethics of competition, from markets to relationships, and what fairness looks like in a Jewish context;
The weight and responsibility of speech, and how generations of teachers have understood words as both tools and moral obligations.

Whether you are curious about Jewish ethics, eager to experiment with digital text tools, or hoping to see how ancient wisdom speaks to everyday life, this course offers a thoughtful, accessible path into the heart of Jewish learning.

videos.title: The Talking Golem: A Jewish Conversation on AI
videos.description:
Recorded March 17, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Debra EisenmanWhat makes us human in an age of intelligent machines? In this one-hour Taste of Melton class, Rabbi Debra Eisenman explores timeless Jewish texts alongside today’s most pressing technological questions. From the Torah’s vision of humanity created in the image of God to the Talmudic legend of the golem, we will ask whether speech, intellect, or moral discernment defines personhood, and whether a chatbot could ever be considered a friend, a teacher, or something more. This thought-provoking session invites learners to reflect on the sacred responsibility of human creativity in a rapidly evolving world. Part of the Taste of Melton series, this class is designed especially for those new to the Melton method, offering an engaging introduction to text-based, interactive Jewish learning. At the same time, its rich sources and contemporary relevance make it equally compelling for experienced Melton learners seeking a fresh and timely conversation.

videos.title: Neshama Carlbach
What is the Hallel Jewish Prayer of Praise
הודו לה' [לעולם חסדו] - לב ישראל חי

videos.title: The Mitzvah of the Mind: Caring for Cognitive Health
videos.description:
Recorded March 12, 2026
Instructors: Dr. Mary Norman and Yael Wyte, MSW
How do we care for our minds as we age? And what might Jewish wisdom add to today’s most promising research on brain health? In this data-rich session, Dr. Mary Norman, board-certified geriatric physician and founder of Just This Moment, shares clear, evidence-based insights into brain health, exploring how lifestyle choices such as movement, nutrition, intellectual engagement, and social connection can meaningfully support cognitive function over time. Drawing on her decades of experience in geriatric medicine and dementia care, Dr. Norman translates research into practical guidance for everyday life.

Framing the conversation, Yael Wyte, MSW, Program & Education Manager for the California Southland Chapter of the Alzheimer’s Association, grounds the discussion in Jewish sources that speak to our responsibility to care for body and soul. Together, they bridge contemporary medical science and enduring Jewish values, offering a holistic vision of well-being rooted in knowledge, community, and sacred obligation. Participants leave this session informed, inspired, and empowered to approach brain health not only as a medical concern, but as an expression of intentional Jewish living.

videos.title: B'tzelem: Created to Create
videos.description:
Recorded March 11, 2026
Instructor: Pam Moore
Jewish tradition begins with an act of creation and places human creativity at its center. In this interactive and reflective session, writer and stand-up comic Pam Moore explores what it means to live and create as beings made b’tzelem Elohim, in the image of a Creator. Drawing on Torah, rabbinic wisdom, and contemporary Jewish voices, we’ll consider creativity as a sacred capacity woven into Jewish life itself.

At the heart of the class is the idea of shefa, the flow of divine abundance that moves through the world and through us when we are open to giving and receiving. Together, we explore creativity as a spiritual practice rooted in attention, openness, and connection. Along the way, Pam shares accessible, practical ways to nurture creativity in everyday life through writing, movement, rest, and intentional focus. This class invites you to reconnect with your creative spark and discover how Jewish ideas can nourish inspiration, meaning, and renewal.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: Washington and the War
videos.description:
Recorded March 10, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski
Rabbi Uri Pilichowski brings an unusually informed, on-the-ground perspective in this urgent Melton Meets the Moment session. In the days leading up to the joint US/Israel operation against Iran, he was at the White House in conversation with well-placed policy professionals and trusted staff, hearing firsthand how the administration was thinking about Iran’s future. He then returned to Israel in time for Shabbat and the opening of Operation Roaring Lion and Operation Epic Fury.
 
Join us for a clear background on Iran’s long-standing hostility toward Israel and America, an explanation of why this war is unfolding now, and words of chizuk and inspiration that place this moment in the larger story of Torah and Zionism. You may come for the behind-the-scenes insight, but Rabbi Pilichowski’s closing frame will take you somewhere far deeper and more surprising than the headlines.

videos.title: Ask the Rabbis: What’s Next for North American Judaism with Rabbi Efrem Goldberg
videos.description:
Recorded March 5, 2026
Instructors: Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz and Rabbi Efrem Goldberg
What’s really happening in American Jewish life, and where are we headed?  In this ground-breaking series, Melton+ faculty sit down with some of the most prominent and influential congregational rabbis in North America for candid, wide-ranging conversations about the challenges and possibilities facing Jewish communities today. From synagogue life and spiritual leadership to shifting demographics and new models of connection, no topic is off the table.  We have run one session every other month, beginning in November 2025. Our first guest was Rabbi David Wolpe, the Max Webb Rabbi Emeritus at Sinai Temple in Los Angeles, and, in January, Rabbi Rachel Bovitz spoke with Rabbi Robyn Fryer Bodzin of Toronto's Beth Tzedeq, the largest synagogue in North America.

For our third conversation, Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz speaks with Rabbi Efrem Goldberg, the Senior Rabbi of the Boca Raton Synagogue, the largest Orthodox synagogue in the Southeast United States. Serving a vibrant and rapidly growing community of over 1,000 member families and more than 1,000 children, BRS offers expansive programming in adult education, youth engagement, and community outreach, making it a national model for Orthodox communal growth and innovation. Rabbi Goldberg is a nationally respected voice on matters of faith, leadership, and contemporary Jewish life. His weekly “Parsha Perspectives” podcast and the popular interview series “Behind the Bima” reach thousands of listeners across the Jewish spectrum and beyond. He is a frequent contributor to the Times of Israel, and his talks—often addressing spiritual resilience, modern ethics, and Jewish unity—are widely shared across social media, YouTube, and communal forums well beyond the Orthodox world. He also holds several influential leadership roles, serving nationally as an authority on Kashrut, conversion, and U.S.–Israel advocacy.

videos.title: The Wisdom of Wealth- Maximizing Your Jewish Philanthropic Legacy
videos.description:
Recorded March 5, 2026
Instructor: Lottie Nilsen, CAP®, CFRE
How can your giving reflect not just your values today, but your hopes for the Jewish future? Melton has always been powered by visionary learners who believe in the value of Jewish education and who want to ensure it continues for generations to come. In this free seminar, legacy and fundraising expert Lottie Nilsen offers practical guidance on how to do just that. From planned giving and donor-advised funds to the evolving tax landscape, we’ll explore how to align your philanthropy with your values and make a lasting impact on the communities and causes you care about most. In true Melton fashion, the conversation will be grounded in enduring Torah principles: responsibility, generosity, Torah study, and the sacred obligation to pass wisdom forward.

videos.title: Week 1-Clickable Wisdom- Uncover Everyday Ethics with Sefaria
Melton Meets the Moment: Israel and the United States at War with Iran
videos.description:
Recorded March 4, 2026
Instructor: Iftah Burman, Middle East Learning Academy
As this war enters its fifth day, the situation between Israel, the United States, and Iran continues to evolve rapidly. The headlines are urgent, the stakes are high, and many in our community are seeking a clear, reliable understanding of what is happening and what it means. In this special Melton Meets the Moment briefing, Middle East scholar and geopolitical expert Iftah Burman will provide a timely analysis of the unfolding conflict. He will examine the military developments of the first days of the war, the strategic objectives of Israel and the United States, Iran's regional posture and use of proxy forces, and the broader implications for the Middle East and global stability. Beyond the battlefield, we will also consider what this moment means on the ground in Israel. How are daily life, security, and national resilience being tested? What are families experiencing as events continue to unfold?

Melton Meets the Moment sessions are designed to help our community respond to current events with knowledge, context, and perspective. Join us as we seek clarity and understanding at a pivotal moment in a rapidly changing situation.

videos.title: Week 3-Restless Soul: Mordecai Kaplan and the Reinvention of American Judaism
videos.description:
Recorded February 26, 2026
Instructors: Rabbi Ed Feinstein and Professor Jenna Weissman Joselit
Rabbi Mordecai Kaplan was admired by many, challenged by others, and never stopped trying to reinvent what it meant to live a Jewish life in the modern world. In this special three-part Melton+ series presented in cooperation with Valley Beth Shalom, Rabbi Ed Feinstein sits down with historian Jenna Weissman Joselit, author of the forthcoming Jewish Lives biography Mordecai M. Kaplan: Restless Soul, for a candid and compelling exploration of Kaplan’s life and legacy. Part 1: Kaplan’s World – An introduction to the early 20th-century American Jewish landscape and the tensions that gave rise to Kaplan’s bold critique.
Part 2: Upheaval – A close and personal look at how Kaplan transformed not only American Jewish life but also himself.
Part 3: In His Own Words – A deep dive into Kaplan’s writings, including some of his earliest and most overlooked works.
Jenna Weissman Joselit, Charles E. Smith Professor of Judaic Studies and Professor of History at George Washington University, is known for her engaging, accessible, and original approach to Jewish cultural history. With warmth, insight, and a healthy dose of humor, Rabbi Ed Feinstein—a Melton+ favorite—guides the conversation and helps bring Kaplan’s voice into dialogue with the Jewish present.

videos.title: Subjects of Citizens? Jewish Law, French Power, and Napoleon’s Sanhedrin
videos.description:
Recorded February 25, 2026
Instructor: Dr. Gil Graff
Why did Napoleon convene a Jewish Sanhedrin, and what did he hope to gain? In 1806 and 1807, the French emperor called together a group of Jewish leaders to answer twelve pointed questions about Jewish law, citizenship, and loyalty to the modern state. What followed was both unprecedented and deeply revealing: a bold experiment in Jewish-state relations, modern religious authority, and public identity.

In this thought-provoking session, Dr. Gil Graff explores the historical Sanhedrin Napoleon created, the legal and ethical questions it raised—including intermarriage, allegiance to the state, and the role of halacha in modern society—and the echoes it still generates today. We’ll also examine earlier and later attempts to reestablish the Sanhedrin, asking what the Sanhedrin represented to those seeking to reconstitute it and what this ancient institution symbolizes.

videos.title: From Sinai to Mordechai- Coercion, Commitment, and Covenant
videos.description:
Recorded February 25, 2026
Instructor: Joe Septimus
“Rabbi Avdimi bar Ḥama bar Ḥasa said: The Holy One, Blessed be He, held the mountain over them and said, ‘If you accept the Torah, excellent. If not, there will be your burial.’“— Babylonian Talmud, Shabbat 88a

How can covenant begin under threat? In this provocative session, Joe Septimus will guide us through one of the most unsettling stories in the Talmud: the giving of the Torah at Sinai, framed by the rabbis not as mutual agreement but as divine coercion. The Talmudic story continues with the Rabbis reading a verse from the Book of Esther (written centuries later) as a reversal of the initial coercion into a willing, joyful, fully human acceptance of Torah. Why does our Talmudic tradition link these two moments? What connects the awesome thunder of Sinai with the joyful celebrations of Purim? And what does it mean to "willingly" reaffirm something which was previously accepted under duress? This class invites you into one of the most profound theological tensions in Jewish tradition and asks: What does it mean to choose the covenant all over again?

videos.title: Week 1-Israeli Literature as a Window into a Diverse and Vibrant Society
videos.description:
Recorded February 23, 2025
Instructor: Aviva Laskov
What does it mean to long for a homeland, to wrestle with identity, or to seek peace in a land shaped by conflict and hope? In this 10-week course, you’ll explore the richness and complexity of Israeli society through its literature: raw, poetic, provocative, and deeply human.

Designed and taught by Israeli educator Aviva Laskov, this course is a rare opportunity to learn with the teacher who created this popular Melton Signature Course, now offered online via Melton+ for the first time. You’ll encounter the voices of Israel’s most celebrated writers—including Leah Goldberg, Yehuda Amichai, Dalia Ravikovitch, and Amos Oz—alongside powerful works by immigrants, Mizrahi poets, and others outside the cultural mainstream. From questions of language and land to war, peace, and personal memory, each session offers a new literary lens into the Israeli experience. Whether you’re a seasoned reader or just beginning to explore Israeli literature, this journey promises fresh insights, layered discussions, and a deeper understanding of a proud and complex society.

Table of Contents: 
1. If I Forget Thee... Longing For Zion
2. Our Words:  Speak Hebrew!
3. Tel Aviv: The First Hebrew City
4. A Poet and His City: Amichai’s Jerusalem
5. The Trauma of the Shoah
6. From the Four Corners: Immigrants Write Themselves into Israeli Society
7. The Clash Between Land and Identities
8. Pro Patria Mori: War and Its Impact
9. Yearning For Peace
10. On a Personal Note

videos.title: Melton & The Braid - Do the Right Thing
videos.description:
Recorded February 19, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Adam Schaffer
True stories. Tough choices. Jewish values in action. Melton+ partners with The Braid, the go-to Jewish story company, to bring their 2026 season of signature storytelling theatre to our community of learners. Each professionally acted production includes about a dozen true stories, and each show is followed by a Melton+ learning session that delves into the issues with rigor, discussion, and Jewish depth only Melton can provide.

The season begins with Do the Right Thing, a gripping and inspiring exploration of what it means to act with integrity, even when it’s really hard. With humor, heart, and rising stakes, this performance brings to life Jewish stories of questioning power, speaking up, and standing alone for what’s right. You’ll experience the stories of: a nervous flier who halts a plane on the runway after spotting what he fears is an engine fire, a student journalist who saves lives by withholding a scoop during a campus protest, and a woman trying to deliver a Holocaust-era letter to the surviving family. These are real voices, unforgettable dilemmas, and timeless questions of conscience.

This session is an interactive Melton+ discussion led by Rabbi Adam Schaffer, Director of the Jewish Learning Cooperative at HaMakom in West Hills, CA. Together, we’ll ask: What do Jewish texts and traditions teach us about civil courage, moral clarity, and doing the right thing—especially when no one else will?

videos.title: The Roads from Russia: Russian Roots of Israel's Streets
videos.description:
Recorded February 17, 2026
Instructor: Evgenia KempinskiWho were the people behind the names on Israel’s street signs? And what do their stories reveal about the birth of a nation?

In this vivid and engaging virtual tour, Jewish heritage educator Evgenia Kempinski takes us on a journey through the streets of Tel Aviv, Jerusalem, and Haifa. We’ll stop at names like Trumpeldor, Arlosoroff, Sokolov, Gruzenberg, and Hankin, then trace them back to their origins in the Russian Empire. From small shtetls to major cities in modern-day Ukraine, Belarus, Poland, and Russia, we’ll explore the Eastern European roots of the Zionist movement through the lives of poets, politicians, pioneers, and other remarkable figures who helped shape Jewish history. This is more than a tour. It is a layered exploration of memory, migration, and meaning. Come ready to travel through time and across continents as we uncover the stories behind the signs.

videos.title: MeltonX Winter 2026 - Faculty Sessions
videos.description:

videos.title: Week 3-Theodor Herzl: Dreaming a Jewish Future
videos.description:
Recorded February 16, 2026
Instructor: Professor Derek Penslar
Theodor Herzl wasn’t the first to imagine a Jewish homeland, but his vision, charisma, and political acumen turned that dream into a global movement. In this special Melton+ course, produced in partnership with Keren Kayemeth LeIsrael-Jewish National Fund in Israel as well as Jewish National Fund-USA, and the World Zionist Village - The World’s First Global Campus for the Greatest Conversation on Zionism!, renowned historian Professor Derek Penslar, author of Theodor Herzl: The Charismatic Leader, guides us through Herzl’s personal journey, public leadership, and prophetic imagination.

The course marks the 130th anniversary of Herzl’s Der Judenstaat, the publication that transformed a private alarm into a public program and helped launch a modern political movement. This anniversary offers a timely opportunity to revisit Herzl’s ideas with fresh eyes and consider how his bold propositions continue to echo in Jewish life. Over three sessions, we’ll trace how Herzl became the unlikely father of modern political Zionism and how his deeds and writings continue to shape conversations about Jewish peoplehood and the State of Israel today.

Session 1 - Herzl’s Path to Zionism: What led an assimilated Jewish journalist to become an impassioned Zionist?
Session 2 - Herzl as a Zionist Leader: Through Herzl’s words and actions, we explore his leadership style, the movement he created, and the challenges he faced.
Session 3 - Herzl’s Vision of the Jewish Future: We conclude with Altneuland, Herzl’s utopian novel, asking how his imagined future speaks to today’s realities—and dreams yet unfulfilled.
Whether you’re new to Herzl or returning to his legacy with fresh eyes, this class offers an opportunity to explore the roots of Zionism with one of the foremost scholars of modern Jewish history.

videos.title: Statutes and Stories: Learning from Torah's Two Voices
videos.description:
Recorded February 11, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Hillel LichtmanThe Torah speaks in two voices: one of law, and one of story. But if Jewish life is shaped by commandments, why are so many of the Torah’s most powerful moments found in its narratives? And can those stories be used to guide real-life decisions?

In this provocative session, Rabbi Hillel Lichtman explores how Jewish legal authorities have drawn on biblical stories to shape contemporary behavior. What happens when the law is silent, but a narrative seems relevant? Can the actions of our ancestors serve as precedent, even when the Torah doesn’t tell us whether they were right or wrong? Through real cases drawn from rabbinic literature—including debates around the ethics of civilian casualties and the drafting of yeshiva students—we’ll examine how stories have sometimes filled the gaps between law and life. And we’ll ask: when does narrative offer moral clarity, and when does it raise more questions than it answers?

videos.title: The Patient as a Person: A Jewish Vision of Care and Dignity
videos.description:
Recorded February 11, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Richard Address
What does it mean to treat a patient as a full human being, as more than just a body, not as a diagnosis but as a soul created in the Divine image? In this course, Rabbi Richard Address, founder and director of Jewish Sacred Aging, guides a thoughtful exploration of Abraham Joshua Heschel’s landmark essay The Patient as a Person, offering a Jewish vision for holistic, dignified health care rooted in compassion and sacred responsibility. As medicine advances and life spans lengthen, we face new ethical challenges; this class asks how ancient wisdom can help us respond with humanity, respect, and care.

Drawing on Heschel’s writings, Jewish texts, and decades of experience in aging, healthcare, and pastoral care, Rabbi Address invites us into a thoughtful conversation about vulnerability, empathy, and the moral worth of every individual in the context of hospitals, communities, and families. What does it mean to uphold dignity in a system driven by efficiency? How can we center the soul alongside the body? And how might Jewish values help us reimagine healing itself?

videos.title: Week 6-OMG, Can You Believe?!
videos.description:
Recorded February 9, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Amy S. Wallk
In this six-week exploratory course, we dive into the BIG questions around God and God’s relationship to Humankind. We’ll examine the many different understandings of God, free will, and mitzvot expressed by Jewish philosophers, rabbis, leaders past and present – and you. We’ll explore the divine, the prophetic, and even the supernatural, as we amplify and dissect your deepest God-questions. Just do not expect to leave with any final answers.

Table of Contents

Lesson 1 - God-ness Gracious
Lesson 2 - The Human Journey: Are we there yet?!
Lesson 3 - If not-for-Prophets...
Lesson 4 - The Super, the Natural, and the Supernatural
Lesson 5 - Wins and Sins: Do God and Free Will Go Together?
Lesson 6 - There’s a Mitzvah for that!

videos.title: Three Trees, Three Transformations: A Tu B'Shevat Bibliodrama
videos.description:
Recorded February 3, 2026
Instructor: Yael Unterman
Tu B’Shevat invites us to reflect on trees both as part of the natural world and as powerful symbols of growth, change, and awakening. In this experiential Torah study session, we’ll explore three trees that appear at transformative points in the lives of biblical figures: the Tree of Knowledge, the Burning Bush, and the Palm of Deborah.

Guided by internationally renowned educator and facilitator Yael Unterman, we’ll use Bibliodrama, a creative and reflective approach to Torah study that invites participants to step into the voices and perspectives of biblical characters. Through this process, the text becomes personal, dynamic, and alive. No acting is required: just imagination, deep listening, and a willingness to encounter the Torah in a new way.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment- Australian Jewish Leadership, Learning, and Life
videos.description:
Recorded January 28, 20206
Instructor: Rabbi Benjamin Elton
In this timely and inspiring Melton Meets the Moment session, Rabbi Dr. Benjamin Elton, Chief Minister of The Great Synagogue in Sydney, offers a ground-level update on Jewish communal life in Australia, alongside a Torah-rich reflection on resilience, leadership, and renewal. Drawing on the symbolism of Pesach Sheni — the “Second Passover,” a biblical opportunity to try again after missing the first — Rabbi Elton invites us to consider what second chances look like at the communal level. He’ll also explore the golden years of Australian Jewry and share reflections on Chanukah as a timeless response to challenge and change. This is a unique chance to hear directly from a leading voice in Australian Jewish life, and to learn Torah that meets the moment with strength, dignity, and clarity.

videos.title: What Comes After Freedom? The Questions that Follow Redemption
videos.description:
Recorded January 30, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Grossblatt Pessah
The sea has split, and the people are free, but the story is far from over.
The Splitting of the Sea is one of the most breathtaking miracles in the Torah, and it’s followed immediately by fear, hunger, exhaustion, and doubt. Why does suffering continue after redemption? And what does it mean to build faith after the miracle, not just during it? In this emotionally honest exploration, Rabbi Amy Grossblatt Pessah guides us through the Torah’s raw portrayal of post-traumatic struggle and the spiritual growth that can emerge from it.
This is a journey for anyone who has ever felt uncertain after a breakthrough or unsteady in the wake of survival. Together, we will explore how hope can return slowly, how trust can be rebuilt, and how strength can take root in the very places we once felt weakest.

videos.title: Week 2-Happiness on Command: The Obligation and Challenge of Joy
videos.description:
Recorded January 27, 2026
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
Judaism doesn’t just celebrate joy, it commands it. Our tradition treats simcha not as a fleeting emotion, but as a spiritual practice. In this two-part series with Dr. Sandra Lilienthal, we’ll explore joy as a mitzvah: something to cultivate, to practice, and to bring into the lives of others, even when it doesn’t come easily.

Drawing on biblical, rabbinic, medieval, and modern sources, we’ll ask: What does it mean to “serve God with joy”? Why are wine, song, and dance such essential ingredients in Jewish celebration? And how can ritual help us access joy, even in hard times? Come ready to rethink what joy really means, and why it matters.

Session 1: “And You Shall Rejoice Before the Lord” — Simcha as Obligation
In this session, we’ll explore the surprising idea that joy isn’t optional in Jewish life: it’s a requirement. Through biblical and rabbinic sources, we’ll examine how and why our tradition mandates simcha, and what it means to pursue happiness as a spiritual and ethical responsibility.

Session 2: “Bringing Joy to the Bride, the Guest, and the World” — The Practice of Simcha
Joy isn’t just a feeling; it’s an action. In this session, we’ll look at the rituals and practices that cultivate joy in Jewish tradition, from weddings to holidays to everyday acts of kindness. What does it mean to help someone else feel joy? And how can those practices help us find our own?

videos.title: Wailing at the Window: What the Shofar Wants Us to Hear
videos.description:
Recorded January 27, 2026
Instructor: Yael Unterman
Why do the sounds of our holiest ritual echo the cries of our enemy's mother? The Talmud teaches that the blasts of the shofar are modeled on the sobs of Sisera's mother: a Canaanite woman mourning her fallen son, a cruel military commander and enemy of the Israelites. Why would the rabbis root our sacred soundscape in her grief? Timed to coincide with the week of Parshat Beshalach, when we read about Siserra's mother in the Haftarah, this class invites us into a rich and surprising conversation. Through close readings of biblical and rabbinic texts, Yael Unterman—poet, author, and educator—will lead us into the world of two women whose voices, though opposite in tone, converge in unexpected ways. Between the lines of their stories lies a message the shofar still carries: one of transformation, memory, and something more.

videos.title: Week 2-Dismantling Tyranny: Pharaoh, Plagues, and Power
videos.description:
Recorded January 22, 2026
Instructor: Rav Johnny Solomon
The Ten Plagues are more than divine fireworks; they are a step-by-step dismantling of an immoral civilization built on domination.

This course explores the deeper meaning behind the Plagues narrative through the moral lens of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks. What kind of society produces slavery? Why does God target not just Pharaoh, but Egypt’s entire system of false power? And how do the plagues function not as punishments, but as a deliberate unmaking of religious, political, and psychological tyranny?

Over two sessions, we’ll explore how the Plagues narrative introduces a revolutionary moral idea: that might does not make right, that power is not virtue, and that human beings are not the ultimate arbiters of justice. Guided by Rav Johnny Solomon, drawing on the moral vision of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks, we’ll see how each plague confronts Egypt’s theology of control and affirms a new truth: that only God can define how humanity must live. This isn’t just a story of liberation. It’s a story about what happens when unchecked power meets a moral God, and what that encounter demands of us today.

The Seder Song Maccabeats
Week 1-Dismantling Tyranny: Pharaoh, Plagues, and Power
videos.description:
Recorded January 15, 2026
Instructor: Rav Johnny Solomon
The Ten Plagues are more than divine fireworks; they are a step-by-step dismantling of an immoral civilization built on domination. This course explores the deeper meaning behind the Plagues narrative through the moral lens of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks. What kind of society produces slavery? Why does God target not just Pharaoh, but Egypt’s entire system of false power? And how do the plagues function not as punishments, but as a deliberate unmaking of religious, political, and psychological tyranny?

Over two sessions, we’ll explore how the Plagues narrative introduces a revolutionary moral idea: that might does not make right, that power is not virtue, and that human beings are not the ultimate arbiters of justice. Guided by Rav Johnny Solomon, drawing on the moral vision of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks, we’ll see how each plague confronts Egypt’s theology of control and affirms a new truth: that only God can define how humanity must live. This isn’t just a story of liberation. It’s a story about what happens when unchecked power meets a moral God, and what that encounter demands of us today.

videos.title: Ask the Rabbis- What's Next for North American Judaism with Rabbi Robyn Fryer Bodzin
videos.description:
Recorded January 13, 2026
Instructors: Rabbi Robyn Fryer Bodzin and Rabbi Rachel Bovitz
What’s really happening in North American Jewish life, and where are we headed? 
In the second installment of this ground-breaking Melton+ series, Melton Executive Director Rabbi Rachel Bovitz will sit down with Rabbi Robyn Fryer Bodzin of Toronto's Congregation Beth Tzedec, one of the largest synagogues in North America. Their candid, wide-ranging conversation will address the challenges and possibilities facing Jewish communities today, from synagogue life and spiritual leadership to shifting demographics and new models of connection. This is the second in a four-part series of interviews with rabbinic leaders of prominent North American congregations. Come for the insight, stay for the honesty.

videos.title: Week 2 - OMG, Can You Believe?!
videos.description:
Recorded January 12, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Amy S. Wallk
In this six-week exploratory course, we dive into the BIG questions around God and God’s relationship to Humankind. We’ll examine the many different understandings of God, free will, and mitzvot expressed by Jewish philosophers, rabbis, leaders past and present – and you. We’ll explore the divine, the prophetic, and even the supernatural, as we amplify and dissect your deepest God-questions. Just do not expect to leave with any final answers.

Table of Contents

Lesson 1 - God-ness Gracious
Lesson 2 - The Human Journey: Are we there yet?!
Lesson 3 - If not-for-Prophets...
Lesson 4 - The Super, the Natural, and the Supernatural
Lesson 5 - Wins and Sins: Do God and Free Will Go Together?
Lesson 6 - There’s a Mitzvah for that!

videos.title: Eating with Our Ancestors: Biblical Foods on the Modern Table
videos.description:
Recorded January 8, 2026
Instructor: Lorne Opler, MEd, CSCS
Wheat, barley, figs, pomegranates, olives, and dates aren’t just biblical symbols; they’re foods that still nourish Jewish life today. In this engaging and practical session, Lorne Opler, a college professor, fitness writer, and personal trainer, explores the nutritional power and spiritual significance of Torah’s original superfoods.

Drawing on ancient texts and modern nutritional science, we’ll take a closer look at the Seven Species and other plant-based staples found throughout the Bible—from lentils and beans to almonds and apples—and how they can inform how we eat and live today. Just in time for Tu BiShvat, you’ll learn how to bring these time-honored ingredients into your own kitchen with accessible, health-conscious recipes and insights that bridge past and present.

videos.title: Donors, Data, and Disruption: How Jewish Philanthropy is Changing in America
videos.description:
Recorded January 8, 2026
Instructor: Dr. Steven Windmueller
A new generation of Jewish donors is transforming how American Jewish life is funded and shaped. In this forward-looking Melton+ session, scholar, researcher, and futurist Dr. Steven Windmueller unpacks how technology—from AI and mobile apps to data analytics and crowdfunding—is upending traditional models of giving. Drawing on his latest research, Dr. Windmueller traces the rise of boutique foundations, decentralized donor networks, and emerging values that prioritize innovation, transparency, and impact. What does this philanthropic disruption mean for Jewish identity, leadership, and communal priorities? Join us to explore the forces reshaping the future of Jewish giving.

videos.title: Hidden in Plain Sight: Modern Miracles in the Land of Israel
videos.description:
Recorded January 6, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski
What if we’re living through a moment of miraculous history and most people simply aren’t noticing? Over the past two years, a series of events in Israel has unfolded that, taken individually, might be explained as coincidence, strategy, or luck. But when viewed together, a more extraordinary pattern emerges. In this widely acclaimed presentation delivered to audiences around the world, Rabbi Uri Pilichowski brings his clear-eyed, rationalist lens to a topic often clouded by exaggeration: miracles. With precision, evidence, and a deep grounding in Jewish history, he examines how improbable outcomes — on the battlefield, in politics, and in everyday life — may point to something larger. Rabbi Pilichowsky is not prone to hyperbole. He doesn’t ask for blind faith. What he offers instead is a careful, compelling case for seeing recent events in Israel as part of a story that is both unfolding now and deeply rooted in the past. You don’t have to believe in miracles to be moved by this presentation. You just have to be willing to see them.

videos.title: Rabbi Debra Eisenan Clip for Melton
videos.description: December 29, 2025

videos.title: New Years Resolutions: Sacred Intentions or Broken Vows?
videos.description:
Recorded: December 29, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Neil Fleischmann
As December winds down toward resolution season, what guidance does Judaism offer for making commitments in the year ahead? Our sources approach resolutions with both inspiration and caution, valuing self-discipline and ethical growth while also warning about the spiritual cost of promises we fail to keep.  The annulment of vows during Kol Nidre underscores the seriousness with which we treat our words, and the wisdom of thinking twice before making a commitment we may not sustain. Yet, the drive to improve ourselves is deeply Jewish. From Mussar and Chasidut to the writings of Maimonides, our tradition invites us to refine our character, act with purpose, and choose a mitzvah as a personal point of focus.

In this timely session, Rabbi Neil Fleischmann leads a reflective and practical exploration of how resolutions—when rooted in honesty and humility—can help us grow without overreaching. Together, we explore what it means to set goals that align with both our values and our calendars.

videos.title: Week 6 - Members of the Tribe: Cornerstones of Jewish Identity
videos.description:
Recorded December 22, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Amy S. Wallk
What does it really mean to be Jewish today? In a world where identity is constantly questioned and belonging is complex, this six-session course offers a powerful exploration of what ties us together as a people. More than a history lesson, this is a vital conversation about what it means to be Jewish right now.

Offered via Melton+, this piece of Melton's Living Wisdom curriculum is now available for the first time to the thousands of learners who live in communities that do not yet offer Melton in-person learning. Additionally, through our online platform, we are excited to be piloting an AI-powered digital teacher’s assistant to assist learners with the exploration and investigation of course material between class sessions.

From the story of the Exodus to the meaning of “Chosenness,” from daily rituals like Kashrut to the role of Israel as a central focus for Jewish identity, we’ll unpack the values, symbols, and struggles that shape our people and find their connection to our daily lives. Together, we’ll wrestle with big questions, discover personal meaning, and uncover how understanding “the Tribe” – our shared history, values, and responsibilities – gives us tools to respond to the challenges of our time, both individually and collectively. Join us on this journey to empower us to live more meaningful Jewish lives and create a more just world.

Lesson 1 (Nov 17) - Living a Jewish Life: Finding Purpose Through Reflection
Lesson 2 (Nov 24) - Let My People Grow: The Exodus from Egypt as a Jewish Calling
Lesson 3 (Dec 1) - Just Remember: Symbols and Reminders in Judaism
Lesson 4 (Dec 8) - Chosen People, Chosen Purpose: The concept of “chosenness” through both a traditional and a contemporary lens
Lesson 5 (Dec 15) - Holy Guacamole: What Does It Mean to Keep Kosher?
Lesson 6 (Dec 22) - Jerusalem If I Forget You…: Israel and the Diaspora

videos.title: Proclaim Liberty: The Hebrew Bible and the American Story
videos.description:
Recorded December 18, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Charles E. Savenor
As the United States approaches its 250th birthday, Rabbi Charles Savenor invites us to reflect on how the Hebrew Bible is the genesis of the American experiment. From the vision of the Founding Fathers to the rhetoric of emancipation, protest, and civic responsibility, biblical texts have served as a moral and imaginative force in America’s pursuit of a more just society.

Through vivid historical examples and resonant passages from the Tanakh, Rabbi Savenor shows how Jewish ideas have guided presidents, inspired reformers, and helped Americans actualize what freedom demands, what justice requires, and what leadership looks like. This session offers a fresh look at how ancient wisdom continues to illuminate modern democracy, and what that legacy asks of us today.

videos.title: Miraculous, Military, or Marketing: What Chanukah Really Means
videos.description:
Recorded December 17, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Samuel Gelman
We light the candles, spin the dreidels, and sing the songs, but what are we actually commemorating? This class takes a clear-eyed, text-driven look at the historical and religious roots of Hanukkah, starting with the Maccabean revolt and its earliest appearance in the Book of Maccabees. Why were those books never canonized? And why does Hanukkah get a measly handful of pages in the Talmud, while Purim earns an entire tractate? Taught by Rabbi Samuel Gelman, Melton’s Educator-in-Residence, this session explores how the rabbis of the Talmud shifted the focus from military victory to the miracle of the oil and what that choice reveals about their values and priorities. We’ll examine Hanukkah’s competing narratives, from political resistance to spiritual endurance to religious freedom, and ask: is there one “correct” reason we celebrate, or might the richness of the holiday lie in its contradictions?

videos.title: Light, Hope, and Miracles: Hanukkah's Challenge for Today
videos.description:
Recorded December 16, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
Chanukah is more than a celebration of oil or military might; it’s a story of resilience, renewal, and spiritual light in the darkest time of year. In this illuminating course with master educator Dr. Sandra Lilienthal, we’ll explore how Jewish tradition understands light, hope, and miracles: not just as metaphors, but as lived experiences shaped by faith, action, and meaning. Through classical and contemporary perspectives, we’ll ask: What does it really mean to hope in difficult times? How do we recognize a miracle when we see one? And what does it take to bring light into the world, not just during Chanukah, but all year long?

videos.title: The Art of Creation: Genesis on Canvas
videos.description:
Recorded December 11, 2025
Instructor: Evgenia Kempinski
What did the Book of Genesis look like through the eyes of Europe’s great painters? This visual journey explores how generations of European masters interpreted the foundational stories of the Torah, from the Creation of the World to the trials of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. In the hands of Michelangelo, Rembrandt, Dürer, El Greco, and others, familiar biblical scenes become reflections on human struggle, divine mystery, and moral choice. Historian and tour guide Evgenia Kempinski will trace the artistic evolution of these stories through changing styles and shifting sensibilities, from medieval symbolism to Renaissance humanism to Baroque drama. Along the way, we’ll consider how image and interpretation work together to bring text to life and reveal how artists have shaped our imagination of these foundational stories.

videos.title: When the Time Comes: Values, Voices, and End-of-Life Choices
videos.description:
Recorded December 10, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Rivka Amado
Most of us know we should write an advance health care directive. But how do we actually do it well? And what does “well” mean when we’re facing deeply personal decisions that touch on faith, family, and mortality? In this important and thought-provoking session, Dr. Rivka Amado—a scholar of Jewish thought and medical ethics—invites us to look beyond checklists and legal forms and explore what it really means to express our values at the end of life. In addition to introducing an innovative narrative-based approach to advance directives, Dr. Amado will explore two essential and often overlooked topics: how Jewish values can shape the writing of ethical wills, and the complex, sometimes conflicting views within Jewish tradition around organ donation. Drawing on her years of service on Israel’s national commission on transplant ethics, she brings rare insight and personal depth to some of the most challenging decisions our loved ones or we may ever face.

videos.title: Driven by Hope
When Truth-telling Gets Complicated: Torah and the Ethics of Lying
videos.description:
Recorded December 9, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
Abraham is a pillar of faith and a model of righteousness... but he doesn’t always tell the truth. In this thought-provoking class, Rabbi Michele Faudem guides us through the story of Abram and Sarai’s journey to Egypt, where Abram lies about his relationship to Sarai to protect himself. Is this a moment of clever survival, a moral lapse, or something else entirely? From this unsettling episode, we expand outward to explore the Jewish approach to truth and deception: when lying is clearly forbidden, when it's permitted, and when it’s anything but straightforward. Drawing on sources throughout our tradition, Rabbi Faudem invites us to wrestle with some of the Torah’s most enduring moral tensions. Together, we’ll ask not only whether honesty is a virtue, but when, why, and for whom it might need to make room for something more complex. With her signature ability to balance textual depth, moral complexity, and lively discussion, Rabbi Faudem will guide learners through a nuanced exploration of honesty, self-protection, and ethical gray zones anchored in Jewish wisdom.

videos.title: Defying Labels: Wicked, Righteous, and What's In Between
videos.description:
Recorded December 3, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Mara Young
What does it mean to be wicked? What makes someone righteous? Just in time for the release of the second Wicked film, join Rabbi Mara Young for a timely and thought-provoking look at how Jewish tradition defines—and complicates—these moral extremes. Through classic and modern Jewish texts, we’ll explore what our tradition teaches about good, evil, and the many shades in between. We’ll also bring in a little Broadway flair, drawing inspiration from Wicked’s unforgettable characters and its bold challenge to moral labels. As author Gregory Maguire wrote, “It’s people who claim that they’re good… that you have to be wary of.” Come ready to question, laugh, and maybe look at “wickedness” in a whole new way.

Week 2-To Heal a Fractured World: The Vision of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks
videos.description:
Recorded November 25, 2025
Instructor: Rabbanit Sarah Sharman Moser
What made Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks one of the most beloved and influential Jewish thinkers of our time? From his iconic yellow tie to his profound moral clarity, Rabbi Sacks brought Judaism into global conversations about ethics, faith, and the future of society. Taught by Rabbanit Sarah Sharman Moser, a Sacks Scholar, translator, and close colleague of Rabbi Sacks, this course offers a rare insider’s view of his life and teachings.

In the first session, The Man Behind the Yellow Tie, we will explore Rabbi Sacks’ journey from a philosophy student to Chief Rabbi of the UK, uncovering the foundational ideas that shaped his worldview. We will also look at his many writings, which speak both inward to the Jewish people and outward to the wider world. The second session, To Heal a Fractured World, will focus on one of Rabbi Sacks’ most influential works, published twenty years ago yet still as relevant as ever. Together, we will consider his vision of moral responsibility, the role of religion in contemporary society, and his framing of Judaism as a tradition of protest, justice, and ethical action.

This course is offered in celebration of the Rabbi Sacks Global Day of Learning 2025, an annual moment when communities worldwide come together to honour his memory through study and reflection. At a time when our world is marked by moral confusion, political violence, deep divisions, and disagreement as a national pastime, Rabbi Sacks’s wisdom feels more urgent than ever. His voice of clarity, hope, and moral courage continues to offer a path forward, reminding us that Judaism, and indeed humanity, is called to heal rather than fracture, to build rather than break.
