videos.title: Week 3-Justice and the Jewish Story: Three Trials that Changed Everything
videos.description:
Recorded November 24, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Katherine Aron-Beller
What does it mean to be judged by the law, by society, or by history? In this three-part course, we examine pivotal trials involving Jewish figures across time and place, from ancient Judea to modern France. These courtroom moments weren’t just legal proceedings; they were battlegrounds where questions of identity, power, fear, and justice were laid bare. Whether based in fact or fiction, each trial reveals how Jews have been seen by the societies in which they lived, and how they saw themselves. Together, we explore how trial records, literary texts, and public opinion can shape—or distort—the truth. Drawing from legal documents, historical analysis, and dramatic literature, we examine how personal motives, political pressures, and cultural tensions have all played out within the walls of the courtroom. What do these moments reveal about the role of Jews in broader society? And how can we read between the lines to understand the forces at work behind the verdict?

Session 1 - Jesus Before the Sanhedrin and Rome: We begin with perhaps the most scrutinized trial in Western history: the New Testament account of Jesus’ appearance before the Jewish Sanhedrin and Roman authorities. How has this trial shaped centuries of Christian-Jewish relations? What do we know—or assume—about the legal and political dynamics at play? We’ll explore the implications of this trial for Jewish identity and its long legacy in Christian thought.

Session 2 - Shylock in the Dock: In this session, we turn to Shakespeare’s The Merchant of Venice, focusing on the iconic trial scene. Is Shylock a villain, a victim, or something in between? Through a close reading of the text and historical context, we’ll uncover how the law becomes a stage for cultural anxieties about money, mercy, and the outsider. We ask how Shylock’s portrayal has shaped modern images of the Jew in literature and law.

Session 3 - The Dreyfus Affair and the Making of Modern Antisemitism: Our final session brings us to 19th-century France, where Captain Alfred Dreyfus, a Jewish officer in the French army, was falsely accused of treason. We examine how this trial exposed deep fault lines in French society and galvanized both nationalist and antisemitic movements. What does Dreyfus’s case tell us about the promises and perils of Jewish emancipation in modern Europe?

Ask the Rabbis: What's Next for North American Judaism with Rabbi David Wolpe
videos.description:
Recorded November 19, 2025
Instructors: Rabbi David Wolpe and Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz
What’s really happening in American Jewish life, and where are we headed? 
In this new Melton+ series, Rabbi Morey Schwartz will sit down with some of the most prominent and influential congregational rabbis in North America for candid, wide-ranging conversations about the challenges and possibilities facing Jewish communities today. From synagogue life and spiritual leadership to shifting demographics and new models of connection, no topic is off the table.  One session each month, beginning November 2025. Come for the insight, stay for the honesty.  Our first conversation is with Rabbi David Wolpe, the Max Webb Rabbi Emeritus of Sinai Temple in Los Angeles.

videos.title: Week 2-Honor Your Father and Mother: Jewish Tradition Meets Family Psychology
videos.description:
Recorded November 19, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Mona Fishbane
Jewish tradition places honoring parents and maintaining intergenerational continuity at the heart of our values. Yet, we live in a culture that often prioritizes individual needs and independence over obligations to family, community, or heritage. This tension can create distance, conflict, or even estrangement between generations. In this two-part course, Dr. Mona Fishbane—a psychologist and expert in intergenerational family dynamics—draws on Jewish texts and contemporary psychology to explore how these opposing forces shape our lives and how we can respond with resilience and care.

In Session 1: Intergenerational Connection & Disconnection, we will explore biblical and Talmudic sources on honoring parents, which reveal both the challenges and the sacred obligations inherent in family life. By contrast, current cultural trends emphasize individual autonomy and may encourage intergenerational estrangement. How can we balance these contrasting values? More concretely, how can we manage our emotional reactivity with our family of origin? Sharing insights from neurobiology, Mona will offer tools for cultivating intentionality and thoughtfulness in relationships with our parents, shaping the legacies we pass on to the next generation.
Session 2: Healing Intergenerational Wounds will consider the intergenerational family dramas of Genesis, drawing on family psychology and Jewish wisdom to consider how to address hurt, estrangement, and intergenerational rifts. Mona will offer perspectives on cultivating dialogue, strengthening family ties, and promoting personal well-being while staying rooted in Jewish tradition.

With wisdom and compassion, Dr. Fishbane brings Jewish tradition and psychology into conversation to illuminate paths of healing, resilience, and deeper family connection. Her extraordinary teaching makes even complex ideas clear and accessible, offering depth without dilution and insight that speaks to every learner.

videos.title: Standing Idly By: The Cost of Silence and the Courage to Act
videos.description:
Recorded November 18, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Dennis B. Klein
What do we expect from one another in moments of crisis? What happens when we fail to act? In reflecting on the Holocaust, historian and conflict resolution scholar Dennis B. Klein draws on survivor testimony to confront the tragic fact that bystanders enabled genocide by refusing to act. Their inaction carried devastating consequences then, and their stories challenge us to consider what it means to take responsibility now. A Professor Emeritus of History at Kean University and Affiliate Faculty at George Mason’s Carter School for Peace and Conflict Resolution, Klein is a leading scholar of atrocity, transitional justice, and the psychology of violence. In this urgent presentation, he turns the legacy of bystanders into a mirror for our own polarized age, where mistrust and tribalism, as well as fear, predominate. This session invites us to wrestle with hard, urgent questions: Why do some fail to act in the face of harm? What does moral courage look like in fractured times? And how can we create communities rooted not in silence or fear, but in responsibility and care?

videos.title: Week 1-To Heal a Fractured World: The Vision of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks
videos.description:
Recorded November 18, 2025
Instructor: Rabbanit Sarah Sharmon MoserWhat made Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks one of the most beloved and influential Jewish thinkers of our time? From his iconic yellow tie to his profound moral clarity, Rabbi Sacks brought Judaism into global conversations about ethics, faith, and the future of society. Taught by Rabbanit Sarah Sharman Moser, a Sacks Scholar, translator, and close colleague of Rabbi Sacks, this course offers a rare insider’s view of his life and teachings.

In the first session, The Man Behind the Yellow Tie, we will explore Rabbi Sacks’ journey from a philosophy student to Chief Rabbi of the UK, uncovering the foundational ideas that shaped his worldview. We will also look at his many writings, which speak both inward to the Jewish people and outward to the wider world.

The second session, To Heal a Fractured World, will focus on one of Rabbi Sacks’ most influential works, published twenty years ago yet still as relevant as ever. Together, we will consider his vision of moral responsibility, the role of religion in contemporary society, and his framing of Judaism as a tradition of protest, justice, and ethical action.

This course is offered in celebration of the Rabbi Sacks Global Day of Learning 2025, an annual moment when communities worldwide come together to honour his memory through study and reflection. At a time when our world is marked by moral confusion, political violence, deep divisions, and disagreement as a national pastime, Rabbi Sacks’s wisdom feels more urgent than ever. His voice of clarity, hope, and moral courage continues to offer a path forward, reminding us that Judaism, and indeed humanity, is called to heal rather than fracture, to build rather than break.

videos.title: Week 1-Members of the Tribe: Cornerstones of Jewish Identity
videos.description:
Recorded November 17, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Amy S. Wallk
What does it really mean to be Jewish today? In a world where identity is constantly questioned and belonging is complex, this six-session course offers a powerful exploration of what ties us together as a people. More than a history lesson, this is a vital conversation about what it means to be Jewish right now.

Offered via Melton+, this piece of Melton's Living Wisdom curriculum is now available for the first time to the thousands of learners who live in communities that do not yet offer Melton in-person learning. Additionally, through our online platform, we are excited to be piloting an AI-powered digital teacher’s assistant to assist learners in exploring and investigating course material between class sessions. From the story of the Exodus to the meaning of “Chosenness,” from daily rituals like Kashrut to Israel's role as a central focus of Jewish identity, we’ll unpack the values, symbols, and struggles that shape our people and explore their connection to our daily lives. Together, we’ll wrestle with big questions, discover personal meaning, and uncover how understanding “the Tribe” – our shared history, values, and responsibilities – gives us tools to respond to the challenges of our time, both individually and collectively. Join us on this journey to empower us to live more meaningful Jewish lives and create a more just world.

Lesson 1 (Nov 17) - Living a Jewish Life: Finding Purpose Through Reflection
Lesson 2 (Nov 24) - Let My People Grow: The Exodus from Egypt as a Jewish Calling
Lesson 3 (Dec 1) - Just Remember: Symbols and Reminders in Judaism
Lesson 4 (Dec 8) - Chosen People, Chosen Purpose: The concept of “chosenness” through both a traditional and a contemporary lens
Lesson 5 (Dec 15) - Holy Guacamole: What Does It Mean to Keep Kosher?
Lesson 6 (Dec 22) - Jerusalem If I Forget You…: Israel and the Diaspora

videos.title: Week 2-Justice and the Jewish Story: Three Trials that Changed Everything
videos.description:
Recorded November 17, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Katherine Aron-Beller
What does it mean to be judged by the law, by society, or by history? In this three-part course, we examine pivotal trials involving Jewish figures across time and place, from ancient Judea to modern France. These courtroom moments weren’t just legal proceedings; they were battlegrounds where questions of identity, power, fear, and justice were laid bare. Whether based in fact or fiction, each trial reveals how Jews have been seen by the societies in which they lived, and how they saw themselves.

Together, we explore how trial records, literary texts, and public opinion can shape—or distort—the truth. Drawing from legal documents, historical analysis, and dramatic literature, we examine how personal motives, political pressures, and cultural tensions have all played out within the walls of the courtroom. What do these moments reveal about the role of Jews in broader society? And how can we read between the lines to understand the forces at work behind the verdict?

Session 1 - Jesus Before the Sanhedrin and Rome: We begin with perhaps the most scrutinized trial in Western history: the New Testament account of Jesus’ appearance before the Jewish Sanhedrin and Roman authorities. How has this trial shaped centuries of Christian-Jewish relations? What do we know—or assume—about the legal and political dynamics at play? We’ll explore the implications of this trial for Jewish identity and its long legacy in Christian thought.

Session 2 - Shylock in the Dock: In this session, we turn to Shakespeare’s The Merchant of Venice, focusing on the iconic trial scene. Is Shylock a villain, a victim, or something in between? Through a close reading of the text and historical context, we’ll uncover how the law becomes a stage for cultural anxieties about money, mercy, and the outsider. We ask how Shylock’s portrayal has shaped modern images of the Jew in literature and law.

Session 3 - The Dreyfus Affair and the Making of Modern Antisemitism: Our final session brings us to 19th-century France, where Captain Alfred Dreyfus, a Jewish officer in the French army, was falsely accused of treason. We examine how this trial exposed deep fault lines in French society and galvanized both nationalist and antisemitic movements. What does Dreyfus’s case tell us about the promises and perils of Jewish emancipation in modern Europe?

videos.title: Say Hello to Milton
Grateful Hearts, Timeless Stories: Jewish Storytelling for Parents, Grandparents, and Educators
videos.description:
Recorded November 13, 2025
Instructor: Vered Hanken, PhD
Why do stories we hear as children stay with us for a lifetime? This Thanksgiving-season workshop invites you to explore the enduring power of Jewish storytelling as a way to share gratitude, memories, and values across generations. Led by master storyteller Vered Hankin, PhD—dubbed “the leading storyteller of her generation” by the New York Jewish Week—you’ll reflect on what makes a story memorable and meaningful, and start crafting a personal story to pass on to the young people in your life. Whether you’re a parent, grandparent, or educator, this session offers a creative and heartfelt way to deepen your connection to Jewish tradition. We gather in memory of Sherrie Weiss, z”l, a beloved Melton learner and leader who believed in the transformative power of stories to shape identity and transmit values. Come for the inspiration; leave with the spark of a story only you can tell.

videos.title: Week 1-Honor Your Father and Mother: Jewish Tradition Meets Family Psychology
videos.description:
Recorded November 12, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Mona Fishbane
Jewish tradition places honoring parents and maintaining intergenerational continuity at the heart of our values. Yet, we live in a culture that often prioritizes individual needs and independence over obligations to family, community, or heritage. This tension can create distance, conflict, or even estrangement between generations. In this two-part course, Dr. Mona Fishbane—a psychologist and expert in intergenerational family dynamics—draws on Jewish texts and contemporary psychology to explore how these opposing forces shape our lives and how we can respond with resilience and care.

In Session 1: Intergenerational Connection & Disconnection, we will explore biblical and Talmudic sources on honoring parents, which reveal both the challenges and the sacred obligations inherent in family life. By contrast, current cultural trends emphasize individual autonomy and may encourage intergenerational estrangement. How can we balance these contrasting values? More concretely, how can we manage our emotional reactivity with our family of origin? Sharing insights from neurobiology, Mona will offer tools for cultivating intentionality and thoughtfulness in relationships with our parents, shaping the legacies we pass on to the next generation.
Session 2: Healing Intergenerational Wounds considers the intergenerational family dramas of Genesis, drawing on family psychology and Jewish wisdom to consider how to address hurt, estrangement, and intergenerational rifts. Mona will offer perspectives on cultivating dialogue, strengthening family ties, and promoting personal well-being while staying rooted in Jewish tradition.
With wisdom and compassion, Dr. Fishbane brings Jewish tradition and psychology into conversation to illuminate paths of healing, resilience, and deeper family connection. Her extraordinary teaching makes even complex ideas clear and accessible, offering depth without dilution and insight that speaks to every learner.

videos.title: A Passion for Torah: Honoring Rabbi Sacks
videos.description:
Recorded November 11, 2025
Instructor: Rav Johnny Solomon
What made Rabbi Jonathan Sacks such a transformative Torah teacher? On the Sacks Global Day of Learning, join Melton+ favorite Rav Johnny Solomon for a special class marking Rabbi Sacks’s fifth yahrzeit. Together we’ll explore some of his most powerful teachings about Torah itself: what it means to study, to teach, and to live Torah with depth, urgency, and joy. Rav Johnny studied under Rabbi Sacks, worked closely with him, and continues to bring his voice into contemporary Jewish learning worldwide. With warmth and insight, he’ll guide us through a few of Rabbi Sacks’s most memorable reflections on the purpose and power of Torah study and help us carry forward a legacy that still speaks to the mind, heart, and soul.

videos.description:
Recorded November 10, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Katherine Aron-Beller

videos.title: The Shalshelet- A Chain of Meaning in the Melody
videos.description:
Recorded November 6, 2025
Instructor: Seth Dimbert
Most learners focus on the words of the Torah, but there’s meaning in the melody, too. This course explores a rare cantillation mark that appears in the Torah only a handful of times, drawing attention not to what it says but to how it sounds. When we learn to listen for it, we uncover a layer of Torah that speaks to silence, struggle, and moral depth. Taught by Seth Dimbert, Melton’s Director of Online Learning, this class invites learners to revisit familiar verses with new ears. Along the way, we’ll encounter a tradition that expresses its most profound truths in melody, and discover the chain that links generation to generation.

videos.title: Al haNissim at the Concertgebouw 2018
AL HANISIM (music by Dov Frimmer)
Jeremy Gimpel - Al Hanisim (Official Music Video) - [ג רמי גימפל - על הניסים [קליפ רשמי
Noah Examined: Righteous Role Model or Religious Robot?
videos.description:
Recorded November 5, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Dovi Fischer
Was Noah a true hero, or just someone who followed orders? The Torah tells us he was “righteous in his generation,” but commentators have long debated whether that praise is wholehearted or faint. Did Noah rise to meet a moral moment, or did he simply survive it?
Join Rabbi Dovi Fischer for a fresh, thoughtful exploration of one of the Bible’s most familiar yet enigmatic figures. Drawing on the timeless teachings of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks, we’ll wrestle with the complexity of Noah’s silence, his obedience, and his loneliness. What can his story teach us about what it means to be righteous in a flawed world? And how do we carry that lesson into our own lives, when moral clarity isn’t always obvious?

Week 3-Jewish Vienna: A City of Two Tales
videos.description:
Recorded November 3, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Steven Chervin
At the dawn of the 20th century, Vienna was home to both extraordinary Jewish creativity and alarming antisemitic fervor. Out of this complex and combustible environment emerged two dramatically different responses to what was then called the “Jewish Problem”: one that dreamed of a safe and sovereign future, and another that led the world into darkness. In this three-part series, Dr. Steven Chervin takes us inside the intellectual and political world of pre-war Vienna, exploring how this one city shaped both the birth of modern Zionism and the rise of genocidal antisemitism and what this geographical dichotomy might teach us about the challenges of Jewish life today.
Session 1 – The Atmosphere in Vienna: We’ll examine the figures who stirred public sentiment against Jews and shaped the city’s volatile social climate: political leaders like Karl Lueger, thinkers like Heinrich von Treitschke and Georg von Schönerer, and cultural critics like Otto Weininger and Karl Kraus. What ideas took hold, and why? How did Jewish identity begin to fracture under pressure?
Session 2 – Herzl and the Zionist Dream: Turning to Theodor Herzl, we’ll trace how a secular, assimilated playwright came to envision political Zionism as the only solution to Europe’s antisemitism. Through excerpts from Der Judenstaat, Altneuland, and key Zionist congresses, we’ll explore how Herzl’s vision was both embraced and critiqued by Jewish contemporaries across the ideological spectrum.
Session 3 – Hitler and the Path to Destruction: Finally, we’ll investigate Hitler’s early life in Vienna and the roots of the hatred that would shape his ideology. Using selections from Mein Kampf and public speeches, we’ll explore how antisemitism became central to his worldview and how the ideas circulating in Vienna helped pave the way for what came next.

videos.title: Sneak Peek - A Hidden Gem- Mexico's Remarkable Jewish Story
CSI Babylonia- Investigating Talmudic Justice
videos.description:
Recorded October 29, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Hillel Lichtman
Think Law & Order, Talmud edition. Can a case go forward without two eyewitnesses? The rabbis thought so, and their methods might surprise you. Join Rabbi Hillel Lichtman for a legal/crime procedural like no other, featuring circumstantial evidence, judicial intuition, a pre-modern blood test, and even a literal roll of the dice. Part legal drama and part rabbinic mystery, this session reveals how ancient Jewish courts wrestled with justice, doubt, and truth. Building on the drama, the lecture will survey Biblical, Talmudic, and post-Talmudic sources that debate when and how physical or forensic evidence could be admitted in both capital and monetary cases. Participants will examine the surprising role of a judge’s intuition in reaching a verdict, and explore rabbinic proposals for situations where the facts point in different directions yet no clear conclusion emerges. Along the way, students will encounter a legal tradition that is at once rigorous and imaginative, one that continues to speak to contemporary questions about evidence, fairness, and the pursuit of truth.

videos.title: Week 2- Inside the Shtetl: A Jewish Universe
videos.description:
Recorded October 28, 2025
Instructor: Evgenia Kempinski
The shtetl was the heart of Ashkenazi Jewish life for centuries, a place where daily rhythms of work, worship, and community shaped a world that still echoes in our memories and stories. From bustling marketplaces to intimate synagogues, and from close-knit family ties to complex relationships with non-Jewish neighbors, the shtetl was at once ordinary and extraordinary, grounding Jewish identity in ways that still matter today.
In this two-part series, Jewish educator and tour guide Evgenia Kempinski brings the shtetl vividly to life. Through storytelling and historical insight, we’ll explore what made these towns unique, why some call them a “Jewish Atlantis,” and how they continue to inspire both nostalgia and curiosity. Along the way, we’ll reflect on how the lives of our ancestors in these communities continue to shape our sense of belonging and resilience. Whether you’re tracing your family roots, drawn to Jewish memory, or simply curious about the everyday world that sustained generations, this course offers an intimate window into the shtetl as a universe of its own, rich in tradition, complexity, and enduring meaning.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: Crisis and Community in Jewish New York
videos.description:
Recorded October 27, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Charles E. Savenor
How did a mayoral race in New York City become a national Jewish flashpoint? Why are many Jewish voters acting in ways that seem out of step with past political patterns? And what does this reveal about how American Jews are navigating identity, Israel, and communal responsibility right now? In this timely Melton Meets the Moment session, Civic Spirit Executive Director Rabbi Charlie Savenor guides us through the emotional and ideological divides surrounding the candidacy of Zohran Mamdani, not to debate politics, but to understand what’s driving the conversation. Drawing on classical Jewish texts, we’ll explore how our tradition approaches disagreement, loyalty, leadership, and communal boundaries. Whether you live in New York or far beyond, this session offers an opportunity to make sense of the headlines, reflect on Jewish values, and envision a way forward together for the city that many still consider the capital of the Diaspora.

videos.title: Original Sin: Fruit, Free Will, and Fracture
videos.description:
Recorded October 23, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Nachum GutierrezIt wasn’t just a bite, it was a break: a break from innocence, from reason, from eternity. But what exactly was the original sin? Was it the pull of desire over discipline? The birth of the ego? Or the moment we traded immortality for experience? Join Rabbi Nachum Gutierrez as we trace the story of Eden through Jewish eyes across the ages – ancient storytellers, medieval philosophers, and early modern commentators – each offering a radically different understanding of what went wrong in the Garden and why it still matters today. What if the story of Eden isn’t about what went wrong, but about what it means to be human?

videos.title: Spiritually Fit: Jewish Wisdom on Personal Wellness
videos.description:
Recorded October 20, 2025
Instructor: Lorne Opler, MEd, CSCS
Now that the holidays are behind us and a new Jewish year has begun, many of us are thinking about how to carry the spirit of renewal forward. We’ve taken stock of our souls—now it’s time to consider the state of our bodies. What does Jewish tradition have to say about physical well-being, and what might it mean to treat health itself as a mitzvah? Join Professor Lorne Opler, a certified personal trainer and educator in fitness and health promotion, for a unique exploration of wellness through a Jewish lens. We’ll study Torah texts that emphasize the importance of caring for the body and delve into the writings of Maimonides, a physician, philosopher, and fierce advocate for daily movement and mindful eating. His teachings, written nearly 900 years ago, remain strikingly relevant today. Throughout the session, we’ll bring the sources to life with brief, optional movement woven into the learning. You’ll come away with spiritual insight, practical tools, and a deeper understanding of what it means to treat your health as a sacred aspect of your life.

videos.title: Week 1-Jewish Vienna: A City of Two Tales
videos.description:
Recorded October 20, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Steven Chervin

videos.title: Cover Up or Double Down? Jewish Visibility in a Time of Rising Antisemitism
videos.description:
Recorded: October 16, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Jonah Rank
Across college campuses, city streets, and social media feeds, Jews are more visible—and more targeted—than they’ve been in decades. Should we hide signs of Jewishness, like a kippah or Star of David, to stay safe? Or should we lean into our identity as an act of pride and defiance? What does Jewish tradition actually say about how we present ourselves in public, especially when the stakes are high? In this urgent and thoughtful session, Rabbi Jonah Rank offers a timely and authoritative historical perspective on Jewish dress, public identity, and personal safety. We’ll explore:

How Talmudic sages weighed the risks of wearing ritual items like tefillin in public, and what that reveals about navigating identity under pressure.
The debated, and surprisingly recent, origins of the Kippah
Whether our tradition ever requires visible Jewish markers, or leaves it to personal preference

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: Is Absolution Absolute, or is Forgiveness Limited?
videos.description:
Recorded October 16, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski
When a person is publicly wronged, society often applauds the moral power of forgiveness. But does Judaism demand that we forgive those who cause pain, no matter what? When Erika Kirk publicly forgave her husband’s murderer in the spirit of Christian doctrine, she raised a profound and deeply Jewish question: What does our tradition actually teach about forgiveness, justice, and moral responsibility? As we emerge from the High Holiday season, Rabbi Uri Pilichowski presents a thought-provoking exploration of classical and modern sources—from the Torah to Maimonides to Rabbi Jonathan Sacks—to explore when forgiveness is a mitzvah and when it may actually be forbidden. Together, we’ll examine how Judaism’s approach to forgiveness differs from Christianity’s, why repentance is a precondition for reconciliation, and what it means to balance compassion with accountability. This is not a class about headlines, it’s about timeless values that feel more urgent than ever.

videos.title: Radical_Listening
Baby Conversion to Judaism at the Mikveh- Mayyim Hayyim
Brit Milah_ Jewish Traditions for Welcoming a Baby Boy
Zionism Course Recommendation
Carole King- She Made the Earth Move
videos.description:
Recorded October 9, 2025
Instructor: Jane Eisner
Carole King’s iconic music shaped the sound of American life, but the story behind the artist is equally powerful. In this class, journalist and author Jane Eisner offers a behind-the-scenes look at her new biography, Carole King: She Made the Earth Move, and the cultural legacy of one of pop music’s most enduring voices. Eisner traces King’s journey from her Jewish Brooklyn roots to Brill Building stardom, from the groundbreaking success of Tapestry to her evolution as a mother, activist, and performer. Along the way, she uncovers how King’s Jewish background shaped her creativity, values, and resilience, revealing a deeper story about American identity, visibility, and voice. Whether you’ve already picked up the book or are just discovering King’s story, this event offers a rich companion to the text and a chance to hear directly from the author about what makes Carole King’s life so uniquely resonant.

videos.title: The Old Lady and the Sukkah: Unsettling Foundations
videos.description:
Recorded October 9, 2025
Instructor: Joe Septimus
At the intersection of power, law, morality, discrimination, and personality—where ideological, communal, and personal conflicts collide—lies the world of Aggadah, or Talmudic tales. These stories, scattered between our tradition’s legal debates, most frequently do not offer resolution. Instead, they give voice to the real problems of humanity falling between the cracks. In this challenging session, Joe Septimus brings us one of his personal favorites: a tale from Tractate Sukkah in which an old woman confronts the Jewish Court when her wood is stolen to build the Exilarch’s sukkah. The law may be upheld, the ruling supported, the structure left standing… but something foundational is troubling. Through close reading and discussion, we’ll explore what this story reveals about accountability, leadership, and the price of confusing the law with moral justice.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment- The Latest Ceasefire and Peace Plan for Gaza
videos.description:
Recorded October 5, 2025
Instructor: Dan Feferman
The ceasefire and peace plan for Gaza is changing by the hour. What is actually happening, what do Israelis really think, and what may happen next? Analyst and former Israeli intelligence officer Dan Feferman offers an insider’s view of the negotiations, the shifting realities on the ground, and the hard questions that remain in real time Sunday, October 5, 2025.

videos.title: The Temple Mount: Politics, Faith, and Power
videos.description:
Recorded September 30, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Eva Gurevitch
The Temple Mount is the holiest site in Judaism, the place where the two ancient Temples stood at the heart of Jewish life and memory. Yet since Israel captured it in 1967, the question of who controls this sacred ground has remained one of the most contentious issues in Jewish and Middle Eastern politics. In this timely and provocative class, Dr. Eva Gurevich, postdoctoral fellow in Israel Studies at Brandeis University, guides us through the forces that make the Temple Mount such a lightning rod. Together, we’ll examine Zionist thought that views sovereignty over the Mount as a final goal, explore movements such as Women for the Temple Mount and the Temple Mount Movement that advocate for Jewish prayer there, and consider the political maneuvers of figures like Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir. Along the way, we’ll trace the long shadow of the “Status Quo” doctrine and the designation of “Holy Sites,” two legal concepts that have shaped policy in Jerusalem for centuries.
 
Ultimately, we’ll investigate: why does the Temple Mount remain so charged, and what is at stake for Jews, Israelis, and the wider world in the debates that continue to unfold there?

videos.title: Sneak Peek - Jewish Cuba- A Tapestry of Cultures
videos.description:
Recorded September 29, 2025
Instructor: Miriam Levinson
Join us for a sneak peek into our upcoming Melton Travel journey to Cuba. Feel the vibrant colors, hear the music in the streets, and sense the everyday rhythms of a city where history and hope live side by side. We will preview key neighborhood hubs and community spaces, share stories about the people who sustain Jewish life today, and place it all in historical context so you see how this small, resilient community endures now. This remarkable Jewish community will surprise you and warm your heart. Guiding us is Melton field educator Miriam Levinson, an expert on Jewish life in Cuba, a native Jewish Cuban, and a gifted storyteller who brings the city to life with warmth and insight.

videos.title: Sneak Peek Behind Closed Doors: Jews of Spain and Portugal (Edited)
Sneak Peek Behind Closed Doors: Jews of Spain and Portugal
videos.description:
Recorded September 18, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Katherine Aron-Beller
Join us for a lively preview of our upcoming Melton Tour to Spain and Portugal. Step inside a world where Sephardic brilliance once flourished, then follow the story behind closed doors as families kept tradition alive in private. We will trace the arc of our trip from Granada and Córdoba to Porto and Belmonte, highlighting the sites, questions, and stories that bring this history to life. You will leave with a clear frame for conversos, hidden practice, memory, and renewal, and a taste of what we will explore together on the ground. Featuring Dr. Katherine Aron-Beller, a leading expert on Jewish history and the Inquisition, and a gifted educator who makes complex history vivid and engaging.

videos.title: Week 6-The Next Chapter - It's a Date: Marking Time the Jewish Way
videos.description:
Recorded September 17, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi John Carrier
Whether you look forward to the Passover Seder all year or avoid it like the plague, add “It’s a Date” to your calendars. Gain new insight into days that mark profound moments in history, and others that mark when to harvest the grain — and pop open the wine. In this unit, we will explore how the Jewish calendar works. We will explain and discuss our holidays and holiday rituals. Rabbi John Carrier, instructor, has taught for the Melton School since 2015 and regards its curriculum as “the best next step” for people who develop a passion for Jewish learning during their conversion process.
Table of Contents
Lesson 1 - Marking the Moons: The Jewish Calendar
Lesson 2 - Awe-some Holy Days: New Year, New Beginning
Lesson 3 - Mistakes and Mindfulness: A Day of Forgiveness
Lesson 4 - Uniting in Joy: Sukkot, Shemini Atzeret, Simchat Torah
Lesson 5 - Retelling and Reconnecting: The Pesach Seder Up-Close
Lesson 6 - Harvesting the Field and Soul: Shavuot through the Ages

videos.title: Week 3-Israel in the Headlines- Fighting Back on the Media Battlefield
videos.description:
Recorded September 17, 2025
Instructor: Jacki Alexander, CEO and President, HonestReporting
Why is Israel constantly in the global spotlight, and why does so much of that coverage feel distorted, misleading, or incomplete? In this three-part workshop series, HonestReporting’s executive team breaks down the media forces shaping how Israel is portrayed in the world, and how everyday people can push back with clarity, confidence, and credibility.

Part 1 - The Shifting Landscape of Information: Jacki Alexander, Global CEO of HonestReporting, explores how information is created, consumed, and weaponized in today’s media ecosystem, and why understanding that shift is essential to defending truth.
Part 2 - Eye on the Media: Executive Director Gil Hoffman reveals how and why global media outlets fixate on Israel, exposing patterns of bias, omission, and double standards that shape public opinion worldwide.
Part 3 - Taking Action on Social Media: Learn practical strategies for becoming a more effective digital advocate: how to share compelling content, respond strategically, and support Israel’s image in the spaces where narratives are formed.
Taught by two seasoned media professionals with deep experience in journalism, advocacy, and public communication, this course offers a unique insider’s view of the challenges Israel faces on the global stage. Whether you’re new to these issues or looking to sharpen your skills, you’ll leave empowered to speak up—and speak out—with greater impact.

videos.title: Week 3-The Mavens: the Pulpit, the Pen, and the Podcast
videos.description:
Recorded September 17, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Steven Chervin
What does it take to become a voice people trust? For over a century, a surprising number of Jewish writers, thinkers, and therapists have taken on the public role of advice-givers, responding to questions about family, identity, sex, culture, and ethics. In this three-session course, Dr. Steven Chervin will explore the Jewish personalities behind some of America’s most iconic advice columns and counseling platforms, and how their responses reflected and shaped the cultural and moral concerns of their time.

The Bintel Brief (Sept. 3) - In the early 20th century, waves of Eastern European Jewish immigrants arrived in New York’s Lower East Side, bringing with them disorienting questions about identity, modernity, and belonging. The Bintel Brief, a Yiddish advice column in The Forverts, became a trusted outlet for navigating the conflicts of immigrant life: work and family, tradition and change, American ambition and Jewish values. We’ll read and discuss the letters that captured a generation’s struggle to find its footing in a new world.
Dueling Sisters Ann Landers and Dear Abby (Sept. 10) - Jewish advice went mainstream in the mid-20th century, thanks to twin sisters from Sioux City, Iowa—Esther Pauline and Pauline Esther Friedman—better known as Ann Landers and Dear Abby. Their syndicated columns reached tens of millions of readers across backgrounds and beliefs, shaping American conversations about everything from etiquette to ethics, marriage to morality. We’ll explore their rise to fame, their lasting cultural impact, and the fascinating rivalry that defined their careers.
Kosher Sex and Modern Intimacy (Sept. 17) - In more recent decades, a new generation of Jewish advice-givers emerged to tackle deeply personal—and often taboo—subjects. Holocaust survivor and media icon Dr. Ruth Westheimer, Orthodox provocateur Rabbi Shmuley Boteach, and psychotherapist Esther Perel each opened up conversations about sex, love, and emotional connection. Drawing on Jewish tradition and modern psychology, they redefined the public conversation around intimacy, trust, and identity in a changing world.
Taught by Dr. Steven Chervin, a veteran Melton educator and insightful interpreter of Jewish culture and history, this class combines humor, history, and real-life dilemmas. Whether you grew up reading these columns or are encountering them for the first time, you’ll leave with a deeper understanding of how advice dispensed from podiums, pulpits, and print shapes Jewish identity, values, and voice.

videos.title: MeltonX, A Partner Exchange - Fall 2025
videos.description: Held 16-Sept-2025

videos.title: Week 2-Machzor Masterclass: Inside the High Holiday Prayerbook
videos.description:
Recorded September 16, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Wallk
For many, the High Holiday services are the most emotionally charged—and most confusing—moments of the Jewish year. Even for seasoned synagogue-goers, the unfamiliar melodies, lengthy prayers, and theological language of the Mahzor can feel overwhelming. What are we saying? Why are we saying it? And how can we enter this season with greater understanding and intention?

In this two-session course, Rabbi Amy Wallk offers a guided journey through some of the most central and stirring pieces of the High Holiday liturgy. We’ll explore the unique three-part structure of the Rosh Hashanah Mussaf service and take a closer look at the confessional prayers of Yom Kippur. Along the way, we’ll consider both traditional and modern interpretations, uncovering layers of meaning that can deepen our experience in the sanctuary. Whether you’re a High Holiday regular or returning to services after time away, this class will help you find greater clarity, connection, and intention in the year’s holiest days, all guided by Rabbi Wallk’s insight, warmth, and scholarly depth.

videos.title: Sacred Bites: The Ritual Foods of Rosh HaShanah
videos.description:
Recorded September 16, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Dovi Fischer
Pomegranates, leeks, dates, apples, fish, and yes, even the almond myth. These aren’t just tastes; they’re omens, intentions, and ancient prayers served on a plate. In this spiritually rich session, Rabbi Dovi Fischer delves into the hidden meanings behind the symbolic foods of Rosh HaShanah and how they can help us enter the new year with clarity, hope, and a sense of purpose. Drawing on centuries of tradition and his own deep experience as a teacher in Jerusalem’s Old City, Rabbi Dovi brings warmth, insight, and a touch of surprise to every bite.

videos.title: Week 2-In Defense of Judgment: Moral Clarity in a Too-Tolerant Time
videos.description:
Recorded September 15, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Neil Fleischmann
“Don’t judge me.” “No judgment.” “I’m not judging you.” These phrases show up everywhere in modern conversation, but what if we’re missing something essential? As we approach the High Holidays, when Divine judgment takes center stage, it’s worth asking: What does Jewish tradition say about judging others… and ourselves? In this relevant and reflective session, Rabbi Neil Fleischmann explores Jewish sources that challenge the idea that all judgment is harmful. Is judgment always a negative act, or can it be an expression of care and responsibility? When should we speak up, and when should we step back? And what does it mean to believe in a judging God in a culture that insists we never judge at all? Rabbi Neil brings psychological insight, gentle humor, and a grounded teaching style to this vital conversation. With clarity and compassion, he helps us think more deeply about what it means to live in relationship with ourselves, with others, and with a tradition that insists judgment and compassion belong together.

videos.title: Whats Mine is Yours
Week 2-Crossroads and Clashes: Jewish Turning Points in Conflict
videos.description:
Recorded September 11, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Charles E. Savenor
At pivotal moments in Jewish history, the path forward was anything but clear. Leaders rose with bold visions for the future, only to find themselves opposed by equally passionate voices with different answers to the same urgent question: What does the Jewish people need right now? In this two-part exploration, Rabbi Charlie Savenor guides us through two watershed conflicts, examining the personalities, politics, and values at stake, and why these debates continue to resonate today. We’ll begin with the biblical rebellion of Korach against Moses, a dramatic clash over leadership and legitimacy in the wilderness. Then, in part two, we’ll turn to the Second Temple era, where Sadducees and Pharisees fought for the soul of Jewish life, torn between priestly tradition in Jerusalem and rabbinic authority in Babylonia.

Rabbi Savenor brings deep expertise in how communities govern themselves, how leaders make decisions, and how civic responsibility takes shape. Known for drawing learners directly into the conversation, he creates a space where history comes alive through spirited discussion and shared discovery. Together, you’ll see how pivotal debates in Jewish history set patterns that still echo in the way we lead, follow, and build community today.

videos.title: Week 5-The Next Chapter - It's A Date: Marking Time the Jewish Way
videos.description:
Recorded September 10, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi John Carrier
Whether you look forward to the Passover Seder all year or avoid it like the plague, add “It’s a Date” to your calendars. Gain new insight into days that mark profound moments in history, and others that mark when to harvest the grain — and pop open the wine. In this unit, we will explore how the Jewish calendar works. We will explain and discuss our holidays and holiday rituals. Rabbi John Carrier, instructor, has taught for the Melton School since 2015 and regards its curriculum as “the best next step” for people who develop a passion for Jewish learning during their conversion process.

Lesson 2 - Awe-some Holy Days: New Year, New Beginning
Lesson 3 - Mistakes and Mindfulness: A Day of Forgiveness
Lesson 4 - Uniting in Joy: Sukkot, Shemini Atzeret, Simchat Torah
Lesson 5 - Retelling and Reconnecting: The Pesach Seder Up-Close
Lesson 6 - Harvesting the Field and Soul: Shavuot through the Ages

videos.title: Becoing Unstuck: Teshuva and the Science of Lasting Change
videos.description:
Recorded September 8, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Mona Fishbane
Ever feel stuck in patterns you can’t seem to break? As the High Holidays approach, the practice of Teshuva invites us to return, repair, and renew. But can we really change, especially as adults? In this timely Elul course, Dr. Mona Fishbane brings together ancient Jewish wisdom and cutting-edge neuroscience to explore the possibilities (and limits) of transformation. We’ll examine how habits form, why they’re so hard to break, and how guilt, apology, and forgiveness play essential roles in healing our relationships with others and ourselves. The latest research on neuroplasticity suggests that real, lasting change is possible, even in later life. But it requires intention, awareness, and practice. Drawing on Maimonides, William James, and contemporary psychology, we’ll uncover tools to rewire our brains and reshape our lives. If you’re seeking a path forward—toward your better self or a repaired connection—this class offers insight, encouragement, and a map for the journey.

videos.title: When Words Fail Us: Women, Sound, and the High Holidays
videos.description:
Recorded September 4, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Ziva Hassenfeld
What happens when the words we have are not enough? The High Holiday season is filled with liturgy, longing, and sound, but also with silence, miscommunication, and raw emotion. In this evocative class, Dr. Ziva Hassenfeld explores the stories of Sarah, Hagar, and the Binding of Isaac, Biblical narratives where language falters and meaning slips between the cracks. Together, we reflect on how these stories—and the wordless blast of the shofar—help us confront the complexity of teshuvah, judgment, and divine connection. What does it mean to cry out and not be heard? To speak and be misunderstood? And where do we find meaning when language runs out? This is Ziva’s Melton+ debut, and we’re proud to welcome her. A scholar of Jewish education and text at Brandeis University, she is known for her clarity, warmth, and ability to draw out the emotional depth of even the most familiar stories. Her teaching is both accessible and profound, making it perfect for learners seeking insight as the High Holidays approach.

videos.title: Week 2-The Complex Drama of forgiveness: Stories of Struggle, Power, and Repair
videos.description:
Recorded September 2, 2025
Instructor: Joe Septimus
Some of Judaism’s most powerful teachings are hidden in the heart of human interaction, as told through Talmudic stories. In this pre-high holiday class, Joe Septimus delves into two profound rabbinic narratives that explore the complex emotional terrain of personal flaws, offensive behavior, resentment, apology, and the hope for repair. At the intersection of power, judgment, social norms, and personal limits, these stories reveal our rabbis’ deep awareness of human vulnerability. They show how Jewish law alone can’t address every emotional or ethical challenge, and how stories helped the sages wrestle with human failure, conflict, and real-life struggles. Through layered discussion and thoughtful interpretation, we’ll explore what these narratives say about the human cost of misunderstanding, the challenge of forgiveness, and the courage it takes to move forward. The class presents a meaningful opportunity to embark on your own season of introspection, with honesty, compassion, and a commitment to change.

videos.title: Introduction to the Book of Ecclesiastes (Kohelet)
videos.description: By Rabbi Daniel Reifman

videos.title: Introduction to the Book of Ruth
videos.description: By Rabbi Daniel Reifman

videos.title: Introduction to Timeless Wisdom from the Books of Ecclesiastes, Lamentations, and Song of Songs
videos.description: By Rabbi Daniel Reifman

videos.title: Politics, Memory, and the Making of a Holy Site
videos.description:
Recorded August 28, 2025
Dr. Eva Gurevich
In the immediate aftermath of the Six-Day War, Jews returned to the Western Wall, but the question of what that space should become sparked intense debate. Would the newly reclaimed Kotel serve as a national symbol, a religious shrine, or something else entirely? In this one-hour session, Brandeis University postdoctoral historian Eva Gurevich guides us through the heated summer of 1967, when rabbis, politicians, journalists, and everyday citizens wrestled with competing visions of the Wall’s future. Drawing on rich firsthand sources from iconic photographs to fiery op-eds, we’ll explore how political power, public memory, and gender roles converged in shaping what became Judaism’s most iconic site.

videos.title: Week 3-The Next Chapter - It's a Date: Marking Time the Jewish Way
videos.description:
Recorded August 27, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi John Carrier
Whether you look forward to the Passover Seder all year or avoid it like the plague, add “It’s a Date” to your calendars. Gain new insight into days that mark profound moments in history, and others that mark when to harvest the grain — and pop open the wine. In this unit, we will explore how the Jewish calendar works. Lesson 2 - Awe-some Holy Days: New Year, New Beginning
Lesson 3 - Mistakes and Mindfulness: A Day of Forgiveness
Lesson 4 - Uniting in Joy: Sukkot, Shemini Atzeret, Simchat Torah
Lesson 5 - Retelling and Reconnecting: The Pesach Seder Up-Close
Lesson 6 - Harvesting the Field and Soul: Shavuot through the Ages

videos.title: Week 2-Crossing Lines: Women and Moral Risk
videos.description:
Recorded August 27, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
In two of the Bible’s most provocative narratives, women stand at the center, making bold, unsettling choices in the face of power, silence, and catastrophe. This two-part course explores the stories of Tamar and the daughters of Lot, women whose actions challenged social norms and sparked lasting debate in Jewish tradition. Tamar seduces the man who wronged her, disguising herself as a harlot to secure justice and reclaim her dignity without exposing him to public shame. Lot’s daughters, believing they are the last survivors of a destroyed world, initiate an act of incest to preserve the human race. These stories are raw, haunting, and morally complex. They raise enduring questions about justice, survival, sexuality, and the price of agency in a world where women are often left with impossible choices. Rabbi Michele Faudem, a gifted educator with deep experience in Jewish learning and gender studies, is uniquely suited to lead this conversation. Known for her dynamic teaching and compassionate approach to complex texts, Michele creates space for learners to engage honestly with these stories—and discover the ethical and emotional power they still hold today.

videos.title: Week 1-The Complex Drama of Forgiveness: Stories of Struggle, Power, and Repair
videos.description:
Recorded August 26, 2025
Instructor: Joe Septimus
Some of Judaism’s most powerful teachings are hidden in the heart of human interaction, as told through Talmudic stories. In this pre-high holiday class, Joe Septimus delves into two profound rabbinic narratives that explore the complex emotional terrain of personal flaws, offensive behavior, resentment, apology, and the hope for repair. At the intersection of power, judgment, social norms, and personal limits, these stories reveal our rabbis’ deep awareness of human vulnerability. They show how Jewish law alone can’t address every emotional or ethical challenge, and how stories helped the sages wrestle with human failure, conflict, and real-life struggles. Through layered discussion and thoughtful interpretation, we’ll explore what these narratives say about the human cost of misunderstanding, the challenge of forgiveness, and the courage it takes to move forward. The class presents a meaningful opportunity to embark on your own season of introspection, with honesty, compassion, and a commitment to change.

videos.title: 24 Hours in the Woods With AI Chatbots (Segment)
videos.description:
Segment of a video recorded by the Wall Street Journal's Joanna Stern, exploring whether AI Chatbots can be be real friends. 

Meta AI, Google Gemini, OpenAI ChatGPT and Microsoft Copilot now have eerily human voices. WSJ’s Joanna Stern strapped the AI bots to a tripod and took them to a cabin in the woods to test their skills as helpers—and as friends.

Published November 13, 2024

videos.title: Soundtrack of the Season: Approaching the High Holidays
videos.description:
Recorded August 25, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Wallk
“One thing I ask of God, only that do I seek…” With these words, Psalm 27 captures the longing at the heart of the High Holidays: a desire for closeness, clarity, and comfort in uncertain times. But why did the sages choose this psalm, out of all 150, to accompany us from the start of Elul through the end of the season? In this insightful one-session class, Rabbi Amy Wallk leads an inspiring exploration of King David's poem, blending close textual study with musical interpretation. Together, we’ll reflect on its themes of fear and faith, yearning and presence, and consider how this ancient prayer helps us open our hearts to the season of renewal. 

Rabbi Amy Wallk has served as the rabbi of Temple Beth El in Springfield, MA since 2008. Her RA service has included the Vaad HaKavod, the Siddur Lev Shalem editorial committee, and chairing the 2023 Annual Campaign. Prior to her pulpit career, she worked in Jewish education for children and adults. She has also been the longtime Conservative contributor for Moment magazine's "Ask the Rabbis" column.

videos.title: Week 3-Return and Renew: High Holiday Teachings of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks
videos.description:
Recorded August 25, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
The High Holidays ask us to confront life’s biggest questions: What does it mean to be alive? What responsibilities do we carry into a new year? And how do we repair what’s been broken, both within and between us? In this three-part course, Rav Johnny Solomon guides us through the wisdom of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks, offering a deeply personal and profoundly Jewish meditation on what it means to reflect, return, and begin again. Drawing from the former Chief Rabbi’s commentary in the Koren Machzor and other writings, Rav Johnny will explore three major themes from Sacks's High Holiday teachings: 
“The Gift and Responsibilities of Life” - What does it mean to be given life, and what are we called to do with it?
“Why Life Is Not Easy, and Why That Isn’t a Bad Thing” - How do struggle and challenge shape us, and why are they essential to spiritual growth?
“The Blessing to Forgive and to Be Forgiven” - What role does forgiveness play in renewal, and how can we approach it with honesty and courage?

videos.title: Introduction to the Book of Esther
videos.description: By Rabbi Daniel Reifman

videos.title: Introduction to Timeless Tales, Modern Sensibilities: A Deep Look at the Books of Esther and Ruth
videos.description: By Rabbi Daniel Reifman

videos.title: Week 3-Israel Current Events - the Daily Alert
videos.description:
Recorded August 21, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Adam Schaffer
The news out of Israel changes nearly every minute. Now more than ever, it is crucial to stay informed, but finding reliable news is difficult, and finding unbiased news is even harder... until now. Enter Melton+ and the Daily Alert. The Daily Alert is a curated news site founded by the Conference of Presidents of Major American Jewish Organizations and crafted by expert analysts at the Jerusalem Center for Security and Foreign Affairs. It collects news stories from publications across the ideological and editorial spectrum, allowing readers to view the same stories from multiple perspectives. Rabbi Adam Schaffer, our instructor, will co-create the class with our students by leading them through a guided exploration of stories from that week’s news. We will examine not only the content of the stories but also the discrepancies and hidden biases that appear in different publications’ coverage of the same events. In doing so, we aim to equip students with the ability to understand the news more effectively and appreciate the diverse perspectives in the coverage.

videos.title: Voicesin Verse-A Summer of Poetry: Leah Goldberg - The Music of Elegance, Intellect, and Grace
videos.description:
Recorded August 12, 2025
Instructor: Cantor David A. Lipp
Experience a century of Hebrew poetry shaped by the dreams and tensions of Zionism. This is one of three sessions that explores a poet through musical performance and cultural context, tracing how language, land, and longing have shaped the Israeli soul. These poems offer not only a record of national transformation but also a soundtrack to its emotional journey. Leah Goldberg’s poetry blends European lyricism with emotional depth, creating work that continues to inspire composers and performers. In this session, we’ll listen to musical settings of poems like Halicha LeKesariya (“A Walk to Caesarea”) and Lamdeini, as interpreted by artists such as Chava Alberstein. Join Cantor David Lipp as we explore how melody draws out the quiet strength and philosophical nuance in Goldberg’s writing, revealing new layers of meaning through sound.

videos.title: Taste of "Secrets of the Jewish South": A Sneak Peek of Our Upcoming Trip to Savannah and Charleston
videos.description:
Recorded August 11, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Joellyn Zollman
Master Melton Educator Dr. Joellyn Zollman will lead a Melton Travel excursion to Charleston and Savannah in November, and you're invited to preview this visit to two of the South’s most charming destinations. Southern Jewish life has a rich history, and this virtual visit to two of the oldest Jewish communities in the country will provide a sense of what to expect on the trip.

videos.title: From Ruins to Renewal: Making Meaning from the Mourning of Tisha B'Av
videos.description:
Recorded August 6, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Neil Fleischmann
Every summer, the Jewish calendar marks a lesser-known day of mourning called Tisha B’Av, a time set aside to remember the destruction of the ancient Temple in Jerusalem. But why do Jews still grieve for a building lost thousands of years ago? Is Judaism asking us to stay mired in sorrow, or is something deeper at work? In this reflective session, Rabbi Neil Fleischmann invites learners to explore how Jewish mourning practices carry meaning far beyond loss. Together, we’ll consider how sadness can create space for growth, connection, and even joy, and how remembering the Temple may ultimately be less about ruins and more about rebuilding. Whether you’re familiar with Tisha B’Av or new to its significance, this class offers a meaningful opportunity to explore how grief can open the door to connection, growth, and rebuilding.

videos.title: Reclaiming Joy: Tu B'Av, the Hidden Holiday of Healing
videos.description:
Recorded August 6, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Grossblatt Pessah
What if there was a Jewish holiday devoted to second chances, quiet miracles, and unexpected moments of reconciliation, but you’d never heard of it? Tu B’Av is one of the most joyful days on the Hebrew calendar, yet its stories are rarely told. Beneath its surface lie powerful themes of forgiveness, reunion, and women finding their own voice. This class invites you into those stories and asks: What would it take to bring that kind of healing into our lives today? We explore ancient moments of return, when old divides softened, when estranged communities found their way back to one another, when cycles of loss gave way to renewal. These narratives describe what it means to grow, to love, and to begin again. By the end of the session, you may find yourself wondering how you ever missed this holiday, and how you’ll honor it moving forward.

The class is taught by Rabbi Amy Grossblatt Pessah, a soulful and compassionate teacher known for helping learners connect Jewish tradition to their own life journeys. With warmth, insight, and scholarship, she guides us through forgotten texts into joy, healing, and renewal.

videos.title: Voices in Verse: A Summer of Jewish Poetry - Muriel Rukeyser and the Poetry of Witness
videos.description:
Recorded August 5, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Joellyn Zollman
Muriel Rukeyser carried the legacy of poets like Lazarus and Margolin into the charged politics of the 20th century. A poet, activist, and truth-teller, she believed that poetry could—and should—speak to the injustices of the world. In this final session, we explore how Rukeyser placed herself within a Jewish tradition of resistance and moral imagination. Through her mid- to late-century writing, we consider poetry as both a personal reckoning and a form of public witness. Dr. Zollman holds a PhD in Jewish History from Brandeis University. Her areas of specialization include religious material culture and American Jewish History. She has worked with exhibits and collections at the Smithsonian Institution, the Skirball Museum, and the San Diego History Center. Dr. Zollman has been teaching in the Melton Program since 2011.

videos.title: Week 3-Between Text and Temple: Jewish Life in a Time of Transition
videos.description:
Recorded August 4, 2025
Instructor: Catriella Freedman
What happens when a civilization shifts its spiritual center? In the centuries surrounding both the rebuilding and ultimate destruction of the Second Temple, Jewish life underwent a profound transformation: from sacrifice to study, priest to sage, and prophecy to interpretation. In this three-part course, historian and scholar Catriella Freedmanbrings us into this pivotal era of Jewish history, exploring the political, religious, and philosophical forces that reshaped the Jewish world.

Session 1 - Transmission and Authority in a Post-Temple World: What does it mean to inherit sacred tradition without a central sanctuary? We’ll examine how early rabbinic texts, including the famous “chain of transmission,” reflect efforts to preserve authority and identity after the exile.
Session 2 - Shimon HaTzaddik and the Question of Power: A priest and a sage, Shimon HaTzaddik stood at the intersection of Temple leadership and rabbinic authority. We’ll explore his role—and the broader power dynamics—during the turbulent transition from biblical to rabbinic Judaism.
Session 3 - Theology in a Hellenistic World: How did Jewish thinkers respond to the rise of Greek philosophy and shifting ideas about reward, justice, and divine service? Through the teachings of figures like Antigonos Ish Socho, we’ll consider how new theological frameworks took root in a rapidly changing world.
With clarity, depth, and a gift for making the ancient feel urgent, Catriella Freedman invites learners to explore a defining era of Jewish history that continues to shape the questions we ask and the texts we live with today.

videos.title: In the Presence of Power- Jewish Law and Political Leadership
videos.description:
Recorded July 31, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski
What does Jewish law say about how we should act in the presence of a president or any great leader? In this thought-provoking session, Rabbi Uri Pilichowski explores traditional halachic sources that guide Jewish behavior before kings and rulers. We will examine blessings, customs, and texts that have shaped Jewish interactions with power across centuries.
Then we will bring the conversation into the present. Does a president count as a “king”? Do these laws apply to democratically elected leaders? And what does it mean today to show respect without compromising one’s values? Join Rabbi Pilichowski for a timely and spirited exploration of leadership, reverence, and responsibility through the lens of Jewish tradition.

videos.title: Week 3-Ethics Under Fire- Jewish Law in Times of War
videos.description:
Recorded July 30, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. Danny Schiff
What does Jewish tradition say about the most difficult questions war presents: life and death, innocence and guilt, justice and restraint? In this timely three-part course, Rabbi Dr. Danny Schiff, a leading scholar of Jewish ethics, draws on classical texts and modern realities to examine the moral boundaries of warfare.
Session 1: When Is War Permitted?If civilian casualties are all but inevitable, can war ever be ethical? Under what circumstances do Jewish sources permit going to war?
Session 2: Guilt, Deterrence, and the Enemy - Is it ethical to fight more aggressively to prevent future attacks? Can we truly distinguish between combatants and civilians? What do the rabbis say about the danger of dehumanizing the enemy?
Session 3: Siege, Strategy, and the IDF - Does Jewish law permit cutting off food or supplies during siege? How closely does the IDF align with the ethical expectations of Jewish tradition? Rabbi Schiff brings deep scholarship and moral clarity to a topic that remains both painful and pressing. This course provides learners with the tools to think critically about wartime ethics with empathy, integrity, and a foundation in Jewish values.

videos.title: Home Grown Rabbis: The Birth of American Rabbinic Training
videos.description:
Recorded July 29, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Gil Graff
Before 1840, no rabbi had settled permanently in the United States, and it wasn’t until the 1880s that the first ordination occurred on American soil. Yet within a single generation, seminaries like Hebrew Union College, the Jewish Theological Seminary, and RIETS were founded, radically transforming American Jewish life. In this class, Dr. Gil Graff will explore the visionary leaders, institutional priorities, and cultural conditions that fueled this rapid shift from scarcity to structure.
A renowned Jewish historian and Executive Director of Builders of Jewish Education in Los Angeles, Dr. Gil Graff brings both scholarly depth and storytelling skill to the classroom. A popular speaker at the Melton Conference of Learners in December 2024, he returns to Melton+ to guide learners through one of the most consequential transformations in American Jewish history.

videos.title: Week 2- Between Temple and Text: Jewish Life in a Time of Transition
videos.description:
Recorded July 28, 2025
Instructor: Catriella Freedman
What happens when a civilization shifts its spiritual center? In the centuries surrounding both the rebuilding and ultimate destruction of the Second Temple, Jewish life underwent a profound transformation: from sacrifice to study, priest to sage, and prophecy to interpretation. videos.title: The Age of the Influencer
videos.description:
Recorded July 22, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Benjamin Kramer
What kind of influence do you have, and what kind should you want? In today’s culture, visibility is power. From social media platforms like TikTok and X to everyday conversations at work or in our communities, what we say and do can shape the thoughts and actions of others, often in ways we don’t even realize. But long before online platforms, Jewish tradition was already asking: What responsibility comes with influence? In this timely class, Rabbi Ben Kramer will explore core ideas from Jewish law and philosophy that speak directly to this question. We’ll study concepts like the responsibility to avoid placing a stumbling block before the blind (lifnei iver) and the need to be mindful of even the appearance of wrongdoing (marit ayin). We’ll also consider how these ancient teachings can help us navigate the moral complexity of modern life. Whether you're a parent, a leader, a teacher, or simply someone who wants to act with more awareness and integrity, this class will offer insight into how Jewish values can shape the way we influence others with clarity, accountability, and purpose.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: Intelligence Failures, Military Realities, and Political Consequences
videos.description:
Recorded July 22, 2025
Instructor: Dan Feferman
What went wrong before October 7? What’s really happening on the ground in Gaza and across the region today? And how are military, intelligence, and political decisions shaping what comes next? In this one-session briefing, Dan Feferman brings a rare combination of insider experience and analytical depth to help us make sense of a rapidly shifting conflict. Feferman is a former IDF intelligence officer, policy advisor, and national security analyst with deep access to military and diplomatic circles. In this session, he’ll offer an integrated look at the war's evolving dynamics—connecting the dots between battlefield realities, intelligence strategy, and shifting global politics. With clarity, nuance, and firsthand insight, Feferman will help us understand not just where things stand, but what’s at stake.

videos.title: Week 2-Grief and Growth: Ritual, Feeling, and the Jewish Way to mourn
videos.description:
Recorded July 21, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
What do Jewish mourning rituals ask of us, not just in action, but in feeling? And what do we do when those feelings don’t come easily, or at all? In this two-part course with Dr. Sandra Lilienthal, we’ll explore the emotional and spiritual power of Jewish mourning practices through the lens of law and tradition.
In the first session, we’ll examine how grief is expressed in Jewish law and custom, ranging from personal mourning to communal periods of sorrow, such as the fast days associated with the destruction of the Temple. What behaviors are prescribed during these times—fasting, refraining from music or celebration—and why do they take the shape they do?
In the second session, we’ll ask more profound questions about the relationship between ritual and emotion. Can a practice evoke a feeling? Does a feeling count without a practice? And what is lost if we abandon rituals meant to guide us through the ups and downs of individual and collective memory? Drawing on sources from the Torah, rabbinic literature, medieval codes of Jewish law, and the works of modern thinkers, Dr. Lilienthal invites learners into a nuanced conversation about how tradition helps us feel, and how it continues to evolve when it doesn’t.

videos.title: Melton Meetup - July 21, 2025
Week 1- Between Temple and Text: Jewish Life in a Time of Transition
videos.description:
Recorded July 21, 2025
Instructor: Catriella Freedman
What happens when a civilization shifts its spiritual center? In the centuries surrounding both the rebuilding and ultimate destruction of the Second Temple, Jewish life underwent a profound transformation: from sacrifice to study, priest to sage, and prophecy to interpretation.
