Session 1 - Transmission and Authority in a Post-Temple World: What does it mean to inherit sacred tradition without a central sanctuary? We’ll examine how early rabbinic texts, including the famous “chain of transmission,” reflect efforts to preserve authority and identity after the exile.
Session 2 - Shimon HaTzaddik and the Question of Power: A priest and a sage, Shimon HaTzaddik stood at the intersection of Temple leadership and rabbinic authority. We’ll explore his role—and the broader power dynamics—during the turbulent transition from biblical to rabbinic Judaism.
Session 3 - Theology in a Hellenistic World: How did Jewish thinkers respond to the rise of Greek philosophy and shifting ideas about reward, justice, and divine service? Through the teachings of figures like Antigonos Ish Socho, we’ll consider how new theological frameworks took root in a rapidly changing world.
With clarity, depth, and a gift for making the ancient feel urgent, Catriella Freedman invites learners to explore a defining era of Jewish history that continues to shape the questions we ask and the texts we live with today.

videos.title: Week 1-Ethics Under Fire: Jewish Law in Times of War
videos.description:
Recorded July 16, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. Danny Schiff
What does Jewish tradition say about the most difficult questions war presents: life and death, innocence and guilt, justice and restraint? In this timely three-part course, Rabbi Dr. Danny Schiff, a leading scholar of Jewish ethics, draws on classical texts and modern realities to examine the moral boundaries of warfare.

Session 1: When Is War Permitted? If civilian casualties are all but inevitable, can war ever be ethical? Under what circumstances do Jewish sources permit going to war?
Session 2: Guilt, Deterrence, and the Enemy - Is it ethical to fight more aggressively to prevent future attacks? Can we truly distinguish between combatants and civilians? What do the rabbis say about the danger of dehumanizing the enemy?
Session 3: Siege, Strategy, and the IDF - Does Jewish law permit cutting off food or supplies during siege? How closely does the IDF align with the ethical expectations of Jewish tradition? 

Rabbi Schiff brings deep scholarship and moral clarity to a topic that remains both painful and pressing. This course provides learners with the tools to think critically about wartime ethics with empathy, integrity, and a foundation in Jewish values.

videos.title: Voices in Verse - A Summer of Jewish Poetry, Voice and Witness: Bialik in Harmony- From National Poet to Folk Hero
videos.description:
Recorded July 15, 2025
Instructor: Cantor David A. Lipp
In Voices of the Land: A Century of Israeli Poetry in Song, experience a century of Hebrew poetry shaped by the dreams and tensions of Zionism with Cantor David Lipp. Each session explores one poet through musical performance and cultural context, tracing how language, land, and longing have shaped the Israeli soul. These poems offer not only a record of national transformation but a soundtrack to its emotional journey. Chaim Nachman Bialik, often called Israel’s national poet, left behind a legacy that stretches from epic laments on exile to playful children’s rhymes. In this class, we’ll explore musical settings of his poetry, from traditional arrangements to modern choral and orchestral interpretations. How does music express Bialik’s tension between sorrow and hope, and what can these adaptations tell us about the evolution of Israeli identity?

videos.title: Week 1-Grief and Growth: Ritual, Feeling, and the Jewish Way to Mourn
videos.description:
Recorded July 14, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
What do Jewish mourning rituals ask of us, not just in action, but in feeling? And what do we do when those feelings don’t come easily, or at all? In this two-part course with Dr. Sandra Lilienthal, we’ll explore the emotional and spiritual power of Jewish mourning practices through the lens of law and tradition.

In the first session, we’ll examine how grief is expressed in Jewish law and custom, ranging from personal mourning to communal periods of sorrow, such as the fast days associated with the destruction of the Temple. What behaviors are prescribed during these times—fasting, refraining from music or celebration—and why do they take the shape they do?

In the second session, we’ll ask more profound questions about the relationship between ritual and emotion. Can a practice evoke a feeling? Does a feeling count without a practice? And what is lost if we abandon rituals meant to guide us through the ups and downs of individual and collective memory?

Drawing on sources from the Torah, rabbinic literature, medieval codes of Jewish law, and the works of modern thinkers, Dr. Lilienthal invites learners into a nuanced conversation about how tradition helps us feel, and how it continues to evolve when it doesn’t.

videos.title: Faith and Founding: Celebrating America's Independence Day
videos.description:
Recorded July 9, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Amy S. Wallk
As the US celebrates its Independence Day, join Rabbi Amy Wallk for a powerful look at what the promise of America meant to its earliest Jewish residents. We’ll read George Washington’s landmark letter to the Jews of Newport and the message that prompted it from synagogue leader Moses Seixas, who dared to envision full Jewish belonging in a new republic. We’ll also hear from Rebecca Samuel, a young immigrant writing home in the 1790s, offering a rare first-hand account of the joys and hardships of Jewish life in a land with no built-in community. Rabbi Wallk, a beloved Melton teacher and noted scholar of Jewish thought and American Judaism, brings warmth, insight, and depth to this one-session exploration. Together, we’ll ask what stories we tell about being Jewish in America, where those stories come from, and what they might leave out. Through letters, prayers, and personal voices, we’ll reflect on the tensions between freedom and loneliness, faith and founding, and how those early experiences continue to shape Jewish life today.

videos.title: Voices in Verse: A Summer of Jewish Poetry - Anna Margolin and the Poetry of Immigration
videos.description:
Recorded July 8, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Joellyn Zollman
In Voice and Witness: The Poetry of Three American Jewish Women with Dr. Joellyn Zollman, journey through a century of American Jewish women’s poetry shaped by immigration, identity, and activism. Each session focuses on one poet, weaving together biography, history, and close reading. Together, these voices form a lyrical archive of what it has meant to be Jewish, female, and American—across generations of hope, displacement, protest, and belonging. An immigrant from Russia, Anna Margolin arrived in New York as part of the very wave of Jewish migration that Emma Lazarus had championed decades earlier. Writing in Yiddish with lyrical intensity and deep ambiguity, she captured the chaos and beauty of urban Jewish womanhood in the early 20th century. We will explore her life and her verse in both Yiddish and English, listening for what still echoes.

videos.title: Belief and Betrayal: The Heretical Messiah
videos.description:
Recorded July 7, 2025
Instructor: Dr. David Sclar
In the 1660s, centuries before social platforms or celebrity culture, Sabbatai Tsevi was one of the most widely followed Jewish figures of his time: a self-proclaimed messiah whose charisma and claims captivated communities from Amsterdam to Yemen. During a time of political upheaval and spiritual longing across the Jewish world, Tsevi was hailed by many as the long-awaited messiah. He inspired extraordinary devotion until his unexpected conversion to Islam in 1666 sent shockwaves through global Jewry. This course explores the rise and unraveling of the Sabbatean movement through contemporary Hebrew texts, kabbalistic thought, and period engravings that reflect both the fervor and the fallout. Led by historian Dr. David Sclar, a scholar of early modern Jewish religious life with appointments at Harvard, Princeton, and Oxford, the class will bring academic rigor to one of the most complex chapters in Jewish history, inviting reflection on the nature of belief during moments of crisis and change. Why do communities embrace bold promises of redemption? What happens when those hopes collapse? As Jewish life continues to grapple with uncertainty and reinvention today, the story of Sabbatai Tsevi offers a lens through which to examine the enduring tension between messianic longing and historical reality.

videos.title: Parenting on a Prayer Teaser
Jerusalem Destroyed: What Was Lost, What Endured
videos.description:
Recorded July 7, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Katherine Aaron-Beller
When the Second Temple fell in 70 CE, it marked more than the loss of stone and sanctuary: it was a spiritual, cultural, and political crisis. Jews lost their center, their sovereignty, and their connection to Jerusalem in an exile that scarred Jewish life for nearly 1,900 years.
In this powerful exploration with Dr. Katherine Aron-Beller, we will revisit this foundational catastrophe, not only as historians but as archaeologists and anthropologists. We’ll explore the writings of the ancient historian Josephus, who struggled to make sense of the disaster even as he lived through it: How could such a holy city—so wealthy, so ancient, so globally connected—be brought low in siege and sorrow? We examine this moment from a historical distance, but also with an eye to the present. In the wake of recent violence and rising fear, the story of 70 CE echoes now more than ever. As we enter the month of Tammuz and a season devoted to mourning the loss of Jerusalem, we’ll explore how the ruins of the past still shape the questions we ask today.

videos.title: A Melton Legacy of Leadership & Learning - Honoring Randi Brenowitz
videos.description: Recorded 9-June-2025 at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem

videos.title: Week 2-Where Shall I Find Redemption: Past or Future?
videos.description:
Recorded July 3, 2025
Instructor: Joe Septimus
In this two-part series, beloved Melton+ teacher Joe Septimus will introduce us to the very first piece of Aggadah in the Talmud: a haunting and beautiful tale of the first-century sage Rabbi Yossi, a ruined Jerusalem, and an unexpected encounter with the Prophet Elijah, offering essential wisdom for the Jewish future. Aggadah—the storytelling tradition within the Talmud—includes legends, parables, theological reflections, and moral teachings. If the Talmud is a work of law, why are fantastical stories included at all? What do these poetic interludes reveal about the inner world of the Rabbis, and what might they reveal about ours? As we encounter this ancient story of ruins and revelation, we may also uncover timeless wisdom about what it means to rebuild, both personally and collectively.

Through lively discussion and close reading, Joe will bring the text to life, blending profound scholarship with open-hearted curiosity. Together, we will explore what Aggadah is, why the Rabbis chose to open a vast legal text with this surreal narrative, and how its themes—destruction and resilience, prayer and presence, divine grief and human response—still speak to us today.

videos.title: Week 4-Double Takes: Reading Foundational Stories with New Eyes
videos.description:
Recorded July 2, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. David Harbater
What if the Book of Genesis isn’t just one story—but two? In this eye-opening course, Melton curriculum author Rabbi David Harbater explores the familiar tales of Noah, the Flood, and Abraham’s journey with fresh eyes. A careful reading reveals a dual narrative structure—two versions of creation, two versions of the flood, and two versions of Abraham’s calling—each offering a different window into God’s relationship with humanity. This course builds on Rabbi Harbater’s earlier Melton+ class on the Creation story, applying the same analytical lens to the next chapters of Genesis. Whether you joined that first journey or are coming in fresh, this class is designed to be both a continuation and a compelling standalone experience. Discover what these paired accounts reveal about divine justice and mercy, chosenness and covenant, and the unfolding drama that sets the stage for the Jewish story. Rabbi Harbater’s thoughtful approach opens up a layered and literary view of Torah—one that invites both critical insight and spiritual reflection.

videos.title: El nora alila
Cantor Azi Schwartz Kol Nidre
videos.description: WMIY

videos.title: World Class Melton Celebration and Online Farewell Tribute to Haim Aronovitz 2022
Week 3-"In Our Time"- 60 Years of Jewish-Catholic Dialogue
videos.description:
Recorded July 1, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Steven Chervin
In 1965, during a time of global upheaval—marked by the civil rights movement, sweeping changes within the Catholic Church, and the growing influence of the American Jewish community—the Second Vatican Council released Nostra Aetate (“In Our Time”), a groundbreaking document that redefined the Church’s relationship with Judaism and other non-Christian religions. Sixty years later, Nostra Aetate remains a landmark of reconciliation and a lens for understanding what progress in interfaith dialogue can look like. In this three-part series, Dr. Steve Chervin will guide us through the roots, realities, and reverberations of Nostra Aetate. Each session will offer a deeper understanding of the evolving relationship between Jews and Catholics:

Session 1: What Came Before will examine the origins of Christian anti-Judaism in the New Testament, trace its violent expressions through the Middle Ages, and explore the historical forces that led to change at Vatican II.
Session 2: A Document in the Making will uncover the process behind Nostra Aetate—from Jewish advocacy and internal Church debates to countless revisions—and then engage in a close reading of the document itself, exploring its structure, subtleties, and spirit.
Session 3: Sixty Years Later will assess how Nostra Aetate has shaped Jewish–Catholic relations over the past six decades. What progress has been made? Where have efforts fallen short? And what might come next?
In an era shaped by Pope Leo’s new leadership and rising global antisemitism, the questions raised by this document remain urgent, timely, and unfinished. Jewish participants are encouraged to invite Catholic and Christian friends to register and join the conversation.

videos.title: Union Square's High-Concept Sukkahs
videos.description:
On September 19, the finalists of the Sukkah City design competition unveiled their re-imagined sukkahs in New York's Union Square. Locals and tourists alike rubbed shoulders to get a closer look at the ritual huts, which transformed the downtown park into an architectural showcase.

Uploaded on Sep 20, 2010 at 9:17 pm by The Forward (https://vimeo.com/15134424)

videos.title: What is Yom Kippur_ The Jewish High Holiday
videos.description: WMIY

videos.title: Rosh Hashana
videos.description: WMIY

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: Reflections on Israel at War
videos.description:
Recorded June 26, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Steven Windmueller
Join renowned political analyst and scholar Dr. Steven Windmueller for a penetrating look at Israel’s current war and its far-reaching implications. With decades of experience decoding Jewish political behavior, Israeli policy, and Middle East dynamics, Windmueller brings unmatched clarity to a moment defined by uncertainty and upheaval. In this hour-long presentation, Dr. Windmueller examines how Israel arrived at this point, what’s unfolding beneath the headlines, and how the war is reshaping not just strategy and sovereignty, but the soul of the nation. Drawing connections between history, geopolitics, and Jewish communal life, Windmueller helps us think critically and feel deeply—equipping us to meet this moment with insight, perspective, and purpose.

videos.description:
Recorded June 26, 2025
Instructor: Joe Septimus
In this two-part series, beloved Melton+ teacher Joe Septimus will introduce us to the very first piece of Aggadah in the Talmud: a haunting and beautiful tale of the first-century sage Rabbi Yossi, a ruined Jerusalem, and an unexpected encounter with the Prophet Elijah, offering essential wisdom for the Jewish future. videos.description:
Recorded June 25, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. David Harbater

Discover what these paired accounts reveal about divine justice and mercy, chosenness and covenant, and the unfolding drama that sets the stage for the Jewish story. Rabbi Harbater’s thoughtful approach opens up a layered and literary view of Torah—one that invites both critical insight and spiritual reflection.

videos.title: What Does the Quran Say About Jews and Judaism?
videos.description:
Recorded June 19, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Miriam Goldstein
How does the Quran talk about Jews, and what can Jewish readers learn from it today? This course will explore the Quran's complex portrait of Jews and Judaism: sometimes affirming, sometimes critical, always layered and revealing. Along the way, we'll encounter familiar biblical figures–like Abraham, Joseph, and Moses–through a new lens, and wrestle with the Quran's portrayal of Jews as both spiritual kin and subjects of reproach. This is a rare opportunity for Jewish learners to engage thoughtfully and respectfully with Islam's foundational text, deepening understanding across religious boundaries. Guiding our exploration is Professor Miriam Goldstein, a leading scholar of medieval Judeo-Arabic literature and interreligious exchange and a member of the Department of Arabic Language and Literature at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem. Trained at Harvard, Cambridge, and Hebrew University, Goldstein brings a historian's rigor and a storyteller's sensitivity to the class.  This class is sponsored by Jenny and Julian Josephson of La Jolla, California. It is also co-sponsored by the American Friends of the Hebrew University of Jerusalem, the Canadian Friends of the Hebrew University of Jerusalem, and the Florence Melton School Institute at Hebrew University of Jerusalem.

videos.title: Week 2-Double Takes: Reading Foundational Stories with New Eyes
videos.description:
Recorded June 18, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. David Harbater
What if the Book of Genesis isn’t just one story—but two? In this eye-opening course, Melton curriculum author Rabbi David Harbater explores the familiar tales of Noah, the Flood, and Abraham’s journey with fresh eyes. videos.title: Voices in Verse: A Century of Israeli Poetry - The Poet Rachel in Song: Longing, Landscape, and Loss
videos.description:
Recorded June 17, 2025
Instructor: Cantor David A. Lipp

Experience a century of Hebrew poetry shaped by the dreams and tensions of Zionism. Each session explores one poet through musical performance and cultural context, tracing how language, land, and longing have shaped the Israeli soul. These poems offer not only a record of national transformation, but a soundtrack to its emotional journey. Rachel Bluwstein, known simply as “the poet Rachel,” gave voice to the inner life of early Zionism: its loneliness, its yearning, and its deep connection to the land. In this session, we’ll explore how iconic poems like Zemer Nugeh (“A Sad Song”) and Ve’ulai (“Perhaps”) have been reimagined in music by composers such as Naomi Shemer and Yehudit Ravitz. Though her life was brief, the resonance of her quiet brilliance still echoes through the voices and instruments of Israeli artists today.

videos.title: Week 2 - "In Our Time"- 60 Years of Jewish-Catholic Dialogue
videos.description:
Recorded June 17, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Steven Chervin
In 1965, during a time of global upheaval—marked by the civil rights movement, sweeping changes within the Catholic Church, and the growing influence of the American Jewish community—the Second Vatican Council released Nostra Aetate (“In Our Time”), a groundbreaking document that redefined the Church’s relationship with Judaism and other non-Christian religions. Sixty years later, Nostra Aetate remains a landmark of reconciliation and a lens for understanding what progress in interfaith dialogue can look like. In this three-part series, Dr. Steve Chervin will guide us through the roots, realities, and reverberations of Nostra Aetate. Each session will offer a deeper understanding of the evolving relationship between Jews and Catholics:
Session 1: What Came Before will examine the origins of Christian anti-Judaism in the New Testament, trace its violent expressions through the Middle Ages, and explore the historical forces that led to change at Vatican II.
Session 2: A Document in the Making will uncover the process behind Nostra Aetate—from Jewish advocacy and internal Church debates to countless revisions—and then engage in a close reading of the document itself, exploring its structure, subtleties, and spirit.
Session 3: Sixty Years Later will assess how Nostra Aetate has shaped Jewish–Catholic relations over the past six decades. What progress has been made? Where have efforts fallen short? And what might come next?
In an era shaped by Pope Leo’s new leadership and rising global antisemitism, the questions raised by this document remain urgent, timely, and unfinished. Jewish participants are encouraged to invite Catholic and Christian friends to register and join the conversation.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: War with Iran in Real-Time
videos.description:
Recorded June 16, 2025
Instructor: Lazar Berman
Few journalists have the access, expertise, and on-the-ground perspective of Times of Israel diplomatic correspondent Lazar Berman. In this urgent briefing, Berman draws on his deep reporting and personal connections to explain what’s unfolding in Israel’s war with Iran. For American Jews, the headlines can feel overwhelming and the distance disorienting. What should we be watching for? What does meaningful support look like? And how are our Israeli friends and family really doing? Berman will walk us through how the conflict began, where the situation stands now, and what might come next. Then he’ll share what life is really like for Israelis living through it, from air raids to resilience. The session ends with an open Q&A, giving you direct access to one of the most trusted voices in the region. Berman won’t just report the facts—he’ll help us make sense of what they mean for our communities, our conversations, and our connection to Israel in a time of war.

videos.title: Tracing Our Names, Finding Your Roots
videos.description:
Recorded June 16, 2025
Instructor: Evgenia Kempinski
What does your last name say about where—and who—you come from? Join Jewish heritage educator Evgenia Kempinski for a fascinating journey into the origins of Ashkenazi Jewish surnames. From names like Goldberg and Shapiro to more unusual ones you may never have heard before, this session explores the surprising, sometimes touching, and often political stories behind the names carried through generations. You’ll also gain practical tips for beginning your own genealogical search, including questions to ask, sources to explore, and pitfalls to avoid.

videos.title: Week 1-Double Takes: Reading Foundational Stories with New Eyes
videos.description:
Recorded June 11, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. David Harbater
What if the Book of Genesis isn’t just one story—but two? In this eye-opening course, Melton curriculum author Rabbi David Harbater explores the familiar tales of Noah, the Flood, and Abraham’s journey with fresh eyes. videos.title: Voices in Verse : A Summer of Jewish Poetry - Emma Lazarus and the Poetry of Belonging
videos.description:
Recorded June 10, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Joellyn Zollman
Best known for her iconic words at the base of the Statue of Liberty, Emma Lazarus was a Sephardic New Yorker whose poetry gave voice to both Jewish pride and American promise. In this session, join American Jewish historian Dr. Joellyn Zollman to explore Lazarus’s 19th-century life and work, reading beyond “The New Colossus” to discover a writer who helped define what it meant to be American and unapologetically Jewish.

videos.title: Week 1- "In Our Time"- 60 Years of Jewish-Catholic Dialogue
videos.description:
Recorded June 10, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Steven Chervin
In 1965, during a time of global upheaval—marked by the civil rights movement, sweeping changes within the Catholic Church, and the growing influence of the American Jewish community—the Second Vatican Council released Nostra Aetate (“In Our Time”), a groundbreaking document that redefined the Church’s relationship with Judaism and other non-Christian religions. videos.title: Week 6-The Next Chapter - Between the Lines
videos.description:
Recorded June 5, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi John Carrier
thought and practice for over 3,000 years.
But how do we connect to words written so long ago? What do they reveal today? And how do they inspire action? In this course, we will examine the many ways that we bring the words of our texts into our daily lives. We’ll look at the hints and clues in the letters of sacred Jewish texts: the Torah, the Prophets, the Talmud (no previous Hebrew knowledge required). We’ll exam structure and authorship. We’ll discuss the commentary of yesterday. We’ll share where we stand today.

Lesson 1 - A Learned People: Connecting through Jewish Text
Lesson 2 - Depths of Learning: The Roots of Jewish Texts
Lesson 3 - Who Wrote the Torah: Why Does It Matter?
Lesson 4 - From Text to Context: The Evolution of Jewish Practice
Lesson 5 - Open Your Hand and Your Heart: Tracing Jewish Values from Text to Life
Lesson 6 - Mussar and Middot: Ethics, Character Traits, and Being a Good Jew

videos.title: "No Talent"? Jewish Artists Who Proved the Critics Wrong
videos.description:
Recorded June 5, 2025
Instructor: Ellaine Rosen
“The Jews have displayed little talent for the visual, and almost none for the figurative arts.”
–Bernard Berenson, famed 20th-century art critic.

It’s a bold and troubling claim. Was Berenson reflecting reality, or overlooking centuries of creativity shaped—and sometimes suppressed—by Jewish tradition itself? Join historian Ellaine Rosen for a sweeping exploration of Jewish visual expression, from the Tabernacle in the desert to the Agam room in the Pompidou Center in Paris. For generations, the Second Commandment cast a long shadow over Jewish artistic practice, limiting images while elevating other forms of sacred beauty. But as political emancipation, secular education, and new philosophies spread through 19th- and 20th-century Europe, Jewish artists stepped boldly onto the world stage—and changed it.

Wait until you see what’s in store: Realism with Maurycy Gottlieb, Impressionism with Camille Pissarro, Fauvism with Jules Pascin, Cubism with Jacques Lipchitz, and the luminous worlds of Modigliani, Chagall, Soutine, and Rothko. Through their work, we’ll explore how Jewish identity and visual imagination collided, flourished, and redefined what art—and Jewish identity—could look like.

videos.title: Week 5 - The Next Chapter - Between the Lines
videos.description:
Recorded May 29, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi John Carrier

videos.title: The Mystery of Sweetness: Why Do We Eat Cheesecake on Shavuot?
videos.description:
Recorded May 29, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Deborah Silver
Cheesecake is delicious—but is that really why it became the iconic food of Shavuot? In this rich and surprising class, Rabbi Deborah Silver explores the deeper meanings behind the tradition. Shavuot began as an ancient harvest festival and later became the celebration of receiving Torah. So how did dairy dishes and sweet desserts become part of the story. What does it mean to mark a moment as awesome as Sinai with something as simple as cheesecake? And what does this say about the Jewish relationship to learning, to nourishment, to joy? With her signature blend of intellect, heart, and spiritual curiosity, Rabbi Silver guides learners toward a deeper understanding of the holiday—and the delicious metaphors it serves up.

videos.title: Reimagining Revelation- How We Reinvented Shavuot
videos.description:
Recorded May 28, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
Shavuot is one of Judaism’s three major festivals—a time of gathering, learning, and celebrating our connection to Torah. But it hasn’t always looked the way it does today. In the Torah, it’s a harvest festival. In rabbinic tradition, it becomes the anniversary of the revelation at Sinai. In Reform communities, it’s also Confirmation Day—a moment of personal and communal affirmation. In this session, Dr. Sandra Lilienthal will explore how Jewish communities across time and geography have reimagined Shavuot—adding new rituals, meanings, and spiritual layers. From poetry and pilgrimage to cheesecake and all-night learning, the evolution of Shavuot tells a bigger story: that Jewish tradition is not frozen in time, but constantly growing in response to the people who live it.

videos.title: Miriam's Desert Journey: Isolation, Revelation, and Song
videos.description:
Recorded May 27, 2025
Instructor: Alicia Jo Rabins
Miriam, the revered prophetess and sister of Moses, faced a profound trial when she was struck with leprosy and isolated from the Israelite camp for seven days. This episode, often overshadowed by her leadership at the Red Sea, offers deep insights into themes of solitude, transformation, and resilience.  In this interactive workshop, we:

Examine Miriam's Isolation: Delve into the narrative of Miriam's time alone in the desert, exploring its significance and parallels to Moses' experience on Mount Sinai.
Seek Personal Wisdom: Reflect on the lessons Miriam's journey imparts for our own moments of challenge and seclusion.
Experience Musical Interpretation: Engage with a live performance of Alicia Jo Rabins' original song from her Girls in Trouble series, which brings Miriam's story to life through contemporary music.

videos.title: Unlocking Secrets of the Jewish South: A Preview
videos.description:
Recorded May 27, 2025
Instructors: Dr. Joellyn Zollman and Andrea Gardenhour
Join Andrea Gardenhour and Dr. Joellyn Zollman for an insider’s look at Melton’s upcoming journey through Charleston and Savannah, home to two of America’s oldest and most vibrant Jewish communities. Discover hidden histories, landmark synagogues, and stories of resilience that shaped the Jewish South. This special session offers a taste of the rich learning, personal connections, and exclusive experiences that await on Secrets of the Jewish South this fall. Click the link for more information.

videos.title: Randi Video - RabbiKoritzinsky
Sandy on Randi
Week 4-The Next Chapter - Between the Lines
videos.description:
Recorded May 22, 2025

Lesson 5 - Open Your Hand and Your Heart: Tracing Jewish Values from Text to Life
Lesson 6 - Mussar and Middot: Ethics, Character Traits, and Being a Good Jew

videos.title: Let's Go GODShopping- A Hands-On Exploration of Personal Theology
videos.description:
Recorded: May 22, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Mara Young
Judaism affirms that God is one, yet our tradition offers a vast array of ways to understand and experience the Divine. From the Ruler of the Universe to the Still, Small Voice, Jewish texts and teachings present diverse and authentic concepts of God. This is not a lecture—it’s a workshop. Led by Rabbi Mara Young, Senior Rabbi of Woodlands Community Temple and an educator known for her engaging and inclusive teaching style, this highly interactive session invites you to explore different theological perspectives and “go shopping” for the one that resonates most with your own beliefs. Through discussion, reflection, and guided activities, you’ll actively engage with ideas about God and articulate your personal relationship with the Divine. The more you put in, the more you’ll take away.

videos.title: Week 3-The Path of Moral Wisdom: A Journey Through the Book of Proverbs
videos.description:
Recorded May 21, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
three-session journey through the Book of Proverbs (Mishlei)—King Solomon’s biblical masterpiece, rich with ethical insight and spiritual depth. Together, we explore how this ancient collection of moral teachings speaks to the everyday challenges of modern life. Our map through the text is provided by Rabbi Samson Raphael Hirsch, the 19th-century thinker who overlayed philosophical clarity and thematic structure on Mishlei. Rather than reading verse by verse, Hirsch invites us to explore key topics that run through the book. This series focuses on six of them:
Speech – the ethics and impact of our words
Parenting – raising children with wisdom and values
Money – navigating wealth, poverty, and dignity
Friendship – the nature of real relationships
Anger – the costs of losing control
Pride and Humility – the balance of self-worth and modesty
With Rav Johnny’s gentle, wise, and heartfelt teaching style, we’ll uncover practical insight and personal meaning. His thoughtful presence, combined with Hirsch’s moral clarity, opens a path for learners to reflect, connect, and rediscover Mishlei as a living guide for ethical Jewish living.

videos.title: Week 3-What the Sages Said: A Chain of Wisdom from Pirkei Avot
videos.description:
Recorded May 20, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Neil Fleischmann
Take a closer look at Pirkei Avot (Ethics of Our Fathers) with Rabbi Neil Fleischmann in this fresh and thought-provoking exploration of its first chapter. Often quoted but rarely studied in sequence, this chapter traces the chronological chain of Jewish leadership—from Moses at Sinai through the rabbis of the Mishnah—revealing a carefully constructed lineage of learning and law. Rabbi Fleischmann returns to Melton+ after his acclaimed course on Jewish allegory, bringing his signature blend of wit, warmth, and wide-ranging sources. With insight and curiosity, he invites learners to rediscover these ancient voices and the enduring values they passed down, generation to generation.

videos.title: Why These Ten? Decoding Sinai's Blueprint for Freedom
videos.description:
Recorded May 20, 2025
Instructor: Rabbanit Sarah Sharman Moser
What makes the Ten Commandments more than a list of ancient laws? For Rabbi Jonathan Sacks, they were nothing less than the moral blueprint of a free society—a divine response to the dehumanization of Egypt. With Rabbanit Sarah Sharman Moser, a distinguished Sacks Scholar and expert in Tanach and Jewish thought, we’ll explore how each commandment functions as a “chapter title” for Torah itself, echoing ancient covenantal treaties and anchoring the ethical vision of the biblical world.

This course also brings to light Rabbi Sacks’ powerful distinction between cherut (true freedom) and hofesh (mere license). What kind of freedom are we really called to pursue? Through this lens, Sinai becomes not just a moment in our collective past, but a living guide for cultivating justice, compassion, and responsibility today. Come ready to rediscover the Decalogue—not as history, but as humanity’s highest calling.

videos.title: Sacred Time: Spending Shabbat with Abraham Joshua Heschel
videos.description:
Recorded May 19, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Steven Chervin
What does it mean to experience Shabbat, not just observe it? And how might it feel to experience rest not as a rule, but as a refuge in time?
 
In this class, longtime Melton teacher Steven Chervin will lead a warm and inspiring exploration of Abraham Joshua Heschel’s beloved work: The Sabbath. First published in 1951, this short and poetic book became an instant spiritual classic, offering Jews and Christians alike a vision of holiness rooted not in space or achievement, but in sacred time. Through guided discussion and selected readings, the course will uncover Heschel’s most powerful ideas: the Sabbath as a “cathedral in time,” as a taste of eternity, as a pause from the noise of doing in order to embrace the mystery of being.
 
For anyone seeking a more mindful, meaningful relationship with Shabbat, this class will offer more than ideas—it will offer companionship on a journey. Whether Shabbat is a longtime practice or a new frontier, learning with Heschel will provide a deeper experience of sacred rest and a renewed appreciation for the spiritual power of stopping.

videos.title: What We Choose to Remember: Holocaust Memory as Civic Education
videos.description:
Recorded May 15, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Charles E. Savenor
Held in honor of Yom HaShoah--Israel's Holocaust Remembrance Day--this dynamic, discussion-based session invites learners to reflect on how we remember, and why it matters. Led by Rabbi Charles Savenor, Executive Director of Civic Spirit, we'll explore Holocaust memorials in the U.S., Israel, and Germany as windows into civic values and collective identity. What lessons do these monuments teach about responsibility, resilience, and the role of memory in shaping the future? Join us for a powerful conversation on a day dedicated to remembrance and moral reckoning.

videos.description:
Recorded May 14, 2025
Instructor: Rav Johnny Solomon

videos.title: Week 2-I Go Where You Go: Ruth and the Covenant of Connection
videos.description:
Recorded May 12, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
As we count the days from Passover to Shavuot, the Book of Ruth offers timeless wisdom for building a more just and compassionate community. What do we owe one another? Who belongs—and who decides? And how do kindness and power shape the society we create? In this two-session course, Rabbi Michele Faudem—a favorite among Melton learners for her clarity, passion, and deep commitment to student understanding—guides us through Ruth’s bold journey into the heart of what it means to be Jewish, and the enduring values that her biblical story calls us to live by today. This series is sponsored by Linda Feinfeld.

videos.title: Week 4-The Greatest Israeli Stories Never Told
videos.description:
Recorded May 1, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski
History is often shaped by the stories we know—but what about the ones we don’t? In this fascinating four-part series, rabbi and raconteur Uri Pilichowski unearths the little-known yet remarkable moments from Israeli history that shaped the nation. From the time of the Temples to modern military feats, these stories reveal the resilience, ingenuity, and sheer audacity that define Israel’s past and present. 

Pre-State - Ancient Legacies and the Fight for Independence: Long before the establishment of the State of Israel, Jewish history in the land was shaped by legendary figures, pivotal battles, and cultural shifts that continue to influence Israeli identity today. Why do so many Jewish people bear the Greek name Alex, Alexander, or Alexa, while names like Theos and Damian are almost nonexistent? And how did the 1929 riots, remembered as the Hebron riots, actually begin in Jerusalem? This session traces the hidden narratives of Jewish perseverance and rebellion from Temple times through the struggle against the British Mandate. 

The Start of Israel - Founders, Fighters, and Fortunes: The early years of the Jewish state were a whirlwind of impossible victories, bold leadership, and strategic gambles that defined the country’s character. How did a group of Ultra-Orthodox Yeshiva students- who don’t typically serve in the IDF- manage to stop the Jordanian army from conquering Jerusalem in 1948? And what happened to the billions of dollars in German reparations that disappeared under Ben-Gurion’s watch? This session uncovers the untold stories of the founders who laid the groundwork for the Israel we know today.

The Ground Settles - From Survival to Success: As Israel transitioned from a struggling new state to an emerging powerhouse, it faced moments of both unexpected challenge and triumph. Which future Israeli Prime Minister was wounded as a soldier before rising to power? And how did Menachem Begin accidentally dismantle an entire political system—all because he misunderstood Israeli slang? This session brings to light the quirks, courage, and key turning points that helped shape Israel’s evolving national identity. 

Modern Times - Innovation, Espionage, and Global Impact: Israel’s modern history is a tapestry of daring operations, diplomatic showdowns, and astonishing breakthroughs that continue to surprise the world. How did Israel pull off top-secret missions involving stolen documents, exploding beepers, and high-stakes intelligence? And which leader wound up competing with President Biden over the best Golda Meir story? This final session brings Israel’s most recent, mind-boggling tales to life, proving that the country’s history is still being written in ways both unexpected and extraordinary.

videos.title: From Memory to Independence: Yom Hazikaron and Yom Haatzmaut
videos.description:
Recorded April 29, 2025
Instructor: Mickey Flaumenhaft
Israel’s Memorial Day and Independence Day sit side by side on the calendar—two days, one powerful progression. In this timely and moving course, IDF reservist Mickey Flaumenhaft will trace the establishment of Yom HaZikaron and Yom HaAtzmaut, offering a deeper look at their historical roots and their continued impact on Israeli society. Drawing on both national narratives and personal stories, Flaumenhaft will illuminate the emotional complexity of moving from mourning to celebration. Through moments both collective and intimate, he will explore what these days reveal about the soul of a nation—its memory, resilience, and evolving identity.

videos.title: Facing West: One Family's Journey from India to Israel (1945-1955)
videos.description:
Recorded April 28, 2025
Instructor: Rahel Musleah
This immersive presentation brings to life the experiences of a family making their way from India to Israel in the pivotal decade leading up to and following statehood. Told through original letters, archival photographs, and the music of the era, it paints a vivid portrait of life in Palestine (Israel) on the brink of independence. Pioneer songs reflect the determination of early settlers, while Baghdadi-Indian Jewish pizmonim express a deep yearning for Zion. A powerful and evocative way to celebrate Yom Ha'atzmaut.

videos.title: Week 3-The Greatest Israeli Stories Never Told
videos.description:
Recorded April 24, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski

videos.title: Week 3-From Talmud to Table: The Origins of the Seder Rituals
videos.description:
Recorded April 23, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Benjamin Kramer
We all know the horseradish, the dipping, the leaning, the drinking, —but where do these beloved Passover Seder traditions actually come from? In this engaging three-part series, Melton educator Rabbi Ben Kramer takes us on a deep dive into the Talmudic roots of the Seder’s most familiar rituals. We’ll uncover the ancient debates, trace the evolution of these practices through centuries of rabbinic interpretation, and see how they became the customs we recognize today. Through lively discussion and close reading of classical texts, we’ll explore:

The Origins of the Seder’s Key Rituals – What were the sages actually arguing about?
The Meaning of the Matzah, Maror, and More – Symbolism, structure, and surprises in the Haggadah
How Local Customs Became Universal Practice – Why do some traditions still vary today?
Passover is a holiday of questions, so come discover the answers behind the rituals we experience year after year!

videos.title: The Price of Freedom- Hostage Diplomacy in Israel and the U.S.
videos.description:
Recorded April 23, 2025
Instructor: Lazar Berman
Join Lazar Berman, diplomatic correspondent for The Times of Israel, for an up-to-the-minute report on the Israeli hostage crisis and an analysis of the ongoing negotiations for their return. This session also explores the history of how Israel and the United States have handled hostage situations in the past, revealing the strategies, dilemmas, and consequences of high-stakes diplomacy. Gain critical insight into the complexities of negotiating for lives in a turbulent geopolitical landscape.

videos.title: Israel History - video compilation to go with Beyond Borders supplemental material
videos.description: Submitted by Harvey Leven

videos.title: Sketches and Stories: Voices from the Holocaust
videos.description:
Recorded April 22, 2025
Instructor: Evgenia Kempinski
Explore the profound narratives of two remarkable women whose artistic expressions and literary works provide invaluable insights into life during the Holocaust. Born in Latvia, Esther Lurie emigrated to Palestine (then under British Mandate) in the 1930s. A twist of fate brought her to Kovno, Lithuania, in 1940, just as the Nazi occupation began. Confined to the Kovno Ghetto and later imprisoned in Stutthof, Esther documented the lives of fellow Jews through her art, sketching both daily scenes and intimate portraits. After surviving the Holocaust, she returned to Israel in 1945 and dedicated her life to preserving the memory of those she encountered through her powerful visual testimony. A teenager when the Nazis occupied Vilnius, Maria Rolnikaite was deported with her family to the Vilnius Ghetto and later to a concentration camp. She had begun keeping a diary before the war and continued writing throughout her ordeal. After surviving, Maria achieved the extraordinary: she published her diary in Soviet Lithuania—a rare act of remembrance under a regime that often suppressed Jewish narratives. Her work was later translated into more than 30 languages, establishing her as a leading voice for Holocaust memory and education in the post-Soviet world. Join historian and educator Evgenia Kempinski as she brings these two voices to life. Through a compelling presentation enriched with historical context, personal stories, and visual materials, participants will encounter the enduring legacy of two women who bore witness for generations to come.

videos.title: Exile in the East: Jewish Stories from Asia During the Holocaust
videos.description:
Recorded April 16, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Geraldine Gudefin
When we think of the Holocaust, we often focus solely on its European dimensions. Yet the reach of Jewish displacement and persecution during World War II extended well beyond the borders of Europe into the heart of Asia.

This eye-opening session explores the little-known experiences of the approximately 40,000 Jews—refugees and long-established communities alike—who lived in Asia during the war. We’ll examine the intersecting histories of Baghdadi Jews in Calcutta, the influx of European Jewish refugees into Shanghai, and the harrowing internment of Jews in Singapore under Japanese occupation.
Set against the broader backdrop of Chinese and Japanese wartime policy toward Jews, this course uncovers a complex and global chapter of Jewish history—one of unlikely encounters, temporary sanctuaries, and shifting political landscapes.

Led by historian Dr. Geraldine Gudefin, whose research bridges Jewish migration, family life, and legal pluralism across continents, this session draws from her current work based in Southeast Asia to illuminate a rarely told story with scholarly depth and personal insight.

We’ll uncover the ancient debates, trace the evolution of these practices through centuries of rabbinic interpretation, and see how they became the customs we recognize today. Through lively discussion and close reading of classical texts, we’ll explore:

The Origins of the Seder’s Key Rituals – What were the sages actually arguing about?
The Meaning of the Matzah, Maror, and More – Symbolism, structure, and surprises in the Haggadah
How Local Customs Became Universal Practice – Why do some traditions still vary today?
Passover is a holiday of questions, so come discover the answers behind the rituals we experience year after year!

videos.title: Week 2- Seder Secrets- Unlocking the Hidden Story in the Haggadah
videos.description:
Recorded April 8, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Wallk
Each year at the Passover Seder, we follow 14 steps, recite familiar words, and retell the Exodus story—but beneath the rituals lie hidden meanings and powerful messages. How do these steps truly connect to the deeper themes of freedom, redemption, and hope? How can they spark meaningful conversations around our tables?

In this engaging session with Rabbi Amy Wallk, we will unlock the secrets of the Maggid section, exploring the key lessons of the Exodus and their relevance today. We’ll also dive into the mysteries of Eliyahu HaNavi, the call of "Next Year in Jerusalem," and the deeper messages woven into the structure of the Seder itself. Whether you lead your family’s discussion or simply want to bring fresh insights to the table, this class will help you uncover new layers of meaning and make your Passover experience more engaging and memorable.

videos.title: Week 4- Empire, Exile, and Survival: The Jewish Odyssey in Greece
videos.description:
Recorded April 8, 2025
Instructor: Avi Ben-Hur
For over two millennia, Jewish communities in Greece have navigated shifting empires, cultural transformations, and moments of both triumph and tragedy. In this four-part journey, historian Avi Ben-Hur unpacks the layered history of Greek Jewry, from its ancient roots to its near-destruction during the Holocaust.
1-Empire & Encounter - When Alexander Met the Priests: Jewish lore tells us that when Alexander the Great approached Jerusalem, he was met by the High Priest on Mt. Scopus and acknowledged the primacy of the One God. This moment marked the beginning of a long and complex relationship between Judaism and Hellenistic civilization. As Jews migrated to Greece during the Hellenistic period (4th–1st centuries BCE), they established the oldest Jewish Diaspora community in Europe. This session explores the forces behind that migration and the intricate “love-hate” relationship that developed between Jewish tradition and Greek culture.
2-Exile & Transformation – Under the Cross and the Crescent: With the Christianization of the Roman Empire in the 4th century CE, Greek Jews found themselves increasingly marginalized under Byzantine rule. Yet, despite these challenges, they developed distinct liturgies, traditions, and customs that set them apart from both Ashkenazi and Sephardi communities. In the late Middle Ages, Greece gradually came under Ottoman control, and with it came a new era for Greek Jewry. The Ottoman Empire welcomed Sephardic Jews expelled from Spain and Portugal, dramatically shifting the demographics of Greek Jewish communities. Nowhere was this transformation more profound than in Salonika, where the Spanish-speaking Sephardim reshaped the cultural and religious landscape, turning the city into the famed “Jerusalem of the Balkans.” This session examines the impact of these sweeping changes and the complex interplay between indigenous Romaniote Jews and newly arrived Sephardim.
3-Survival & Identity – Navigating Modernity and Nationhood: The 19th century brought seismic political shifts to Greece, forcing its Jewish communities to choose between competing identities: Ottoman or Greek, modernist or traditionalist. For the first time in their millennial history, Greek Jews faced widespread antisemitic violence. When Salonika—the third largest city in the Ottoman Empire—was absorbed into the Greek state on the eve of World War I, its Jewish population found itself transformed overnight from Ottoman subjects into Greek citizens. This session explores the choices and challenges that defined Greek Jewry during this period, from their responses to nationalism to their struggles for social and economic stability in a rapidly changing world.
4-Catastrophe & Resilience – The Shoah and Its Aftermath: During World War II, Greece suffered under a brutal tripartite occupation—Nazi, Italian, and Bulgarian. Jewish citizens fought valiantly in Greece’s resistance efforts, but their fates diverged depending on which foreign power controlled their region. Italian-occupied Jews initially found protection, while Bulgarian forces deported Macedonian Jews to Nazi extermination camps. The Nazis, in turn, systematically targeted Greek Jews for destruction, sending the vast majority of Salonika’s Jewish population to Auschwitz, where they were murdered. Yet, in several towns, local Christian authorities intervened to save their Jewish neighbors. This session follows the trajectory of Greek Jewry from the eve of the Holocaust to the present day, examining how and why their fate unfolded as it did—and how the remnants of these once-thriving communities continue to persevere.
This series offers invaluable insights into the unique Jewish heritage of Greece and serves as excellent preparation for those planning to join our upcoming "Islands of Memory: An Aegean Jewish Odyssey to Greece" tour in Summer 2025. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 3-Trailblazing Jewish Women: Conversos, Spies, Farmers, and Fighters
videos.description:
Recorded April 7, 2025
Instructor: Daniella Gefen
Throughout history, remarkable Jewish women have defied societal norms, shattered glass ceilings, and left indelible marks on their communities and beyond. In this three-part Melton+ course, esteemed educator Daniella Gefen will guide us through the lives and legacies of four extraordinary figures, each a beacon of resilience and leadership in her era: 

Part 1: My heart is in the West, but I am in the East- The stories of two women–one at the beginning of the Ottoman era in Israel, and one at the end–and the role they both played in the relief and safety of the Jews in Israel. From the catastrophe of the Spanish expulsion to the turn of a new promise in the early 20th century, we will encounter two very different women, their struggles and achievements, and the road they paved their people

Part 2: We can do it! - Through the story of the founding mother of women's work in Israel, we will explore the ideology, dreams, and achievements of the Second Aliyah—the wave of Socialist immigration that aspired to build a just and renewed Israel. This movement sought to forge a new kind of Jew: a farmer, deeply connected to the land, cultivating both its soil and its future.

Part 3: Still Orthodox - A glimpse into a contemporary pioneer and trailblazer who is still leading change for the women of Israel and her community. We will explore the fastest growing society in Israel, the ultra-Orthodox Haredis, and investigate their attitudes towards the state, service and women in the public sphere. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Going to "Baht" for Thailand's Jewish Community: Leading High Hoidays in Bangkok
videos.description:
Recorded April 3, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Jack Schlacter
Discover the unique story of Bangkok’s progressive Jewish community, a diverse group of expats who have made Thailand their home. While the congregation’s services are self-directed throughout the year, a rabbi is invited each fall to lead the community in High Holiday observances. In this session, Rabbi Jack Schlacter, of Sante Fe, New Mexico, offers an insider’s perspective on what it’s like to guide these meaningful services, alongside a vibrant exploration of Bangkok’s rich culture. Through colorful visuals and engaging storytelling, gain a glimpse into Jewish life in Thailand and the experiences of a prayer leader in this fascinating setting.

videos.title: Trailer - Talmud to Table
Trailer - Great Stories
Week 1-The Greatest Israeli Stories Never Told
videos.description:
Recorded April 3, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski
History is often shaped by the stories we know—but what about the ones we don’t? In this fascinating four-part series, rabbi and raconteur Uri Pilichowski unearths the little-known yet remarkable moments from Israeli history that shaped the nation. From the time of the Temples to modern military feats, these stories reveal the resilience, ingenuity, and sheer audacity that define Israel’s past and present. 

Pre-State - Ancient Legacies and the Fight for Independence: Long before the establishment of the State of Israel, Jewish history in the land was shaped by legendary figures, pivotal battles, and cultural shifts that continue to influence Israeli identity today. Why do so many Jewish people bear the Greek name Alex, Alexander, or Alexa, while names like Theos and Damian are almost nonexistent? And how did the 1929 riots, remembered as the Hebron riots, actually begin in Jerusalem? This session traces the hidden narratives of Jewish perseverance and rebellion from Temple times through the struggle against the British Mandate. 
The Start of Israel - Founders, Fighters, and Fortunes: The early years of the Jewish state were a whirlwind of impossible victories, bold leadership, and strategic gambles that defined the country’s character. How did a group of Ultra-Orthodox Yeshiva students- who don’t typically serve in the IDF- manage to stop the Jordanian army from conquering Jerusalem in 1948? And what happened to the billions of dollars in German reparations that disappeared under Ben-Gurion’s watch? This session uncovers the untold stories of the founders who laid the groundwork for the Israel we know today.
The Ground Settles - From Survival to Success: As Israel transitioned from a struggling new state to an emerging powerhouse, it faced moments of both unexpected challenge and triumph. Which future Israeli Prime Minister was wounded as a soldier before rising to power? And how did Menachem Begin accidentally dismantle an entire political system—all because he misunderstood Israeli slang? This session brings to light the quirks, courage, and key turning points that helped shape Israel’s evolving national identity. 
Modern Times - Innovation, Espionage, and Global Impact: Israel’s modern history is a tapestry of daring operations, diplomatic showdowns, and astonishing breakthroughs that continue to surprise the world. How did Israel pull off top-secret missions involving stolen documents, exploding beepers, and high-stakes intelligence? And which leader wound up competing with President Biden over the best Golda Meir story? This final session brings Israel’s most recent, mind-boggling tales to life, proving that the country’s history is still being written in ways both unexpected and extraordinary.

videos.title: Week 4-Got Chutzpah? A Brief History of Boldness and Strength
videos.description:
Recorded March 26, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Ed Feinstein
“Chutzpah” is Yiddish for insolence, audacity, or nerve. But the word is older than Yiddish. In the Talmud, “chutzpah” means irrepressible boldness and strength. Chutzpah describes the rare quality of moral courage at the heart of Judaism. It depicts a revolutionary vision of human responsibility and human possibilities, as well as a radical idea of our relationship to God. As we struggle with global issues, Chutzpah is a message the world is desperate to hear. In our learning together, we will explore how Chutzpah is expressed in the Jewish religious imagination and in the history of Jewish thought. This course is taught by Rabbi Ed Feinstein and is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 3-After October 7: Heroism, Healing, and Hope
videos.description:
Recorded March 25, 2025
Instructor: Avi Frier
When history unfolds in tragedy and resilience, the most powerful voices are those who lived it. This compelling three-part series brings you first-hand accounts from individuals whose lives were forever changed by the events of October 7 and its aftermath. Through personal testimony, we will explore themes of heroism, loss, survival, and the power of rebuilding in the face of destruction. Each session highlights a different perspective—from the battlefield, from a kibbutz on the front lines, and from an effort to restore hope through the magic of human connection.
Session 1: Strength in the South: Rebuilding Kerem Shalom - Amit Caspi, a 25-year resident of Kerem Shalom, tells the story of this extraordinary kibbutz—one where religious and secular Israelis forged a shared life together. He recounts the heroism of his community on October 7 and their ongoing efforts to rebuild in the wake of destruction.
Session 2: Service and Sacrifice: The Heroic Life and Death of Maoz Morell - Parents Eitan and Varda Morell share the story of their son, Maoz, an IDF soldier who fell in Gaza while defending Israel. Through the lens of three generations of Jewish survival, they recount his life of heroism, his sacrifice, and the legacy he leaves behind.
Session 3: Making Smiles Appear: Magic4Israel - American entrepreneur, entertainer, and educator Avi Frier turned despair into action, launching Magic4Israel in early 2024. Through this initiative, he and his team have been “making smiles appear” for soldiers and displaced families across Israel, proving that sometimes, even the smallest gestures can restore hope.

This series is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Breaking News, Breaking Truth: The Munich Massacre and the Ethics of Media
videos.description:
Recorded March 24, 2025
Instructor: Amy Kronish
More than fifty years ago, the world watched in horror as the 1972 Munich Olympics turned into a terrorist battleground. But what happened behind the cameras? Director Tim Fehlblum's September 5 isn’t just a film about the attack—it’s a gripping dive into the high-stakes world of live journalism, misinformation, and the power of media to shape history.

Join film expert Amy Kronish in a thoughtful discussion of this new film, its messages, and its modern-day echoes. How does media coverage impact public perception of terrorism? What happens when the race to report collides with the responsibility to tell the truth? We’ll examine September 5 through a contemporary lens, exploring the evolving nature of journalism, fake news, and the ongoing struggle against anti-Israel terrorism.

This discussion-based class assumes that participants have already seen the film, and learners are strongly encouraged to view it before the session to fully engage in the conversation. 

This program is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: For Such a Time as This: Rising to the Moment, Shaping the Jewish Future
videos.description:
Recorded March 20, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Elliot Cosgrove
Melton+ presents Rabbi Dr. Elliot J. Cosgrove, Senior Rabbi of Park Avenue Synagogue, for an interactive conversation on the themes of his new book, For Such a Time as This: On Being Jewish Today. At a moment when Jewish identity is being tested in unprecedented ways, Rabbi Cosgrove offers a compelling exploration of how history shapes our present, how resilience can counter rising antisemitism, and how Jewish tradition provides both strength and meaning in uncertain times. Through a thought-provoking discussion, Rabbi Cosgrove will examine the evolving challenges and opportunities facing Jewish communities today, urging us to reflect on what it means to be Jewish in a rapidly changing world. Whether you are deeply engaged in Jewish life or seeking a renewed sense of connection, this session will offer insight, inspiration, and a call to action for all who care about the Jewish future. This session is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.description:
Recorded March 19, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Ed Feinstein

videos.title: Tales from the Talmud: A Living Source of Wisdom
videos.description:
Recorded March 18, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi David Helfand
The Talmud isn’t just for scholars—it’s a treasure trove of human drama, timeless wisdom, and personal humanity waiting to be rediscovered. In A Bride for One Night, Dr. Ruth Calderon—a trailblazing Talmud scholar and former Knesset member—brings these ancient texts to life, reimagining their stories with a novelist’s eye and a modern thinker’s sensibility. Her groundbreaking work has captivated readers across the Jewish world, making the Talmud accessible, relevant, and deeply personal. Join Rabbi David Helfand for a dive into The Fishpond, the opening chapter of Calderon’s book. This gripping retelling of a tale from the Babylonian Talmud follows a teacher’s radical approach to inspiring struggling students—raising profound questions about leadership, humility, and the power of human connection. Together, we’ll explore how Calderon’s bold interpretations invite us to see the Talmud not as a closed book of laws but as a living source of meaning, filled with stories that still challenge and inspire us today. No prior experience with Talmud is needed—just curiosity and a willingness to see ancient words in a new light. This class is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 2-Confronting Evil: Lessons from Biblical and Modern Times
videos.description:
Recorded March 12, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
The nation of Amalek is infamous for its unprovoked and cowardly attack on the vulnerable Israelites during their journey through the wilderness, making it a lasting symbol of moral evil. Paradoxically, in Deuteronomy 25, the Bible commands us to both remember what Amalek did and erase their memory. In this two-part class, Dr. Sandra Lilienthal explores the story of Amalek from the Book of Exodus, the commandment to remember and eradicate their legacy, and the enduring challenge of confronting acts of cruelty and injustice in our own time.
This series is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 2- Got Chutzpah? A Brief History of Boldness and Strength
videos.description:
Recorded March 12, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Ed Feinstein
“Chutzpah” is Yiddish for insolence, audacity, or nerve. But the word is older than Yiddish. In the Talmud, “chutzpah” means irrepressible boldness and strength. Chutzpah describes the rare quality of moral courage at the heart of Judaism. It depicts a revolutionary vision of human responsibility and human possibilities, as well as a radical idea of our relationship to God. As we struggle with global issues, Chutzpah is a message the world is desperate to hear. In our learning together, we will explore how Chutzpah is expressed in the Jewish religious imagination and in the history of Jewish thought. This course is taught by Rabbi Ed Feinstein and is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Visions of Esther: Painted Portraits of Faith and Fate
videos.description:
Recorded March 11, 2025
Instructor: Evgenia Kempinski
Throughout history, artists from different cultures have offered unique perspectives on Queen Esther, Mordechai, King Ahasuerus, and Haman, shaping our understanding of their roles and the broader meaning of the Purim story.  

This fascinating session will examine how Italian, French, and Dutch artistic traditions have depicted the Book of Esther, with a special focus on 17th-century Dutch art, where Esther emerged as a symbol of independence during the Netherlands' struggle against Spanish rule. Artists like Rembrandt and his contemporaries infused the story with themes of resilience, courage, and identity, reflecting the social and political realities of their time.  

Through virtual visits to renowned institutions such as the Hermitage Museum in St. Petersburg, the Pushkin Museum in Moscow, the Louvre in Paris, and the Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam, we will analyze celebrated and controversial paintings. Discussions will focus on Esther's evolving imagery—from young and innocent to mature and decisive—as well as the complex characterizations of Ahasuerus, Mordechai, and Haman.  

This session is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 1-Empire, Exile, and Survival: The Jewish Odyssey in Greece
videos.description:
Recorded March 11, 2025
Instructor: Avi Ben-Hur
For over two millennia, Jewish communities in Greece have navigated shifting empires, cultural transformations, and moments of both triumph and tragedy. In this four-part journey, historian Avi Ben-Hur unpacks the layered history of Greek Jewry, from its ancient roots to its near-destruction during the Holocaust.
1-Empire & Encounter - When Alexander Met the Priests: Jewish lore tells us that when Alexander the Great approached Jerusalem, he was met by the High Priest on Mt. Scopus and acknowledged the primacy of the One God. This moment marked the beginning of a long and complex relationship between Judaism and Hellenistic civilization. As Jews migrated to Greece during the Hellenistic period (4th–1st centuries BCE), they established the oldest Jewish Diaspora community in Europe. This session explores the forces behind that migration and the intricate “love-hate” relationship that developed between Jewish tradition and Greek culture.
2-Exile & Transformation – Under the Cross and the Crescent: With the Christianization of the Roman Empire in the 4th century CE, Greek Jews found themselves increasingly marginalized under Byzantine rule. Yet, despite these challenges, they developed distinct liturgies, traditions, and customs that set them apart from both Ashkenazi and Sephardi communities. In the late Middle Ages, Greece gradually came under Ottoman control, and with it came a new era for Greek Jewry. The Ottoman Empire welcomed Sephardic Jews expelled from Spain and Portugal, dramatically shifting the demographics of Greek Jewish communities. Nowhere was this transformation more profound than in Salonika, where the Spanish-speaking Sephardim reshaped the cultural and religious landscape, turning the city into the famed “Jerusalem of the Balkans.” This session examines the impact of these sweeping changes and the complex interplay between indigenous Romaniote Jews and newly arrived Sephardim.
3-Survival & Identity – Navigating Modernity and Nationhood: The 19th century brought seismic political shifts to Greece, forcing its Jewish communities to choose between competing identities: Ottoman or Greek, modernist or traditionalist. For the first time in their millennial history, Greek Jews faced widespread antisemitic violence. When Salonika—the third largest city in the Ottoman Empire—was absorbed into the Greek state on the eve of World War I, its Jewish population found itself transformed overnight from Ottoman subjects into Greek citizens. This session explores the choices and challenges that defined Greek Jewry during this period, from their responses to nationalism to their struggles for social and economic stability in a rapidly changing world.
4-Catastrophe & Resilience – The Shoah and Its Aftermath: During World War II, Greece suffered under a brutal tripartite occupation—Nazi, Italian, and Bulgarian. Jewish citizens fought valiantly in Greece’s resistance efforts, but their fates diverged depending on which foreign power controlled their region. Italian-occupied Jews initially found protection, while Bulgarian forces deported Macedonian Jews to Nazi extermination camps. The Nazis, in turn, systematically targeted Greek Jews for destruction, sending the vast majority of Salonika’s Jewish population to Auschwitz, where they were murdered. Yet, in several towns, local Christian authorities intervened to save their Jewish neighbors. This session follows the trajectory of Greek Jewry from the eve of the Holocaust to the present day, examining how and why their fate unfolded as it did—and how the remnants of these once-thriving communities continue to persevere.
This series offers invaluable insights into the unique Jewish heritage of Greece and serves as excellent preparation for those planning to join our upcoming "Islands of Memory: An Aegean Jewish Odyssey to Greece" tour in Summer 2025. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 2-Unmasking Esther: Power, Providence, and Palace Intrigue
videos.description:
Recorded March 10, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
The Book of Esther is more than a tale of palace intrigue and dramatic reversals—it is a story of identity, resilience, and hidden meaning. As we prepare for Purim, this class takes a fresh look at the Megillah, exploring key moments from different chapters through the lens of the original text, classical commentaries, and modern insights. Join Rabbi Michele Faudem, a beloved Melton+ teacher, for an engaging and thought-provoking journey into the characters of Esther, Mordechai, Ahasuerus, and Haman. Through an in-depth study of their motivations, struggles, and transformations, we uncover timeless lessons on leadership, courage, and the complexities of fate. Whether you’re revisiting the Megillah or encountering it in a new way, this class will enrich your understanding of Purim and provide new insights to bring to your holiday celebrations.
This series is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 1 - Strength in the South
videos.description:
Recorded March 10, 2025
Instructor: Amit Caspi
When history unfolds in tragedy and resilience, the most powerful voices are those who lived it. This compelling three-part series brings you first-hand accounts from individuals whose lives were forever changed by the events of October 7 and its aftermath. Through personal testimony, we will explore themes of heroism, loss, survival, and the power of rebuilding in the face of destruction. Each session highlights a different perspective—from the battlefield, from a kibbutz on the front lines, and from an effort to restore hope through the magic of human connection.

Session 1: Strength in the South: Rebuilding Kerem Shalom - Amit Caspi, a 25-year resident of Kerem Shalom, tells the story of this extraordinary kibbutz—one where religious and secular Israelis forged a shared life together. He recounts the heroism of his community on October 7 and their ongoing efforts to rebuild in the wake of destruction.

Session 2: Service and Sacrifice: The Heroic Life and Death of Maoz Morell - Parents Eitan and Varda Morell share the story of their son, Maoz, an IDF soldier who fell in Gaza while defending Israel. Through the lens of three generations of Jewish survival, they recount his life of heroism, his sacrifice, and the legacy he leaves behind.

Session 3: Making Smiles Appear: Magic4Israel - American entrepreneur, entertainer, and educator Avi Frier turned despair into action, launching Magic4Israel in early 2024. Through this initiative, he and his team have been “making smiles appear” for soldiers and displaced families across Israel, proving that sometimes, even the smallest gestures can restore hope.
This series is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: "Got Chutzpah?" Preview
videos.description: A short preview of Rabbi Ed Feinstein's Melton+ class, "Got Chutzpah? A Brief History of Boldness and Strength."

videos.title: A Melton Appreciation Event - Torah, Technology, and Trust: Jewish Ethics in the AI Age
videos.description:
Recorded March 6, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Daniel Nevins
As artificial intelligence becomes more powerful, it is no longer just a tool—it’s making decisions, executing tasks, and even shaping human experiences. But when machines act in our place, who bears the moral responsibility? Should we delegate ethically charged decisions to AI, and if not, how do we ensure that human values remain at the core of technological advancement?
 
Join Rabbi Daniel Nevins, author of Torah and Technology: Circuits, Cells, and the Sacred Path, for this exclusive event exploring AI through the lens of Jewish ethics. Drawing on centuries of Jewish legal thought, we will examine how tradition grapples with human agency, accountability, and the sanctity of decision-making. Can Jewish wisdom help us navigate a world where AI influences medicine, warfare, finance, and even personal relationships? Together, we’ll confront the pressing question: How do we benefit from AI without surrendering our humanity?

This class is just one example of many that we are able to offer thanks to your generous support. We are pleased to delve into this critical, contemporary issue and to share our gratitude with you.

videos.title: Threads of Identity: Priestly Robes and Purim Masks
videos.description:
Recorded March 6, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
What do the sacred garments of the High Priest and the festive costumes of Purim have in common? In this thought-provoking class, Rav Johnny Solomon explores the deep connections between the weekly Torah portion of Parshat Tetzaveh, which details the intricate priestly vestments, and Purim’s Megillat Esther, a story shaped by masks, hidden identities, and divine concealment. 
Through a close reading of both texts, we will examine how clothing functions as more than just fabric and becomes a symbol of identity, power, and transformation. Join us as we peel back the layers of meaning behind these garments, revealing how they shape personal and communal identity—and what they teach us about the masks we wear in our own lives. This class is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family and dedicated to the memory of Joy Helena Solomon, Rav Solomon's mother.

videos.title: Trailer: Got Chutzpah?
videos.description: A short excerpt of part 1 of Rabbi Ed Feinstein's class, "Got Chutzpah? A Brief History of Boldness and Strength"

videos.title: Week 1-Confronting Evil: Lessons from Biblical and Modern Times
videos.description:
Recorded March 5, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
The nation of Amalek is infamous for its unprovoked and cowardly attack on the vulnerable Israelites during their journey through the wilderness, making it a lasting symbol of moral evil. Paradoxically, in Deuteronomy 25, the Bible commands us to both remember what Amalek did and erase their memory. In this two-part class, Dr. Sandra Lilienthal explores the story of Amalek from the Book of Exodus, the commandment to remember and eradicate their legacy, and the enduring challenge of confronting acts of cruelty and injustice in our own time.
This series is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 1 - Got Chutzpah? A Brief History of Boldness and Strenth
videos.description:
Recorded March 5, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Ed Feinstein
“Chutzpah” is Yiddish for insolence, audacity, or nerve. But the word is older than Yiddish. In the Talmud, “chutzpah” means irrepressible boldness and strength. videos.title: Week 3-The Prince and the Prophet- Machiavelli, Moses, and  the Journey to Freedom
videos.description:
Recorded March 4, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Rivka Amado
Machiavelli, often seen as an amoral political thinker, found unexpected inspiration in the biblical story of Exodus. His vision of republican self-governance and the transformation of passive subjects into active citizens echoes Moses’ leadership and the Israelites’ journey from bondage to freedom. Join Dr. Rivka Amado—an expert in applied ethics and an advocate for cultural preservation—for a fascinating exploration of how these two worlds intersect, revealing timeless lessons on leadership, autonomy, and collective responsibility.

Session 1: Discover Machiavelli’s biography and the historical context shaping his revolutionary ideas.
Session 2: Explore Machiavelli’s concepts of “Virtù” and spiritual transformation through key excerpts from The Prince and Discourses.
Session 3: Examine Moses’ leadership challenges and how they parallel Machiavelli’s vision of creating a republican political community.
This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
