videos.title: Seeing Through Jewish Eyes: Responses to Christian Art
videos.description:
Recorded March 3, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Katherine Aron-Beller
Medieval and early modern European Jews lived in communities surrounded by Christian imagery—crucifixes, sacred icons, and religious processions were ever-present parts of daily life. How did Jews perceive and respond to these symbols of Christian sanctity? What role did rabbinic teachings play in shaping their reactions and guiding their perceptions? Dr. Katherine Aron-Beller, a historian specializing in Jewish-Christian relations and the Italian Inquisition, leads this exploration of diverse Jewish texts and traditions. Drawing on her extensive research, including her recently published book Christian Images and their Jewish Desecrators: The History of an Allegation 400-1700: The History of an Allegation on Christian imagery and Jewish responses, the course examines the ways Jews navigated a world shaped by Christian visual culture. Engage with her unique insights to uncover the interplay of art, religion, and identity during this fascinating period. This session is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 3-Touchpoints of Faith: Finding Judaism in Everyday Life
videos.description:
Recorded February 20, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Amy S. Wallk
How can Jewish rituals add meaning and connection to our lives? Join Rabbi Amy Wallk for this engaging three-part series exploring the big ideas behind the everyday ritual traditions of Jewish life. From the significance of a mezuzah to the nuances of keeping kosher, and the beauty of celebrating Shabbat at home, this class will focus on the “why” rather than the “how to. Together, we will explore the values and principles that underlie these practices, offering a welcoming space for learners of all backgrounds. Come with curiosity and leave with inspiration to embrace the rituals that resonate with you, regardless of your level of observance. 

Session 1: Discover the meaning behind the mezuzah and explore where and why to hang it in your home. 
Session 2: Learn about the various approaches to keeping kosher and how dietary laws can enrich daily life. 
Session 3: Dive into Shabbat rituals and traditions, creating a meaningful celebration in your home.

This series is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: "You Are Beautiful": A Modern Love Song of Songs
videos.description:
Recorded February 19, 2025
Instructor: Elana Goldberg
Discover the timeless connection between ancient poetry and contemporary music through Hinach Yafah - "You Are Beautiful," a Hebrew love song by The Idan Raichel Project. Together, we will examine the song's lyrics alongside Hebrew expressions of love inspired by Shir HaShirim (Song of Songs). Through engaging discussions, we’ll explore the song's poetic depth, analyze its roots in biblical imagery, and reflect on how these themes resonate in today’s world of music and relationships. This session is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family and dedicated in loving memory of Genevieve Freeman.

videos.title: Mystery and History: Washington's Letter to the Jews
videos.description:
Recorded February 19, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Charles E. Savenor
When George Washington penned his now-famous letter to the Jewish community of Newport, Rhode Island, he offered more than words of goodwill—he laid the foundation for religious liberty in the United States. But what was the true significance of this letter? Why did Washington write it, and how did it shape the relationship between America's leadership and its Jewish citizens? Join Rabbi Charles Savenor, Executive Director of Civic Spirit, an organization committed to strengthening civic education across diverse communities. With his expertise in history, education, and Jewish leadership, Rabbi Savenor will guide us through the historical context, the powerful language, and the enduring legacy of Washington’s words. Through close reading, historical analysis, and spirited discussion, this class will uncover the mystery behind the document and the history it continues to influence today. This session is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 1-The Prince and the Prophet: Machiavelli, Moses, and the Journey to Freedom
videos.description:
Recorded February 18, 2025

videos.title: Sacred Stories: The Art of Jewish Allegory
videos.description:
Recorded February 13, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Neil Fleischmann
Mashal, or Jewish allegory, has been a cornerstone of Jewish literary tradition, conveying profound values through the art of storytelling. In this course, we’ll delve into classic examples of Mashal, uncovering the rich meanings layered within these timeless narratives. Guided by Rabbi Neil Fleischmann, a celebrated educator and humorist crowned as “The Funniest Rabbi in New York,” this class will illuminate how Mashal is a powerful vehicle for transmitting Jewish wisdom across generations. Together, we’ll explore the enduring insights Mashal offers into life, ethics, and spirituality. Join us to discover the beauty and significance of Mashal in shaping Jewish thought and identity. This class is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: The Mystical and Ethical Roots of Tu B'Shevat
videos.description:
Recorded February 12, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Grossblatt Pessah
Tu B’Shevat, often called the “birthday of the trees,” has evolved into a profound celebration of nature and spirituality. Known by some as Jewish Earth Day, this holiday invites us to reflect on our role as caretakers of God’s garden and our deep connection to the natural world. In this class, Rabbi Amy Grossblatt Pessah guides us through the mystical and ethical layers of Tu B’Shevat. Together, we’ll explore powerful texts such as the metaphor of man as a tree and the kabbalistic teaching that eating fruit can expiate the first sin: eating the fruit of the Tree of Knowledge. Through engaging discussion, we will uncover how this ancient festival continues to inspire mindfulness, spiritual growth, and environmental responsibility. Join us in rediscovering Tu B’Shevat as a celebration of creation, transformation, and our sacred relationship with the earth.

This class is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: The Ramban's Guide to Spiritual Growth
videos.description:
Recorded: February 4, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
Delve into the timeless wisdom of the Iggeret HaRamban, a cherished work of Jewish ethical literature penned by Rabbi Moshe ben Nachman (1194–1270), also known as the Ramban. Written as a letter to his son in the first half of the Thirteenth Century, the short epistle offers profound guidance on living a life of humility, self-restraint, and spiritual awareness. Many Jews study it weekly, drawing timeless inspiration and incorporating its lessons into their lives.

The session is taught by Rabbi Michele Faudem, a longtime Melton instructor known for inspiring learners to engage deeply with Jewish texts and traditions. She brings her unique blend of warmth, wisdom, and insight to this exploration of one of Judaism’s most treasured texts. This session is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 4-The American Presidents and Israel
videos.description:
Recorded February 3, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski
American presidents are often labeled as pro- or anti-Israel, but what does that really mean? How are these judgments formed, and what influences the policies and decisions of the U.S. presidency regarding Israel? Over four compelling sessions, Rabbi Uri Pilichowski, a Zionism educator and expert on Israel and U.S.-Israel relations, will guide you through the complex, evolving relationship between American presidents and the State of Israel. Session One: Foundations of a Relationship (Lincoln to Truman) Explore the early years of American engagement with the land of Israel, tracing the ideological and political roots that would shape future policy. From Lincoln’s vision of a homeland for Jews to Truman’s pivotal role in recognizing the State of Israel, this session examines the seeds of the U.S.-Israel relationship.
Session Two: From Cold War to Camp David (Eisenhower to Reagan) Delve into the era of post-World War II geopolitics and the Cold War, examining how presidents from Eisenhower to Reagan navigated U.S. interests in the Middle East. Topics include Kennedy's arms sales, Johnson's lack of support during the Six-Day War, Nixon’s support in the Yom Kippur War, Carter's historic Camp David Accords, and Reagan's criticism of Israel's attack on Iraq.
Session Three: Strategic Partnerships and Challenges (Bush 41 to Obama) Analyze the growing strategic alliance between the U.S. and Israel, focusing on critical moments such as the Gulf War, the Oslo Accords, and the Iran deal. This session explores how domestic and international factors influenced the policies of Presidents Bush (41), Clinton, Bush (43), and Obama.
Session Four: Recent Policies and Defining a Pro-Israel Presidency (Trump and Biden)
Examine the most recent administrations and their impact on U.S.-Israel relations, from Trump’s recognition of Jerusalem as Israel’s capital to Biden’s support and pressure during the 2023-2024 war. This session concludes with a discussion of the factors that define a "pro-Israel" president and the evolving criteria for this designation. Join us to uncover the history, politics, and personal convictions that have defined this enduring alliance. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: 21st Century American Jewish Trends: What's Changing, Why It Matters, and What's Next
videos.description:
Recorded January 29, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Steven Windmueller
American Judaism is in the midst of a profound transformation. From the impact of social media and shifting generational behaviors to the rise of unaffiliated Jews ("Religious Nones"), current events are reshaping the communal and spiritual landscape. Join Times of Israel columnist Dr. Steven Windmueller as he sheds light on trends like the growth of the “national synagogue” and the emergence of “privatized Judaism,” while drawing on cutting-edge research to unpack the forces driving these changes, explore their implications, and consider what the future may hold for American Jewish life. This program is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 4-Israel Current Events - The Daily Alert
videos.description:
Recorded January 28, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Adam Schaffer
The news out of Israel changes nearly every minute. Now more than ever, it is crucial to stay informed, but finding reliable news is difficult, and finding unbiased news is even harder...until now. Enter Melton+ and the Daily Alert. The Daily Alert (https://dailyalert.org/) is a curated news site founded by the Conference of Presidents of Major American Jewish Organizations and crafted by expert analysts at the Jerusalem Center for Security and Foreign Affairs. It collects news stories from publications across the ideological and editorial spectrum so that readers can see the same stories from multiple points of view. Rabbi Adam Schaffer, our instructor, will co-create the class with our students by leading them through a guided exploration of stories from that week’s news. We will look not just at the content of the stories but also at the discrepancies and hidden biases that appear in different publications’ coverage of the same events. In doing so, we aim to give students the ability to better understand the news and to appreciate the many different points of view in the coverage. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

Week 2-Sephardic Jewry: Between Two Worlds
videos.description:
Recorded January 22, 2025
Instructor: Avi Ben-Hur
Step into the rich and complex world of Sephardic Jewry on the Iberian Peninsula (what is now modern Spain and Portugal) at a time when Jewish life flourished amid two dominant civilizations. From groundbreaking poetry and philosophy to contributions that shaped Spanish society, Sephardic Jews navigated the dynamic intersection of Muslim and Christian cultures with resilience and brilliance. How did they preserve their unique identity while navigating these two powerful cultures? What conditions enabled their political and cultural golden age, and what forces brought it to its abrupt and tragic end? Join Melton Travel Guide and Educator Avi Ben-Hur for this engaging two-session journey to uncover the incredible legacy of Sephardic Jewry. Together, we’ll explore how their achievements and experiences continue to inspire and influence Jewish traditions and identity today. This program is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 3-Israel Current Events - the Daily Alert
videos.description:
Recorded January 21, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Adam Schaffer
The news out of Israel changes nearly every minute. Now more than ever, it is crucial to stay informed, but finding reliable news is difficult, and finding unbiased news is even harder...until now. Enter Melton+ and the Daily Alert. The Daily Alert (https://dailyalert.org/) is a curated news site founded by the Conference of Presidents of Major American Jewish Organizations and crafted by expert analysts at the Jerusalem Center for Security and Foreign Affairs. It collects news stories from publications across the ideological and editorial spectrum so that readers can see the same stories from multiple points of view. Rabbi Adam Schaffer, our instructor, will co-create the class with our students by leading them through a guided exploration of stories from that week’s news. We will look not just at the content of the stories but also at the discrepancies and hidden biases that appear in different publications’ coverage of the same events. In doing so, we aim to give students the ability to better understand the news and to appreciate the many different points of view in the coverage. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 2-The American Presidents and Israel
videos.description:
Recorded January 20, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski
American presidents are often labeled as pro- or anti-Israel, but what does that really mean? How are these judgments formed, and what influences the policies and decisions of the U.S. presidency regarding Israel? Over four compelling sessions, Rabbi Uri Pilichowski, a Zionism educator and expert on Israel and U.S.-Israel relations, will guide you through the complex, evolving relationship between American presidents and the State of Israel. Session One: Foundations of a Relationship (Lincoln to Truman) Explore the early years of American engagement with the land of Israel, tracing the ideological and political roots that would shape future policy. From Lincoln’s vision of a homeland for Jews to Truman’s pivotal role in recognizing the State of Israel, this session examines the seeds of the U.S.-Israel relationship.
Session Two: From Cold War to Camp David (Eisenhower to Reagan) Delve into the era of post-World War II geopolitics and the Cold War, examining how presidents from Eisenhower to Reagan navigated U.S. interests in the Middle East. Topics include Kennedy's arms sales, Johnson's lack of support during the Six-Day War, Nixon’s support in the Yom Kippur War, Carter's historic Camp David Accords, and Reagan's criticism of Israel's attack on Iraq.
Session Three: Strategic Partnerships and Challenges (Bush 41 to Obama) Analyze the growing strategic alliance between the U.S. and Israel, focusing on critical moments such as the Gulf War, the Oslo Accords, and the Iran deal. This session explores how domestic and international factors influenced the policies of Presidents Bush (41), Clinton, Bush (43), and Obama.
Session Four: Recent Policies and Defining a Pro-Israel Presidency (Trump and Biden)
Examine the most recent administrations and their impact on U.S.-Israel relations, from Trump’s recognition of Jerusalem as Israel’s capital to Biden’s support and pressure during the 2023-2024 war. This session concludes with a discussion of the factors that define a "pro-Israel" president and the evolving criteria for this designation. Join us to uncover the history, politics, and personal convictions that have defined this enduring alliance. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: "I Will Be What I Will Be"...
videos.description:
Recorded January 16, 2025
Instructor: Abby Eisenberg
Dive into the profound and mysterious phrase spoken by God in response to Moses' request for God's name: E'heyeh asher e'heyeh, often translated as "I will be what I will be" or "I am what I am." In this class, NYC community Jewish educator Abby Eisenberg will unpack the layers of meaning in this divine epithet using traditional and modern commentaries, reflecting on how it shapes our understanding of God, divinity, and identity. Together, we'll explore interpretations that speak to us today as we journey through the rich text of Exodus and its timeless wisdom. This session is sponsored by The Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: The Women Who Shaped a Leader: Unsung Heroines of Exodus
videos.description:
Recorded January 15, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
Explore the remarkable stories of the women in the book of Exodus who laid the foundation for the leadership of Moses and the future of the Israelite nation. From the courage of midwives who defied Pharaoh's decree to the love and ingenuity of Yocheved and Miriam and Batya's transformative adoption of Moses, these women exemplify bravery, compassion, and faith. Through the study of the biblical text and midrashic interpretations, Melton curriculum author Dr. Sandra Lilienthal will reveal how the women of Exodus set the stage for the redemption of the Israelites and explore timeless lessons about leadership and resilience. 

This program is sponsored by The Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Melton Stands with Los Angeles: California Wildfires Solidarity Program
videos.description:
Recorded January 14, 2025
Led by Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz, International Director
As a Worldwide Melton family, our hearts and thoughts are with our Los Angeles community who are affected by the ongoing wildfires across Los Angeles. To express our support for the victims of the wildfires and show our solidarity, we came together in this program from 11:00-11:30 a.m. PT (2:00-2:30 p.m. ET) Tuesday, January 14, 2025.

videos.title: What Really Happened on College Campuses?
videos.description:
Recorded January 6, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Joy Getnick, PHD
The events of October 7th shocked the world—and ignited a firestorm of activism, tension, and transformation on American college campuses. For Jewish students, it was a turning point, exposing deep-seated challenges and sparking urgent questions about identity, safety, and solidarity. Dr. Joy Getnick, Executive Director of Hillel at the University of Rochester, brings a firsthand perspective to this timely discussion. Having recently been targeted alongside her colleagues by antisemitic messaging on her campus, Dr. Getnick will unpack the stories behind the headlines, share hard-won insights, and explore the path forward for Jewish students and their allies. This program is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 2-Light the Menorah, Light the Darkness, Light the World: Correlating Hanukkah and the Winter Solstice
videos.description:
Recorded December 24, 2024
Instructor: Joe Septimus
In this course, taught by Joe Septimus, we will delve into two thought-provoking Talmudic passages: one that explores the lighting of Hanukkah candles, and another that reflects on the winter solstice. By weaving these texts together, we will shed light (pun intended) on how the symbolism of the candle-lighting ritual can deepen our understanding of time, change, and the seasons. Through this exploration, we’ll uncover how Jewish tradition and other cultures interpret these universal themes, fostering a richer connection between ritual practice and the cycles of nature. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Beyond the Miracle: Seeing the Festival in a New Light
videos.description:
Recorded December 23, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Amy S. Wallk
Join Rabbi Amy S Wallk for a captivating exploration of Hanukkah's origins. Delve beyond the familiar tale of the miraculous cruse of oil to discover the historical context, cultural significance, and spiritual depth that make this holiday a cherished tradition. We'll uncover the hidden stories and profound insights that have shaped Hanukkah for centuries, learning about the ancient customs, the modern interpretations, and the enduring lessons that continue to inspire us today. This enlightening lecture offers a fresh perspective on Hanukkah: learn new ways (or rather, old, forgotten ways) to relate to a familiar holiday. This class is generously sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Does the Torah Answer Every Question?
videos.description:
Recorded December 19, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Joshua Hoffman
The story of Joseph is more than simple inspiration for a Broadway musical. The failures and fortunes of Joseph's story suggest a world in which fate is a tool for divine connection. Do we also live in a world where our choices are part of a cosmic scheme? Join Rabbi Joshua Hoffman to discover how holy questions are essential to understanding the Torah's wisdom in our own--and every-- generation. This class is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: The Time Traveling Teacher Becomes the Student
videos.description:
Recorded December 18, 2024
Instructor: Catriella Freeman
Join us, as together with Melton curriculum writer, Catriella Freedman, we unpack one of the most unusual Talmudic stories, a rabbinic case of traveling back to the future. In this legend, when the great leader Moses time travels to the 1st century in Rabbi Akiva’s classroom, he embarrassingly finds himself placed in the last row of the classroom – the seats reserved for the least experienced students. This imaginative tale gives us a powerful opportunity to examine how the ancient rabbis understood the development of Jewish law, the oral tradition, and their political and historical role in its transmission. We look forward to traveling back in time with you! This class is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 1-Light the Menorah, Light the Darkness, Light the World: Correlating Hanukkah and the Winter Solstice
videos.description:
Recorded December 17, 2024
Instructor: Joe Septimus
In this course, taught by Joe Septimus, we delve into two thought-provoking Talmudic passages: one that explores the lighting of Hanukkah candles, and another that reflects on the winter solstice. By weaving these texts together, we will shed light (pun intended) on how the symbolism of the candle-lighting ritual can deepen our understanding of time, change, and the seasons. Through this exploration, we’ll uncover how Jewish tradition and other cultures interpret these universal themes, fostering a richer connection between ritual practice and the cycles of nature. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Beyond Dreidels and Latkes- An Indian Hanukkah Story
videos.description:
Recorded December 16, 2024
Instructor: Rahel Musleah
From Maine to California, Jews in America celebrate Hanukkah with dreidels, latkes, sufganiot, and rainbow-colored candles. How do Jews in India celebrate the Festival of Lights? In this interactive, visual, and eye-opening program, explore the history and culture of India's Jewish communities and their special connection to Hanukkah. In this age of antisemitism, learn how India has always been a safe haven for Jews from around the world. This program is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: From Jacob to Israel: Wrestling with Destiny
videos.description:
Recorded December 12, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
Join Rabbi Michele Faudem to explore Jacob’s transformative journey from a self-centered individual to a patriarch of the Israelite nation. This look at the weekly Parsha will examine the symbolic significance of his wrestling match with the angel and the complex dynamics of the Dinah narrative, examining how these experiences impacted Jacob's spiritual growth from family man to father of the Children of Israel. This session is generously sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: The Genesis Women
videos.description:
Recorded December 9, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. David Harbater
Genesis offers rich and often conflicting portrayals of womanhood from the first woman in Eden to the matriarchs Sarah, Rachel, and Leah. In this class, Melton curriculum author Rabbi David Dr. Harbater will explore three paradigms—partner, enabler, and mother—rooted in the tasks assigned to the first woman. We’ll examine how these roles unfold in the lives of the matriarchs, shedding light on the tensions and complexities of their stories and the enduring lessons they hold. This session is generously sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: The 'How' and 'Why' of Jewish Survivial- A Midrashic Approach
videos.description:
Recorded December 10, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Fred Morgan
The events of October 7 raised two challenging questions: How do we keep the Jewish people alive in a time of duress? And what is our aim or purpose in doing so? These are not new questions; they were asked in Greco-Roman times by one of the earliest collections of Rabbinic Midrash, the Mekhilta deRabbi Ishmael, which offers us an intriguing answer. This class will use this Midrash to explore these questions in our own time.  Our diverse sources will include an ethical will composed by the Chatam Sofer (founder of ultra-Orthodoxy in the 19th century), as well as a seasonal reflection on the festival of Chanukah! The session will give us a special opportunity to explore our own attitudes towards the strategies and purposes of Jewish survival. This session is generously sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Portion of "A History of Hatred: The Ever-Present Threat of Antisemitism"
Donor Appreciation-A History of Hatred: The Ever-Present Threat of Antisemitism
videos.description:
Recorded December 3, 2024
Instructor: Ronnie Gotkin, Co-Author of A History of Hatred: The Ever-Present Threat of Antisemitism, Melton's new 6-session course. Communities throughout the United States and Canada have already begun scheduling the course starting in January 2025. In this session, Ronnie Gotkin provides a brief preview of the course. Ronnie is introduced by Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz, International Director.

videos.title: Week 4-Untangling the Toughest of Talmudic Tales
videos.description:
Recorded December 3, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
The Talmud records numerous stories. Some of these stories seem to provide insights that are easily applicable to our lives today, while others require more "creativity" to understand and find relevant. The more challenging stories often describe unusual events which seem impossible to imagine having actually occurred or contain cryptic teachings which make little sense if taken literally. In this four-part course with expert Melton teacher Rabbi Johnny Solomon, we will study four of these types of Talmudic stories and then consider the different approaches which have been suggested to decode and find the underlying meaning in these fascinating tales and teachings. Through studying these stories and learning how to understand them, we will gain new skills and perspectives critical to learning Talmud. This course is generously sponsored by The Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 3-Untangling the Toughest of Talmudic Tales
videos.description:
Recorded November 26, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon

videos.title: Thanksgiving: Cultivating the Virtue of Gratitude
videos.description:
Recorded November 25, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Shmuly Yanklowitz
Where does the concept of Hakarat HaTov ("recognition of the good" or "gratitude") originate in the Torah, and how do the Sages think about it? Why is Hakarat HaTov important, and how might we cultivate it? How have American Jews embraced Thanksgiving, and how might we do so? In this class with Rabbi Shmuly, we'll pause before Thanksgiving to consider various Jewish texts that help us embrace the week intellectually and spiritually. This session is generously sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Torah: The Book of a People and the People of the Book
videos.description:
Recorded November 21, 2024
Instructor: Lynne Lieberman
The Bible, the best-selling book of all time, has sustained the Jewish people throughout the eons. The Bible–and in particular, its first five books, the Torah–is the central source text of the Jewish people. Why? Join master educator Lynne Lieberman on a global day of Jewish learning in commemoration of the fourth Hebrew anniversary Yahrzeit of Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks z”l, to explore together how more than the people sustain the Torah, the Torah sustains the Jewish people. This class is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 4-Sacred Stones:The Journey of Jewish Nationhood through Israel's Ancient Sites
videos.description:
Recorded November 20, 2024
Instructor: Daniella Gefen
This captivating series delves into the formation of Jewish national and religious identity by exploring the creation of holy sites and the intricate relationship between faith, people, ritual, memory, and salvation. Through the lens of groundbreaking archaeological discoveries in Israel, spanning from the era of King David to the 6th century A.C., participants journey through ancient history, uncovering the roots and evolution of a people and their enduring legacy. This class is generously sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 2-Untangling the Toughest of Talmudic Tales
videos.description:
Recorded November 19, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
The Talmud records numerous stories. Some of these stories seem to provide insights that are easily applicable to our lives today, while others require more "creativity" to understand and find relevant. The more challenging stories often describe unusual events which seem impossible to imagine having actually occurred or contain cryptic teachings which make little sense if taken literally. videos.title: A Sacrifice of Doubt- The Binding of Isaac and Abraham's Crisis of Faith
videos.description:
Recorded November 14, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz
This session revisits the well-known and controversial biblical story of the near-sacrifice of Isaac by his father, Abraham. Considered a moment of monumental faith by some and a shocking travesty by others, the episode related in the week’s Torah portion of Vayera beggars understanding. Melton’s International Director, Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz, will closely read the text and offer a new twist on the story that challenges traditional interpretations. By examining the subtle nuances of the text, we will uncover the underlying doubts and fears that Abraham grapples with as we consider a unique, alternative interpretation that hints at the challenges of faith in a world filled with uncertainty. This session is generously sponsored by The Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Week 3-Sacred Stones: The Journey of Jewish Nationhood through Israel's Ancient Sites
videos.description:
Recorded November 13, 2024
Instructor: Daniella Gefen

videos.title: Holy Harvesting Hebrews: The Surprising and Significant History of Jewish Farming in America
videos.description:
Recorded November 11, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Joellyn Zollman
Did your grandfather farm tomatoes? Did your grandmother keep chickens? In between the harvest holidays of Sukkot and Thanksgiving, let's delve into the often-overlooked connection between American Jews and agriculture. Join Dr. Joellyn Zollman for a fascinating exploration of the surprising, significant history of Jewish farming in America. This session is generously sponsored by The Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Disagreement with Dignity: Jewish Teachings on Respectful Dialogue
videos.description:
Recorded November 7, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
In this text-based session, Rabbi Michele Faudem delves into the rich tradition of Jewish thought to explore timeless lessons on civil and political disagreement. By examining stories from the Torah and Talmud, we will uncover strategies for navigating differences of opinion within the Jewish community and beyond. This session aims to provide practical insights that can be applied in today's increasingly polarized political landscape, helping to foster a culture of respect and understanding even in the face of significant disagreements. Generously sponsored by The Goldsmith Family

videos.title: Week 2-Sacred Stones: The Journey of Jewish Nationhood through Israel's Ancient Sites
videos.description:
Recorded November 6, 2024
Instructor: Daniella Gefen

This program is generously sponsored by The Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Should We Rejoice at our Enemy's Demise?
videos.description:
Recorded November 4, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski
Israel’s recent military achievements ignited a debate over how to react to Israel’s destruction of its enemies, a conversation as old as the Bible itself. Rabbis, pundits, and laypeople worldwide argued whether expressions of public joy or something more subdued were appropriate for the moment. The debate over the proper reaction to the Israeli success over Hezbollah has centered around three areas: morality, strategy, and Jewish power. Join expert educator Rabbi Uri Pilichowski to traverse Jewish literature and ideas in this age-old debate. This class is sponsored by The Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment-Voting: An American Jewish Cultural Experience
videos.description:
Recorded October 31, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Steven Windmueller
Melton Meets the Moment presents unique offerings that enable participants to learn more about and discuss together a contemporary and timely topic through a Jewish lens.Trained as a political scientist, Dr. Steven Windmueller will be exploring the significance of the vote for American Jewish audiences. His presentation will examine the specifics of “the Jewish Contract with America” and identify political patterns and social trends in connection with how and why Jews vote.  Steven will also be exploring the specifics of the 2024 election and its particular impact for the Jewish community.

videos.title: Week 1-Sacred Stones: The Journey of Jewish Nationhood through Israel's Ancient Sites
videos.description:
Recorded October 30, 2024
Instructor: Daniella Gefen
This captivating series delves into the formation of Jewish national and religious identity by exploring the creation of holy sites and the intricate relationship between faith, people, ritual, memory, and salvation. Through the lens of groundbreaking archaeological discoveries in Israel, spanning from the era of King David to the 6th century A.C., participants journey through ancient history, uncovering the roots and evolution of a people and their enduring legacy.

videos.title: Week 4-Make It Your Business: What Judaism Has to Say About Business Ethics
videos.description:
Recorded October 29, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Benjamin Kramer
Whether in person or online, we are involved in the buying and selling of goods and services on a day-to-day basis. In this modern world, all these transactions are potentially fraught with ethical dilemmas! Fortunately, Jewish tradition provides us with a moral compass to help us successfully navigate the often-complex world of commerce. In this course with expert Melton educator Rabbi Ben Kramer, we will explore traditional Jewish legal sources – such as the Talmud and the Shulchan Aruch - to understand the fundamental “kosher” business principles and how we can use them to guide our behavior. By exploring such topics as price fraud, ethical competition, and more we will gain new insights into how we conduct ourselves and engage with the world around us.

videos.title: Week 4-From Creation to the Flood: The Dual Foundations of Judaism
videos.description:
Recorded October 21, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. David Harbater
One of the most joyful moments in the Jewish calendar year is the celebration of restarting the Torah, a holiday known as Simchat Torah. In the lead up to Simchat Torah, join us for this special course in which we will examine the foundations of Judaism as they are presented in the opening chapters of the Torah. Led by renowned author of In the Beginnings: Discovering the Two Worldviews Hidden Within Genesis 1-11, Rabbi Dr. David Harbater, we will do a close and careful reading of the text to discover two narratives that reflect two distinct worldviews regarding the nature of God, human beings, our role in the world, the relationship between man and woman, and much more. Join us for this insightful new look at how it all began.

videos.title: A More Perfect Union: Jewish Values and American Democracy
videos.description:
Recorded October 10, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Charles E. Savenor
Judaism and other faiths elevate the transmission of tradition from one generation to another. With political polarization challenging American society, we will learn how civic education prepares the next generation to participate in democracy and its vital role in rebuilding trust in the system and in each other.

videos.title: The Yom Kippur Avodah: Anatomy of a Temple Service
videos.description:
Recorded October 9, 2024
Instructor: Devorah Chasky 
The Seder Avodah, one of the central prayers on Yom Kippur, describes the routine and service performed by the Kohen Gadol (high priest) on Yom Kippur and the preceding days. In preparation for the Day of Judgement, we will explore the symbolism, historical significance, and depth woven into the historical reality of what occurred in the Beit HaMikdash (Holy Temple) each year. Once the Seder Avodah is understood in a step-by-step sequence, it can be experienced in synagogue on Yom Kippur on a whole new level.

videos.title: Week 4-30 Years After the Death of the "Rebbe": An Inside Look at Chabad
videos.description:
Recorded September 26, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon 
Most Jews today are no longer surprised to find a Chabad house in virtually every community around the world, even in the most remote corners of the earth. This massive movement just commemorated the 30th yahrzeit of the Lubavitcher Rebbe, Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson. In this four-part course with esteemed Melton teacher Rabbi Johnny Solomon, we will take a closer look at the ubiquitous Chabad movement: What makes Chabad unique as a Chassidic movement? What are its goals? How did it survive the death of its leader? What can we learn about its strengths? And what might we see as its weaknesses? Join us for this in-depth look at this fascinating movement.

videos.title: Secrets of the Jewish South-A Sneak Peek of Our Upcoming Trip to Savannah and Charleston
videos.description:
Recorded September 25, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Joellyn Zollman 
Master Melton Educator Dr. Joellyn Zollman leads us on virtual preview of  Melton's Travel excursion to Charleston and Savannah coming up in November to two of the South’s most charming destinations. Southern Jewish life has a rich history, and this virtual visit to two of the oldest Jewish communities in the country will provide a sense of what to expect on the trip.

videos.title: The Difficult Moral Choices Everyday Heroes Had to Make on October 7
videos.description:
Recorded September 24, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem 
One of the great ethical dilemmas is the question of whether we save ourselves or put others first in an emergency. We’ve all been on flights in which they tell us specifically to put our own mask on before helping others but is that concept applicable during other types of emergencies? On October 7, Israelis were faced with this exact question. Some ran to help their family and friends, some ran to enlist and fight back, and too many had no other choice but to run for their lives. In this moving new program from expert Melton teacher Rabbi Michele Faudem, we will explore the tough choices that had to be made by looking at the essential moral questions: What is our obligation to save others? What type of danger must we subject ourselves in order to save others?  Does war change our obligation? Using the Talmud, the book of Esther, Maimonides, and more, we will gain new insights into these age-old questions and grapple with some of the hardest choices that Israelis had to make on that horrific day.

videos.title: Week 6-Jewish Answers to Life's Most Challenging Questions
videos.description:
Recorded September 24, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz 
This is your invitation to join the conversation as we grapple with some of life’s toughest questions, like “Why do good people suffer?”, “Is everything that happens pre-ordained?” and “Can’t God make a miracle for me?” In this six-part course, we’ll explore multiple understandings of these timeless questions through the eyes of the rabbinic sages as well as those of modern thinkers — including our own. The course is based on “Where’s My Miracle: Jewish Traditions for Dealing with Tragedy,” by Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz, Melton’s International Director. This course is graciously sponsored by Rochelle Friedman.

videos.title: Live From Israel: The Local Discourse on What Matters to Israelis Today
videos.description:
Recorded September 23, 2024
Instructor: Daniella Gefen 
While Israel seems to be at the center of the global conversation, there are also critical conversations happening within Israeli society today that get far less attention in the international media. In this eye-opening new program from Israeli field educator Daniella Gefen, we’ll take a look at some of the most challenging debates happening in Israel today. We’ll discuss topics like ultra-religious military service, the cost of life in Israel’s war with Hamas, what to do about the growing threat of Iran, how to engage with potential new allies in Saudi Arabia, and more. This class will be designed around what is happening Israel right when the class is taking place, so topics may change. We hope you can join us for an insightful perspective about the discourse happening in Israel right now.

videos.title: Week 3-Spiritually Preparing for the High Holidays in the Wake of October 7th
videos.description:
Recorded September 19, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Wallk 
The Jewish High Holidays are designed to both inspire and humble us. They are times for profound introspection and an opportunity for meaningful personal change. But this year each of us as Jews have more on our minds than our personal growth. In the wake of all that happened on October 7 and all that has followed, how do we spiritually prepare ourselves for the High Holidays in a different way? What are the themes and ideas that can help us to make meaning after this incredibly painful year? In this new course from High Holiday liturgical expert Rabbi Amy Wallk, we will talk about how our liturgy and traditions give us the tools to look towards the future, holding space for the pain of yesterday while dreaming for a better tomorrow.

What are its goals? How did it survive the death of its leader? What can we learn about its strengths? And what might we see as its weaknesses? Join us for this in-depth look at this fascinating movement.

videos.title: Is the "Golden Age" Over for American Jews?
videos.description:
Recorded September 16, 2024
Instructor: Dr. David Kaufman 
In March 2024, Franklin Foer published an article in The Atlantic titled, “The Golden Age of American Jews is Ending,” arguing that since “Anti-Semitism Has Gone Mainstream” we are seeing the “close [of] an unprecedented period of safety and prosperity for Jewish Americans,” and even worse, the “demolish[ing of] the liberal order they helped establish.” Was Foer’s lament an accurate take, a true prophecy of our fate, or was it perhaps more indicative of the longstanding Jewish habit of an "ever-dying" pessimism? Join American Jewish historian Dr. David Kaufman in a careful evaluation of these questions, looking both at the rise and decline of the American Jewish "golden age" and at the larger issue of American Jewish historical "exceptionalism."

videos.title: Week 3-Evolving Tradition: Deepening Our Understanding of Conservative Judaism
videos.description:
Recorded September 11, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Wallk 
Conservative Judaism finds itself at a crossroads. How does the denomination stay true to the traditions and laws of the past while continuing to speak meaningfully to the present moment? In this three-part program with acclaimed Melton teacher Rabbi Amy Wallk, we will explore the history of Conservative Judaism, how the movement began, the key principles and ideals are that serve as its foundation, and where it is going. Through this lens we will engage with a wide variety of topics such as Zionism, driving on Shabbat, the role of women, LGBTQ+ ordination and engagement, and the role of the non-Jew in synagogue life. Join us for an insightful look at how one of modern Judaism’s denominations has and continues to navigate the 21st century. This course is graciously sponsored by Susan and Bill Firestone. It is the final course in a four-part series Melton is proudly offering in 5784 exploring the major denominations in Judaism. You do not need to have taken the others to enjoy this one!

videos.title: The Real Heroines of Sephardic Culture
videos.description:
Recorded September 9, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Rivka Amado 
Sephardic women have played a heroic role in keeping their culture alive. Thanks to their unshakable commitment and determination, Sephardic women were at the center of the recovery process after expulsions and exiles, and managed to not only keep the traditions and customs alive but thriving. In this enlightening new program, Dr. Rivka Amado, a Sephardic historian, will explore the role of two groups of Sephardic women: those who fled Spain and Portugal after the expulsion and the conversó women who remained in the Iberian Peninsula and kept the Jewish faith in secret. Through this exploration of the role of these women in Sephardic culture, we will gain new insights into this important segment of the Jewish community and Judaism as a whole.

videos.title: Unraveling Kafka
videos.description:
Recorded August 26, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Rachel Seelig 
Step into the enigmatic world of Franz Kafka with literary scholar Dr. Rachel Seelig. This new Melton International class offers a journey through Kafka's labyrinthine mind, where writing is “impossible” and understanding remains elusive. We will read short fictional texts alongside letters and diary entries to gain a deeper understanding not only of Kafka’s oeuvre but also of his inner world, in which Jewish identity played a significant role. We will also consider Kafka’s legacy and the legal battle over his literary estate, a “kafkaesque” struggle in its own right that hinged upon questions of national belonging. The blend of humor, terror, and existential uncertainty that defines Kafka's universe offers profound insights into Jewish identity against the surreal backdrop of our times.

videos.title: Lives and Afterlives: Kabbalah and Reincarnation
videos.description:
Recorded August 21, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Hartley Lachter 
What happens after we die is a question that has haunted humanity for centuries. Judaism has its own set of answers. What may be surprising to learn is that some Jews believe we are reincarnated. While some important Jewish thinkers in the Middle Ages, such as Saadia Gaon, rejected the idea, many kabbalists starting in the late 13th century embraced the notion of reincarnation as a basic tenet of Judaism. In this special program with Kabbalah expert Dr. Hartley Lachter, we will explore ways that reincarnation has been described by Jewish thinkers. We will also discuss how kabbalists used this idea as a way of imagining how the Jewish people move through history and how they are rewarded for enduring painful national misfortunes over the course of more than one lifetime. Join us on this mystical journey through the afterlife!

videos.title: Week 1-Jewish Answers to Life's Most Challenging Questions
videos.description:
Recorded August 20, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz 
This is your invitation to join the conversation as we grapple with some of life’s toughest questions, like “Why do good people suffer?”, “Is everything that happens pre-ordained?” and “Can’t God make a miracle for me?” In this six-part course, we’ll explore multiple understandings of these timeless questions through the eyes of the rabbinic sages as well as those of modern thinkers — including our own. The course is based on “Where’s My Miracle: Jewish Traditions for Dealing with Tragedy,” by Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz, Melton’s International Director.

This course is graciously sponsored by Rochelle Friedman.

videos.title: The Ketubah: What's Love Got to Do with It?
videos.description:
Recorded August 19, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Wallk 
The ketubah, or Jewish marriage contract, is one of the most well-known Jewish legal texts being actively used today. But what's love got to do with it? Join esteemed Melton educator Rabbi Amy Wallk on Tu B'Av (think: Jewish Valentine’s Day) as we explore the origins and development of this time-honored Jewish wedding document. Both a symbol and a binding document for the wedding couple, there’s more to the ketubah than meets the eye!

videos.title: Munk Anti-Zionism Debate
videos.description: A History of Hatred

videos.title: Tisha B'av as a Healing Journey Through Grief
videos.description:
Recorded August 12, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Deborah Silver 
Tisha B’Av is the saddest day in the Jewish calendar. It is the time when we remember catastrophe, brokenness, and many losses. But hidden within the day is the very beginning of a journey to wholeness that will lead us out of the depths and on to the spiritual peaks of the High Holydays. In this moving program from expert Melton teacher Rabbi Deborah Silver, we will explore multiple dimensions of Tisha B’Av - brokenness, exile, the spiritual significance of Jerusalem, and how the afternoon of Tisha B’Av marks both the deepest point of our grief and the starting point of our journey towards Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.

videos.title: Week 3-What Do We Do Now: Jewish Responses to Communal Catastrophe
videos.description:
Recorded August 7, 2024
Instructor: Harman Avery Grossman 
In the wake of October 7, many of us are struggling to find ways to cope and move forward. Over thousands of years, the Jewish people have had far too many opportunities to develop responses to communal tragedy. In this insightful new course from expert Melton teacher Harman Grossman, we will examine texts – from the Bible through modernity – reflecting different strategies to deal with collective catastrophe embedded in our tradition. From there, we will consider which, if any, of these traditional responses might help us today. Join us for this meaningful exploration of how we handle the hardest moments the world throws at our community.

videos.title: Week 3-Is the Evil Inclination the Devil or My Teacher?
videos.description:
Recorded August 6, 2024
Instructor: Joe Septimus 
The Jewish concept of the “evil inclination” or Yetzer ha’Rah is one that is discussed throughout Jewish literature. It can seem like a big, scary bad-guy or a tempter whispering in our ear, but it can also be a motivator for us to be productive and grow. In this three-part program with expert Melton educator Joe Septimus, we will learn an array of texts referencing the Yetzer ha’Rah to see the circumstances when it appears and the variety of opinions about its value. Together we will discover the reasons for its being so feared while at the same time being so central in Jewish thought. So come join us as we exorcise Judaism’s inner demons!

videos.description:
Recorded July 25, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Emily Hyatt 
Reform Judaism is the largest of the Jewish denominations in America, but at its core, it is a movement that emphasizes each individual as a critical component of sustainable and meaningful community. In this class, taught by Rabbi Emily Hyatt, we will trace the history of Reform Judaism, understand the dynamics of the movement today, and discover how Reform Judaism creates opportunities to navigate theology, heritage, ritual, and community in today’s modern world.

videos.title: Organ Donation: Mitzvah or Murder
videos.description:
Recorded July 23, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Ari Perl 
Though saving life is a supreme Jewish value, many Jews still pass on the opportunity to donate lifesaving organs. What is Judaism's approach to organ donation, both live donation and after death? What about donating corneas or other tissues? Are there circumstances where Jewish law prohibits donation? Are there differences in approach amongst the major denominations? Is there ever a problem with accepting a donated organ for transplant? Should a Jew register to be an organ donor? In this insightful program from Jewish medical ethics expert Rabbi Ari Perl, we will demystify the often-misunderstood process of organ donation and explore the topic from the perspective of Jewish law and ethics.

videos.title: Week 4-Voices From the Tenements
videos.description:
Recorded July 22, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Shana Rosenblatt Mauer 
Some of our most vivid, engaging descriptions of the hardscrabble life of turn-of-the-20th century Jewish immigrants come from portraits created by writers of fiction. Describing the sights and sounds of their immigrant neighborhoods, along with their poverty, religious conflicts, anti-Semitism, generational divides, and culture shock, these authors created an animated, impassioned body of work that depicts the challenges of facing a foreign culture, a new language, and an unfamiliar way of life. Moreover, they dramatized how these trials specifically impacted Eastern European Jews, who were jolted by the realities of this new country, but also determined to make it their own. In this follow-up to her hit course, “The Master Storyteller: Isaac Bashevis Singer,” Dr. Shana Mauer will focus on the writings of the famed triumvirate of immigrant writers Abraham Cahan, Mary Antin, and Anzia Yezierska, whose work has practically come to define early 20th century American Jewish literature. Join us for this insightful look at the life, times, and words of some of the tenements’ most prolific writers.

videos.title: Melton Development LGL to Events Review - Follow-Up 3
Antisemitism, Media, and Jewish Identity: Two Centuries of Advocacy and Innovation
videos.description:
Recorded July 16, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Phil Keisman 
In 1857, rabbis in communities across Eastern Europe tried something new: they wrote classified ads on behalf of women locked in a traditional marriage to a man who had disappeared. Today, Jewish activists on TikTok, Twitter, and the like use social media to protect more Jews who might otherwise fall through the proverbial cracks of society. Jews in the LGBTQ+ community, Jews of color, and those looking to leave insular, ultra-Orthodox communities all have new-found voices due to the technology available at our fingertips. This class will examine two hundred years of marginalized Jews who used new media technologies to advocate for themselves and, in the process, revolutionized Jewish peoplehood.

This class is graciously sponsored by Bruce Segall and Robin Rosenberg and by Keith Chasin.

Recorded July 11, 2024

videos.title: Calendar Invite- Why is the Jewish Calendar the Way That It Is?
videos.description:
Recorded July 10, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Adam Schaffer 
For Jews around the world following the secular “January through December” calendar, figuring out when the Jewish holidays fall can sometimes be a confusing project. A holiday that falls in one month one year may fall in a different month the next year. This is because the Jewish calendar is a lunisolar calendar, but what does that actually mean? Why is it on a 19-year cycle? How do we make up for a whole leap month some years? Why do we celebrate some holidays for two days when we’re outside of Israel but only one day when we’re in Israel? Join expert Melton educator Rabbi Adam Schaffer for a fun and insightful exploration of the Jewish calendar’s idiosyncrasies. It’s sure to make you “week” in the knees!

videos.title: The Pursuit of Liberty and the Earliest American Synagogues
videos.description:
Recorded July 9, 2024 
Instructor: Lynne Lieberman 
When early Jewish settlers arrived in the Americas, they established new lives which included the building of new Jewish communities. They did so by founding congregations and synagogues, some of which are still standing and bringing Jews together to this day! In this special program with a Melton-favorite teacher Lynne Lieberman, we will explore some of America’s oldest, most famous synagogues, including the first six that existed at the founding of the United States. How were they established? By whom? What were the priorities of their communities at that time? By examining these humble Jewish beginnings in America, we will gain new insights into what has shaped the American Judaism of today.

videos.title: Week 3-One Page at a Time: Study Talmud Like a Pro
videos.description:
Recorded July 1, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Benjamin Kramer 
The Talmud is one of the most important texts in Judaism, but it can also be one of the most intimidating. In this three-part program with renowned Melton teacher Rabbi Ben Kramer, we will engage in an in-depth study of a single page of Talmud. Using an English translation, we will make our way line by line through Tractate Bava Metzia 21, in which the Talmud discusses the obligation to return lost objects. In addition to studying the text of the Talmud itself, we will also study relevant portions of the traditional commentaries of Rashi and Tosafot, which are featured on the page itself. During our study, we will discuss the development of the Talmud and the history of its printing, which will enhance our appreciation of the text. If you have always wanted to experience the joy of deep Talmud study, this is the perfect opportunity!

videos.title: Week 2-Shavuot as Metamorphosis
videos.description:
Recorded June 10, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon 
The holiday of Shavuot celebrates two very different occurrences: it’s both a celebration of the harvest and a celebration of when we received the Ten Commandments. In this two-part series with acclaimed Melton teacher Rabbi Johnny Solomon, we will explore the metamorphosis of Shavuot from being a harvest festival to becoming the festival celebrating the receiving of the Torah. We will also reflect on the personal and spiritual metamorphosis of Ruth, whose remarkable Biblical story is read on Shavuot. Join us for an enlightening exploration of one of Judaism’s most joyful holidays.

This series is sponsored by Randi Brenowitz in honor of the fabulous men and women on the International Melton Board of Directors.

videos.title: Pillars of the Community-The Roles of Medieval Jewish Women
videos.description:
Recorded June 6, 2024
Instructor: Professor Elisheva Baumgarten 
Women’s roles in Judaism have evolved profoundly over time. We see different perspectives today than even a generation ago! So what role did Jewish women take within their communities in the more distant past? In this thought-provoking new class from Hebrew University’s Professor Elisheva Baumgarten we look at evidence from medieval Germany and France to get a sense of what role women played in their communities during that time. Using this evidence, we will gain a deeper understanding of Jewish life in the medieval period.

videos.title: Melton Development LGL to Events Review - Session 6
Life After Death-Posthumous Reproduction and the Legacy of Fallen Israeli Soldiers
videos.description:
Recorded June 5, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. Eddie Reichman 
With the latest advances in reproductive technology, it is now possible to harvest male reproductive seed after death and use it to conceive. Currently, the Israeli Knesset is debating legislation permitting such methods in relation to fallen male soldiers for future reproductive use. This type of assisted reproductive technology and the treatment of infertility are some of the most fascinating and complex areas of Jewish law. In this session, we will provide an overview of the Jewish approach to artificial insemination and highlight its use, particularly as it relates to the ongoing war against Hamas. This complex discussion will uncover the Jewish ethical and legal aspects of this proposed legislation. Join medical ethics expert Rabbi Dr. Edward Reichman as we explore this intimate issue that could shape generations to come.

videos.title: “If Not Zionism, Then Nothing”- Henrietta Szold and Zionism in Her Own Words
videos.description:
Recorded June 3, 2024
Instructor: Francine Klagsbrun 
Even before Theodor Herzl published his treatise on Zionism, Der Judenstaat, Henrietta Szold, the founder of Hadassah, had lectured about the centrality of Zionism in Jewish thought. She believed that Judaism was a way of life, and as such, Jews needed an attachment to their homeland to complete their identity. Using her writings and ideas, this program will investigate Szold’s view of Zionism. How did her approach differ from Herzl's and that of her fellow American, Louis Brandeis? What did Szold mean by, “if not Zionism, then nothing—then extinction for the Jew”? Did founding Hadassah, the Women’s Zionist Organization of America, fulfill her Zionist dream? Join Francine Klagsbrun, author of Henrietta Szold: Hadassah and the Zionist Dream, for this insightful look at one of modern Judaism’s most impactful thought-leaders.

Week 4-All in the Family: Torah's Take on Family Relationships
videos.description:
Recorded May 29, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
Family dynamics can be fraught with complexity and tension. Even the people we love most in the world can frustrate us as much as they bring joy to our lives. This is not unique to the modern era. In this insightful new course from acclaimed Melton teacher and course author Dr. Sandra Lilienthal, we will explore significant family relationships in the Torah, using them as a mirror with which to examine our own relationships. Whether it’s the relationship between a husband and wife, a brother and sister, or the most treacherous “in-laws”, the Torah is packed with complex interactions that can shed new light on our own lives. Join us as we go all in on family.

Week 3-Behind the Music: The Heart of the Jewish Experience
videos.description:
Recorded May 20, 2024 
Instructor: Cantor Matt Austerklein 
Music is an integral part of the Jewish experience. Through prayer or pop music, singing or dancing, music is at the heart of so many meaningful Jewish experiences. Join beloved Melton teacher Cantor Matt Austerklein for a song-filled journey through the many cultures, styles, and eras of Jewish music. Throughout this exploration, we will grapple with deep questions of how music reflects our experience as a people and how it can help us in defining our own Jewish selves.

videos.title: Standing as One Nation- Honoring Yom Ha'Atzmaut and Yom HaZikaron
videos.description:
Recorded May 13, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz
Join the worldwide Melton community for this meaningful and poignant online ceremony marking the observance of Yom Hazikaron (Israel’s Memorial Day) and the transition to Yom HaAzmaut (Israel’s Independence Day). The 30-minute ceremony, live from Israel, will be led by Melton's International Director, Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz. Through study and song, we will honor the brave soldiers who have sacrificed their lives, remember the victims of October 7th, and pay tribute to the miracle of the State of Israel as it marks 76 years since its establishment in 1948. Together we will mourn. Together we will rejoice. Together we will witness the resilience and strength of the State of Israel. Am Israel chai!

videos.title: Week 2-Looking Into It: A Rabbinic Guide to Personal Growth
videos.description:
Recorded May 9, 2024
Instructor: Abby Eisenberg
In the classic rabbinic text, Pirkei Avot, the rabbis of late antiquity offer wisdom to guide us to become our best selves. Pirkei Avot suggest how we can improve upon our interpersonal (and intrapersonal) skills and develop an openness to learning and growth which are at the heart of Jewish living. As we analyze the many diverse rabbinic statements in this ancient text, we will glean intriguing (and humorous) observations about relationships with ourselves, family, and friends. Join us for this two-part series with hit Melton teacher Abby Eisenberg as we explore this essential text that is full of insights into human nature.

Recorded May 2, 2024
Instructor: Sivan Rotholz
In this six-week program from Melton-favorite teacher and gynocentric Torah scholar Sivan Rotholz, we will explore the Jewish calendar through the lens of the women featured in the Jewish holidays. From the women of Passover as a model of civil disobedience to the medieval practice of remembering Jephthah's daughter on the winter solstice, from Chag Habanot (the North African Jewish Festival of Daughters) to Lailah and the Tree of Souls for Tu B’Shvat, we will discover some of the lesser-known female heroines of the Jewish year. Through this discovery, we will gain insights into new role models and see the multi-dimensionality of some of our more famous Biblical women. And when we explore Esther and Vashti and the feminine origins of Hamantaschen, it will give you something unforgettable to share at next year's Purim party!

videos.title: Week 1-Looking Into It: A Rabbinic Guide to Personal Growth
videos.description:
Recorded May 2, 2024
Instructor: Abby Eisenberg
In the classic rabbinic text, Pirkei Avot, the rabbis of late antiquity offer wisdom to guide us to become our best selves. Pirkei Avot suggest how we can improve upon our interpersonal (and intrapersonal) skills and develop an openness to learning and growth which are at the heart of Jewish living. As we analyze the many diverse rabbinic statements in this ancient text, we will glean intriguing (and humorous) observations about relationships with ourselves, family, and friends. Join us for this two-part series with hit Melton teacher Abby Eisenberg as we explore this essential text that is full of insights into human nature. 
We strive to ensure access and meaningful participation by all. As such, this program will have closed captioning.
Sponsored by Karen Zuckerstein in honor of the Eisenberg clan.

videos.title: Melton Development LGL to Events Review - Session 1
Peace Process: Bringing Calm Home
videos.description:
Recorded May 1, 2024 
Instructor: Lynne Lieberman 
We are living through a moment of intense turmoil throughout the world as both Jews and as human beings. We’re witnessing immense global upheaval, war, and financial turbulence. Thankfully, Jewish tradition has set out standards to bring peace and calm into our lives and homes, encompassed in the Hebrew phrase Shalom Bayit. Shalom Bayit literally means “wholeness or fulfillment in [one’s] home”. In this special program with renowned Melton teacher Lynne Lieberman, we will discuss several Jewish values ranging from honor/respect (kavod) to forgiveness (slichah) that provide us guidance to generate and nurture peaceful interactions in our home and within our family. Through the exploration of Jewish texts, we will gain new insights into our lives and the homes, helping to bring calm into our lives and to the world as a whole.

videos.title: Living Next to Gaza: Film Screening, Talkback, and the Legacy of October 7th Victim Yahav - Q&A with Shaylee Atary
Week 5-Holiday Heroines
videos.description:
Recorded April 25, 2024
Instructor: Sivan Rotholz

videos.title: Living Next to Gaza: Film Screening, Talkback, and the Legacy of October 7th Victim Yahav Winner
videos.description:
Recorded April 25, 2024 
In May 2023, Yahav Winner released a poignant short film entitled “The Boy” that told the story of an Israeli father and son living next to the Gaza Strip and how they handled an approaching war. It was a deeply personal project for Winner. In his words, “I was born in Kibbutz Kfar Aza in 1986, the eldest son of two urban parents who chose to come to a Kibbutz to fulfill the dream of a house with a garden. My father decided to give me the name Yahav, meaning security, hope. From the moment I was born, I felt the yoke of God on my shoulders, because in my head, there were dreams, reflections, and high sensitivities that made me cry from almost every situation.” There was no way for Winner to know that this would be his final film. He was killed in the attacks on October 7th. Since then, his story has become more relevant and compelling than ever before. Join us for a special screening of this film followed by an exclusive talkback with Winner’s widow and frequent artistic collaborator, Shaylee Atary.

videos.title: Week 3-Books They Never Taught Us
videos.description:
Recorded April 18, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Richard AddressThe Torah is one of the most widely studied books in Jewish life. We return to it year after year, gaining fresh insights each go around. But we don’t focus as much on many of the other books that make up the larger Hebrew Bible, aka the Tanakh? Stories found in Samuel and Kings show us examples of the peaceful transfer of power. The book of Jeremiah highlights how the Babylonian exile transformed Jewish understandings. And the book of Daniel explores Jewish mysticism and views on the afterlife! In this exciting new course from acclaimed Melton teacher Rabbi Richard Address, we will spend time looking over some of the lesser studied books of the Tanach and, in doing so, provide new insights into our history and contemporary Jewish issues we continue to focus on today.

videos.title: Events to LGL: Module 1 Intro Video by Stephanie Cole
A Taste of Passover from Baghdad to the Balkins
videos.description:
Recorded April 17, 2024
Instructor: Rahel Musleah 
Passover is one of the most widely celebrated holidays in the Jewish tradition. Jews may spend countless hours preparing their kitchens for the holidays, cooking recipes passed down for generations and enjoying the Seder with those closest to us. This year, Melton is proud to welcome award-winning journalist Rahel Musleah to teach us about global Passover traditions. Through music, food, and celebration, we will learn how different Jewish communities across the planet personalize and re-enact the experience of leaving Egypt, explore the classic dish of haroset as it is interpreted globally, celebrate contemporary ideas for children and families, and more. This program is sure to inspire you to bring new ideas to your own seder table. Join us for a vibrant exploration of Passover around the world!

videos.title: Parent Empowerment Initiative - Antisemitism: The Oldest Hatred
videos.description:
Recorded April 16, 2024
Instructor: Sivan Rotholz 
As we see an uptick in antisemitic incidents around the world, this class will uncover the deep roots of antisemitism and how it has changed throughout the ages. Focusing on the current forms of anti-Zionism and anti-Israel sentiment, we will explore the newest incarnations of one of history’s oldest forms hatred.

videos.title: Reclaiming Our Narrative- Jewish Peoplehood Post-October 7th
videos.description:
Recorded April 15, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Zohar Raviv
For Jews around the world, life completely changed after October 7th. How we relate to Israel, each other, and the world around us has brought up new feelings and enhanced emotions we might have had for years. This timely session with Birthright’s International Vice President of Education Dr. Zohar Raviv will look at some of the patterns that have been embedded in Jewish civilization and identity and have resurfaced since October 7th. From there, Dr. Raviv will introduce contemporary challenges and opportunities that can help guide our vision for the future. Join us for this in-depth look at narrowing the chasm between what is and what could be for the Jewish people at this revolutionary moment in our evolving story.

What does responsible parenting look like in the social media age? In this program, we will delve into social media usage, online conduct, and digital citizenship through a Jewish ethical lens.

videos.title: Naomi Shemer: "Beyond Jerusalem of Gold"
videos.description:
Recorded April 1, 2024 
Instructor: Cantor David Lipp 
Israeli music owes a lot to the artist Naomi Shemer. In many ways, Shemer helped define the tone and trajectory of Israeli popular music. For example, the genre Shirei Eretz Yisrael (Songs of the Land of Israel) would be far poorer without this sui generis singer/song-writer. In this moving follow up to Cantor David Lipp’s hit course “Israeli Pop Jamz”, we get an overview of her lasting influence and legacy on the corpus of Israeli artistic expression. Join us for a rockin’ good class!

videos.title: Week 4-The Master Storyteller: Isaac Bashevis Singer
videos.description:
Recorded March 25, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Shana Rosenblatt Mauer 
Isaac Bashevis Singer is one of the most important writers of the 20th century as a voice that crossed countless boundaries in the Jewish world, in addition to being a Nobel laureate. Raised in Torah-observant communities of rural Poland and Warsaw, Singer wrote with a religious spirit coupled with a secular zeal. He was a European and a native Yiddish speaker who took the American literary scene by storm and ensured that English reading audiences would have access to his work. While he did not directly fall victim to the Holocaust, Singer was still all too aware of the impending doom for the Jews of Europe having lost both his mother and brother to the Nazi terror. In this moving four-part program from Dr. Shana Mauer, teacher of the hit Melton International program “Emma Lazarus: Beyond the Statue of Liberty”, we will get a sense of this paradoxical writer who had an unparalleled imagination and an unprecedented storytelling voice.

videos.title: Parent Empowerment Initiative - Purity of Arms: The Israeli Military’s Code of Ethics
videos.description:
Recorded March 20, 2024 
Instructor: Joel Chasnoff, who is the author of Israel 201, this year’s recipient of the National Jewish Book Award, as well as a former Israeli soldier.
As the world watches the ongoing war in Gaza, the media is raising questions about whether or not Israel’s military is carrying out its operations ethically. Join us for an inside look at the Israeli army’s code of ethics as we examine this critical issue.

videos.title: Tipsy Turvy: The Obligation to Get Drunk on Purim
videos.description:
Recorded March 20, 2024 
Instructor: Rabbi Ben Kramer 
Beginning with the story of Noah, Jewish tradition repeatedly warns us about the dangers of imbibing too much wine. But on the holiday of Purim there seems to be an obligation to get drunk! In this special holiday class with acclaimed Melton teacher Rabbi Ben Kramer, we will study a variety of classical rabbinic texts to better understand the nature of this unusual obligation and the meaning behind it. We will also explore how rabbis throughout the generations have tried to reconcile this obligation with the generally negative attitude toward drunkenness in Jewish tradition.

videos.title: Week 3-Israel: The Present Situation and What Lies Ahead
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded March 19, 2024
Instructor: Daniella Geffen 
October 7th was a transformative moment in Israel. Perceptions of Israeli life and safety changed along with the ways that the Israeli people stepped up in the wake of the violent attacks on their country. In this urgent new course from Melton Travel educator Daniella Gefen, we will break down the complex history that led to this moment, explore ways that Israel is confronting this new reality, and look into the future with an eye for what comes next. What are the origins and fates of those living in Gaza? How has Israel’s military been reshaped by technology and gender roles? Is a two-state solution still possible or are there alternatives developing for how Israeli and Palestinian people will live in the same land in the future? Please join us as we explore these complex questions and more in this important follow up to Daniella’s impactful Melton International class, “Melton Meets the Moment: Understanding the Backstory of the War in Israel”.  

This course is graciously sponsored by Zachary Narrett.

videos.title: Events Tutorial - 03.14.24
In the Beginnings: Perspectives and Views on Creation
videos.description:
Recorded March 13, 2024 
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. David Harbater 
Throughout history, billions of people worldwide have considered the biblical account of creation without realizing that there is not one account but rather two separate and conflicting accounts. In this special program from Rabbi Dr. David Harbater, author of “In the Beginnings”, we will explore these two accounts and the perspectives that emerge from each. Whether one accepts the traditional or the critical view of the Torah’s origin, these perspectives offer a powerful testimony to the Torah’s depth, richness, and multivocality, with profound implications for the ways it is to be understood and lived in our day. Join us for this exciting program that is sure to shed new light on one of the most famous passages in the Torah.

videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded March 6, 2024
Instructor: Amy Kronish
In the last 25 years, Israeli cinema has turned its lens to multiple subjects dealing with humanistic stories, issues of gender, and social issues. In this engaging follow-up to Israeli media expert Amy Kronish’s hit Melton series “Examining Israeli Identity through Film and Television”, we will concentrate on portrayals of women in film and, in many of the cases, examine films directed by women. With these films as the launching point, we can better understand the complexity, nuance, and humanity found in Israeli society.
