videos.title: Reconnect
videos.description: Response to the Oct 7th Hamas attack inlcudes a desire to recoonect with Jewish Roots.

videos.title: I'm Not Alone and Neither Are You
videos.description:
Recorded February 29, 2024
Instructors: Cathy Zacks Gildenhorn, Beth Glassman, and Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz
When Cathy Zacks Gildenhorn was diagnosed with a rare genetic disorder, she set out to connect with others who were suffering from a rare disease or medical challenge. This mission led to the establishment of the People’s Choice nominated podcast, “It Happened to Me… I’m Not Alone and Neither Are You.” Hosting experts alongside patients, Cathy and her co-host, Beth Glassman, work together to build community and help people who feel isolated and overwhelmed because of medical challenges to find ways to live a full and satisfying life. Join Cathy, Beth and Melton International Director, Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz for an exploration into the very real world of hope in the face of suffering, exploring it through both the eyes of the sufferers and the study of some very insightful teachings from Jewish tradition.

videos.title: Week 4-The Human Body in Jewish Law
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded February 29, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Danny Schiff
Tattooing, body-piercing, cosmetic surgery, cremation, embalming, hair cutting, hair covering, immersing in water…In most contemporary societies these issues are matters of personal preference rather than religious law. Jewish law, however, has significant, thought-provoking things to say about how we treat the human body and what we do to it. Join world-renowned educator Rabbi Danny Schiff as we explore how Jewish law suggests we should treat our bodies.

videos.title: How to Build a Registration Page with Add-Ons
Melton Meets the Moment: The History of Antisemitism in the USA
videos.description:
Recorded February 26, 2024
Instructor: Professor Jonathan Sarna
Recently, we have witnessed a jarring spike in antisemitic activities in the United States. But what role has antisemitism actually played in American history? Is it a pervasive phenomenon, a late and alien phenomenon, or something in between? Join world-renowned Jewish historian Prof. Jonathan Sarna for this timely and insightful session tracing the course of antisemitism in the United States and considering the question of whether the Jewish American experience can be considered exceptional.

Recorded February 13, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Richard Address
Statistics indicate that many of us are one medical crisis away from financial devastation. The American health system is at the heart of this concern; making sure adults have equal access to health care is probably the most significant social issue the Baby Boomer generation is facing. What can Jewish ethics and thought contribute to this subject? Can our tradition provide any guidance? In this discussion with Rabbi Richard Address, Founder and Director of Jewish Sacred Aging, we will look at a series of approaches from across denominational lines to see how Jewish scholars approach this pressing social issue.

videos.title: Setting Up Travel Program in Events
Week 3-Evolving Tradition: Deepening Our Understanding of Orthodox Judaism
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded February 12, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Asher LopatinMany people associate Orthodox Judaism with a specific traditional practice that has not changed in hundreds of years, but today’s Jewish orthodox community is diverse and constantly evolving. Join Orthodox thought-leader Rabbi Asher Lopatin for this thrilling three-part exploration of the evolution of Orthodox Judaism. We’ll start with a breakdown how Orthodox Jews themselves see their history and evolution. From there we’ll take a look at how Orthodoxy developed as a reaction to other movements and grew in different ways as a direct result. How has it survived and thrived? What is its role in in the United States and in Israel? Finally, we will explore Modern, Liberal, Open, and Ultra Orthodoxy as well as Yeshivish, Chasidish, and Social Orthodoxy. Join us for this exciting deep dive into one of Judaism’s most fascinating denominations!

This is the second in a four-part series Melton is proudly offering in 5784 exploring major denominations in Judaism. Stay tuned for more information about courses exploring Reform and Conservative Judaism in the year ahead!

videos.title: Week 4-Jewish Ghost Stories
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded February 8, 2024
Instructor: Dr. David Shyovitz
Fantasy is one of the most popular genres on earth. From Harry Potter to Lord of the Rings to Game of Thrones, our society has been enraptured by the magical possibilities of far-off lands, monsters, and adventure. But what about Jewish fantasy? Judaism has a rich history of "folk narratives" all the way from the Middle Ages: tales of wandering ghosts, magical duels, distant lands, and monstrous creatures. Join Jewish historian Dr. David Shyovitz for this thrilling four-part exploration of Jewish fantasy and ghost stories. As we shall see, belief in the otherworldly and supernatural were not merely popular superstitions. Rather, these stories transmitted deeply held values, and reflected abstract and sophisticated theological tenets. Read carefully, they provide us with a fascinating vantage point onto the challenges faced by Jews living in an often-hostile cultural climate.

videos.title: Week 1 - The Human Body in Jewish Law
videos.description:
Week 1
Recorded February 8, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. Danny Schiff
Tattooing, body-piercing, cosmetic surgery, cremation, embalming, hair cutting, hair covering, immersing in water…In most contemporary societies these issues are matters of personal preference rather than religious law. Jewish law, however, has significant, thought-provoking things to say about how we treat the human body and what we do to it. Join world-renowned educator Rabbi Danny Schiff as we explore how Jewish law suggests we should treat our bodies.

Melton Meets the Moment- Stories of Israeli Resistance
videos.description:
Recorded February 6, 2024
Instructor: Viva Sarah Press
In the wake of the horrific attacks in Israel on October 7, Israelis have found new and innovative ways to be resilient during such a difficult time. Ranging from efforts in the technology sector all the way to Israel’s vibrant street art scene, Israel is showing the world that fighting back can mean more than just with weapons. Join veteran journalist and storyteller Viva Sarah Press to hear about inspiring stories of daily living, grassroots solidarity initiatives, and innovations-for-good. Give yourself the gift of hearing the heartwarming news that tells such a different story than the international headlines.

videos.title: Week 2-Images of Women in Israeli Cinema
videos.description:
Week 2
Recorded January 31, 2024
Instructor: Amy Kronish
In the last 25 years, Israeli cinema has turned its lens to multiple subjects dealing with humanistic stories, issues of gender, and social issues. In this engaging follow-up to Israeli media expert Amy Kronish’s hit Melton series “Examining Israeli Identity through Film and Television”, we will concentrate on portrayals of women in film and, in many of the cases, examine films directed by women. With these films as the launching point, we can better understand the complexity, nuance, and humanity found in Israeli society.

videos.title: Week 4-Thought Process: Jewish Law and Mental Health
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded January 30, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
Every decade or so, societal shifts emerging from economic development, technological innovation, and medical research force rabbis to clarify Torah values and to issue Jewish legal guidelines on new matters that need to be addressed. Recently, one of the main topics is mental health and Jewish law. In this four-part series taught by expert Melton educator Rabbi Johnny Solomon, we will explore some of the questions being asked today, as well as the responses being provided. We will begin with a session on methodological considerations to see how Jewish law is determined in response to questions of mental health. This will then be followed by three sessions examining the way religious observance is challenging for individuals dealing with depression, anxiety, OCD, and eating disorders. We hope you can join us for this important course offered during a time when many are struggling with mental health challenges.

videos.title: Jewcology, Israel, and Tu B’Shvat
videos.description:
Recorded January 24, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Mark Cohn
How did a holiday that originated in Temple times wind up inspiring Jewcology (Jewish environmentalism)? Tu B’Shvat has developed alongside the changing needs and focus of the Jewish people. Whereas the holiday has its origins in the biblical tax code, it has changed over time incorporating mystical, Zionist, and environmental ideas. In this one-hour course with expert Melton teacher Rabbi Mark Cohn, we travel through the various eras to uncover how Tu B’Shvat may be the most evolved holiday on the Jewish calendar.

videos.title: The Book Banning Brouhaha
videos.description:
Recorded January 23, 2024
Instructor: Lynne Lieberman
In the United States of America, freedom of expression is one of our most core values. But people across the country are feeling more and more threatened by ideas found not just in the spoken word, but in the written word as well. This has led to a rise in the banning of certain books across the United States. Thankfully, Judaism has some keen insights to help us navigate this sensitive subject. The Jewish people, in fact, have a lot of experience with book banning, even, at times, declaring certain books off limits for Jewish readers! Join expert Melton educator Lynne Lieberman as we explore Jewish history’s relationship with book banning and consider how it relates to current events.  Looking at ancient Jewish texts, famous pieces of Jewish literature, and our people’s history with this issue, Lynne helps us shed new light on this rising concern.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: Israel on the American College Campus
videos.description:
Recorded January 22, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Joy Getnick
As we face the global rise of antisemitism, American college campuses are particularly volatile with pro-Palestinian protests that delegitimize the right of the State of Israel to exist and use antisemitic tropes and language. As tensions flare and demonstrations become more intimidating for Jewish college students, it’s important to understand more clearly what is going on and how we got here. Join Dr. Joy Getnick, the Executive Director of Hillel at University of Rochester, for this timely topic and most importantly, for a discussion about what we can do to support Jewish students across the country.

videos.title: Emma Lazarus: Beyond the Statue of Liberty
videos.description:
Recorded January 10, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Shana Mauer
In this special session we focus on one of the earliest and most high profile Jewish American writers, and how she became an indelible part of American history. Known most famously for having penned the iconic poem that was eventually emblazoned on the base of the Statue of Liberty, Emma Lazarus was not only a highly regarded poet of the 1800s, but a woman who defied her family’s assimilationist aspirations and became fiercely dedicated to the Jewish people. Born into an affluent and upwardly mobile Sephardic Jewish family, Lazarus, from her teenage years, embarked on a journey of Jewish learning and commitments. Eventually, she became a powerful Jewish voice, jeopardizing her reputation, status, and even her health when she chose to serve as a scribe of her people and  a brave Jewish activist. At a time when the established, well-heeled Jewish community was reluctant to be too conspicuous or voluble, Emma Lazarus advocated for America’s recent Jewish immigrants from Eastern Europe and enlisted like-minded Jewish leaders to join in her activism. Join Jewish literature expert Dr. Shana Mauer for this masterful exploration of Lazarus’s life and work.

Week 1
Recorded January 9, 2024

Every decade or so, societal shifts emerging from economic development, technological innovation, and medical research force rabbis to clarify Torah values and to issue Jewish legal guidelines on new matters that need to be addressed. videos.title: The Jewish Value of Giving in the New Year
videos.description:
Recorded January 8, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
Charitable giving, or “tzedakah”, is a central Jewish obligation. Starting in the Torah, we are taught the value of donating and sharing what we have with those in need. We also instill a culture of giving at specific moments: at weekly collections at Jewish preschool, when one has a bar/bat mitzvah, before we light Shabbat candles, and more. This session takes the value of tzedakah beyond the acculturated practice to ask some of the bigger questions. How do we decide where to give? What do we do when we know there are so many places that need our financial help?  How do we view people who do unethical things but also are generous donors? Should our organizations accept tzedakah from them? There are more complexities and nuances to this ancient Jewish value than you might think! Join expert Melton teacher Rabbi Michele Faudem as we explore what it means to give tzedakah in 2024.

videos.title: Week 4-An Ethical Checkup: Jewish Views on Modern Medical Issues
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded December 20, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
The world of medicine is constantly evolving and with it so are our questions. From seemingly small-scale issues of confidentiality to larger, more complex questions around altering DNA before conception, Jewish law has a wealth of information and perspectives to help us consider some of the most complex questions. Join expert Melton educator Rabbi Michele Faudem for this four-part series exploring how Jewish law views privacy, how we determine who gets what medical treatment when resources are scarce, autopsies, and gene manipulation before birth.

videos.title: Week 3-History Behind the Headlines: Israel, Hamas, and the Current War
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded December 19, 2023
Instructor: Professor Howard Lupovitch
With so much misinformation swirling around us, it is more important than ever to understand the complexities of Israel from its inception until now. Join us for this special limited series of three sessions that provides a brief but comprehensive Israel education for learners of all backgrounds. In the first session, we will consider the long history Jews have had in the land of Israel as well as the 19th and 20th Zionist philosophies that formed the ethos of the modern State of Israel. Next, we will examine the Israel-Palestinian Conflict and its origins, complexities, and attempted solutions. Finally we will focus on the emergence of Hamas as a mainstay of Palestinian leadership and authority and how its charters speak about Israel, Jews, and Palestinian national aspirations through a radical Islamic framework. Professor Howard Lupovitch, professor of history at Wayne State University and author of Melton’s acclaimed course The Star and the Crescent, will provide us with a deep understanding of Israel’s history that can help us to better understand this critical moment for Israel and Jews around the world.

videos.title: Week 4-Script & Scripture: Torah & Shakespeare’s Comedy of Errors
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded December 18, 2023
Instructor: Cantor Matt Austerklein
In this expansion of Cantor Austerklein’s hit Melton class, “The Bard & the Bible”, we will be taking a deep dive into the shared themes from the Torah and William Shakespeare’s classic “Comedy of Errors”. Join Cantor Austerklein for a fun and exciting exploration of the religious imaginations of the rabbis and the bard of Stratford-on-Avon. This four-part series discusses marriage and magic, exploring surprising ideas in both traditions that will resonate today. In the words of Shakespeare himself, “The love of heaven makes one heavenly.”

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: An Inside Look at the Foundation to Combat Antisemitism
videos.description:
Recorded December 14, 2023
Instructor: Matthew Berger, Foundation to Combat Antisemitism Senior Advisor
Jews around the world are feeling the impact of the war in Israel. While focused on what’s happening in Israel, we’re also keenly aware of the scary rise of antisemitism. While Jews make up only 2.4% of the U.S. population, they are the victims of 55% of all religious based hate crimes. Yet about half of Americans do not think that antisemitism is a major problem in this country. That disparity was central to the Foundation to Combat Antisemitism's decision to launch the Stand Up to Jewish Hate campaign last spring featuring their now signature Blue Square. The $25 million advertising campaign, specifically targeting people outside the Jewish community, raised awareness about antisemitism and built empathy for the Jewish community. Now, the Foundation is focused on reaching even more Americans and sharing the Blue Square as the universal symbol to rally support against Jewish hate. Join the Foundation to Combat Antisemitism’s senior advisor Matthew Berger as he shares with us what the Foundation is doing to fight back against antisemitism right now.

videos.title: Light, Might and Beyond: Chanukah and Meaning-Making in 2023
videos.description:
Recorded December 7, 2023
Instructor: Abby Eisenberg
Chanukah has been called "a type of holy Rorschach test." The story and rituals of this holiday have many different meanings depending on how you look at them. Join dynamic Jewish educator Abby Eisenberg to explore the development of Chanukah and how its various messages have evolved over time. We analyze historical, religious, and liturgical texts and, in doing so, attempt to determine if there is one dominant theme of the holiday or if Chanukah can truly be based on multiple - perhaps conflicting - themes. We aim to glean meaning that resonates with each of us, for the holiday, for this year, and beyond.

Israel Revealed-November
videos.description:
Session 3-November
Recorded November 28, 2023
Instructor: Avi Ben Hur
Israel is a colorful and multifaceted country filled with people of all backgrounds, religions, and cultures. While the headlines we read emphasize the political issues, there’s a lot more going on in Israel than politics. Through archeological discoveries, internationally sought after movies and TV shows, technological breakthroughs, and more, Israel regularly leaves a global impact that cannot be ignored. Join us for a breakdown of the latest and greatest developments and little-known nuggets from the Promised Land, presented by the co-founder of Israel Studies Online, expert Israeli educator and Melton Travel leader Avi Ben-Hur!

videos.title: Living in an Upside Down World
videos.description: This class will draw on Rabbi Ralph Genende’s recent book, an anthology of his writings on the many crises and challenges of the 21st century - from polarisation and loneliness, love and death, to climate change, and the war against Israel. It’s about our universal experiences and their moorings in a distinctive Jewish tradition as well as the unique Jewish conflicts and confrontations of our age. In the class we will explore some of the challenges of discovering meaning in a turbulent time with some readings and anecdotal stories from the book.

videos.description:
Recorded November 20, 2023
Instructor: Daniella Gefen
The horrific events of October 7th and the war that followed have left Israeli society and the Jewish people in a state of shock, pain, and horror. In response, Israelis have sprung into action in truly remarkable ways, enlisting to serve in the army, volunteering to support soldiers in a myriad of ways, and organizing to ensure the wellbeing of close to half a million people evacuated from their homes in the North and South. Alongside all these acts of kindness there is still so much we struggle to wrap our heads around. In this program, led by Melton Travel educator Daniella Gefen, we break down some of the most challenging questions facing Israel right now. How do Israelis understand their new reality? What sorts of immediate and long-term challenges are of greatest concern? What can we learn about Gaza’s history that can provide us with important context to understanding this war? How do Israelis view American support? And, most importantly, how will Israel get through this? Join us for another powerful Melton program exploring the current situation in Israel and Gaza.

videos.title: Session 4: Israel on the American College Campus
videos.description:
Recorded November 15, 2023


In this final session, parents will be presented with real-life cases – illustrative of the political climate related to Israel – that college students are encountering on their campuses. The case studies will serve as openings for parents to discuss their concerns about these issues and consider together how they can best prepare and support their kids should they encounter these or similar scenarios.

videos.title: Session 4-Israel on the American College Campus
videos.description: Recorded November 16, 2023

videos.title: Melton eBooks System Roll-out - Directors Webinar
videos.description:
Led by Alison Epstein
Recorded 16-Nov-2023

videos.title: Session 3: The Hamas Problem
videos.description:
Recorded November 14, 2023
How did Hamas emerge at the end of the 1980s as an extreme expression of Palestinian opposition to Israeli rule in the West Bank and Gaza, discontent with Palestinian leadership, and absolute rejection of diplomacy and negotiation between the State of Israel and the Palestinian people? What does the emergence of Hamas as a mainstay of Palestinian leadership and authority reflect about (1) the attitudes of Palestinians toward the State of Israel and Israeli Jews and (2) the role of radical Islam in Palestinian national aspirations.

videos.title: Session 3-Parent Empowerment Initiative
videos.description: Recorded November 15, 2023

videos.title: Week 2-Give It a Rest: A Guide to Shabbat Prayers
videos.description:
Week 2
Recorded November 15, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Wallk
With war raging in Israel, how might the Shabbat liturgy help us cope with this moment? How can we find comfort from our Shabbat prayers right now? Guided by Jewish liturgy expert Rabbi Amy Wallk, we explore the layers and themes of the Shabbat morning service in the context of this moment and, in doing so, discover prayers that might challenge or inspire us. We encourage you to bring your questions and curiosity!

videos.title: Touching Talmud Tales
videos.description:
Recorded November 14, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Devorah Jacobson
For many, the Talmud is a source of detailed, legal discussions. But what about the stories it tells? What about its fables? What insights can we gain from those? Join seasoned Melton teacher Rabbi Devorah Jacobson as we explore a couple of the Talmud’s most moving narratives. Through these stories we gain insight into the impact of our teachers, the talents of gifted individuals, the presence of God in our lives, and more.

videos.title: Session 2: The Israel-Palestinian Conflict: Origins, Complexities, and (Attempted) Solutions
videos.description:
Recorded November 13, 2023
How did the century-long conflict between Israelis and Palestinians begin, and why does it now look so seemingly irreconcilable? In this session, we study the competing national aims of Zionism and Palestinian nationalism -- two historical claims for the same territory -- how each intensified after the founding of the State of Israel, and the challenges of a two-state solution.

videos.title: Session 1: The Path to Statehood
videos.description:
Recorded November 12, 2023
The creation of the State of Israel in 1948 culminated nearly a century of state-building by Jews in the Land of Israel.  In this session, we take a close look at the complexities of this remarkable, arduous task that expanded an enclave of small Jewish communities in Eretz Yisrael in 1880 — the Old Yishuv — into the New Yishuv, an extensive network of prosperous agriculture settlements and modern cities. We consider also how various Zionist ideas and visions of the Jewish State guided this state-building process and became pillars of the State of Israel.

videos.title: Session 2-Parent Empowerment Initiative: Israel Education for Parents of Teens and College Students
videos.description:
Session 2
Recorded November 13, 2023
Instructors: Professor Howard Lupovitch, Dr. Joy Getnick
With so much misinformation swirling around us, even Jews struggle to understand the complexities of Israel from its inception until now. Join us for this special limited series that provides a multi-faceted, yet brief, Israel education for parents of teens and college-age students in relevant and accessible ways that empower all learners to be more knowledgeable parents, Jews, and citizens of the world.

videos.title: Week 4-The Bible's #MeToo Moments
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded November 13, 2023
Instructor: Sivan Rotholtz
The Hebrew Bible is no stranger to rape, murder, victim-blaming, and some of the earliest recorded #MeToo moments in human history. Each week during this four-part series we'll pair the story of biblical women who suffered at the hands of violent patriarchal misogyny with midrash and poetry that adds depth and nuance to our understanding. We will reflect on what each of these women has to teach us, adding our own voices to an age-old tradition of wrestling with difficult Jewish texts. Join feminist Torah scholar and soon-to-be-Rabbi Sivan Rotholz as we consider, reconsider, and take a deep dive into a selection of #MeToo tales from biblical literature.

videos.title: Session 1-Parent Empowerment Initiative: Israel Education for Parents of Teens and College Students
videos.description:
Session 1

videos.title: Week 4-Shtetl Fabulous
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded November 9, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Rachel Seelig
The shtetl (Yiddish for “small town”) is one of the most recognized symbols of Jewish life in Eastern Europe before the Holocaust. But what exactly was it? Since the mid-nineteenth century, Yiddish writers have mythologized, mocked, and memorialized the shtetl. In this four-session course, we will explore literary representations of the shtetl ranging from the sublime to the ridiculous. How has the shtetl been portrayed, and why does it still have such a strong hold on the Jewish imagination? Back after her hit Melton class, “Yiddish: Wanted Dead or Alive”, join Yiddish culture expert Dr. Rachel Seelig as she explores the ups and downs of shtetl living.

videos.title: Week 1-Give It a Rest: A Guide to Shabbat Prayers
videos.description:
Week 1
Recorded November 8, 2023

Guided by Jewish liturgy expert Rabbi Amy Wallk, we explore the layers and themes of the Shabbat morning service in the context of this moment and, in doing so, discover prayers that might challenge or inspire us. We encourage you to bring your questions and curiosity!

videos.title: Week 4-Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks z'l- A Spiritual Biography
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded November 8, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
If you know a scholar well enough, you see how the ideas they share are windows to their soul. In this ground-breaking course, Rabbi Johnny Solomon, a dedicated student of Rabbi Lord Sacks z’l, will share a range of teachings of Rabbi Sacks on themes relating to identity, self-esteem, failure, and joy. While doing so, we will gain a knowledge and understanding of the great man who was the Chief Rabbi of England but whose teachings impacted contemporary Jews from around the world.

videos.title: Ancient Artifacts of the Galilee and What They Mean Today
videos.description:
Recorded November 7, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Sophia Avants
In Israel today, we see the polarization of a Jewish society where one of the significant issues is how people define what it means to be Jewish. Ancient Jewish texts and modern archaeological finds have revealed the roots of this thousands’ year-old issue and, in doing so, offer of a glimpse into how to resolve issues we experience today. After the defeat of the Bar Kokhba revolt, and the move of many to the Galilee region, these societies developed their legal systems to accommodate interactions with Jews who did not keep their particular practices. Taught by Jewish historian Dr. Sophia Avants, this class explores these communities through selections from the Mishnah and examining the archaeological remains of Galilean homes. In addition to the history, our exploration will shed light on ancient strategies that can help us accommodate each other in today’s increasingly diverse world.

videos.title: Israel Revealed-October
videos.description:
Session 2-October
Recorded October 26, 2023
Instructor: Avi Ben Hur
Israel is a colorful and multifaceted country filled with people of all backgrounds, religions, and cultures. While the headlines we read emphasize the political issues, there’s a lot more going on in Israel than politics. Through archeological discoveries, internationally sought after movies and TV shows, technological breakthroughs, and more, Israel regularly leaves a global impact that cannot be ignored. Join us for a breakdown of the latest and greatest developments and little-known nuggets from the Promised Land, presented by the co-founder of Israel Studies Online, expert Israeli educator and Melton Travel leader Avi Ben-Hur! This is the second in a monthly series of updates from Avi on the latest news from Israel that you might not hear about elsewhere.

videos.title: My Heart is in the East
Week 3-Shalom Y'all: Jewish History and Identity in the American South
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded October 25, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Joellyn Zollman
This three-part class introduces students to the deep roots and regional features of Southern Jewish life, with a focus on the two communities that we will travel to in November: Savannah and Charleston. Our learning adventure will start in the classroom, where we all take a deep dive into three key areas of Southern Jewish life: settlement, Civil War and Civil Rights. We invite you to continue the journey on the cobblestone streets of Charleston and Savannah from November 5-10, 2023.

videos.title: Week 3 - Evolving Tradition: Deepening Our Understanding of Reconstructionist Judaism
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded October 24, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Boris Dolan
The newest of the major Jewish denominations may be one of the smallest but it has had an outsized impact on the contemporary Jewish world. Reconstructionism and its founder, Mordecai Kaplan, can be credited for everything from the first Bat Mitzvah to the vision of Jewish Community Centers, creative theology to social justice activism. In this participatory class, we learn about Mordecai Kaplan and see how his ideas and new ways of understanding Jewish community have impacted Jews across the spectrum of denominations. We also delve into new ways of understanding theology and faith and discuss the many ways that a Reconstructionist philosophy can help us make sense of contemporary issues and challenges. Join Reconstructionist thought-leader Rabbi Boris Dolin for this insightful exploration about the evolution of Reconstructionist Judaism.

videos.title: The Holiness of Doubt
videos.description: Questions are among the most powerful tools for creating and maintaining loving connections. But they can also be aggravating, even divisive. You may be surprised to learn that our sacred Torah text is filled with some 275 questions. Through its many questions, our holy book acknowledges and values uncertainty. Join Torah scholar Rabbi Joshua Hoffman as we explore the thinking behind his new book, The Holiness of Doubt, which guides us through a journey of studying the questions in the Torah.

videos.title: Melton Community Gathering in Support of Israel
videos.description: All of us at Melton are devastated by the horrific attack on Israel that began this past Shabbat morning. We know how dedicated and concerned our community is for the Israeli people. As such, we will be gathering online as a community along with our Melton leadership in solidarity with Israel and to offer prayers for her citizens and soldiers. Please join us as an international community to stand together at this challenging time.

videos.title: A Paradoxical Powerhouse-The Rise of Haredi Feminism in Israel
videos.description:
Recorded September 21, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Tanya Zion-Waldoks
This session examines the emergence of a revolutionary and novel feminist movement within Israel’s Haredi (Ultra-Orthodox) community; a highly conservative, illiberal, and relatively insular religious community committed to upholding ancient traditions and strict gender roles. In recent decades, haredi society in Israel has been shaped by profound political, economic, and social changes. Gender relations are a central site of contention and transformation, affecting haredi society and its interactions with Israel writ large. Feminist haredi activists, invested in championing women's rights while remaining within a collectivist conformist society and conservative tradition, push for the reimagination of individual lives, communal structures, and Israeli society.

videos.title: Week 2-Asking Forgiveness
videos.description:
Week 2
Recorded September 20, 2023
Instructor: Joe Septimus
Asking forgiveness from people we may have aggrieved is a core practice in Judaism, one that is described in some of our earliest rabbinic literature. The process of forgiveness is particularly emphasized during the high holiday season leading up to Yom Kippur. But as we know, interpersonal issues are complex and often force us to ask some tough questions before we can resolve them. Who started it? Who is to blame? Who should initiate the request for forgiveness? What if I’m not ready to forgive?  What is the objective of pursuing forgiveness? What are our own personal characteristics that cause us to offend others? Join expert Melton teacher Joe Septimus as we explore these issues and more through a thorough exploration of two insightful Talmudic stories.

videos.title: Week 3-Mean What You Pray: A Guide to High Holiday Prayers
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded September 11, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Amy S. Wallk
Have you ever found yourself thinking, “High Holiday services seem long and repetitive and I don’t really understand what the prayers are saying!” And yet, something draws us back to these services every year. In this three-part course taught by Jewish liturgy scholar and expert Melton teacher Rabbi Amy Wallk, we will examine some of the most important High Holiday prayers. Together we will find ways to make these ancient texts accessible and meaningful, enriching our holiday season.

videos.title: Week 4-Abraham on Trial
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded September 7, 2023
Instructor: Lynne Lieberman
According to the bible, our forefather Abraham is asked to make the ultimate sacrifice: to slaughter his beloved son Isaac atop Mt. Moriah. His faith in God seems to know no boundaries as he carries out God’s order, only to be stopped at the last minute by a ministering angel. Rather than resist this commandment, and thereby model a rejection of human sacrifice, he goes through with it and is revered throughout history for his unbendable faith! This exploration of the Akedah (binding), or near sacrifice of Isaac, investigates the subsequent and continuing reverberations of this controversial biblical story. Join expert Melton teacher Lynne Lieberman as she guides us through texts that reveal the impact that this narrative has had on western society throughout history to the present day.

videos.title: Purity of Arms-The IDF's Code of Ethics
videos.description:
Recorded September 6, 2023
Instructor: Joel Chasnoff
Almost all Western countries have a code of military ethics, but Israel's code, called “Tohar Haneshek” (Purity of Arms) is different in significant ways. In this text-based session, we'll analyze the IDF's Purity of Arms code and look at the rabbinic and Biblical source material that informed how and why the code was created. We'll also discuss real-world examples of the ethical dilemmas IDF soldiers have faced in combat and how the Purity of Arms code did or didn't help soldiers navigate the moral quandaries they encountered. Join “Israel 201” author Joel Chasnoff for this insightful look at the IDF’s code of ethics.

videos.title: Mixed Messages: Harkening to the Voice of the Shofar
videos.description: The sounding of the shofar is a bit of mystery. As we gather together in synagogues around the world to hear the ancient blasts of the shofar, we cannot help but wonder what this timeless practice is really all about.  Why is this the central custom associated with the Jewish New Year? What are we meant to be thinking or feeling as the repeated, familiar blasts fill the hushed sanctuary?

videos.title: Week 4-Soul Searching- Jewish Views on the Afterlife
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded August 28, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
In Judaism we use the word “soul” and the phrase “the world to come,” but do we really know what they mean? We won’t truly know about the afterlife until our moment arrives, leaving us with many more questions than answers. How does Judaism view the idea of the “soul”? What happens to the soul after we die? Do we have one soul during our lifetime or do we have a chance to develop our souls after life ends? Do Jews believe in reincarnation or people communicating with us from beyond the grave? What does Judaism think of mediums? Join expert Melton teacher Rabbi Michele Faudem for this meaningful, four-part exploration of Jewish views on all these topics related to the afterlife.

videos.title: Week 1-Rabbi Sacks on the Days of Awe
videos.description: What were Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks' unique teachings about the meaning and function of Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur? In this 2-part series, Rabbi Johnny Solomon will help us identify some of the most significant ideas that Rabbi Sacks shared about Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, and in so doing, he will help us in our efforts to prepare – intellectually, emotionally and spiritually – for the upcoming High Holy Days.

videos.title: Week 4-Religious Pluralism - Threat or Opportunity
videos.description: The fact that diverse religious opinions and positions stand side by side with one another in our world is inescapable. Religious pluralism is a feature of living in a multicultural world. As a result, it has become challenging for any one position to claim absolute authority and truth. How does Judaism deal with this modern dilemma? In this course, we’ll look at the various problems that religious pluralism raises for 20th century Jewish thinkers, against the backdrop of Jewish history and theology. Among the figures we’ll be considering will be Rabbi Joseph B. Soloveitchik, Rabbi Abraham Joshua Heschel, Rabbi Eugene Borowitz, Rabbi David Hartman, Rabbi Yitz Greenberg, and Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks.

videos.description: What were Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks' unique teachings about the meaning and function of Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur? videos.title: Week 2-Abraham on Trial
videos.description:
Week 2
Recorded August 24, 2023
Instructor: Lynne Lieberman
According to the bible, our forefather Abraham is asked to make the ultimate sacrifice: to slaughter his beloved son Isaac atop Mt. Moriah. His faith in God seems to know no boundaries as he carries out God’s order, only to be stopped at the last minute by a ministering angel. Rather than resist this commandment, and thereby model a rejection of human sacrifice, he goes through with it and is revered throughout history for his unbendable faith! This exploration of the Akedah (binding), or near sacrifice of Isaac, investigates the subsequent and continuing reverberations of this controversial biblical story. Join expert Melton teacher Lynne Lieberman as she guides us through texts that reveal the impact that this narrative has had on western society throughout history to the present day.

videos.title: Sacred Numbers
videos.description:
Recorded August 23, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Mark Cohn
The study of the Hebrew language is an ancient art centered around hidden meanings, rabbinic interpretations, and more. One of the most intriguing areas of exploration is gematria, the study of how Hebrew letters correspond to numbers, unlocking codes that teach us something deeper than our surface understanding of words. Join gematria expert, Rabbi Mark Cohn, as we explore the Jewish fascination, use, and presence of numbers as a means to dig deeper into our most sacred texts and connect with something bigger. As Israeli singer-songwriter, Shaanan Street of HaDag Nachash, wrote, "I’m just like every other Jew - busy with numbers 24/7/12."

videos.title: A Melton Board Toast to the New Year and Fond Farewell to Sandy Benjamin

videos.title: Shalom Berlin! Germany’s Most Jewish City
videos.description:
Recorded August 16, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Rachel Seelig
Berlin, the place where “The Final Solution to the Jewish Question” was conceived, has been likened to a phoenix rising from the ashes. Historically, Berlin was a magnet for Jewish newcomers, particularly from Eastern Europe, until Hitler’s rise to power put an abrupt end to that era. Since reunification and the fall of communism, the city has once again become an alluring destination for Israelis and Jews from the former Soviet Union. How did this happen? Is Berlin becoming a Jewish center once again? Discover the storied past and captivating transformation of Jewish Berlin with expert guide Dr. Rachel Seelig. Through an exciting exploration of literature, art, and even food, uncover Berlin’s rich Jewish heritage as it rises anew today.

videos.title: Koolulove  Yesh Bi Ahava  Arkadi Duchin  Latroun Park
Greek Jewry- Europe's Oldest Jewish Community
videos.description:
Recorded August 9, 2023
Instructor: Avi Ben-Hur
When we think about ancient Greece, what typically comes to mind are words like "Democracy", "History", "Philosophy", "Politics". And these are merely a tiny fraction of Greece's contribution to world culture. But how many people are aware of the fact that Greece competes with Turkey for the first Jewish communities to established in Europe! We find traces of Jewish life in Greece as early as the 3rd-2nd Centuries BCE, archaeologically and textually. The Jews of Greece figure prominently in the New Testament, with the apostle Paul’s failed attempts to convert Greek Jews to his new faith. Fast-forwarding in time, in 1900 the Jews of Salonika constituted the largest ethic population in the City (45%), with Ladino (Judeo-Spanish) the language of commerce. In 1943 the Nazis murdered this last great Sephardic community wiping out 97% of Salonikan Jewry.  This study session provides a taste of the rich Jewish life that existed in Greece for over two millennia as well as the present state of Greek Jewry and what is happening in Greece today.

videos.title: Week 4-A Spiritual Pathway for Aging into the Year Ahead
videos.description:
Recorded August 8, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Richard Address
Jewish texts and ideas provide a significant amount of support and guidance as we enter our third and fourth life stages. In this meaningful new course with Rabbi Richard Address, Founder and Director of Jewish Sacred Aging, we examine a series of Judaism’s most fundamental teachings as we prepare for the High Holidays and the year ahead. Rabbi Address engages learners in a dialogue with classical and contemporary Jewish thought to create a spiritual roadmap for the year ahead.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment- Israeli Judicial Reform
videos.description:
Recorded August 3, 2023
Instructor: Avi Ben-Hur
Finding it hard to understand the headlines about what is going on Israel? Concerned about the unprecedented current polarization of Israeli society? Wondering how we got here? You are not alone. In this presentation, expert Israeli educator specializing in Land of Israel studies Avi Ben-Hur shares a historical perspective and identifies what factors contributed to the current situation. Ben-Hur reviews the government’s proposed reforms, clarifies the key arguments for and against the reforms, and explores the potential political, economic, social, international and security ramifications of passing them. Join us for an objective analysis of one of Israel’s current most divisive political challenges.

Week 3

videos.title: LGBTQ+ Poetic Voices in Israel
videos.description:
Recorded August 1, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Rachel Korazim
Israeli poetry is as diverse as its population and offers a window into the unique and varied voices of Israeli society. In this special one-time session with world-renowned Israeli poetry expert, Rachel Korazim, we focus on three of Israel’s most prominent LGBTQ+ poets, whose works span the decades of Israel's history. We explore the works of Robert Friend, one of the earliest openly gay Israeli poets, who lived at a time in which very few homosexuals felt comfortable writing about their personal lives. The poems of Dory Manor take us into the complexities of homosexual love across ideological divides in Israel. And Daniel Segal Weingarten's voice brings us to the present day, when there is relative acceptance but fears of a darker future. Through the journeys and works of these three incredible poets, we gain a better understanding not just of Israeli culture, but of Israeli society.

videos.title: Week 4-Technology Ethics
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded July 31, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
With TikTok and ChatGPT in our daily headlines, it feels as though technology is inescapable. The fact is that technology is a major part of all our lives and it seems like it will be even more so in the future.  With this reality, it is more important than ever to seriously consider the ethical issues technology brings to the forefront. In this four-part course taught by expert Melton teacher Rabbi Johnny Solomon, we will examine a number of these ethical dilemmas. We will explore the moral issues that exist from sharing our lives and interacting with others on social media. We’ll also examine a range of ethical issues emerging from the use of Artificial Intelligence in travel, healthcare, and by the military. We don’t only highlight the problems, though, as we also consider the positive ways in which communities are enriched by these technologies. Join us for this timely course exploring some of our most pressing contemporary ethical issues.

videos.title: The Music of Tish'a B'av
videos.description:
Recorded July 25, 2023
Instructor: Cantor David Lipp
Music and Tisha B'Av are almost a contradiction in terms. After all, it was because of the destruction of the Second Temple that instrumental music was banned in Jewish prayer for centuries. But the singing of scripture, liturgy, and scholarly text seems to be a practice which never abated. Join expert Melton teacher Cantor David Lipp to learn some of the history and practice of the music of Tisha B'Av which includes the Book of Lamentations, the alteration of liturgical modes and the Kinot, the poems of despair that Jews traditionally chant to commemorate the loss of the Temple.

videos.title: Week 10-Biblical Women: Emerging from the Margins through Midrash
videos.description:
Week 10
Recorded July 19, 2023
Instructor: Kelila Slonim
Tracing major female figures of the Bible through classic and modern midrashic commentary, we meet a dazzling line up of more than a dozen biblical heroines — some you may know and some you may have never heard of! We go behind the scenes to gain multiple perspectives on their lives, relationships, and choices, as we understand their roles in Jewish history and literature as full, well-rounded characters.

videos.title: Decoding Medieval Jewish Art
videos.description:
Recorded July 20, 2023
Instructor: Dr. David Shyovitz
Pre-modern Jews are often described as "people of the book," but Jews produced visual art over the centuries as well; images in illuminated Bibles, Mahzorim, and Haggadot that are complex and sometimes deeply subversive. How does our understanding of Torah shift when we add the study of Jewish images into our learning? Join expert medieval historian Dr. David Shyovitz for this insightful exploration of medieval Jewish art.

videos.title: Week 4-Femme Fatale or Fierce Woman Warrior?
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded July 13, 2023
Instructor: Professor Sivan Rotholtz
Although written into Jewish tradition as a femme fatale, a demoness, a black widow, and a skull-crusher, Delilah, Lilith, Tamar, and Yael are far more complex personalities. Each week during this four-part series we'll pair the story of one of these unforgettable biblical women with midrash and poetry that shed new light upon her character. We will also reflect on what each of these women has to teach us, adding our own voices to the age-old tradition of wrestling with Jewish texts. Join feminist Torah scholar Sivan Rotholz as we consider, reconsider, and take a deep dive into the stories of four powerful women of biblical literature.

videos.title: The Problem of Evil
Week 6-Melton Learning with Melton's Leaders
videos.description:
Week 6
Recorded July 12, 2023
Instructors: Rabbi Rachel Bovitz and Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz
Jewish tradition has preserved a wellspring of timeless teachings for us, offering wisdom that can enrich almost every aspect of our lives. The challenge is that much of it can feel inaccessible, or veiled in a language that needs to be unpacked in order to be fully appreciated. Join the senior leadership of the Florence Melton School, Executive Director Rabbi Rachel Bovitz and International Director Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz, for a six-part exploration of the foundational teachings found in Pirkei Avot, a 2nd century tractate of the oral tradition passed down generation after generation and understood to include many of the most important and ever-relevant Jewish ethical values for living our lives. Through a close reading of these short but powerful texts, learners will gain a renewed appreciation for these invaluable pearls of wisdom.

videos.title: The Jews of Mexico City
videos.description:
Recorded July 11, 2023
Instructors: Andrea Gardenhour and Dr. Sara Aroeste
Throughout our history, Jewish journeys have included many stops and detours in various countries and continents. In this class we will discover the Jewish journeys that brought people from around the Jewish world to settle in Mexico City. Besides examining the rich historical narratives, we will also discuss the makeup of the diverse and extraordinary Jewish community living there today. This exciting new offering is being taught by Melton’s Director of Educational Travel, Andrea Gardenhour, and Sara Aroeste, PhD, a gifted Jewish educator whose name in Mexico City is synonymous with educational excellence.

videos.title: Week 4-Moses Meets Madison: Comparing Jewish and American Law
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded June 27, 2023
Instructor: Harman Grossman
As American Jews, we have a foot in two different legal systems. How do Jewish and American law treat specific issues that confront us today – capital punishment, abortion, and more? What are Jewish and American perspectives on the role of law in our lives, overall? What do the similarities and differences between the two legal systems reveal about the cultures that produced them? Most importantly, what do these two bodies of law say about us – what we believe, what we will fight for, who we are? Join veteran lawyer and Jewish Studies teacher Harman Grossman for a text-based exploration of two rich legal traditions, and ultimately, of ourselves.

videos.title: Classic Israeli Souvenirs on Display
videos.description:
Recorded June 19, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Rachael Goldman
The establishment of the State of Israel was a monumental moment in Jewish history. It also led to a breakthrough in Jewish art, with the creation of new sorts of ritual pieces. A new type of art emerged that celebrated the traditional images of the new State: Israeliana. These mass-produced pieces, designed by Israelis, aimed to put Israel’s new identity into the hands of Jews around the world. Visitors to this exciting, newborn, and developing state celebrated it by being consumers of “luxury” goods and trinkets. Bringing these art pieces home, Jews around the world could proudly display what Israel represented to them in, both in practice and in theory. Join Israeliana expert Dr. Rachael Goldman as we examine this unique art-form and discuss whether its history belongs in a museum or only on our shelves.

Instructors: Rabbi Rachel Bovitz and Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz
Jewish tradition has preserved a wellspring of timeless teachings for us, offering wisdom that can enrich almost every aspect of our lives. The challenge is that much of it can feel inaccessible, or veiled in a language that needs to be unpacked in order to be fully appreciated. Join the senior leadership of the Florence Melton School, Executive Director Rabbi Rachel Bovitz and International Director Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz, for a six-part exploration of the foundational teachings found in Pirkei Avot, a 2nd century tractate of the oral tradition passed down generation after generation and understood to include many of the most important and ever-relevant Jewish ethical values for living our lives. Through a close reading of these short but powerful texts, learners will gain a renewed appreciation for these invaluable pearls of wisdom.

videos.title: Week 2-Moses Meets Madison: Comparing Jewish and American Law
videos.description:
Week 2
Recorded June 13, 2023
Instructor: Harman Grossman
As American Jews, we have a foot in two different legal systems. How do Jewish and American law treat specific issues that confront us today – capital punishment, abortion, and more? What are Jewish and American perspectives on the role of law in our lives, overall? What do the similarities and differences between the two legal systems reveal about the cultures that produced them? Most importantly, what do these two bodies of law say about us – what we believe, what we will fight for, who we are? Join veteran lawyer and Jewish Studies teacher Harman Grossman for a text-based exploration of two rich legal traditions, and ultimately, of ourselves.

videos.title: Israeli Music Goes Mizrahi-Melton Supporter Appreciation Event
videos.description:
Recorded June 13, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Galeet Dardashti
Have you ever wondered how Israeli music today got its unique and varied sound? Join us for this not-to-be-missed event just for you – our most dedicated Melton donors. While the music from Israel’s first few decades represented the European tastes of its founders who defined “Israeliness,” by the 1970s Israeli music started changing with the significant influence of musicians of Yemenite, Persian, and North African origins. Join Dr. Dardashti for a musical exploration of the beautiful and diverse musical offerings of the State of Israel.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment- Combating Antisemitism with JewBelong
videos.description:
Recorded June 12, 2023
Instructor: Archie Gottesman
The current rise in global antisemitism is startling. How can we feel safe in a world that is increasingly hostile to Judaism? And what should we be doing about it? In this session, we will do a deep dive into a very visible response we are seeing across American cities.  In an attempt to bring the danger of antisemitism to the forefront of society’s consciousness, JewBelong launched a multi-media campaign to call it out as loudly as possible. You might have seen its bright pink ads and billboards with such eye-catching language as “Does your church need armed guards? ‘Cause our synagogue does. #EndJewHate” or “Being woke and antisemitic is like being a vegan who eats veal. #EndJewHate”. How did this campaign come to be? What is its goal? And what can we be doing to help? Join JewBelong’s Co-founder Archie Gottesman to understand more deeply what JewBelong is doing to fight back against antisemitism.

videos.title: Directors Webinar: eBook Rollout
videos.description:
Led by Jody Litwack
Recorded 8-JUN-2023

videos.title: Week 04 Biblical Women Australia
Week 03 Biblical Women Australia
The Bard & the Bible: Comparing Shakespeare to the Torah
videos.description:
Recorded June 8, 2023
Instructor: Cantor Matt Austerklein
To learn, or not to learn? That is the question. And here at Melton, we always vote “to learn”. It is especially fun to learn about how the Torah’s writing style compares to that of one of history’s greatest writers: William Shakespeare! Join Cantor Matt Austerklein for a lively hour of reading both Shakespearean and sacred text. This class will first cover tools for analyzing rhetoric, then apply this framework to discover how it can help us elucidate both the Bard & the Bible.

Week 1
Recorded June 7, 2023

videos.title: Week 4-Our Animals, Our World, and Ourselves
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded June 6, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Jonathan Bernhard
Back by popular demand, join Jewish Initiative for Animals’ Executive Director Rabbi Jonathan Bernhard as he expands on his hit Melton class, “Animal Welfare, Not Warfare”! Open to those who did or did not take his first class, this four-part course will explore the fundamentals of Jewish Animal Ethics from a variety of perspectives. What foundational concepts can be found in the Torah and Talmud? How are these ideas expanded upon in the works and worldviews of modern Jewish thinkers such as author I.B. Singer and Rabbi Ovadia Yosef, the former Sephardic Chief Rabbi of Israel? Through these questions and more, Rabbi Bernhard will bring into focus key Jewish principles that allow us to grow our own souls and help us approach our world in a healthier way.

videos.title: Meir Shalev z”l: Magical Storytelling At Its Best
videos.description:
Recorded June 5, 2023
Instructor: Aviva Laskov
This past April we lost one of Israel’s most prolific and impactful storytellers: Meir Shalev. His fantastic, magical writing captured the attention and love of readers in Israel and around the world. Shalev played imagination against reality and fiction against facts, visiting deserts and villages, featuring snakes, bears and pigeons, writing with a Marquez-like flair and a Chagall-like style. Shalev brought stories and histories together, peppering them with biblical imagery and an impressive knowledge of plants and wildlife. As Israeli President Isaac Herzog said at Shalev’s funeral, “Shalev made us love the Hebrew language, the Hebrew Bible, and, of course, ourselves, the people of Israel.” Join Israeli literature expert, Aviva Laskov, for this special, one-time, one-hour exploration of the works of this master storyteller.

videos.title: Week 4-Jewish Genealogy 101
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded May 31, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Dan Oren
Many of us have become interested in knowing more about our families’ roots. How does one start this process? What can one hope to achieve? This four-session course will deliver the critical building blocks to begin this exploration into Jewish Genealogy. Join Yale University professor Dr. Dan Oren as he guides us through this exciting, question-filled journey. How does one think like a Jewish genealogist? How does one store one’s data? What resources are available? How can DNA testing be useful in starting this investigation? What are the mistakes to try and avoid? When and whom do you call when you need help? This interactive, how-to course is designed to help you pursue research into your family’s history.

videos.title: Week 02 Biblical Women Australia
videos.description:
Week 02
Recorded May 24 2023
Kelila Slonim
Tracing major female figures of the Bible through classic and modern midrashic commentary, we meet a dazzling line up of more than a dozen biblical heroines — some you may know and some you may have never heard of! Let’s go behind the scenes to gain multiple perspectives on their lives, relationships, and choices, as we understand their roles in Jewish history and literature as full, well-rounded characters.

Recorded May 22, 2023

Israeli pop music is the heartbeat of Israeli culture. Within it, you can find references to the army, politics, community tensions, Jewish life, and much more. In this three-session course with beloved Melton teacher Cantor David Lipp, we'll survey decades of Israeli pop music with an ear for how these “jamz” reflect modern Israeli identity. How do they reveal who Israelis are today? Based on this music, what do they care about? How do they express themselves in the public square? Join us to jam and understand a bit more about contemporary Israel.

videos.title: Week 4-Character Development Through Mussar
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded April 25, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Ben Kramer
Whether by studying our texts such as the Bible and Talmud, or through weekly sermons and community dialogue, Jews are constantly discussing and evaluating how to best live out our religious ideals. We also have a specific genre of Jewish literature, Mussar, that provides us with a practical approach to developing our character in consonance with these values. In this course, taught by expert Jewish educator Rabbi Ben Kramer, we will study a variety of Mussar texts to develop an understanding of this approach and learn how to effectively apply its principles to our own lives. Join us for this special course dedicated to the Jewish practice of self-growth.

videos.title: Week 4-The Burden and Blessing of Exile
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded April 24, 2023
Instructor: Lynne Lieberman
Since the beginning, the Jewish people have been persecuted and forced to wander from land to land. Hinted at in the book of Daniel is the idea of there being Arba Galuyot, or Four Exiles. Our first exile to Egypt was self-imposed due to famine. Later, conquerors of Israel destroyed the Temple in Jerusalem and sent us away. Then as Christianity spread, growing antisemitism forced Jews to be on the move. This was followed by the Crusades, expulsions, pogroms, and the Holocaust which forced further exiles. We will explore each of these “four exiles” with a focus on both how the experiences have shaped the Jewish people as well as the Jewish people’s influence on the societies where they made their new homes. Join expert Melton educator, Lynne Lieberman, on this four-part exploration of Jewish migration across land and time to shed light on how our global journey has had profound implications for our Jewish self-identity and the Jewish influences that can be felt in the communities where we’ve lived.

videos.title: Joint Decisions: Rabbinic Perspectives on Weed
videos.description:
Recorded April 20, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
As the world continues to explore the moral and legal issues surrounding the use of marijuana, we have an opportunity to also consider some Jewish perspective on its use. There are, after all, clear medical benefits in specific situations. But what about consuming marijuana for recreational purposes? Is that to be condoned?  Is it kosher? It’s a sticky subject, for sure. This spring, join expert Melton teacher Rabbi Michele Faudem as we explore how dope Judaism really is.

videos.title: Melton Online Educator Orientation to the basics of Melton Teaching

Week 3

videos.title: Injustice Illuminated: The Holocaust Art of Arthur Szyk
videos.description:
Recorded April 17, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Irvin Ungar
In 1940, the Polish-Jewish artist Arthur Szyk — best known for his illustrated Haggadah — left Europe for the United States, intending to ignite American support for the war against Hitler. From New York, he worked as a self-appointed “soldier in art,” producing thousands of anti-Axis works designed for mass reproduction in United States newspapers, magazines, and more. Years before the world knew the full horrors of the Holocaust, Szyk publicly denounced Germany’s anti-Semitism and Nazi brutality, advocating tirelessly for the rescue of European Jewry from persecution, slavery, and death. His resonant artwork provides a unique Jewish perspective on the people, policies, and world events surrounding the Shoah. This presentation on Yom HaShoah by world renowned Szyk scholar, Rabbi Irvin Ungar, is dedicated to the hallowed memory of Europe’s martyred Jews and in honor of the survivors among us.

videos.title: Week 4-The Music and Teachings of Our Exodus
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded March 30, 2023
Instructors: Rabbi Amy Wallk and Cantor David Lipp
Passover is one of the most observed Jewish holidays around the world centered around one of the greatest stories ever told, the Exodus from Egypt. We retell this story using the Haggadah, which even translates to “The Telling”. So, what exactly is the story we are supposed to tell on the eve of Passover? How are we meant to go about telling it? How do the tunes and the songs that accompany the telling contribute to the story? And what are the many different ways the Haggadah helps us tell this foundational story? Join Rabbi Amy Wallk and Cantor David Lipp as they share some of their favorite teachings and music that you can bring into your own Passover seder this year!

videos.title: Chag Sameach from Melton!
videos.description:
A Passover message from one of our teachers, Cantor David Lipp.


For the full song, click here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ElxCwnUBW9E

videos.title: Week 2-Binding Isaac: Blind Obedience vs. Thoughtful Choice
videos.description:
Recorded March 29, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
The Binding of Isaac is one of the most famous and controversial stories in the Torah. As parents, children, and human beings, this story is at times both harrowing and confusing, often leaving us with more questions than answers. Did the binding of Isaac begin with a command or with a request? Why did Abraham not protest when he had just protested the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah? In this special, two-part course we will use the biblical texts as our foundation and explore the varied commentaries that speak to this challenging moment. Join skilled Melton educator, Rabbi Michele Faudem, in this thought-provoking exploration of one of our toughest tales.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: The State of Israel as a Jewish and Democratic State
videos.description:
Recorded March 28, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz
It is commonly thought that Jewish and democratic values are one and the same; however, current events in Israel and throughout the Western World are uncovering some uncomfortable truths that are indicating otherwise. Join Melton’s International Director, Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz, as he guides us through the study of some important, provocative foundational Jewish texts that will shed light on Israel's new reality.

videos.title: Yiddish: Wanted Dead or Alive
videos.description:
Recorded March 20, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Rachel Seelig
Upon receiving the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1978, Isaac Bashevis Singer commented: “I like to write ghost stories and nothing fits a ghost better than a dying language. The deader the language the more alive is the ghost.” While receiving this literary honor over four decades ago, Singer expressed the view that Yiddish, the language he wrote in, was in decline. And yet, today we are seeing a resurgence of this supposedly dead language! In this exciting one-session class by Yiddish historical expert Dr. Rachel Seelig, we explore the fate of Yiddish culture since World War II. Did Yiddish culture survive after the Holocaust? Where? Who was responsible for sustaining it? And how do we understand the renewed interest in Yiddish today? Join us as we explore these questions and more in this fascinating new Melton class.

Instructors: Rabbi Amy Wallk and Cantor David Lipp
Passover is one of the most observed Jewish holidays around the world centered around one of the greatest stories ever told, the Exodus from Egypt. We retell this story using the Haggadah, which even translates to “The Telling”. So, what exactly is the story we are supposed to tell on the eve of Passover? How are we meant to go about telling it? How do the tunes and the songs that accompany the telling contribute to the story? And what are the many different ways the Haggadah helps us tell this foundational story? Join Rabbi Amy Wallk and Cantor David Lipp as they share some of their favorite teachings and music that you can bring into your own Passover seder this year!

videos.title: Week 4-Missed Connections: Linking the Haftarah to a Fresh Start
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded March 8, 2023
Instructor: Kira Sirote
Jewish life presents us with many opportunities for personal and national renewal. One of those opportunities occurs during the weeks leading up to Passover. During this period of time, we have a series of special Torah and Haftarah readings (from the books of the Prophets) that explore themes such as how society values money, what makes a war worth fighting for, the importance of leadership, and more. Join Haftarah expert Kira Sirote, as we discover the connections between these special Biblical readings that teach us about our tradition’s high expectations of the society in which we live. This societal roadmap lays out an urgent new perspective on this season of national renewal.

videos.title: Week 4-Perfect Pitch: Music’s Role in Jewish History
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded March 2, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
The Jewish people have a rich history of music playing a vital role in our celebrations, prayers, the way we communicate with one another, and even our mourning rituals. In this 4-week course taught by Melton-favorite Rabbi Johnny Solomon, participants learn about song and music throughout different historical periods, in the Synagogue, in the Jewish homes, and in other Jewish communal settings. Throughout this series we ask significant questions such as: What makes Jewish music Jewish? What role does music play in times of joy and sorrow? How can we harness the power of music to bring greater joy and unity to the Jewish people and to the world? Through the exploration of our musical past, we will gain new insights into the shaping of Jewish culture today.

videos.title: What Makes Jewish Art Jewish?.mp4
videos.description:
Recorded February 21, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Linda Sandhaus
Jewish art as a distinct category of art has been discussed, embraced, and even denied by Jewish artists and art scholars for the last century. What are the features that define “Jewish art”? Is it Jewish if the artist is Jewish? Can it be considered Jewish if the subject matter is Jewish but not the artist? And if a Jewish artist creates something about secular or even non-Jewish religious ideas, is it still “Jewish art”? Join scholar Dr. Linda Sandhaus, as we get to the root of these questions and their implications. Viewing selected artworks to explore this issue, participants will reflect upon the relevance of Jewish art today and its role in preserving a lasting Jewish tomorrow.

videos.title: Jewish Italy-An Informational Session
videos.description:
Recorded February 9, 2023
Instructor: Professor Marc Michael Epstein
Vassar Scholar, National Jewish Book Award winner and Director of Beit Venezia, home of International Jewish Studies in Venice, Italy, Professor Marc Epstein discusses the highlights to be explored on this unique educational travel experience in northern and central Italy. Included in our trip will be the magic of the visual art, Medieval and Renaissance culture, and the often turbulent relationship between Jews, Christians and Muslims.

videos.title: A Musical, Mystical, and Mouthwatering Indian Tu B’Shvat Seder
videos.description:
Recorded February 6, 2023
Instructor: Rahel Musleah
Tu B'Shvat is a joyous holiday with many varied traditions surrounding it. This year, Melton is proud to welcome award-winning journalist Rahel Musleah to teach us about Indian-Jewish customs around Tu B’Shvat. In this one-time offering, Rahel teaches us about the mystical “Tob Shevat” seder based on the kabbalistic book Pri Etz Hadar and about the malida, a ritual of thanks unique to the Bene Israel community of India held on Tu B'Shvat and other occasions. Join us and enjoy spirited and lyrical Baghdadi and Indian-Jewish melodies to enrich your own celebrations.

videos.title: Week 4-A Prophetic Journey: Exploring the Balance Between Fate and Free Will
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded February 2, 2023
Instructor: Lynne Lieberman
In Jewish tradition, the Hebrew prophets are the mouthpieces of God. In modern day life, visionaries are like prophets. Past or present, each offers us a glimpse into a world that could be. They point us toward the same question: To what extent are our lives bound by fate or determined by the choices we freely make. From Isaiah to Martin Luther King, Jr. and Rabbi Abraham Joshua Heschel, we find contemporary meaning in the teachings of prophets, both Biblical and modern. Join expert Melton educator, Lynne Lieberman, to discover how the prophets’ messages move us from ignorance to truth, from injustice to justice, and from despair to joy.

videos.title: Week 4-Living Forward and Becoming Our Elders
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded February 1, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Richard F. Address
Jewish texts and ideas provide a significant amount of support and guidance as we enter our later life stages. In this meaningful new course with Rabbi Richard Address, Founder and Director of Jewish Sacred Aging, we examine the power and passion of our tradition as it speaks to changes that we all experience as we age. We examine Jewish approaches to caregiving, physical and mental health, and discover new rituals designed for this life stage. Through challenging conversations and ideas, Rabbi Address aims to provide a roadmap for navigating this stage of life using Jewish traditions, texts, and experiences. We hope you’ll join us for this important learning opportunity.

videos.title: Fake News: Jewish vs. American Perspectives on Lying, Libel, and the Law
videos.description:
Recorded January 29, 2023
Instructor: Harmon Grossman
How we value the truth says something about our communal culture.  Jewish teachings place a very different value on truth than American law does. In discussing this difference, we must start with one key question: Is it ever okay to lie? Once we grapple with the answers to that question, we inherently find ourselves asking more: What other values might outweigh the value of telling the truth? How seriously do we take the truth as Americans? As Jews? American and Jewish legal scholar, Harman Grossman, explores with us American and Jewish perspectives on the value of truth-telling and consider how these perspectives affect libel law in the American and Jewish legal systems.

videos.title: Cracking the Code: How to Read a Jewish Tombstone
videos.description:
Recorded January 24, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Dan Oren
Hebrew language on Jewish tombstones often provides important insights into personal and family history. Some tombstones are true works of verbal and visual artistry which can provide useful information. This interactive "how to" presentation, taught by Yale University professor Dr. Dan Oren, focuses on decoding common elements on Hebrew tombstones. These elements include looking for Hebrew names, dates, and phrases used on the tombstones. And traditional Jewish tombstone art will be examined as elements of genealogical information. Learners who have at least some familiarity with the Hebrew alphabet will feel most comfortable.
