videos.title: Animal Welfare, Not Warfare: Reconciling Human Needs with Our World’s Stability
videos.description:
Recorded January 9, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Jonathan Bernhard
Living on this planet involves being a part of something bigger than ourselves. The way we engage with Earth’s other living inhabitants has a profound impact on the world as a whole. Jewish tradition has a deep understanding of our obligations to the animals with whom we share this land. In this special one-hour program taught by Rabbi Jonathan Bernhard (Executive Director of Jewish Initiative for Animals), we explore specific views on animal rights as well as a broader framework for understanding our place as human beings in this world.

videos.title: Friendship as a Jewish Priority
videos.description:
Recorded January 5, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Rachel Bovitz
As we begin the secular new year and continue emerging from the shadow of the pandemic, many of us are reflecting on what we lost, what we gained, and, maybe most importantly, what we want to prioritize in our lives going forward. For most of us, relationships often rise to the top of that list of priorities. In this special, one-time, text-study class taught by Melton’s Executive Director, Rabbi Rachel Bovitz, we look at how our rabbis understood friendship and consider what wisdom we can glean from their writings.

videos.title: Melton Governance Committee - 21-Dec-2022
Week 4-Beyond the Ashkenorm
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded December 21, 2022
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
In many Jewish circles, conversations focus mostly, if not entirely, on Ashkenazic ideas and customs, leaving out the rich culture of so many Jews who trace their heritage to other parts of the world. This course, taught by Sephardic rabbi, Rabbi Johnny Solomon, seeks to explore the history, attitudes, customs, and culture of the Spanish and Portuguese (Western) Sephardic community, the Iraqi/Indian (Oriental) Sephardic communities, the Moroccan Sephardic community, and the Beta Israel Ethiopian Jewish community. Learners will listen to the songs of these communities, learn about their unique values, find out about their special foods, and consider how their communities have adapted since immigrating to Israel, the United States, and elsewhere in the world. Join us on this journey to expand our understanding of global Jewry.

videos.title: Invite to Between the Lines
Week 4-Jewish Ethical Wills: How Will You Be Remembered?
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded December 20, 2022
Instructor: Dr. Lesley Simpson
Everyone has a story, and how we preserve our stories can ensure that they inspire future generations. This writing workshop offers an introduction to writing your own ethical will, or legacy letter, which is a little-known but important, centuries-old Jewish tradition of letter writing. Melton Online teacher Dr. Lesley Simpson will share examples of ethical wills and help you begin to write your own letter in a collaborative learning and writing space. The four-week course is designed for active participation so come ready to write!

videos.title: Judith the Chanukah Heroine
videos.description:
Recorded December 19, 2022
Instructor: Sivan Rotholz
Too scandalous for the Jewish canon, considered the world's first work of historical fiction, and saved from obscurity by early Christians, the Book of Judith contains one of the most riveting tales of Jewish literature. A wise widow, a cunning temptress, and a fierce warrior, Judith is a hero to rival Queen Esther and a femme fatale of Delilah caliber. But what does Judith's story have to do with Chanukah, and why on earth are we supposed to eat cheese on the holiday of fried foods in her honor? Join us for this one-of-a-kind Chanukah learning to discover Judith’s story and its message for today.

Faculty Online Orientation Module 1 - Basic Elements of the Melton Experience
Week 4-Clickable Wisdom: Using Sefaria’s Digital Library to Uncover Everyday Ethical Teachings
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded December 5, 2022
Rabbi Ben Kramer
How is a Jewish idea transmitted and transformed over the course of generations? Judaism has many big ideas about justice and the ideals that shape an ethical and just life. In this four-part course you will explore significant issues of the human experience, their development over the generations, and their everyday applications to modern life. The four topics covered include the right and responsibility of privacy; complicity and bad advice; verbal abuse; and the boundaries of competition. This Melton Online course was developed in partnership with Sefaria, a living library of Jewish texts. In each lesson, learners will join their teacher in utilizing Sefaria to access the texts and trace the development of Jewish ideas from the Bible through the Talmud to the present. This course welcomes anyone curious in the topic and/or interested in exploring this rich online resource. We are proud to offer this course again after a successful run earlier in the year under the title “A Melton - Sefaria Learning Experience Tzedek, Tzedek: Judaism's Pursuit of Everyday Justice”.

videos.title: Cook Along Live: Latke Making with a Sprinkle of History
videos.description:
Recorded December 1, 2022
Instructor: Ilana Epstein
In appreciation of the Melton School's generous donors and in preparation to celebrate Hanukkah, Ilana Epstein teaches us how to make latkes. Ilana is the founder of Ta'am, an organization that engages people through Jewish food, education, and connectivity. Rebbetzen Ilana is also one half of the Rabbinic Team at Cockfosters and N Southgate Synagogue in London, where, together with husband, Rabbi Daniel Epstein, she has been since June 2014.

videos.title: Week 1-Beyond the Ashkenorm
videos.description:
Week 1
Recorded November 30, 2022
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
In many Jewish circles, conversations focus mostly, if not entirely, on Ashkenazic ideas and customs, leaving out the rich culture of so many Jews who trace their heritage to other parts of the world. This course, taught by Sephardic rabbi, Rabbi Johnny Solomon, seeks to explore the history, attitudes, customs, and culture of the Spanish and Portuguese (Western) Sephardic community, the Iraqi/Indian (Oriental) Sephardic communities, the Moroccan Sephardic community, and the Beta Israel Ethiopian Jewish community. Learners will listen to the songs of these communities, learn about their unique values, find out about their special foods, and consider how their communities have adapted since immigrating to Israel, the United States, and elsewhere in the world. Join us on this journey to expand our understanding of global Jewry.

videos.title: Week 1-Jewish Ethical Wills: How Will You Be Remembered?
videos.description:
Week 1
Recorded November 29, 2022
Instructor: Dr. Lesley Simpson
Everyone has a story, and how we preserve our stories can ensure that they inspire future generations. This writing workshop offers an introduction to writing your own ethical will, or legacy letter, which is a little-known but important, centuries-old Jewish tradition of letter writing. Melton Online teacher Dr. Lesley Simpson will share examples of ethical wills and help you begin to write your own letter in a collaborative learning and writing space. The four-week course is designed for active participation so come ready to write!

videos.title: Week 3-Clickable Wisdom: Using Sefaria’s Digital Library to Uncover Everyday Ethical Teachings
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded November 28, 2022
Rabbi Ben Kramer
How is a Jewish idea transmitted and transformed over the course of generations? Judaism has many big ideas about justice and the ideals that shape an ethical and just life. In this four-part course you will explore significant issues of the human experience, their development over the generations, and their everyday applications to modern life. The four topics covered include the right and responsibility of privacy; complicity and bad advice; verbal abuse; and the boundaries of competition. This Melton Online course was developed in partnership with Sefaria, a living library of Jewish texts. In each lesson, learners will join their teacher in utilizing Sefaria to access the texts and trace the development of Jewish ideas from the Bible through the Talmud to the present. This course welcomes anyone curious in the topic and/or interested in exploring this rich online resource. We are proud to offer this course again after a successful run earlier in the year under the title “A Melton - Sefaria Learning Experience Tzedek, Tzedek: Judaism's Pursuit of Everyday Justice”.

videos.title: Week 2-Drawing Us In: Jewish Art from Ancient Works Through the 20th Century
videos.description:
Week2
Recorded November 17, 2022
Instructor: Dr. Sara Aroeste
Jewish art has a long and storied history. How long has Jewish art existed? What styles and themes have influenced Jewish artists throughout the centuries? How do we distinguish “Jewish art”? These questions and many more will be explored in this captivating two-part class presented by art history expert Dr. Sara Aroeste. Part one will cover Jewish art history, beginning with ancient works and taking us through 18th century Jewish artists and the themes of their works. Part two will cover art from the 20th century, focusing on the avant-garde Jewish artists, and the ways in which society both accepted and rejected their works. Join us for this unique adventure as we journey through the kaleidoscope of Jewish art history.

videos.title: No Place Like Home: Immigration Through the Eyes of Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks z"l
videos.description:
Recorded November 14, 2022
Instructor: Lynne Lieberman
Humankind has been on the move for a long time. We now know that all humans can trace their origins to Southern Africa. Migration is our shared human experience and usually involves facing great risk and uncertainty. In this class, we explore three parables offered by the late Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks in his book, The Home We Build Together, to think about how societies may integrate newcomers and how newcomers may develop a sense of belonging to the places they have migrated.

videos.title: Week 4-A Different Moral to Our Stories
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded November 9, 2022
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
The Torah opens with four classic stories: Cain and Abel, the Tower of Babel, the fate of Sarah’s handmaiden, Hagar, and the story of Sodom and Gemorah. Some of these narratives are quite troubling, some downright disturbing. They all raise important questions for consideration. For example, can we view Cain and Abel as a story of brotherly love as opposed to brotherly disdain? Or, what does the story of Babel tell us about community and our engagement with technology? In this course, Rabbi Michele Faudem helps us think outside of the box when looking at these fundamental Jewish stories.

videos.title: Judaism, Democracy + Citizenship.mov
videos.description:
Recorded November 8, 2022
Instructor: Rabbi Amy S. Wallk
This class aims to help us navigate today's highly polarizing and tense moment in USA history by engaging the wisdom of Jewish teachings. How does Judaism inform our civic behaviors? How can Jewish sources inform and influence our efforts to support a well-functioning American democracy? Join us as we explore these questions and more.

videos.title: Week 4 - Jewish Perspectives on the Ballot: Key Issues in the U.S. Midterms Using a Rabbinic Lens
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded November 3, 2022
Instructor: Rabbi Deborah Silver
Emotions are high as we approach the U.S. midterm elections. When we exercise our right to vote, we rely on information from various sources in making our decisions, which may or may not include Jewish wisdom. This series will tackle four contentious topics, looking at how various streams of Jewish thought shed light on these issues. The aim is not to persuade anyone to vote in a particular way but rather to broaden our perspectives around these issues using Jewish lenses. Explore the challenging topics of gun control, abortion, climate change, and civil discourse.

videos.title: Oh Brother: Cain, Abel, and Sibling Rivalry
videos.description:
Recorded October 24, 2022
Instructor: Shira Zeliger
Sibling dynamics can be challenging. Nowhere is that clearer than in the Bible. In this class we take a close look at the story of Cain and Abel - the Bible’s first and quite extreme example of sibling rivalry. We discuss the differences between Cain and Abel and what really led to Abel's murder. Finally, we look at this case study of sibling rivalry through the lens of classical and modern Bible commentaries, using it as a launching point to explore similar relationships throughout the Bible. Adding our own insights and experiences, we consider how the lessons from these stories apply to our lives.

videos.title: Once Upon a Time in Mexico: A Glimpse at Mexican Jewish History
videos.description:
Recorded October 13, 2022
Instructor: Dr. Sara Aroeste

Jewish journeys throughout history have involved many stops and detours in many different countries, and Mexico is no exception. Whether by choice or persecution, the Jewish people have had to establish new communities and make new homes for themselves around the world. In this class we will follow the Jewish journeys that brought different waves of emigration to Mexico and contributed to today’s vibrant Mexican-Jewish community. Dr. Sara Aroeste has spent the last twenty years bringing her contemporary vision for Sephardic culture to audiences around the world. Explore the fascinating history of Jews in Mexico covering Crypto-Jews, 16th century Spain and Portugal, 20th century immigrant arrivals, and more.

יתומי צה״ל שרים לאבא - ׳ניפגש בחלומות׳ בביצוע משותף עם עילי בוטנר וילדי החוץ.mp4
Growing Older, Living Longer: Four Values that Support our Elder Life Stages
videos.description:
Recorded September 22, 2022
Instructor: Rabbi Richard Address
According to recent studies, Jews over 50 years old make up over 40% of our community. As we grow older, we face new challenges both physically and spiritually. New needs arise and new expectations are set. Join this innovative class with Rabbi Richard Address (Founder and Director of Jewish Sacred Aging) in which we will examine the Jewish values of health, healing, wholeness, and holiness. These values combine to create a theology of relationships and growth that support our move into later life stages. Using Torah study and spiritual practice, Rabbi Address guides students through growing older and wiser in the Jewish tradition.

videos.title: Can a Monk Be Considered a Jew: Examining the Israeli Supreme Court "Brother Daniel" Case
videos.description:
Recorded September 19, 2022
Instructor: Dr. Joseph Ringel 
In 1962, the Israeli Supreme Court heard one of its most significant cases in its history: A Catholic monk who had converted from Judaism to Christianity applied to become a citizen of the State of Israel under the Law of Return, which guarantees the right of immigration and immediate citizenship to  Jews world-wide. Known as “Brother Daniel,” this monk claimed that his being born Jewish and his previous activities in the Jewish community of his youth qualified him as a Jew by nationality, even if not a Jew by religion. The case pitted traditional halakhic understandings of “Who is a Jew” against popular conceptions and opened up the possibility of a secular definition of who is Jewish. It also pitted “Western vs Eastern” understandings of religion and belonging. Dr. Joseph Ringel, former professor at Northwestern University, helps us analyze this controversial decision and explores how it reflects deeper cultural tensions that affect Jews around the world to this day.

videos.title: Week 4-A Tale of Two Cities: 4 Virtual Journeys Uncovering Prague and Berlin
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded September 14, 2022
Instructor: Haim Aronovitz
Explore some of the unique elements of this heritage journey with you. Join us as we walk through Josefov, Prague's historic Jewish Quarter, visit the Theresienstadt’s “camp-ghetto” and labor camp, and learn about its important propaganda function for the Germans. In Berlin, we’ll take a deep dive into the complex aspects of what took the city from capital of the 2nd Reich to its current status as an international center of multiculturalism. Don’t miss out on this moving journey through Central-European Jewish history.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: Israel’s Reproductive Rights in the Context of a Post-Roe World
videos.description:
Recorded July 25, 2022
Instructor: Dina Shalev
With the recent SCOTUS decision overturning Roe vs. Wade, reproductive rights are at the forefront of political and legal discussions in the United States. While this topic has been debated for decades in America, the conversations in the Jewish context have been going on for centuries. The State of Israel’s take on this issue differs greatly from the American perspective but is no less controversial. To explore this important issue, we’ve invited Dina Shalev, Director of Lada’at (Choose Well) to share her expertise with us. Lada’at is a Jerusalem based organization dedicated to promoting the reproductive health and rights of women and girls in Israel by addressing the root causes that result in a lack of reproductive health services and infringement on reproductive rights. We hope you are able to join us for this timely program.

videos.title: Love the Stranger: JCC Krakow’s Invitation to Ukrainian Refugees
videos.description:
Recorded July 21, 2022
Instructors: Jonathan Ornstein, Rabbi Avi Baumol, Tamar Jacoby
Introduction: Randi Brenowitz, President
The Torah teaches us: “Love the stranger because you were once strangers in the land of Egypt.” As we witness the atrocities executed by Russia on Ukraine, many of us find ourselves asking the question, “What can I do?” The JCC in Krakow has taken that question and turned it into meaningful action, inviting in Ukrainian refugees and welcoming them into their community. Join Jonathan Ornstein (JCC Krakow Executive Director), Rabbi Avi Baumol (Rabbinic Representative of the Chief Rabbi of Poland in Krakow), and Tamar Jacoby (president of Opportunity America and regular contributor to the Wall Street Journal) as they discuss the Jewish significance of their work with Ukrainian refugees in the Krakow Jewish community.

videos.title: Organizing a Lifetime of Memories
videos.description: Over the course of our lives, we accumulate a trove of objects, experiences and memories that define our stories for generations to come. Sorting through those memories, whether they belong to you or a loved one that is no longer with us, can be a daunting task. Join Roslyn Sholin, associate member of the Council of American Jewish Museums, as she guides us through this intimidating task. Recently facing a similar challenge when she inherited a family home, Roslyn faced a lifetime of memories stashed in shoeboxes, cartons, storage lockers and a spare hall closet.  She will guide us on ways to manage this process and create a lasting tribute to your legacy and the legacies of your loved ones.

videos.title: Week 4 - Ethics of the Moment
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded July 12, 2022
Rabbi Michele Faudem
We live in challenging and confusing times when making the right decision often feels impossible. How often do we have the opportunity to have in-depth conversations about the difficult choices we face in our world today? How does our modern way of living affect our moral obligation to take care of low-income workers? When should we utilize modern medicine to ease loved ones’ suffering? Where do the goals of marriage and the institution of marriage intersect? How do we reconcile our “dominion over every living thing” (Genesis 1:28) with the growing impact of climate change? Join Rabbi Michele Faudem and explore these questions with meaningful and enlightening discussion about these topics and more in this timely new class.

videos.title: Hebrew Connections: Hebrew As The Bridge Between Old and New
videos.description:
Dr. Jeremy Benstein
Recorded June 1, 2022
There is a tension in the Hebrew language between ancient sacred texts and up to the minute daily conversation. But that tension can be a bridge: Hebrew connects the Jewish community across space and time and embodies deep values of peoplehood and identity. Explore Hebrew roots as Jewish concepts, showing how they embody Jewish ideas in both ancient and contemporary usage, binding a people, its language and history together. No knowledge of Hebrew is required.

videos.title: Week 4-The Sinai Revolution: The Inner Workings of Jewish Texts
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded May 18, 2022
Rabbi Dr. Danny Schiff
What was revealed at Sinai and how do other canons of Jewish literature fit in to our story? Join Rabbi Schiff in the weeks leading up to Shavuot as he explores the structure of Jewish texts, beginning with Torah and continuing through contemporary times.  The role of Torah, Tanakh, and Talmud, will be examined to gain a deeper understanding of Jewish law and lore.  As Shavuot approaches, we will take an up close look at the moment of revelation and the texts that flowed from that wondrous encounter.

videos.title: Judaica Roadshow: Passover Edition
videos.description: Join Joellyn Zollman, Ph.D for a curated conversation about our most treasured Jewish objects. Dr. Zollman will use an Antiques Roadshow-style approach to explore how our own Jewish art and artifacts can be legitimate, articulate sources for understanding American Jewish history and identity. Having worked with the Judaica collections at the Smithsonian, the Skirball Museum, and the American Jewish Historical Society, Dr. Zollman can't wait to see the Judaica treasures that come out of your closets. Together, we will consider how these objects connect to Jewish history, Jewish memory, and the creation of Jewish life in America.

Week 4 Israel and Diaspora-Frayed Relations Recorded Mar. 31, 2022 
Yael Weinstein
videos.description: From the birth of the Jewish state until today there has been a widening gap between the Jewish communities of the diaspora and Israel. Discover the human voices that are represented between the two pillars of the Jewish population. Explore where are the differences, what are the similarities, and how the Israeli and Diaspora Jewish societies are coping with the challenges of being one Jewish people with different experiences.

videos.title: Getting (Un)Comfortable with Megillat Esther
videos.description:
Recorded March 14, 2022
Instructor: Rabbi Daniel Reifman, PhD
The book of Esther is one of the best known narratives in the Bible, the core of one of the most popular holidays on the Jewish calendar.  Yet a closer look at this familiar story reveals some unsettling elements, among them the Persian culture that pervades the book and the uncomfortable position that the Jewish characters occupy within it.  What does Esther suggest about Jewish peoplehood in the Diaspora and how does this reflect on Jewish identity in our own time?

videos.title: Superman vs Spiderman: Who is the REAL Jewish Superhero?
videos.description:
Inspirational Insights with Rabbi Neil Zuckerman of Park Avenue Synagogue, NYC

Says Rabbi Zuckerman: "I have long believed that Superman (DC Comics) was the real Jewish superhero. But, recently I read a biography on Stan Lee that has radically changed my thinking. The stories created by Marvel Comics are important cultural documents that say a great deal about those who created them and the times in which they lived. Together we will look at some fundamental Jewish content reflected in Lee’s creations."

Recorded 4 June 2020

Cairo Genizah Curriculum Webinar
videos.description:
Curriculum author and former Melton Director, Dr. Shelley Buxbaum, in conversation with Melton’s Educational Project Coordinator Judy Snowbell Diamond "Exploring a Forgotten World" reveals how a collection of worn-out scraps, fragments of texts and other manuscripts and documents more than 1,000 years old open an otherwise hidden era of history. It explores the daily lives and behaviors of Jewish men, women and families living along the Mediterranean during the middle ages.
 
Recorded 14 November 2019

showcases.title: 30 Years After the Death of the “Rebbe”: An Inside Look at Chabad

videos.title: Week 4-30 Years After the Death of the "Rebbe": An Inside Look at Chabad
videos.description:
Recorded September 26, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon 
Most Jews today are no longer surprised to find a Chabad house in virtually every community around the world, even in the most remote corners of the earth. This massive movement just commemorated the 30th yahrzeit of the Lubavitcher Rebbe, Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson. In this four-part course with esteemed Melton teacher Rabbi Johnny Solomon, we will take a closer look at the ubiquitous Chabad movement: What makes Chabad unique as a Chassidic movement? What are its goals? How did it survive the death of its leader? What can we learn about its strengths? And what might we see as its weaknesses? Join us for this in-depth look at this fascinating movement.

showcases.title: A Different Moral to Our Stories

videos.title: Week 4-A Different Moral to Our Stories
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded November 9, 2022
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
The Torah opens with four classic stories: Cain and Abel, the Tower of Babel, the fate of Sarah’s handmaiden, Hagar, and the story of Sodom and Gemorah. Some of these narratives are quite troubling, some downright disturbing. They all raise important questions for consideration. For example, can we view Cain and Abel as a story of brotherly love as opposed to brotherly disdain? Or, what does the story of Babel tell us about community and our engagement with technology? In this course, Rabbi Michele Faudem helps us think outside of the box when looking at these fundamental Jewish stories.

showcases.title: A Prophetic Journey: Exploring the Balance Between Fate and Free Will

videos.title: Week 4-A Prophetic Journey: Exploring the Balance Between Fate and Free Will
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded February 2, 2023
Instructor: Lynne Lieberman
In Jewish tradition, the Hebrew prophets are the mouthpieces of God. In modern day life, visionaries are like prophets. Past or present, each offers us a glimpse into a world that could be. They point us toward the same question: To what extent are our lives bound by fate or determined by the choices we freely make. From Isaiah to Martin Luther King, Jr. and Rabbi Abraham Joshua Heschel, we find contemporary meaning in the teachings of prophets, both Biblical and modern. Join expert Melton educator, Lynne Lieberman, to discover how the prophets’ messages move us from ignorance to truth, from injustice to justice, and from despair to joy.

showcases.title: A Spiritual Pathway for Aging into the Year Ahead

videos.title: Week 4-A Spiritual Pathway for Aging into the Year Ahead
videos.description:
Recorded August 8, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Richard Address
Jewish texts and ideas provide a significant amount of support and guidance as we enter our third and fourth life stages. In this meaningful new course with Rabbi Richard Address, Founder and Director of Jewish Sacred Aging, we examine a series of Judaism’s most fundamental teachings as we prepare for the High Holidays and the year ahead. Rabbi Address engages learners in a dialogue with classical and contemporary Jewish thought to create a spiritual roadmap for the year ahead.

showcases.title: A Tale of Two Cities: 4 Virtual Journeys Uncovering Prague and Berlin

videos.title: Week 4-A Tale of Two Cities: 4 Virtual Journeys Uncovering Prague and Berlin
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded September 14, 2022
Instructor: Haim Aronovitz
Explore some of the unique elements of this heritage journey with you. Join us as we walk through Josefov, Prague's historic Jewish Quarter, visit the Theresienstadt’s “camp-ghetto” and labor camp, and learn about its important propaganda function for the Germans. In Berlin, we’ll take a deep dive into the complex aspects of what took the city from capital of the 2nd Reich to its current status as an international center of multiculturalism. Don’t miss out on this moving journey through Central-European Jewish history.

showcases.title: A View From the Pulpit: A Behind-the-Scenes Tour of High Holiday Prayers and Music

videos.title: Week 6 A View From the Pulpit: A Behind-the-Scenes Tour of High Holiday Prayers and Music
videos.description:
Week 6
Recorded September 20, 2022
Co-taught by Rabbi Amy Wallk and Cantor David Lipp
A synagogue’s High Holiday experience is months in the making. Great thought is put into crafting services intended to inspire us with the multi-faceted aspects of these holy days. In this course, join Rabbi Amy Wallk and Cantor David Lipp as they talk about the important themes they consider when preparing High Holiday and Sukkot/Simchat Torah services for their congregations.

showcases.title: Abraham on Trial

videos.title: Week 4-Abraham on Trial
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded September 7, 2023
Instructor: Lynne Lieberman
According to the bible, our forefather Abraham is asked to make the ultimate sacrifice: to slaughter his beloved son Isaac atop Mt. Moriah. His faith in God seems to know no boundaries as he carries out God’s order, only to be stopped at the last minute by a ministering angel. Rather than resist this commandment, and thereby model a rejection of human sacrifice, he goes through with it and is revered throughout history for his unbendable faith! This exploration of the Akedah (binding), or near sacrifice of Isaac, investigates the subsequent and continuing reverberations of this controversial biblical story. Join expert Melton teacher Lynne Lieberman as she guides us through texts that reveal the impact that this narrative has had on western society throughout history to the present day.

showcases.title: After October 7: Heroism, Healing, and Hope

videos.title: Week 3-After October 7: Heroism, Healing, and Hope
videos.description:
Recorded March 25, 2025
Instructor: Avi Frier
When history unfolds in tragedy and resilience, the most powerful voices are those who lived it. This compelling three-part series brings you first-hand accounts from individuals whose lives were forever changed by the events of October 7 and its aftermath. Through personal testimony, we will explore themes of heroism, loss, survival, and the power of rebuilding in the face of destruction. Each session highlights a different perspective—from the battlefield, from a kibbutz on the front lines, and from an effort to restore hope through the magic of human connection.
Session 1: Strength in the South: Rebuilding Kerem Shalom - Amit Caspi, a 25-year resident of Kerem Shalom, tells the story of this extraordinary kibbutz—one where religious and secular Israelis forged a shared life together. He recounts the heroism of his community on October 7 and their ongoing efforts to rebuild in the wake of destruction.
Session 2: Service and Sacrifice: The Heroic Life and Death of Maoz Morell - Parents Eitan and Varda Morell share the story of their son, Maoz, an IDF soldier who fell in Gaza while defending Israel. Through the lens of three generations of Jewish survival, they recount his life of heroism, his sacrifice, and the legacy he leaves behind.
Session 3: Making Smiles Appear: Magic4Israel - American entrepreneur, entertainer, and educator Avi Frier turned despair into action, launching Magic4Israel in early 2024. Through this initiative, he and his team have been “making smiles appear” for soldiers and displaced families across Israel, proving that sometimes, even the smallest gestures can restore hope.

This series is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

showcases.title: All in the Family: Torah’s Take on Family Relationships

videos.title: Week 4-All in the Family: Torah's Take on Family Relationships
videos.description:
Recorded May 29, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
Family dynamics can be fraught with complexity and tension. Even the people we love most in the world can frustrate us as much as they bring joy to our lives. This is not unique to the modern era. In this insightful new course from acclaimed Melton teacher and course author Dr. Sandra Lilienthal, we will explore significant family relationships in the Torah, using them as a mirror with which to examine our own relationships. Whether it’s the relationship between a husband and wife, a brother and sister, or the most treacherous “in-laws”, the Torah is packed with complex interactions that can shed new light on our own lives. Join us as we go all in on family.

showcases.title: An Ethical Checkup: Jewish Views on Modern Medical Issues

videos.title: Week 4-An Ethical Checkup: Jewish Views on Modern Medical Issues
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded December 20, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
The world of medicine is constantly evolving and with it so are our questions. From seemingly small-scale issues of confidentiality to larger, more complex questions around altering DNA before conception, Jewish law has a wealth of information and perspectives to help us consider some of the most complex questions. Join expert Melton educator Rabbi Michele Faudem for this four-part series exploring how Jewish law views privacy, how we determine who gets what medical treatment when resources are scarce, autopsies, and gene manipulation before birth.

showcases.title: Ask the Rabbis: What's Next for North American Judaism

videos.title: Ask the Rabbis: What’s Next for North American Judaism with Rabbi Sari Laufer
videos.description:
Recorded May 27, 2026
Instructors: Rabbi Sari Laufer and Rabbi Rachel Bovitz
What does meaningful Jewish engagement look like today, and how is it changing? As North American Jewish life continues to evolve, communities are rethinking not only what they offer but also how they connect.

In this ongoing Melton+ series, Rabbi Rachel Bovitz sits down with leading rabbinic voices for candid, wide-ranging conversations about the state of Jewish life today. In this session, she is joined by Rabbi Sari Laufer, Chief Engagement Officer at Stephen Wise Temple in Los Angeles, whose work focuses on building relationships, fostering belonging, and reimagining how individuals and families connect to Jewish community. Together, they explore the challenges and opportunities shaping contemporary Jewish life, from new models of engagement and participation to the evolving role of synagogue leadership. Drawing on Rabbi Laufer’s experience, this conversation offers an inside look at how communities are adapting and what it might mean for your own Jewish journey. Part of an ongoing series of conversations with rabbinic leaders across North America, this session invites you to listen in, reflect, and consider how these insights might shape your own connection to Jewish life.

videos.title: Ask the Rabbis: What’s Next for North American Judaism with Rabbi Efrem Goldberg
videos.description:
Recorded March 5, 2026
Instructors: Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz and Rabbi Efrem Goldberg
What’s really happening in American Jewish life, and where are we headed?  In this ground-breaking series, Melton+ faculty sit down with some of the most prominent and influential congregational rabbis in North America for candid, wide-ranging conversations about the challenges and possibilities facing Jewish communities today. From synagogue life and spiritual leadership to shifting demographics and new models of connection, no topic is off the table.  We have run one session every other month, beginning in November 2025. Our first guest was Rabbi David Wolpe, the Max Webb Rabbi Emeritus at Sinai Temple in Los Angeles, and, in January, Rabbi Rachel Bovitz spoke with Rabbi Robyn Fryer Bodzin of Toronto's Beth Tzedeq, the largest synagogue in North America.

For our third conversation, Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz speaks with Rabbi Efrem Goldberg, the Senior Rabbi of the Boca Raton Synagogue, the largest Orthodox synagogue in the Southeast United States. Serving a vibrant and rapidly growing community of over 1,000 member families and more than 1,000 children, BRS offers expansive programming in adult education, youth engagement, and community outreach, making it a national model for Orthodox communal growth and innovation. Rabbi Goldberg is a nationally respected voice on matters of faith, leadership, and contemporary Jewish life. His weekly “Parsha Perspectives” podcast and the popular interview series “Behind the Bima” reach thousands of listeners across the Jewish spectrum and beyond. He is a frequent contributor to the Times of Israel, and his talks—often addressing spiritual resilience, modern ethics, and Jewish unity—are widely shared across social media, YouTube, and communal forums well beyond the Orthodox world. He also holds several influential leadership roles, serving nationally as an authority on Kashrut, conversion, and U.S.–Israel advocacy.

videos.title: Ask the Rabbis- What's Next for North American Judaism with Rabbi Robyn Fryer Bodzin
videos.description:
Recorded January 13, 2026
Instructors: Rabbi Robyn Fryer Bodzin and Rabbi Rachel Bovitz
What’s really happening in North American Jewish life, and where are we headed? 
In the second installment of this ground-breaking Melton+ series, Melton Executive Director Rabbi Rachel Bovitz will sit down with Rabbi Robyn Fryer Bodzin of Toronto's Congregation Beth Tzedec, one of the largest synagogues in North America. Their candid, wide-ranging conversation will address the challenges and possibilities facing Jewish communities today, from synagogue life and spiritual leadership to shifting demographics and new models of connection. This is the second in a four-part series of interviews with rabbinic leaders of prominent North American congregations. Come for the insight, stay for the honesty.

Recorded November 19, 2025

What’s really happening in American Jewish life, and where are we headed? 
In this new Melton+ series, Rabbi Morey Schwartz will sit down with some of the most prominent and influential congregational rabbis in North America for candid, wide-ranging conversations about the challenges and possibilities facing Jewish communities today. From synagogue life and spiritual leadership to shifting demographics and new models of connection, no topic is off the table.  One session each month, beginning November 2025. Come for the insight, stay for the honesty.  Our first conversation is with Rabbi David Wolpe, the Max Webb Rabbi Emeritus of Sinai Temple in Los Angeles.

showcases.title: Asking Forgiveness

videos.title: Week 2-Asking Forgiveness
videos.description:
Week 2
Recorded September 20, 2023
Instructor: Joe Septimus
Asking forgiveness from people we may have aggrieved is a core practice in Judaism, one that is described in some of our earliest rabbinic literature. The process of forgiveness is particularly emphasized during the high holiday season leading up to Yom Kippur. But as we know, interpersonal issues are complex and often force us to ask some tough questions before we can resolve them. Who started it? Who is to blame? Who should initiate the request for forgiveness? What if I’m not ready to forgive?  What is the objective of pursuing forgiveness? What are our own personal characteristics that cause us to offend others? Join expert Melton teacher Joe Septimus as we explore these issues and more through a thorough exploration of two insightful Talmudic stories.

showcases.title: Ballots and Power: How Israel Chooses Its Government

videos.title: Week 3-Ballots and Power: How Israel Chooses Its Government
videos.description:
Recorded May 11, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Danny Schiff
How does a country without a constitution govern itself? And what really happens when Israelis head to the polls? Israel’s political system is both familiar and entirely unique, shaped by history, ideology, and the ongoing challenge of defining a Jewish and democratic state.

In this three-part course, Rabbi Danny Schiff guides us through the structure, strategy, and stakes of Israeli elections. From the foundational question of how Israel governs without a formal constitution to the complex process of voting and coalition-building, we will explore how power is organized and exercised. We will also turn to the key issues shaping the 2026 elections, asking what matters most to Israeli voters and what those choices mean for Israel’s future.

In this three-part series, we will explore:

Session 1: A Nation Without a Constitution: Why does Israel have no formal constitution? We will examine the development of the Basic Laws, the structure of the Israeli government, and the systems of checks and balances that shape political power.
Session 2: Electing a New Knesset: How do Israeli elections actually work? We will explore the voting system, the strategic decisions voters face, and the complex process of coalition-building that determines who ultimately governs.
Session 3: The Issues at Stake: What will be on the minds of Israeli voters in 2026? We will examine the central issues shaping the election and consider what they reveal about Israel’s identity and future as a Jewish state.

showcases.title: Behind the Music: The Heart of the Jewish Experience

videos.title: Week 3-Behind the Music: The Heart of the Jewish Experience
videos.description:
Recorded May 20, 2024 
Instructor: Cantor Matt Austerklein 
Music is an integral part of the Jewish experience. Through prayer or pop music, singing or dancing, music is at the heart of so many meaningful Jewish experiences. Join beloved Melton teacher Cantor Matt Austerklein for a song-filled journey through the many cultures, styles, and eras of Jewish music. Throughout this exploration, we will grapple with deep questions of how music reflects our experience as a people and how it can help us in defining our own Jewish selves.

showcases.title: Between Temple and Text: Jewish Life in a Time of Transition

videos.title: Week 3-Between Text and Temple: Jewish Life in a Time of Transition
videos.description:
Recorded August 4, 2025
Instructor: Catriella Freedman
What happens when a civilization shifts its spiritual center? In the centuries surrounding both the rebuilding and ultimate destruction of the Second Temple, Jewish life underwent a profound transformation: from sacrifice to study, priest to sage, and prophecy to interpretation. In this three-part course, historian and scholar Catriella Freedmanbrings us into this pivotal era of Jewish history, exploring the political, religious, and philosophical forces that reshaped the Jewish world.

Session 1 - Transmission and Authority in a Post-Temple World: What does it mean to inherit sacred tradition without a central sanctuary? We’ll examine how early rabbinic texts, including the famous “chain of transmission,” reflect efforts to preserve authority and identity after the exile.
Session 2 - Shimon HaTzaddik and the Question of Power: A priest and a sage, Shimon HaTzaddik stood at the intersection of Temple leadership and rabbinic authority. We’ll explore his role—and the broader power dynamics—during the turbulent transition from biblical to rabbinic Judaism.
Session 3 - Theology in a Hellenistic World: How did Jewish thinkers respond to the rise of Greek philosophy and shifting ideas about reward, justice, and divine service? Through the teachings of figures like Antigonos Ish Socho, we’ll consider how new theological frameworks took root in a rapidly changing world.
With clarity, depth, and a gift for making the ancient feel urgent, Catriella Freedman invites learners to explore a defining era of Jewish history that continues to shape the questions we ask and the texts we live with today.

showcases.title: Beyond the Ashkenorm

videos.title: Week 4-Beyond the Ashkenorm
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded December 21, 2022
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
In many Jewish circles, conversations focus mostly, if not entirely, on Ashkenazic ideas and customs, leaving out the rich culture of so many Jews who trace their heritage to other parts of the world. This course, taught by Sephardic rabbi, Rabbi Johnny Solomon, seeks to explore the history, attitudes, customs, and culture of the Spanish and Portuguese (Western) Sephardic community, the Iraqi/Indian (Oriental) Sephardic communities, the Moroccan Sephardic community, and the Beta Israel Ethiopian Jewish community. Learners will listen to the songs of these communities, learn about their unique values, find out about their special foods, and consider how their communities have adapted since immigrating to Israel, the United States, and elsewhere in the world. Join us on this journey to expand our understanding of global Jewry.

showcases.title: Binding Isaac: Blind Obedience vs. Thoughtful Choice

videos.title: Week 2-Binding Isaac: Blind Obedience vs. Thoughtful Choice
videos.description:
Recorded March 29, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
The Binding of Isaac is one of the most famous and controversial stories in the Torah. As parents, children, and human beings, this story is at times both harrowing and confusing, often leaving us with more questions than answers. Did the binding of Isaac begin with a command or with a request? Why did Abraham not protest when he had just protested the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah? In this special, two-part course we will use the biblical texts as our foundation and explore the varied commentaries that speak to this challenging moment. Join skilled Melton educator, Rabbi Michele Faudem, in this thought-provoking exploration of one of our toughest tales.

videos.title: Israeli Music Goes Mizrahi-Melton Supporter Appreciation Event
videos.description:
Recorded June 13, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Galeet Dardashti
Have you ever wondered how Israeli music today got its unique and varied sound? Join us for this not-to-be-missed event just for you – our most dedicated Melton donors. While the music from Israel’s first few decades represented the European tastes of its founders who defined “Israeliness,” by the 1970s Israeli music started changing with the significant influence of musicians of Yemenite, Persian, and North African origins. Join Dr. Dardashti for a musical exploration of the beautiful and diverse musical offerings of the State of Israel.

showcases.title: Books They Never Taught Us

videos.title: Week 3-Books They Never Taught Us
videos.description:
Recorded April 18, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Richard AddressThe Torah is one of the most widely studied books in Jewish life. We return to it year after year, gaining fresh insights each go around. But we don’t focus as much on many of the other books that make up the larger Hebrew Bible, aka the Tanakh? Stories found in Samuel and Kings show us examples of the peaceful transfer of power. The book of Jeremiah highlights how the Babylonian exile transformed Jewish understandings. And the book of Daniel explores Jewish mysticism and views on the afterlife! In this exciting new course from acclaimed Melton teacher Rabbi Richard Address, we will spend time looking over some of the lesser studied books of the Tanach and, in doing so, provide new insights into our history and contemporary Jewish issues we continue to focus on today.

showcases.title: Caring for Life: Judaism, Ethics, and Modern Medicine

videos.title: Week 2-Caring for Life: Judaism, Ethics, and Modern Medicine
videos.description:
Recorded May 19, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Richard Address
Advances in modern medicine have transformed how we live and how we face illness, aging, and the end of life. Along with these advances come difficult and deeply personal questions. How should we respond when treatment prolongs life but cannot relieve suffering? Who bears responsibility for ensuring that people receive the care they need?

In this two-part conversation, Rabbi Richard Address, a leading voice in Jewish conversations about aging and medical ethics, guides us through two pressing issues: medical aid in dying and the challenge of paying for and accessing health care. Drawing on classical Jewish sources and contemporary rabbinic responses, we will explore how Jewish tradition balances the sanctity of life with compassion, dignity, and communal responsibility. How do ancient texts help us think about medical realities their authors could never have imagined? This series invites thoughtful reflection on how Jewish ethics can guide individuals, families, and communities as we navigate some of the most complex decisions surrounding care, responsibility, and the value of life itself.

showcases.title: Character Development Through Mussar

videos.title: Week 4-Character Development Through Mussar
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded April 25, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Ben Kramer
Whether by studying our texts such as the Bible and Talmud, or through weekly sermons and community dialogue, Jews are constantly discussing and evaluating how to best live out our religious ideals. We also have a specific genre of Jewish literature, Mussar, that provides us with a practical approach to developing our character in consonance with these values. In this course, taught by expert Jewish educator Rabbi Ben Kramer, we will study a variety of Mussar texts to develop an understanding of this approach and learn how to effectively apply its principles to our own lives. Join us for this special course dedicated to the Jewish practice of self-growth.

showcases.title: Clickable Wisdom: Uncover Everyday Ethics with Sefaria

videos.title: Week 3-Clickable Wisdom: Uncover Everyday Ethics with Sefaria
videos.description:
Recorded March 18, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Benjamin Kramer
How does a Jewish idea take shape, grow, and evolve over generations? Judaism’s ethical vocabulary—privacy, competition, responsibility—did not emerge fully formed. These values were debated, refined, and reimagined by Jews across time, creating a living conversation that continues into our own lives. Clickable Wisdom invites learners into that conversation through a three-part exploration of the everyday ethical questions that define human interaction.

Developed in partnership with Sefaria, a dynamic living library of Jewish texts online, this course uses the power of technology to bring our tradition’s ongoing dialogue to life. In each session, learners will use Sefaria’s platform to trace Jewish ideas from their biblical roots through rabbinic interpretation and into contemporary application.

Together, we will explore:

The right and responsibility of privacy, and how Jewish sources imagine personal boundaries;
The ethics of competition, from markets to relationships, and what fairness looks like in a Jewish context;
The weight and responsibility of speech, and how generations of teachers have understood words as both tools and moral obligations.

Whether you are curious about Jewish ethics, eager to experiment with digital text tools, or hoping to see how ancient wisdom speaks to everyday life, this course offers a thoughtful, accessible path into the heart of Jewish learning.

showcases.title: Clickable Wisdom: Using Sefaria’s Digital Library to Uncover Everyday Ethical Teachings

videos.title: Week 4-Clickable Wisdom: Using Sefaria’s Digital Library to Uncover Everyday Ethical Teachings
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded December 5, 2022
Rabbi Ben Kramer
How is a Jewish idea transmitted and transformed over the course of generations? Judaism has many big ideas about justice and the ideals that shape an ethical and just life. In this four-part course you will explore significant issues of the human experience, their development over the generations, and their everyday applications to modern life. The four topics covered include the right and responsibility of privacy; complicity and bad advice; verbal abuse; and the boundaries of competition. This Melton Online course was developed in partnership with Sefaria, a living library of Jewish texts. In each lesson, learners will join their teacher in utilizing Sefaria to access the texts and trace the development of Jewish ideas from the Bible through the Talmud to the present. This course welcomes anyone curious in the topic and/or interested in exploring this rich online resource. We are proud to offer this course again after a successful run earlier in the year under the title “A Melton - Sefaria Learning Experience Tzedek, Tzedek: Judaism's Pursuit of Everyday Justice”.

showcases.title: Confronting Evil: Lessons from Biblical and Modern Times

videos.title: Week 2-Confronting Evil: Lessons from Biblical and Modern Times
videos.description:
Recorded March 12, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
The nation of Amalek is infamous for its unprovoked and cowardly attack on the vulnerable Israelites during their journey through the wilderness, making it a lasting symbol of moral evil. Paradoxically, in Deuteronomy 25, the Bible commands us to both remember what Amalek did and erase their memory. In this two-part class, Dr. Sandra Lilienthal explores the story of Amalek from the Book of Exodus, the commandment to remember and eradicate their legacy, and the enduring challenge of confronting acts of cruelty and injustice in our own time.
This series is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

showcases.title: Crossing Lines: Women and Moral Risk

videos.title: Week 2-Crossing Lines: Women and Moral Risk
videos.description:
Recorded August 27, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
In two of the Bible’s most provocative narratives, women stand at the center, making bold, unsettling choices in the face of power, silence, and catastrophe. This two-part course explores the stories of Tamar and the daughters of Lot, women whose actions challenged social norms and sparked lasting debate in Jewish tradition. Tamar seduces the man who wronged her, disguising herself as a harlot to secure justice and reclaim her dignity without exposing him to public shame. Lot’s daughters, believing they are the last survivors of a destroyed world, initiate an act of incest to preserve the human race. These stories are raw, haunting, and morally complex. They raise enduring questions about justice, survival, sexuality, and the price of agency in a world where women are often left with impossible choices. Rabbi Michele Faudem, a gifted educator with deep experience in Jewish learning and gender studies, is uniquely suited to lead this conversation. Known for her dynamic teaching and compassionate approach to complex texts, Michele creates space for learners to engage honestly with these stories—and discover the ethical and emotional power they still hold today.

showcases.title: Crossroads and Clashes: Jewish Turning Points in Conflict

videos.title: Week 2-Crossroads and Clashes: Jewish Turning Points in Conflict
videos.description:
Recorded September 11, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Charles E. Savenor
At pivotal moments in Jewish history, the path forward was anything but clear. Leaders rose with bold visions for the future, only to find themselves opposed by equally passionate voices with different answers to the same urgent question: What does the Jewish people need right now? In this two-part exploration, Rabbi Charlie Savenor guides us through two watershed conflicts, examining the personalities, politics, and values at stake, and why these debates continue to resonate today. We’ll begin with the biblical rebellion of Korach against Moses, a dramatic clash over leadership and legitimacy in the wilderness. Then, in part two, we’ll turn to the Second Temple era, where Sadducees and Pharisees fought for the soul of Jewish life, torn between priestly tradition in Jerusalem and rabbinic authority in Babylonia.

Rabbi Savenor brings deep expertise in how communities govern themselves, how leaders make decisions, and how civic responsibility takes shape. Known for drawing learners directly into the conversation, he creates a space where history comes alive through spirited discussion and shared discovery. Together, you’ll see how pivotal debates in Jewish history set patterns that still echo in the way we lead, follow, and build community today.

videos.title: Crossroads and Clashes: Jewish Turning Points in Conflict
videos.description:
Recorded September 4, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Charles E. Savenor
At pivotal moments in Jewish history, the path forward was anything but clear. Leaders rose with bold visions for the future, only to find themselves opposed by equally passionate voices with different answers to the same urgent question: What does the Jewish people need right now? In this two-part exploration, Rabbi Charlie Savenor guides us through two watershed conflicts, examining the personalities, politics, and values at stake, and why these debates continue to resonate today. We’ll begin with the biblical rebellion of Korach against Moses, a dramatic clash over leadership and legitimacy in the wilderness. Then, in part two, we’ll turn to the Second Temple era, where Sadducees and Pharisees fought for the soul of Jewish life, torn between priestly tradition in Jerusalem and rabbinic authority in Babylonia.

Rabbi Savenor brings deep expertise in how communities govern themselves, how leaders make decisions, and how civic responsibility takes shape. Known for drawing learners directly into the conversation, he creates a space where history comes alive through spirited discussion and shared discovery. Together, you’ll see how pivotal debates in Jewish history set patterns that still echo in the way we lead, follow, and build community today.

showcases.title: Dismantling Tyranny: Pharaoh, Plagues, and Power

videos.title: Week 2-Dismantling Tyranny: Pharaoh, Plagues, and Power
videos.description:
Recorded January 22, 2026
Instructor: Rav Johnny Solomon
The Ten Plagues are more than divine fireworks; they are a step-by-step dismantling of an immoral civilization built on domination.

This course explores the deeper meaning behind the Plagues narrative through the moral lens of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks. What kind of society produces slavery? Why does God target not just Pharaoh, but Egypt’s entire system of false power? And how do the plagues function not as punishments, but as a deliberate unmaking of religious, political, and psychological tyranny?

Over two sessions, we’ll explore how the Plagues narrative introduces a revolutionary moral idea: that might does not make right, that power is not virtue, and that human beings are not the ultimate arbiters of justice. Guided by Rav Johnny Solomon, drawing on the moral vision of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks, we’ll see how each plague confronts Egypt’s theology of control and affirms a new truth: that only God can define how humanity must live. This isn’t just a story of liberation. It’s a story about what happens when unchecked power meets a moral God, and what that encounter demands of us today.

showcases.title: Double Takes: Reading Foundational Stories with New Eyes

videos.title: Week 4-Double Takes: Reading Foundational Stories with New Eyes
videos.description:
Recorded July 2, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. David Harbater
What if the Book of Genesis isn’t just one story—but two? In this eye-opening course, Melton curriculum author Rabbi David Harbater explores the familiar tales of Noah, the Flood, and Abraham’s journey with fresh eyes. A careful reading reveals a dual narrative structure—two versions of creation, two versions of the flood, and two versions of Abraham’s calling—each offering a different window into God’s relationship with humanity. This course builds on Rabbi Harbater’s earlier Melton+ class on the Creation story, applying the same analytical lens to the next chapters of Genesis. Whether you joined that first journey or are coming in fresh, this class is designed to be both a continuation and a compelling standalone experience. Discover what these paired accounts reveal about divine justice and mercy, chosenness and covenant, and the unfolding drama that sets the stage for the Jewish story. Rabbi Harbater’s thoughtful approach opens up a layered and literary view of Torah—one that invites both critical insight and spiritual reflection.

showcases.title: Drawing Us In: Jewish Art from Ancient Works Through the 20th Century

videos.title: Week 2-Drawing Us In: Jewish Art from Ancient Works Through the 20th Century
videos.description:
Week2
Recorded November 17, 2022
Instructor: Dr. Sara Aroeste
Jewish art has a long and storied history. How long has Jewish art existed? What styles and themes have influenced Jewish artists throughout the centuries? How do we distinguish “Jewish art”? These questions and many more will be explored in this captivating two-part class presented by art history expert Dr. Sara Aroeste. Part one will cover Jewish art history, beginning with ancient works and taking us through 18th century Jewish artists and the themes of their works. Part two will cover art from the 20th century, focusing on the avant-garde Jewish artists, and the ways in which society both accepted and rejected their works. Join us for this unique adventure as we journey through the kaleidoscope of Jewish art history.

showcases.title: Empire, Exile, and Survival: The Jewish Odyssey in Greece showcase

videos.title: Week 4- Empire, Exile, and Survival: The Jewish Odyssey in Greece
videos.description:
Recorded April 8, 2025
Instructor: Avi Ben-Hur
For over two millennia, Jewish communities in Greece have navigated shifting empires, cultural transformations, and moments of both triumph and tragedy. In this four-part journey, historian Avi Ben-Hur unpacks the layered history of Greek Jewry, from its ancient roots to its near-destruction during the Holocaust.
1-Empire & Encounter - When Alexander Met the Priests: Jewish lore tells us that when Alexander the Great approached Jerusalem, he was met by the High Priest on Mt. Scopus and acknowledged the primacy of the One God. This moment marked the beginning of a long and complex relationship between Judaism and Hellenistic civilization. As Jews migrated to Greece during the Hellenistic period (4th–1st centuries BCE), they established the oldest Jewish Diaspora community in Europe. This session explores the forces behind that migration and the intricate “love-hate” relationship that developed between Jewish tradition and Greek culture.
2-Exile & Transformation – Under the Cross and the Crescent: With the Christianization of the Roman Empire in the 4th century CE, Greek Jews found themselves increasingly marginalized under Byzantine rule. Yet, despite these challenges, they developed distinct liturgies, traditions, and customs that set them apart from both Ashkenazi and Sephardi communities. In the late Middle Ages, Greece gradually came under Ottoman control, and with it came a new era for Greek Jewry. The Ottoman Empire welcomed Sephardic Jews expelled from Spain and Portugal, dramatically shifting the demographics of Greek Jewish communities. Nowhere was this transformation more profound than in Salonika, where the Spanish-speaking Sephardim reshaped the cultural and religious landscape, turning the city into the famed “Jerusalem of the Balkans.” This session examines the impact of these sweeping changes and the complex interplay between indigenous Romaniote Jews and newly arrived Sephardim.
3-Survival & Identity – Navigating Modernity and Nationhood: The 19th century brought seismic political shifts to Greece, forcing its Jewish communities to choose between competing identities: Ottoman or Greek, modernist or traditionalist. For the first time in their millennial history, Greek Jews faced widespread antisemitic violence. When Salonika—the third largest city in the Ottoman Empire—was absorbed into the Greek state on the eve of World War I, its Jewish population found itself transformed overnight from Ottoman subjects into Greek citizens. This session explores the choices and challenges that defined Greek Jewry during this period, from their responses to nationalism to their struggles for social and economic stability in a rapidly changing world.
4-Catastrophe & Resilience – The Shoah and Its Aftermath: During World War II, Greece suffered under a brutal tripartite occupation—Nazi, Italian, and Bulgarian. Jewish citizens fought valiantly in Greece’s resistance efforts, but their fates diverged depending on which foreign power controlled their region. Italian-occupied Jews initially found protection, while Bulgarian forces deported Macedonian Jews to Nazi extermination camps. The Nazis, in turn, systematically targeted Greek Jews for destruction, sending the vast majority of Salonika’s Jewish population to Auschwitz, where they were murdered. Yet, in several towns, local Christian authorities intervened to save their Jewish neighbors. This session follows the trajectory of Greek Jewry from the eve of the Holocaust to the present day, examining how and why their fate unfolded as it did—and how the remnants of these once-thriving communities continue to persevere.
This series offers invaluable insights into the unique Jewish heritage of Greece and serves as excellent preparation for those planning to join our upcoming "Islands of Memory: An Aegean Jewish Odyssey to Greece" tour in Summer 2025. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

1-Empire & Encounter - When Alexander Met the Priests: Jewish lore tells us that when Alexander the Great approached Jerusalem, he was met by the High Priest on Mt. Scopus and acknowledged the primacy of the One God. This moment marked the beginning of a long and complex relationship between Judaism and Hellenistic civilization. As Jews migrated to Greece during the Hellenistic period (4th–1st centuries BCE), they established the oldest Jewish Diaspora community in Europe. This session explores the forces behind that migration and the intricate “love-hate” relationship that developed between Jewish tradition and Greek culture.
2-Exile & Transformation – Under the Cross and the Crescent: With the Christianization of the Roman Empire in the 4th century CE, Greek Jews found themselves increasingly marginalized under Byzantine rule. Yet, despite these challenges, they developed distinct liturgies, traditions, and customs that set them apart from both Ashkenazi and Sephardi communities. In the late Middle Ages, Greece gradually came under Ottoman control, and with it came a new era for Greek Jewry. The Ottoman Empire welcomed Sephardic Jews expelled from Spain and Portugal, dramatically shifting the demographics of Greek Jewish communities. Nowhere was this transformation more profound than in Salonika, where the Spanish-speaking Sephardim reshaped the cultural and religious landscape, turning the city into the famed “Jerusalem of the Balkans.” This session examines the impact of these sweeping changes and the complex interplay between indigenous Romaniote Jews and newly arrived Sephardim.
3-Survival & Identity – Navigating Modernity and Nationhood: The 19th century brought seismic political shifts to Greece, forcing its Jewish communities to choose between competing identities: Ottoman or Greek, modernist or traditionalist. For the first time in their millennial history, Greek Jews faced widespread antisemitic violence. When Salonika—the third largest city in the Ottoman Empire—was absorbed into the Greek state on the eve of World War I, its Jewish population found itself transformed overnight from Ottoman subjects into Greek citizens. This session explores the choices and challenges that defined Greek Jewry during this period, from their responses to nationalism to their struggles for social and economic stability in a rapidly changing world.
4-Catastrophe & Resilience – The Shoah and Its Aftermath: During World War II, Greece suffered under a brutal tripartite occupation—Nazi, Italian, and Bulgarian. Jewish citizens fought valiantly in Greece’s resistance efforts, but their fates diverged depending on which foreign power controlled their region. Italian-occupied Jews initially found protection, while Bulgarian forces deported Macedonian Jews to Nazi extermination camps. The Nazis, in turn, systematically targeted Greek Jews for destruction, sending the vast majority of Salonika’s Jewish population to Auschwitz, where they were murdered. Yet, in several towns, local Christian authorities intervened to save their Jewish neighbors. This session follows the trajectory of Greek Jewry from the eve of the Holocaust to the present day, examining how and why their fate unfolded as it did—and how the remnants of these once-thriving communities continue to persevere.
This series offers invaluable insights into the unique Jewish heritage of Greece and serves as excellent preparation for those planning to join our upcoming "Islands of Memory: An Aegean Jewish Odyssey to Greece" tour in Summer 2025. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

showcases.title: Ethics of the Moment

videos.title: Week 4 - Ethics of the Moment
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded July 12, 2022
Rabbi Michele Faudem
We live in challenging and confusing times when making the right decision often feels impossible. How often do we have the opportunity to have in-depth conversations about the difficult choices we face in our world today? How does our modern way of living affect our moral obligation to take care of low-income workers? When should we utilize modern medicine to ease loved ones’ suffering? Where do the goals of marriage and the institution of marriage intersect? How do we reconcile our “dominion over every living thing” (Genesis 1:28) with the growing impact of climate change? Join Rabbi Michele Faudem and explore these questions with meaningful and enlightening discussion about these topics and more in this timely new class.

showcases.title: Ethics Under Fire: Jewish Law in Times of War

videos.title: Week 3-Ethics Under Fire- Jewish Law in Times of War
videos.description:
Recorded July 30, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. Danny Schiff
What does Jewish tradition say about the most difficult questions war presents: life and death, innocence and guilt, justice and restraint? In this timely three-part course, Rabbi Dr. Danny Schiff, a leading scholar of Jewish ethics, draws on classical texts and modern realities to examine the moral boundaries of warfare.
Session 1: When Is War Permitted?If civilian casualties are all but inevitable, can war ever be ethical? Under what circumstances do Jewish sources permit going to war?
Session 2: Guilt, Deterrence, and the Enemy - Is it ethical to fight more aggressively to prevent future attacks? Can we truly distinguish between combatants and civilians? What do the rabbis say about the danger of dehumanizing the enemy?
Session 3: Siege, Strategy, and the IDF - Does Jewish law permit cutting off food or supplies during siege? How closely does the IDF align with the ethical expectations of Jewish tradition? Rabbi Schiff brings deep scholarship and moral clarity to a topic that remains both painful and pressing. This course provides learners with the tools to think critically about wartime ethics with empathy, integrity, and a foundation in Jewish values.

showcases.title: Evolving Tradition: Deepening Our Understanding of Conservative Judaism

videos.title: Week 3-Evolving Tradition: Deepening Our Understanding of Conservative Judaism
videos.description:
Recorded September 11, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Wallk 
Conservative Judaism finds itself at a crossroads. How does the denomination stay true to the traditions and laws of the past while continuing to speak meaningfully to the present moment? In this three-part program with acclaimed Melton teacher Rabbi Amy Wallk, we will explore the history of Conservative Judaism, how the movement began, the key principles and ideals are that serve as its foundation, and where it is going. Through this lens we will engage with a wide variety of topics such as Zionism, driving on Shabbat, the role of women, LGBTQ+ ordination and engagement, and the role of the non-Jew in synagogue life. Join us for an insightful look at how one of modern Judaism’s denominations has and continues to navigate the 21st century. This course is graciously sponsored by Susan and Bill Firestone. It is the final course in a four-part series Melton is proudly offering in 5784 exploring the major denominations in Judaism. You do not need to have taken the others to enjoy this one!

showcases.title: Evolving Tradition: Deepening Our Understanding of Orthodox Judaism

videos.title: Week 3-Evolving Tradition: Deepening Our Understanding of Orthodox Judaism
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded February 12, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Asher LopatinMany people associate Orthodox Judaism with a specific traditional practice that has not changed in hundreds of years, but today’s Jewish orthodox community is diverse and constantly evolving. Join Orthodox thought-leader Rabbi Asher Lopatin for this thrilling three-part exploration of the evolution of Orthodox Judaism. We’ll start with a breakdown how Orthodox Jews themselves see their history and evolution. From there we’ll take a look at how Orthodoxy developed as a reaction to other movements and grew in different ways as a direct result. How has it survived and thrived? What is its role in in the United States and in Israel? Finally, we will explore Modern, Liberal, Open, and Ultra Orthodoxy as well as Yeshivish, Chasidish, and Social Orthodoxy. Join us for this exciting deep dive into one of Judaism’s most fascinating denominations!

This is the second in a four-part series Melton is proudly offering in 5784 exploring major denominations in Judaism. Stay tuned for more information about courses exploring Reform and Conservative Judaism in the year ahead!

showcases.title: Evolving Tradition: Deepening Our Understanding of Reconstructionist Judaism

videos.title: Week 3 - Evolving Tradition: Deepening Our Understanding of Reconstructionist Judaism
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded October 24, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Boris Dolan
The newest of the major Jewish denominations may be one of the smallest but it has had an outsized impact on the contemporary Jewish world. Reconstructionism and its founder, Mordecai Kaplan, can be credited for everything from the first Bat Mitzvah to the vision of Jewish Community Centers, creative theology to social justice activism. In this participatory class, we learn about Mordecai Kaplan and see how his ideas and new ways of understanding Jewish community have impacted Jews across the spectrum of denominations. We also delve into new ways of understanding theology and faith and discuss the many ways that a Reconstructionist philosophy can help us make sense of contemporary issues and challenges. Join Reconstructionist thought-leader Rabbi Boris Dolin for this insightful exploration about the evolution of Reconstructionist Judaism.

showcases.title: Evolving Tradition: Deepening Our Understanding of Reform Judaism

videos.title: Week 3-Evolving Tradition: Deepening Our Understanding of Reform Judaism
videos.description:
Recorded July 25, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Emily Hyatt 
Reform Judaism is the largest of the Jewish denominations in America, but at its core, it is a movement that emphasizes each individual as a critical component of sustainable and meaningful community. In this class, taught by Rabbi Emily Hyatt, we will trace the history of Reform Judaism, understand the dynamics of the movement today, and discover how Reform Judaism creates opportunities to navigate theology, heritage, ritual, and community in today’s modern world.

showcases.title: Examining Israeli Identity through Film and Television

videos.title: Week 4-Examining Israeli Identity through Film and Television
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded February 28, 2023
Instructor: Amy Kronish
Israeli cinema has had a rapid expansion in both the scope of its content and its audience. During the last two decades, Israeli filmmakers have turned their cameras to personal, humanistic stories and social issues, such as feminism, LGBTQ+ rights, ethnic assimilation, and social alienation. In this course, learners watch a curated list of films/TV shows before each class and, guided by Israeli media expert Amy Kronish, analyze their content and context. With these films as the launching point, we can better understand how new themes and ideas can be seen as adding complexity, nuance, and humanity to the political climate.

Femme Fatale or Fierce Woman Warrior

videos.title: Week 4-Femme Fatale or Fierce Woman Warrior?
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded July 13, 2023
Instructor: Professor Sivan Rotholtz
Although written into Jewish tradition as a femme fatale, a demoness, a black widow, and a skull-crusher, Delilah, Lilith, Tamar, and Yael are far more complex personalities. Each week during this four-part series we'll pair the story of one of these unforgettable biblical women with midrash and poetry that shed new light upon her character. We will also reflect on what each of these women has to teach us, adding our own voices to the age-old tradition of wrestling with Jewish texts. Join feminist Torah scholar Sivan Rotholz as we consider, reconsider, and take a deep dive into the stories of four powerful women of biblical literature.

showcases.title: From Creation to the Flood: The Dual Foundations of Judaism showcase

videos.title: Week 4-From Creation to the Flood: The Dual Foundations of Judaism
videos.description:
Recorded October 21, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. David Harbater
One of the most joyful moments in the Jewish calendar year is the celebration of restarting the Torah, a holiday known as Simchat Torah. In the lead up to Simchat Torah, join us for this special course in which we will examine the foundations of Judaism as they are presented in the opening chapters of the Torah. Led by renowned author of In the Beginnings: Discovering the Two Worldviews Hidden Within Genesis 1-11, Rabbi Dr. David Harbater, we will do a close and careful reading of the text to discover two narratives that reflect two distinct worldviews regarding the nature of God, human beings, our role in the world, the relationship between man and woman, and much more. Join us for this insightful new look at how it all began.
