showcases.title: From Sinai to Seinfeld: Jews and Their Jokes

videos.title: Week 3-From Sinai to Seinfeld: Jews and Their Jokes
videos.description:
Recorded May 27, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Alan Freedman
Humor is part of the Jewish soul. It gives us the ability to laugh at our tragedies, question authority, and make meaning out of the challenges we face. In fact, when asked what it means to be Jewish, more people chose “a sense of humor” than religious observance or communal belonging. What is it about Jewish life that has made humor so central?

In this course, we treat jokes as texts, reading them as we would Torah, midrash, or modern literature. Across ten sessions, we journey through Jewish history from the Bible and the Talmud to the Yiddish world, the Borscht Belt, and contemporary comedy. Along the way, we encounter figures like Sholem Aleichem, Fanny Brice, Lenny Bruce, Mel Brooks, Jerry Seinfeld, and Sarah Silverman, asking what their humor reveals about the times and communities they reflect.

As Jewish life changes, so does Jewish humor. Sometimes rooted in deep textual knowledge, sometimes shaped by the experience of being outsiders, Jewish comedy has long transformed hardship into insight and laughter into connection. By exploring the jokes Jews tell and retell, we uncover not only what makes us laugh, but what binds us together across generations.

Table of Contents
Lesson 1 - How to ‘read’ a Jewish joke
Lesson 2 - A lack of graves
Lesson 3 - The goyim annoy’em
Lesson 4 - If you’re so smart...
Lesson 5 - The joy of text
Lesson 6 - Take my wife, please
Lesson 7 - “You can change your noses but not your Moses”
Lesson 8 - Dig, I’m Jewish
Lesson 9 - Mothers and others
Lesson 10 - Seriously funny

showcases.title: From Talmud to Table: The Origins of the Seder Rituals

videos.title: Week 3-From Talmud to Table: The Origins of the Seder Rituals
videos.description:
Recorded April 23, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Benjamin Kramer
We all know the horseradish, the dipping, the leaning, the drinking, —but where do these beloved Passover Seder traditions actually come from? In this engaging three-part series, Melton educator Rabbi Ben Kramer takes us on a deep dive into the Talmudic roots of the Seder’s most familiar rituals. We’ll uncover the ancient debates, trace the evolution of these practices through centuries of rabbinic interpretation, and see how they became the customs we recognize today. Through lively discussion and close reading of classical texts, we’ll explore:

The Origins of the Seder’s Key Rituals – What were the sages actually arguing about?
The Meaning of the Matzah, Maror, and More – Symbolism, structure, and surprises in the Haggadah
How Local Customs Became Universal Practice – Why do some traditions still vary today?
Passover is a holiday of questions, so come discover the answers behind the rituals we experience year after year!

showcases.title: Fundraising
Give It a Rest: A Guide to Shabbat Prayers

videos.title: Week 2-Give It a Rest: A Guide to Shabbat Prayers
videos.description:
Week 2
Recorded November 15, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Wallk
With war raging in Israel, how might the Shabbat liturgy help us cope with this moment? How can we find comfort from our Shabbat prayers right now? Guided by Jewish liturgy expert Rabbi Amy Wallk, we explore the layers and themes of the Shabbat morning service in the context of this moment and, in doing so, discover prayers that might challenge or inspire us. We encourage you to bring your questions and curiosity!

showcases.title: Got Chutzpah A Brief History of Boldness and Strength showcase

videos.title: Week 4-Got Chutzpah? A Brief History of Boldness and Strength
videos.description:
Recorded March 26, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Ed Feinstein
“Chutzpah” is Yiddish for insolence, audacity, or nerve. But the word is older than Yiddish. In the Talmud, “chutzpah” means irrepressible boldness and strength. Chutzpah describes the rare quality of moral courage at the heart of Judaism. It depicts a revolutionary vision of human responsibility and human possibilities, as well as a radical idea of our relationship to God. As we struggle with global issues, Chutzpah is a message the world is desperate to hear. In our learning together, we will explore how Chutzpah is expressed in the Jewish religious imagination and in the history of Jewish thought. This course is taught by Rabbi Ed Feinstein and is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

showcases.title: Grief and Growth: Ritual, Feeling, and the Jewish Way to Mourn

videos.title: Week 2-Grief and Growth: Ritual, Feeling, and the Jewish Way to mourn
videos.description:
Recorded July 21, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
What do Jewish mourning rituals ask of us, not just in action, but in feeling? And what do we do when those feelings don’t come easily, or at all? In this two-part course with Dr. Sandra Lilienthal, we’ll explore the emotional and spiritual power of Jewish mourning practices through the lens of law and tradition.
In the first session, we’ll examine how grief is expressed in Jewish law and custom, ranging from personal mourning to communal periods of sorrow, such as the fast days associated with the destruction of the Temple. What behaviors are prescribed during these times—fasting, refraining from music or celebration—and why do they take the shape they do?
In the second session, we’ll ask more profound questions about the relationship between ritual and emotion. Can a practice evoke a feeling? Does a feeling count without a practice? And what is lost if we abandon rituals meant to guide us through the ups and downs of individual and collective memory? Drawing on sources from the Torah, rabbinic literature, medieval codes of Jewish law, and the works of modern thinkers, Dr. Lilienthal invites learners into a nuanced conversation about how tradition helps us feel, and how it continues to evolve when it doesn’t.

showcases.title: Happiness on Command: The Obligation and Challenge of Jewish Joy

videos.title: Week 2-Happiness on Command: The Obligation and Challenge of Joy
videos.description:
Recorded January 27, 2026
Instructor: Dr. Sandra Lilienthal
Judaism doesn’t just celebrate joy, it commands it. Our tradition treats simcha not as a fleeting emotion, but as a spiritual practice. In this two-part series with Dr. Sandra Lilienthal, we’ll explore joy as a mitzvah: something to cultivate, to practice, and to bring into the lives of others, even when it doesn’t come easily.

Drawing on biblical, rabbinic, medieval, and modern sources, we’ll ask: What does it mean to “serve God with joy”? Why are wine, song, and dance such essential ingredients in Jewish celebration? And how can ritual help us access joy, even in hard times? Come ready to rethink what joy really means, and why it matters.

Session 1: “And You Shall Rejoice Before the Lord” — Simcha as Obligation
In this session, we’ll explore the surprising idea that joy isn’t optional in Jewish life: it’s a requirement. Through biblical and rabbinic sources, we’ll examine how and why our tradition mandates simcha, and what it means to pursue happiness as a spiritual and ethical responsibility.

Session 2: “Bringing Joy to the Bride, the Guest, and the World” — The Practice of Simcha
Joy isn’t just a feeling; it’s an action. In this session, we’ll look at the rituals and practices that cultivate joy in Jewish tradition, from weddings to holidays to everyday acts of kindness. What does it mean to help someone else feel joy? And how can those practices help us find our own?

showcases.title: History Behind the Headlines: Israel, Hamas, and the Current War

videos.title: Week 3-History Behind the Headlines: Israel, Hamas, and the Current War
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded December 19, 2023
Instructor: Professor Howard Lupovitch
With so much misinformation swirling around us, it is more important than ever to understand the complexities of Israel from its inception until now. Join us for this special limited series of three sessions that provides a brief but comprehensive Israel education for learners of all backgrounds. In the first session, we will consider the long history Jews have had in the land of Israel as well as the 19th and 20th Zionist philosophies that formed the ethos of the modern State of Israel. Next, we will examine the Israel-Palestinian Conflict and its origins, complexities, and attempted solutions. Finally we will focus on the emergence of Hamas as a mainstay of Palestinian leadership and authority and how its charters speak about Israel, Jews, and Palestinian national aspirations through a radical Islamic framework. Professor Howard Lupovitch, professor of history at Wayne State University and author of Melton’s acclaimed course The Star and the Crescent, will provide us with a deep understanding of Israel’s history that can help us to better understand this critical moment for Israel and Jews around the world.

showcases.title: Holiday Heroines

videos.title: Week 6-Holiday Heroines
videos.description:
Recorded May 2, 2024
Instructor: Sivan Rotholz
In this six-week program from Melton-favorite teacher and gynocentric Torah scholar Sivan Rotholz, we will explore the Jewish calendar through the lens of the women featured in the Jewish holidays. From the women of Passover as a model of civil disobedience to the medieval practice of remembering Jephthah's daughter on the winter solstice, from Chag Habanot (the North African Jewish Festival of Daughters) to Lailah and the Tree of Souls for Tu B’Shvat, we will discover some of the lesser-known female heroines of the Jewish year. Through this discovery, we will gain insights into new role models and see the multi-dimensionality of some of our more famous Biblical women. And when we explore Esther and Vashti and the feminine origins of Hamantaschen, it will give you something unforgettable to share at next year's Purim party!

showcases.title: Honor Your Father & Mother: Jewish Tradition Meets Family Psychology

videos.title: Week 2-Honor Your Father and Mother: Jewish Tradition Meets Family Psychology
videos.description:
Recorded November 19, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Mona Fishbane
Jewish tradition places honoring parents and maintaining intergenerational continuity at the heart of our values. Yet, we live in a culture that often prioritizes individual needs and independence over obligations to family, community, or heritage. This tension can create distance, conflict, or even estrangement between generations. In this two-part course, Dr. Mona Fishbane—a psychologist and expert in intergenerational family dynamics—draws on Jewish texts and contemporary psychology to explore how these opposing forces shape our lives and how we can respond with resilience and care.

In Session 1: Intergenerational Connection & Disconnection, we will explore biblical and Talmudic sources on honoring parents, which reveal both the challenges and the sacred obligations inherent in family life. By contrast, current cultural trends emphasize individual autonomy and may encourage intergenerational estrangement. How can we balance these contrasting values? More concretely, how can we manage our emotional reactivity with our family of origin? Sharing insights from neurobiology, Mona will offer tools for cultivating intentionality and thoughtfulness in relationships with our parents, shaping the legacies we pass on to the next generation.
Session 2: Healing Intergenerational Wounds will consider the intergenerational family dramas of Genesis, drawing on family psychology and Jewish wisdom to consider how to address hurt, estrangement, and intergenerational rifts. Mona will offer perspectives on cultivating dialogue, strengthening family ties, and promoting personal well-being while staying rooted in Jewish tradition.

With wisdom and compassion, Dr. Fishbane brings Jewish tradition and psychology into conversation to illuminate paths of healing, resilience, and deeper family connection. Her extraordinary teaching makes even complex ideas clear and accessible, offering depth without dilution and insight that speaks to every learner.

showcases.title: I Go Where You Go: Ruth and the Covenant of Connection

videos.title: Week 2-I Go Where You Go: Ruth and the Covenant of Connection
videos.description:
Recorded May 12, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
As we count the days from Passover to Shavuot, the Book of Ruth offers timeless wisdom for building a more just and compassionate community. What do we owe one another? Who belongs—and who decides? And how do kindness and power shape the society we create? In this two-session course, Rabbi Michele Faudem—a favorite among Melton learners for her clarity, passion, and deep commitment to student understanding—guides us through Ruth’s bold journey into the heart of what it means to be Jewish, and the enduring values that her biblical story calls us to live by today. This series is sponsored by Linda Feinfeld.

showcases.title: Images of Women in Israeli Cinema

videos.title: Week 4-Images of Women in Israeli Cinema
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded March 6, 2024
Instructor: Amy Kronish
In the last 25 years, Israeli cinema has turned its lens to multiple subjects dealing with humanistic stories, issues of gender, and social issues. In this engaging follow-up to Israeli media expert Amy Kronish’s hit Melton series “Examining Israeli Identity through Film and Television”, we will concentrate on portrayals of women in film and, in many of the cases, examine films directed by women. With these films as the launching point, we can better understand the complexity, nuance, and humanity found in Israeli society.

showcases.title: In Defense of Judgment: Moral Clarity in a Too-Tolerant Time

videos.title: Week 2-In Defense of Judgment: Moral Clarity in a Too-Tolerant Time
videos.description:
Recorded September 15, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Neil Fleischmann
“Don’t judge me.” “No judgment.” “I’m not judging you.” These phrases show up everywhere in modern conversation, but what if we’re missing something essential? As we approach the High Holidays, when Divine judgment takes center stage, it’s worth asking: What does Jewish tradition say about judging others… and ourselves? In this relevant and reflective session, Rabbi Neil Fleischmann explores Jewish sources that challenge the idea that all judgment is harmful. Is judgment always a negative act, or can it be an expression of care and responsibility? When should we speak up, and when should we step back? And what does it mean to believe in a judging God in a culture that insists we never judge at all? Rabbi Neil brings psychological insight, gentle humor, and a grounded teaching style to this vital conversation. With clarity and compassion, he helps us think more deeply about what it means to live in relationship with ourselves, with others, and with a tradition that insists judgment and compassion belong together.

showcases.title: In Our Time: 60 Years of Jewish-Catholic Dialogue

videos.title: Week 3-"In Our Time"- 60 Years of Jewish-Catholic Dialogue
videos.description:
Recorded July 1, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Steven Chervin
In 1965, during a time of global upheaval—marked by the civil rights movement, sweeping changes within the Catholic Church, and the growing influence of the American Jewish community—the Second Vatican Council released Nostra Aetate (“In Our Time”), a groundbreaking document that redefined the Church’s relationship with Judaism and other non-Christian religions. Sixty years later, Nostra Aetate remains a landmark of reconciliation and a lens for understanding what progress in interfaith dialogue can look like. In this three-part series, Dr. Steve Chervin will guide us through the roots, realities, and reverberations of Nostra Aetate. Each session will offer a deeper understanding of the evolving relationship between Jews and Catholics:

Session 1: What Came Before will examine the origins of Christian anti-Judaism in the New Testament, trace its violent expressions through the Middle Ages, and explore the historical forces that led to change at Vatican II.
Session 2: A Document in the Making will uncover the process behind Nostra Aetate—from Jewish advocacy and internal Church debates to countless revisions—and then engage in a close reading of the document itself, exploring its structure, subtleties, and spirit.
Session 3: Sixty Years Later will assess how Nostra Aetate has shaped Jewish–Catholic relations over the past six decades. What progress has been made? Where have efforts fallen short? And what might come next?
In an era shaped by Pope Leo’s new leadership and rising global antisemitism, the questions raised by this document remain urgent, timely, and unfinished. Jewish participants are encouraged to invite Catholic and Christian friends to register and join the conversation.

showcases.title: Inside the Shtetl: A Jewish Universe

videos.title: Week 2- Inside the Shtetl: A Jewish Universe
videos.description:
Recorded October 28, 2025
Instructor: Evgenia Kempinski
The shtetl was the heart of Ashkenazi Jewish life for centuries, a place where daily rhythms of work, worship, and community shaped a world that still echoes in our memories and stories. From bustling marketplaces to intimate synagogues, and from close-knit family ties to complex relationships with non-Jewish neighbors, the shtetl was at once ordinary and extraordinary, grounding Jewish identity in ways that still matter today.
In this two-part series, Jewish educator and tour guide Evgenia Kempinski brings the shtetl vividly to life. Through storytelling and historical insight, we’ll explore what made these towns unique, why some call them a “Jewish Atlantis,” and how they continue to inspire both nostalgia and curiosity. Along the way, we’ll reflect on how the lives of our ancestors in these communities continue to shape our sense of belonging and resilience. Whether you’re tracing your family roots, drawn to Jewish memory, or simply curious about the everyday world that sustained generations, this course offers an intimate window into the shtetl as a universe of its own, rich in tradition, complexity, and enduring meaning.

showcases.title: Is the “Evil Inclination” the Devil or My Teacher

videos.title: Week 3-Is the Evil Inclination the Devil or My Teacher?
videos.description:
Recorded August 6, 2024
Instructor: Joe Septimus 
The Jewish concept of the “evil inclination” or Yetzer ha’Rah is one that is discussed throughout Jewish literature. It can seem like a big, scary bad-guy or a tempter whispering in our ear, but it can also be a motivator for us to be productive and grow. In this three-part program with expert Melton educator Joe Septimus, we will learn an array of texts referencing the Yetzer ha’Rah to see the circumstances when it appears and the variety of opinions about its value. Together we will discover the reasons for its being so feared while at the same time being so central in Jewish thought. So come join us as we exorcise Judaism’s inner demons!

showcases.title: Israel and the Diaspora: Relations Frayed with Yael Weinstein

videos.title:
Week 4 Israel and Diaspora-Frayed Relations Recorded Mar. 31, 2022 
Yael Weinstein
videos.description: From the birth of the Jewish state until today there has been a widening gap between the Jewish communities of the diaspora and Israel. Discover the human voices that are represented between the two pillars of the Jewish population. Explore where are the differences, what are the similarities, and how the Israeli and Diaspora Jewish societies are coping with the challenges of being one Jewish people with different experiences.

showcases.title: Israel Current Events - the Daily Alert

videos.title: Week 3-Israel Current Events - the Daily Alert
videos.description:
Recorded August 21, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Adam Schaffer
The news out of Israel changes nearly every minute. Now more than ever, it is crucial to stay informed, but finding reliable news is difficult, and finding unbiased news is even harder... until now. Enter Melton+ and the Daily Alert. The Daily Alert is a curated news site founded by the Conference of Presidents of Major American Jewish Organizations and crafted by expert analysts at the Jerusalem Center for Security and Foreign Affairs. It collects news stories from publications across the ideological and editorial spectrum, allowing readers to view the same stories from multiple perspectives. Rabbi Adam Schaffer, our instructor, will co-create the class with our students by leading them through a guided exploration of stories from that week’s news. We will examine not only the content of the stories but also the discrepancies and hidden biases that appear in different publications’ coverage of the same events. In doing so, we aim to equip students with the ability to understand the news more effectively and appreciate the diverse perspectives in the coverage.

showcases.title: Israel in the Headlines: Fighting Back on the Media Battlefield showcase

videos.title: Week 3-Israel in the Headlines- Fighting Back on the Media Battlefield
videos.description:
Recorded September 17, 2025
Instructor: Jacki Alexander, CEO and President, HonestReporting
Why is Israel constantly in the global spotlight, and why does so much of that coverage feel distorted, misleading, or incomplete? In this three-part workshop series, HonestReporting’s executive team breaks down the media forces shaping how Israel is portrayed in the world, and how everyday people can push back with clarity, confidence, and credibility.

Part 1 - The Shifting Landscape of Information: Jacki Alexander, Global CEO of HonestReporting, explores how information is created, consumed, and weaponized in today’s media ecosystem, and why understanding that shift is essential to defending truth.
Part 2 - Eye on the Media: Executive Director Gil Hoffman reveals how and why global media outlets fixate on Israel, exposing patterns of bias, omission, and double standards that shape public opinion worldwide.
Part 3 - Taking Action on Social Media: Learn practical strategies for becoming a more effective digital advocate: how to share compelling content, respond strategically, and support Israel’s image in the spaces where narratives are formed.
Taught by two seasoned media professionals with deep experience in journalism, advocacy, and public communication, this course offers a unique insider’s view of the challenges Israel faces on the global stage. Whether you’re new to these issues or looking to sharpen your skills, you’ll leave empowered to speak up—and speak out—with greater impact.

showcases.title: Israel Revealed

videos.title: Israel Revealed-November
videos.description:
Session 3-November
Recorded November 28, 2023
Instructor: Avi Ben Hur
Israel is a colorful and multifaceted country filled with people of all backgrounds, religions, and cultures. While the headlines we read emphasize the political issues, there’s a lot more going on in Israel than politics. Through archeological discoveries, internationally sought after movies and TV shows, technological breakthroughs, and more, Israel regularly leaves a global impact that cannot be ignored. Join us for a breakdown of the latest and greatest developments and little-known nuggets from the Promised Land, presented by the co-founder of Israel Studies Online, expert Israeli educator and Melton Travel leader Avi Ben-Hur!

showcases.title: Israel: The Present Situation and What Lies Ahead

videos.title: Week 3-Israel: The Present Situation and What Lies Ahead
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded March 19, 2024
Instructor: Daniella Geffen 
October 7th was a transformative moment in Israel. Perceptions of Israeli life and safety changed along with the ways that the Israeli people stepped up in the wake of the violent attacks on their country. In this urgent new course from Melton Travel educator Daniella Gefen, we will break down the complex history that led to this moment, explore ways that Israel is confronting this new reality, and look into the future with an eye for what comes next. What are the origins and fates of those living in Gaza? How has Israel’s military been reshaped by technology and gender roles? Is a two-state solution still possible or are there alternatives developing for how Israeli and Palestinian people will live in the same land in the future? Please join us as we explore these complex questions and more in this important follow up to Daniella’s impactful Melton International class, “Melton Meets the Moment: Understanding the Backstory of the War in Israel”.  

This course is graciously sponsored by Zachary Narrett.

showcases.title: Israeli Literature as a Window into a Diverse and Vibrant Society

videos.title: Week 10-Israeli Literature as a Window into a Diverse and Vibrant Society
videos.description:
Recorded May 4, 2026
Instructor: Aviva Laskov
What does it mean to long for a homeland, to wrestle with identity, or to seek peace in a land shaped by conflict and hope? In this 10-week course, you’ll explore the richness and complexity of Israeli society through its literature: raw, poetic, provocative, and deeply human.

Designed and taught by Israeli educator Aviva Laskov, this course is a rare opportunity to learn with the teacher who created this popular Melton Signature Course, now offered online via Melton+ for the first time. You’ll encounter the voices of Israel’s most celebrated writers—including Leah Goldberg, Yehuda Amichai, Dalia Ravikovitch, and Amos Oz—alongside powerful works by immigrants, Mizrahi poets, and others outside the cultural mainstream. From questions of language and land to war, peace, and personal memory, each session offers a new literary lens into the Israeli experience.

Whether you’re a seasoned reader or just beginning to explore Israeli literature, this journey promises fresh insights, layered discussions, and a deeper understanding of a proud and complex society.

Table of Contents: 

1. If I Forget Thee... Longing For Zion
2. Our Words:  Speak Hebrew!
3. Tel Aviv: The First Hebrew City
4. A Poet and His City: Amichai’s Jerusalem
5. The Trauma of the Shoah
6. From the Four Corners: Immigrants Write Themselves into Israeli Society
7. The Clash Between Land and Identities
8. Pro Patria Mori: War and Its Impact
9. Yearning For Peace
10. On a Personal Note

Designed and taught by Israeli educator Aviva Laskov, this course is a rare opportunity to learn with the teacher who created this popular Melton Signature Course, now offered online via Melton+ for the first time. You’ll encounter the voices of Israel’s most celebrated writers—including Leah Goldberg, Yehuda Amichai, Dalia Ravikovitch, and Amos Oz—alongside powerful works by immigrants, Mizrahi poets, and others outside the cultural mainstream. From questions of language and land to war, peace, and personal memory, each session offers a new literary lens into the Israeli experience. Whether you’re a seasoned reader or just beginning to explore Israeli literature, this journey promises fresh insights, layered discussions, and a deeper understanding of a proud and complex society.

Table of Contents: 

1. If I Forget Thee... Longing For Zion
2. Our Words:  Speak Hebrew!
3. Tel Aviv: The First Hebrew City
4. A Poet and His City: Amichai’s Jerusalem
5. The Trauma of the Shoah
6. From the Four Corners: Immigrants Write Themselves into Israeli Society
7. The Clash Between Land and Identities
8. Pro Patria Mori: War and Its Impact
9. Yearning For Peace
10. On a Personal Note

showcases.title: Israeli Pop Jamz

videos.title: Week 3-Israeli Pop Jamz
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded May 22, 2023
Instructor: Cantor David A. Lipp
Israeli pop music is the heartbeat of Israeli culture. Within it, you can find references to the army, politics, community tensions, Jewish life, and much more. In this three-session course with beloved Melton teacher Cantor David Lipp, we'll survey decades of Israeli pop music with an ear for how these “jamz” reflect modern Israeli identity. How do they reveal who Israelis are today? Based on this music, what do they care about? How do they express themselves in the public square? Join us to jam and understand a bit more about contemporary Israel.

showcases.title: Jewish Answers to Life's Most Challenging Questions

videos.title: Week 6-Jewish Answers to Life's Most Challenging Questions
videos.description:
Recorded September 24, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz 
This is your invitation to join the conversation as we grapple with some of life’s toughest questions, like “Why do good people suffer?”, “Is everything that happens pre-ordained?” and “Can’t God make a miracle for me?” In this six-part course, we’ll explore multiple understandings of these timeless questions through the eyes of the rabbinic sages as well as those of modern thinkers — including our own. The course is based on “Where’s My Miracle: Jewish Traditions for Dealing with Tragedy,” by Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz, Melton’s International Director. This course is graciously sponsored by Rochelle Friedman.

showcases.title: Jewish Ethical Wills in a Post-October 7th World
Jewish Ethical Wills: How Will You Be Remembered

videos.title: Week 4-Jewish Ethical Wills: How Will You Be Remembered?
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded December 20, 2022
Instructor: Dr. Lesley Simpson
Everyone has a story, and how we preserve our stories can ensure that they inspire future generations. This writing workshop offers an introduction to writing your own ethical will, or legacy letter, which is a little-known but important, centuries-old Jewish tradition of letter writing. Melton Online teacher Dr. Lesley Simpson will share examples of ethical wills and help you begin to write your own letter in a collaborative learning and writing space. The four-week course is designed for active participation so come ready to write!

showcases.title: Jewish Genealogy 101

videos.title: Week 4-Jewish Genealogy 101
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded May 31, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Dan Oren
Many of us have become interested in knowing more about our families’ roots. How does one start this process? What can one hope to achieve? This four-session course will deliver the critical building blocks to begin this exploration into Jewish Genealogy. Join Yale University professor Dr. Dan Oren as he guides us through this exciting, question-filled journey. How does one think like a Jewish genealogist? How does one store one’s data? What resources are available? How can DNA testing be useful in starting this investigation? What are the mistakes to try and avoid? When and whom do you call when you need help? This interactive, how-to course is designed to help you pursue research into your family’s history.

showcases.title: Jewish Ghost Stories

videos.title: Week 4-Jewish Ghost Stories
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded February 8, 2024
Instructor: Dr. David Shyovitz
Fantasy is one of the most popular genres on earth. From Harry Potter to Lord of the Rings to Game of Thrones, our society has been enraptured by the magical possibilities of far-off lands, monsters, and adventure. But what about Jewish fantasy? Judaism has a rich history of "folk narratives" all the way from the Middle Ages: tales of wandering ghosts, magical duels, distant lands, and monstrous creatures. Join Jewish historian Dr. David Shyovitz for this thrilling four-part exploration of Jewish fantasy and ghost stories. As we shall see, belief in the otherworldly and supernatural were not merely popular superstitions. Rather, these stories transmitted deeply held values, and reflected abstract and sophisticated theological tenets. Read carefully, they provide us with a fascinating vantage point onto the challenges faced by Jews living in an often-hostile cultural climate.

showcases.title: Jewish Perspectives on the Ballot: Key Issues in the U.S. Midterms Using a Rabbinic Lens

videos.title: Week 4 - Jewish Perspectives on the Ballot: Key Issues in the U.S. Midterms Using a Rabbinic Lens
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded November 3, 2022
Instructor: Rabbi Deborah Silver
Emotions are high as we approach the U.S. midterm elections. When we exercise our right to vote, we rely on information from various sources in making our decisions, which may or may not include Jewish wisdom. This series will tackle four contentious topics, looking at how various streams of Jewish thought shed light on these issues. The aim is not to persuade anyone to vote in a particular way but rather to broaden our perspectives around these issues using Jewish lenses. Explore the challenging topics of gun control, abortion, climate change, and civil discourse.

showcases.title: Jewish Vienna: A Tale of Two Cities

videos.title: Week 3-Jewish Vienna: A City of Two Tales
videos.description:
Recorded November 3, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Steven Chervin
At the dawn of the 20th century, Vienna was home to both extraordinary Jewish creativity and alarming antisemitic fervor. Out of this complex and combustible environment emerged two dramatically different responses to what was then called the “Jewish Problem”: one that dreamed of a safe and sovereign future, and another that led the world into darkness. In this three-part series, Dr. Steven Chervin takes us inside the intellectual and political world of pre-war Vienna, exploring how this one city shaped both the birth of modern Zionism and the rise of genocidal antisemitism and what this geographical dichotomy might teach us about the challenges of Jewish life today.
Session 1 – The Atmosphere in Vienna: We’ll examine the figures who stirred public sentiment against Jews and shaped the city’s volatile social climate: political leaders like Karl Lueger, thinkers like Heinrich von Treitschke and Georg von Schönerer, and cultural critics like Otto Weininger and Karl Kraus. What ideas took hold, and why? How did Jewish identity begin to fracture under pressure?
Session 2 – Herzl and the Zionist Dream: Turning to Theodor Herzl, we’ll trace how a secular, assimilated playwright came to envision political Zionism as the only solution to Europe’s antisemitism. Through excerpts from Der Judenstaat, Altneuland, and key Zionist congresses, we’ll explore how Herzl’s vision was both embraced and critiqued by Jewish contemporaries across the ideological spectrum.
Session 3 – Hitler and the Path to Destruction: Finally, we’ll investigate Hitler’s early life in Vienna and the roots of the hatred that would shape his ideology. Using selections from Mein Kampf and public speeches, we’ll explore how antisemitism became central to his worldview and how the ideas circulating in Vienna helped pave the way for what came next.

Session 1 – The Atmosphere in Vienna: We’ll examine the figures who stirred public sentiment against Jews and shaped the city’s volatile social climate: political leaders like Karl Lueger, thinkers like Heinrich von Treitschke and Georg von Schönerer, and cultural critics like Otto Weininger and Karl Kraus. What ideas took hold, and why? How did Jewish identity begin to fracture under pressure?
Session 2 – Herzl and the Zionist Dream: Turning to Theodor Herzl, we’ll trace how a secular, assimilated playwright came to envision political Zionism as the only solution to Europe’s antisemitism. Together, we explore what made this city a turning point in Jewish history and what echoes we hear in our own time. As Jewish communities today face rising antisemitism, political polarization, and questions of identity and belonging, Vienna’s past offers both a cautionary tale and a call for attention.

showcases.title: Justice and the Jewish Story: Three Trials That Changed Everything

videos.title: Week 3-Justice and the Jewish Story: Three Trials that Changed Everything
videos.description:
Recorded November 24, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Katherine Aron-Beller
What does it mean to be judged by the law, by society, or by history? In this three-part course, we examine pivotal trials involving Jewish figures across time and place, from ancient Judea to modern France. These courtroom moments weren’t just legal proceedings; they were battlegrounds where questions of identity, power, fear, and justice were laid bare. Whether based in fact or fiction, each trial reveals how Jews have been seen by the societies in which they lived, and how they saw themselves. Together, we explore how trial records, literary texts, and public opinion can shape—or distort—the truth. Drawing from legal documents, historical analysis, and dramatic literature, we examine how personal motives, political pressures, and cultural tensions have all played out within the walls of the courtroom. What do these moments reveal about the role of Jews in broader society? And how can we read between the lines to understand the forces at work behind the verdict?

Session 1 - Jesus Before the Sanhedrin and Rome: We begin with perhaps the most scrutinized trial in Western history: the New Testament account of Jesus’ appearance before the Jewish Sanhedrin and Roman authorities. How has this trial shaped centuries of Christian-Jewish relations? What do we know—or assume—about the legal and political dynamics at play? We’ll explore the implications of this trial for Jewish identity and its long legacy in Christian thought.

Session 2 - Shylock in the Dock: In this session, we turn to Shakespeare’s The Merchant of Venice, focusing on the iconic trial scene. Is Shylock a villain, a victim, or something in between? Through a close reading of the text and historical context, we’ll uncover how the law becomes a stage for cultural anxieties about money, mercy, and the outsider. We ask how Shylock’s portrayal has shaped modern images of the Jew in literature and law.

Session 3 - The Dreyfus Affair and the Making of Modern Antisemitism: Our final session brings us to 19th-century France, where Captain Alfred Dreyfus, a Jewish officer in the French army, was falsely accused of treason. We examine how this trial exposed deep fault lines in French society and galvanized both nationalist and antisemitic movements. What does Dreyfus’s case tell us about the promises and perils of Jewish emancipation in modern Europe?

showcases.title: Light the Menorah, Light the Darkness, Light the World: Correlating Hanukkah and the Winter Solstice

videos.title: Week 2-Light the Menorah, Light the Darkness, Light the World: Correlating Hanukkah and the Winter Solstice
videos.description:
Recorded December 24, 2024
Instructor: Joe Septimus
In this course, taught by Joe Septimus, we will delve into two thought-provoking Talmudic passages: one that explores the lighting of Hanukkah candles, and another that reflects on the winter solstice. By weaving these texts together, we will shed light (pun intended) on how the symbolism of the candle-lighting ritual can deepen our understanding of time, change, and the seasons. Through this exploration, we’ll uncover how Jewish tradition and other cultures interpret these universal themes, fostering a richer connection between ritual practice and the cycles of nature. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

showcases.title: Lines in the Land: Meaning, Memory, and Membership

videos.title: Weel 3-Lines in the Lands: Meaning, Memory, and Membership
videos.description:
Recorded May 28, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski
What makes a piece of land part of the Land of Israel? And who gets to decide? For thousands of years, Jewish thinkers have debated how Israel is defined: by geography, by history, by law, or by something else entirely. In this three-part course, Rabbi Uri Pilichowsky guides us through surprising and often overlooked Jewish texts that raise real-world questions. Where do Israel’s borders begin and end? What gives the land its unique status? And why might simply being in Israel change how a person is judged or understood? Each session focuses on one key issue:

1. What Creates the Sanctity of the Land of Israel? Was the land made holy by divine promise, military conquest, or active Jewish presence? Ancient sources offer radically different views, and the debate continues into modern times. This session explores how Jewish legal thinkers have defined — and redefined — what makes the land special.
2. Can You Find Israel on a Map? The borders of Israel have never been fixed: not in the Bible, not in the Talmud, and not today. What do those changing lines tell us about the evolving relationship between the Jewish people and the land itself? We’ll explore maps, texts, and the meanings behind the boundaries.
3. Does Living in Israel Change How We See Ourselves? Some Jewish legal principles apply differently inside the Land of Israel than outside it. Why would that be? This session explores how being in the land might shift not only what we do, but how we think, from private individuals to part of a national story.

Whether you’re drawn to geography, law, identity, or big Jewish questions, this course offers a fresh and thought-provoking look at how the Land of Israel has been understood across time.

videos.title: 04 Live from Israel: From the Mountains to the Sea Recorded Mar. 29, 2022 Haim Aronovitz
videos.description: This series will explore areas north of Hadera and south of Haifa, considering various issues facing contemporary Israel: urbanization and environment; demographic change; and national identity. We will look at the singular contribution of the Rothschilds to this area before the State, at events around the War of Independence, and at the impact of development plans on the future of the region.

showcases.title: Living Forward and Becoming Our Elders

videos.title: Week 4-Living Forward and Becoming Our Elders
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded February 1, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Richard F. Address
Jewish texts and ideas provide a significant amount of support and guidance as we enter our later life stages. In this meaningful new course with Rabbi Richard Address, Founder and Director of Jewish Sacred Aging, we examine the power and passion of our tradition as it speaks to changes that we all experience as we age. We examine Jewish approaches to caregiving, physical and mental health, and discover new rituals designed for this life stage. Through challenging conversations and ideas, Rabbi Address aims to provide a roadmap for navigating this stage of life using Jewish traditions, texts, and experiences. We hope you’ll join us for this important learning opportunity.

Through challenging conversations and ideas, Rabbi Address aims to provide a roadmap for navigating this stage of life using Jewish traditions, texts, and experiences. We hope you’ll join us for this important learning opportunity.

showcases.title: Living in an Upside Down World

videos.title: Living in an Upside Down World
videos.description: This class will draw on Rabbi Ralph Genende’s recent book, an anthology of his writings on the many crises and challenges of the 21st century - from polarisation and loneliness, love and death, to climate change, and the war against Israel. It’s about our universal experiences and their moorings in a distinctive Jewish tradition as well as the unique Jewish conflicts and confrontations of our age. In the class we will explore some of the challenges of discovering meaning in a turbulent time with some readings and anecdotal stories from the book.

showcases.title: Looking Into It: A Rabbinic Guide to Personal Growth

videos.title: Week 2-Looking Into It: A Rabbinic Guide to Personal Growth
videos.description:
Recorded May 9, 2024
Instructor: Abby Eisenberg
In the classic rabbinic text, Pirkei Avot, the rabbis of late antiquity offer wisdom to guide us to become our best selves. Pirkei Avot suggest how we can improve upon our interpersonal (and intrapersonal) skills and develop an openness to learning and growth which are at the heart of Jewish living. As we analyze the many diverse rabbinic statements in this ancient text, we will glean intriguing (and humorous) observations about relationships with ourselves, family, and friends. Join us for this two-part series with hit Melton teacher Abby Eisenberg as we explore this essential text that is full of insights into human nature.

videos.title: Week 1-Looking Into It: A Rabbinic Guide to Personal Growth
videos.description:
Recorded May 2, 2024

We strive to ensure access and meaningful participation by all. As such, this program will have closed captioning.
Sponsored by Karen Zuckerstein in honor of the Eisenberg clan.

showcases.title: Machzor Masterclass: Inside the High Holiday Prayerbook

videos.title: Week 2-Machzor Masterclass: Inside the High Holiday Prayerbook
videos.description:
Recorded September 16, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Wallk
For many, the High Holiday services are the most emotionally charged—and most confusing—moments of the Jewish year. Even for seasoned synagogue-goers, the unfamiliar melodies, lengthy prayers, and theological language of the Mahzor can feel overwhelming. What are we saying? Why are we saying it? And how can we enter this season with greater understanding and intention?

In this two-session course, Rabbi Amy Wallk offers a guided journey through some of the most central and stirring pieces of the High Holiday liturgy. We’ll explore the unique three-part structure of the Rosh Hashanah Mussaf service and take a closer look at the confessional prayers of Yom Kippur. Along the way, we’ll consider both traditional and modern interpretations, uncovering layers of meaning that can deepen our experience in the sanctuary. Whether you’re a High Holiday regular or returning to services after time away, this class will help you find greater clarity, connection, and intention in the year’s holiest days, all guided by Rabbi Wallk’s insight, warmth, and scholarly depth.

showcases.title: Make It Your Business: What Judaism Has to Say About Business Ethics showcase

videos.title: Week 4-Make It Your Business: What Judaism Has to Say About Business Ethics
videos.description:
Recorded October 29, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Benjamin Kramer
Whether in person or online, we are involved in the buying and selling of goods and services on a day-to-day basis. In this modern world, all these transactions are potentially fraught with ethical dilemmas! Fortunately, Jewish tradition provides us with a moral compass to help us successfully navigate the often-complex world of commerce. In this course with expert Melton educator Rabbi Ben Kramer, we will explore traditional Jewish legal sources – such as the Talmud and the Shulchan Aruch - to understand the fundamental “kosher” business principles and how we can use them to guide our behavior. By exploring such topics as price fraud, ethical competition, and more we will gain new insights into how we conduct ourselves and engage with the world around us.

showcases.title: Mean What You Pray: A Guide to High Holiday Prayers

videos.title: Week 3-Mean What You Pray: A Guide to High Holiday Prayers
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded September 11, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Amy S. Wallk
Have you ever found yourself thinking, “High Holiday services seem long and repetitive and I don’t really understand what the prayers are saying!” And yet, something draws us back to these services every year. In this three-part course taught by Jewish liturgy scholar and expert Melton teacher Rabbi Amy Wallk, we will examine some of the most important High Holiday prayers. Together we will find ways to make these ancient texts accessible and meaningful, enriching our holiday season.

showcases.title: Melton Learning with Melton’s Leaders

videos.title: Week 6-Melton Learning with Melton's Leaders
videos.description:
Week 6
Recorded July 12, 2023
Instructors: Rabbi Rachel Bovitz and Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz
Jewish tradition has preserved a wellspring of timeless teachings for us, offering wisdom that can enrich almost every aspect of our lives. The challenge is that much of it can feel inaccessible, or veiled in a language that needs to be unpacked in order to be fully appreciated. Join the senior leadership of the Florence Melton School, Executive Director Rabbi Rachel Bovitz and International Director Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz, for a six-part exploration of the foundational teachings found in Pirkei Avot, a 2nd century tractate of the oral tradition passed down generation after generation and understood to include many of the most important and ever-relevant Jewish ethical values for living our lives. Through a close reading of these short but powerful texts, learners will gain a renewed appreciation for these invaluable pearls of wisdom.

showcases.title: Melton Meets the Moment

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment-Voting: An American Jewish Cultural Experience
videos.description:
Recorded October 31, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Steven Windmueller
Melton Meets the Moment presents unique offerings that enable participants to learn more about and discuss together a contemporary and timely topic through a Jewish lens.Trained as a political scientist, Dr. Steven Windmueller will be exploring the significance of the vote for American Jewish audiences. His presentation will examine the specifics of “the Jewish Contract with America” and identify political patterns and social trends in connection with how and why Jews vote.  Steven will also be exploring the specifics of the 2024 election and its particular impact for the Jewish community.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: The History of Antisemitism in the USA
videos.description:
Recorded February 26, 2024
Instructor: Professor Jonathan Sarna
Recently, we have witnessed a jarring spike in antisemitic activities in the United States. But what role has antisemitism actually played in American history? Is it a pervasive phenomenon, a late and alien phenomenon, or something in between? Join world-renowned Jewish historian Prof. Jonathan Sarna for this timely and insightful session tracing the course of antisemitism in the United States and considering the question of whether the Jewish American experience can be considered exceptional.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment- Stories of Israeli Resistance
videos.description:
Recorded February 6, 2024
Instructor: Viva Sarah Press
In the wake of the horrific attacks in Israel on October 7, Israelis have found new and innovative ways to be resilient during such a difficult time. Ranging from efforts in the technology sector all the way to Israel’s vibrant street art scene, Israel is showing the world that fighting back can mean more than just with weapons. Join veteran journalist and storyteller Viva Sarah Press to hear about inspiring stories of daily living, grassroots solidarity initiatives, and innovations-for-good. Give yourself the gift of hearing the heartwarming news that tells such a different story than the international headlines.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: Israel on the American College Campus
videos.description:
Recorded January 22, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Joy Getnick
As we face the global rise of antisemitism, American college campuses are particularly volatile with pro-Palestinian protests that delegitimize the right of the State of Israel to exist and use antisemitic tropes and language. As tensions flare and demonstrations become more intimidating for Jewish college students, it’s important to understand more clearly what is going on and how we got here. Join Dr. Joy Getnick, the Executive Director of Hillel at University of Rochester, for this timely topic and most importantly, for a discussion about what we can do to support Jewish students across the country.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: An Inside Look at the Foundation to Combat Antisemitism
videos.description:
Recorded December 14, 2023
Instructor: Matthew Berger, Foundation to Combat Antisemitism Senior Advisor
Jews around the world are feeling the impact of the war in Israel. While focused on what’s happening in Israel, we’re also keenly aware of the scary rise of antisemitism. While Jews make up only 2.4% of the U.S. population, they are the victims of 55% of all religious based hate crimes. Yet about half of Americans do not think that antisemitism is a major problem in this country. That disparity was central to the Foundation to Combat Antisemitism's decision to launch the Stand Up to Jewish Hate campaign last spring featuring their now signature Blue Square. The $25 million advertising campaign, specifically targeting people outside the Jewish community, raised awareness about antisemitism and built empathy for the Jewish community. Now, the Foundation is focused on reaching even more Americans and sharing the Blue Square as the universal symbol to rally support against Jewish hate. Join the Foundation to Combat Antisemitism’s senior advisor Matthew Berger as he shares with us what the Foundation is doing to fight back against antisemitism right now.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: Understanding the Backstory of the War in Israel
videos.description:
Recorded November 20, 2023
Instructor: Daniella Gefen
The horrific events of October 7th and the war that followed have left Israeli society and the Jewish people in a state of shock, pain, and horror. In response, Israelis have sprung into action in truly remarkable ways, enlisting to serve in the army, volunteering to support soldiers in a myriad of ways, and organizing to ensure the wellbeing of close to half a million people evacuated from their homes in the North and South. Alongside all these acts of kindness there is still so much we struggle to wrap our heads around. In this program, led by Melton Travel educator Daniella Gefen, we break down some of the most challenging questions facing Israel right now. How do Israelis understand their new reality? What sorts of immediate and long-term challenges are of greatest concern? What can we learn about Gaza’s history that can provide us with important context to understanding this war? How do Israelis view American support? And, most importantly, how will Israel get through this? Join us for another powerful Melton program exploring the current situation in Israel and Gaza.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: Israel at War
videos.description:
Recorded October 24, 2023
Instructor: Boris Dolin

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment- Israeli Judicial Reform
videos.description:
Recorded August 3, 2023
Instructor: Avi Ben-Hur
Finding it hard to understand the headlines about what is going on Israel? Concerned about the unprecedented current polarization of Israeli society? Wondering how we got here? You are not alone. In this presentation, expert Israeli educator specializing in Land of Israel studies Avi Ben-Hur shares a historical perspective and identifies what factors contributed to the current situation. Ben-Hur reviews the government’s proposed reforms, clarifies the key arguments for and against the reforms, and explores the potential political, economic, social, international and security ramifications of passing them. Join us for an objective analysis of one of Israel’s current most divisive political challenges.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment- Combating Antisemitism with JewBelong
videos.description:
Recorded June 12, 2023
Instructor: Archie Gottesman
The current rise in global antisemitism is startling. How can we feel safe in a world that is increasingly hostile to Judaism? And what should we be doing about it? In this session, we will do a deep dive into a very visible response we are seeing across American cities.  In an attempt to bring the danger of antisemitism to the forefront of society’s consciousness, JewBelong launched a multi-media campaign to call it out as loudly as possible. You might have seen its bright pink ads and billboards with such eye-catching language as “Does your church need armed guards? ‘Cause our synagogue does. #EndJewHate” or “Being woke and antisemitic is like being a vegan who eats veal. #EndJewHate”. How did this campaign come to be? What is its goal? And what can we be doing to help? Join JewBelong’s Co-founder Archie Gottesman to understand more deeply what JewBelong is doing to fight back against antisemitism.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: The State of Israel as a Jewish and Democratic State
videos.description:
Recorded March 28, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz
It is commonly thought that Jewish and democratic values are one and the same; however, current events in Israel and throughout the Western World are uncovering some uncomfortable truths that are indicating otherwise. Join Melton’s International Director, Rabbi Dr. Morey Schwartz, as he guides us through the study of some important, provocative foundational Jewish texts that will shed light on Israel's new reality.

videos.title: Melton Meets the Moment: Israel’s Reproductive Rights in the Context of a Post-Roe World
videos.description:
Recorded July 25, 2022
Instructor: Dina Shalev
With the recent SCOTUS decision overturning Roe vs. Wade, reproductive rights are at the forefront of political and legal discussions in the United States. While this topic has been debated for decades in America, the conversations in the Jewish context have been going on for centuries. The State of Israel’s take on this issue differs greatly from the American perspective but is no less controversial. To explore this important issue, we’ve invited Dina Shalev, Director of Lada’at (Choose Well) to share her expertise with us. Lada’at is a Jerusalem based organization dedicated to promoting the reproductive health and rights of women and girls in Israel by addressing the root causes that result in a lack of reproductive health services and infringement on reproductive rights. We hope you are able to join us for this timely program.

showcases.title: Melton's Parent Empowerment Initiative: Israel Education for Parents of Teens and College Students

videos.title: Session 4: Israel on the American College Campus
videos.description:
Recorded November 15, 2023


In this final session, parents will be presented with real-life cases – illustrative of the political climate related to Israel – that college students are encountering on their campuses. The case studies will serve as openings for parents to discuss their concerns about these issues and consider together how they can best prepare and support their kids should they encounter these or similar scenarios.

videos.title: Session 3: The Hamas Problem
videos.description:
Recorded November 14, 2023
How did Hamas emerge at the end of the 1980s as an extreme expression of Palestinian opposition to Israeli rule in the West Bank and Gaza, discontent with Palestinian leadership, and absolute rejection of diplomacy and negotiation between the State of Israel and the Palestinian people? What does the emergence of Hamas as a mainstay of Palestinian leadership and authority reflect about (1) the attitudes of Palestinians toward the State of Israel and Israeli Jews and (2) the role of radical Islam in Palestinian national aspirations.

videos.title: Session 2: The Israel-Palestinian Conflict: Origins, Complexities, and (Attempted) Solutions
videos.description:
Recorded November 13, 2023
How did the century-long conflict between Israelis and Palestinians begin, and why does it now look so seemingly irreconcilable? In this session, we study the competing national aims of Zionism and Palestinian nationalism -- two historical claims for the same territory -- how each intensified after the founding of the State of Israel, and the challenges of a two-state solution.

videos.title: Session 1: The Path to Statehood
videos.description:
Recorded November 12, 2023
The creation of the State of Israel in 1948 culminated nearly a century of state-building by Jews in the Land of Israel.  In this session, we take a close look at the complexities of this remarkable, arduous task that expanded an enclave of small Jewish communities in Eretz Yisrael in 1880 — the Old Yishuv — into the New Yishuv, an extensive network of prosperous agriculture settlements and modern cities. We consider also how various Zionist ideas and visions of the Jewish State guided this state-building process and became pillars of the State of Israel.

showcases.title: Members of the Tribe: Cornerstones of Jewish Identity

videos.title: Week 6 - Members of the Tribe: Cornerstones of Jewish Identity
videos.description:
Recorded December 22, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Amy S. Wallk
What does it really mean to be Jewish today? In a world where identity is constantly questioned and belonging is complex, this six-session course offers a powerful exploration of what ties us together as a people. More than a history lesson, this is a vital conversation about what it means to be Jewish right now.

Offered via Melton+, this piece of Melton's Living Wisdom curriculum is now available for the first time to the thousands of learners who live in communities that do not yet offer Melton in-person learning. Additionally, through our online platform, we are excited to be piloting an AI-powered digital teacher’s assistant to assist learners with the exploration and investigation of course material between class sessions.

From the story of the Exodus to the meaning of “Chosenness,” from daily rituals like Kashrut to the role of Israel as a central focus for Jewish identity, we’ll unpack the values, symbols, and struggles that shape our people and find their connection to our daily lives. Together, we’ll wrestle with big questions, discover personal meaning, and uncover how understanding “the Tribe” – our shared history, values, and responsibilities – gives us tools to respond to the challenges of our time, both individually and collectively. Join us on this journey to empower us to live more meaningful Jewish lives and create a more just world.

Lesson 1 (Nov 17) - Living a Jewish Life: Finding Purpose Through Reflection
Lesson 2 (Nov 24) - Let My People Grow: The Exodus from Egypt as a Jewish Calling
Lesson 3 (Dec 1) - Just Remember: Symbols and Reminders in Judaism
Lesson 4 (Dec 8) - Chosen People, Chosen Purpose: The concept of “chosenness” through both a traditional and a contemporary lens
Lesson 5 (Dec 15) - Holy Guacamole: What Does It Mean to Keep Kosher?
Lesson 6 (Dec 22) - Jerusalem If I Forget You…: Israel and the Diaspora

showcases.title: Missed Connections: Linking the Haftarah to a Fresh Start

videos.title: Week 4-Missed Connections: Linking the Haftarah to a Fresh Start
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded March 8, 2023
Instructor: Kira Sirote
Jewish life presents us with many opportunities for personal and national renewal. One of those opportunities occurs during the weeks leading up to Passover. During this period of time, we have a series of special Torah and Haftarah readings (from the books of the Prophets) that explore themes such as how society values money, what makes a war worth fighting for, the importance of leadership, and more. Join Haftarah expert Kira Sirote, as we discover the connections between these special Biblical readings that teach us about our tradition’s high expectations of the society in which we live. This societal roadmap lays out an urgent new perspective on this season of national renewal.

showcases.title: Mixed Messages: Harkening to the Voice

videos.title: Mixed Messages: Harkening to the Voice of the Shofar
videos.description: The sounding of the shofar is a bit of mystery. As we gather together in synagogues around the world to hear the ancient blasts of the shofar, we cannot help but wonder what this timeless practice is really all about.  Why is this the central custom associated with the Jewish New Year? What are we meant to be thinking or feeling as the repeated, familiar blasts fill the hushed sanctuary?

showcases.title: Moses Meets Madison: Comparing Jewish and American Law

videos.title: Week 4-Moses Meets Madison: Comparing Jewish and American Law
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded June 27, 2023
Instructor: Harman Grossman
As American Jews, we have a foot in two different legal systems. How do Jewish and American law treat specific issues that confront us today – capital punishment, abortion, and more? What are Jewish and American perspectives on the role of law in our lives, overall? What do the similarities and differences between the two legal systems reveal about the cultures that produced them? Most importantly, what do these two bodies of law say about us – what we believe, what we will fight for, who we are? Join veteran lawyer and Jewish Studies teacher Harman Grossman for a text-based exploration of two rich legal traditions, and ultimately, of ourselves.

videos.title: Cairo Genizah Curriculum Webinar
videos.description:
Curriculum author and former Melton Director, Dr. Shelley Buxbaum, in conversation with Melton’s Educational Project Coordinator Judy Snowbell Diamond "Exploring a Forgotten World" reveals how a collection of worn-out scraps, fragments of texts and other manuscripts and documents more than 1,000 years old open an otherwise hidden era of history. It explores the daily lives and behaviors of Jewish men, women and families living along the Mediterranean during the middle ages.
 
Recorded 14 November 2019

showcases.title: OMG, Can You Believe!

videos.title: Week 6-OMG, Can You Believe?!
videos.description:
Recorded February 9, 2026
Instructor: Rabbi Amy S. Wallk
In this six-week exploratory course, we dive into the BIG questions around God and God’s relationship to Humankind. We’ll examine the many different understandings of God, free will, and mitzvot expressed by Jewish philosophers, rabbis, leaders past and present – and you. We’ll explore the divine, the prophetic, and even the supernatural, as we amplify and dissect your deepest God-questions. Just do not expect to leave with any final answers.

Table of Contents

Lesson 1 - God-ness Gracious
Lesson 2 - The Human Journey: Are we there yet?!
Lesson 3 - If not-for-Prophets...
Lesson 4 - The Super, the Natural, and the Supernatural

showcases.title: One Page at a Time: Study Talmud Like a Pro!

videos.title: Week 3-One Page at a Time: Study Talmud Like a Pro
videos.description:
Recorded July 1, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Benjamin Kramer 
The Talmud is one of the most important texts in Judaism, but it can also be one of the most intimidating. In this three-part program with renowned Melton teacher Rabbi Ben Kramer, we will engage in an in-depth study of a single page of Talmud. Using an English translation, we will make our way line by line through Tractate Bava Metzia 21, in which the Talmud discusses the obligation to return lost objects. In addition to studying the text of the Talmud itself, we will also study relevant portions of the traditional commentaries of Rashi and Tosafot, which are featured on the page itself. During our study, we will discuss the development of the Talmud and the history of its printing, which will enhance our appreciation of the text. If you have always wanted to experience the joy of deep Talmud study, this is the perfect opportunity!

showcases.title: Our Animals, Our World, and Ourselves

videos.title: Week 4-Our Animals, Our World, and Ourselves
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded June 6, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Jonathan Bernhard
Back by popular demand, join Jewish Initiative for Animals’ Executive Director Rabbi Jonathan Bernhard as he expands on his hit Melton class, “Animal Welfare, Not Warfare”! Open to those who did or did not take his first class, this four-part course will explore the fundamentals of Jewish Animal Ethics from a variety of perspectives. What foundational concepts can be found in the Torah and Talmud? How are these ideas expanded upon in the works and worldviews of modern Jewish thinkers such as author I.B. Singer and Rabbi Ovadia Yosef, the former Sephardic Chief Rabbi of Israel? Through these questions and more, Rabbi Bernhard will bring into focus key Jewish principles that allow us to grow our own souls and help us approach our world in a healthier way.

showcases.title: Parent Empowerment Initiative: 5 Issues That Matter

videos.title: Parent Empowerment Initiative - Antisemitism: The Oldest Hatred
videos.description:
Recorded April 16, 2024
Instructor: Sivan Rotholz 
As we see an uptick in antisemitic incidents around the world, this class will uncover the deep roots of antisemitism and how it has changed throughout the ages. Focusing on the current forms of anti-Zionism and anti-Israel sentiment, we will explore the newest incarnations of one of history’s oldest forms hatred.

videos.title: Parent Empowerment Initiative - Israel, October 7th, and What Comes Next?
videos.description:
Recorded April 8, 2024
Instructor: Daniella Gefen, a world-renowned Israel educator who has spent the last 17 years leading trips for AIPAC, the Jerusalem Press Club, Hebrew University, Melton, and more 
This program explores the current state and potential of the two-state solution, the players in the region, and what the future might have in store for Israel.

videos.title: Parent Empowerment Initiative - Click, Like, Connect: Digital Conduct, Your Kids, and You
videos.description:
Recorded April 3, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem has taught middle and high school students, college students, and adults for the past 25+ years. 
What does responsible parenting look like in the social media age? In this program, we will delve into social media usage, online conduct, and digital citizenship through a Jewish ethical lens.

videos.title: Parent Empowerment Initiative - Living in a Material World: Business Ethics in Everyday Life
videos.description:
Recorded March 27, 2024 
Instructor: Rabbi Adam Schaffer, Education Director at HAMAKOM in Woodland Hills, California
Wondering if we are being taken advantage of in a business transaction is actually an age-old concern. Together we will investigate Jewish ethical teachings that show us how both businesses and their customers can find a harmonious balance in this material world.

videos.title: Parent Empowerment Initiative - Purity of Arms: The Israeli Military’s Code of Ethics
videos.description:
Recorded March 20, 2024 
Instructor: Joel Chasnoff, who is the author of Israel 201, this year’s recipient of the National Jewish Book Award, as well as a former Israeli soldier.
As the world watches the ongoing war in Gaza, the media is raising questions about whether or not Israel’s military is carrying out its operations ethically. Join us for an inside look at the Israeli army’s code of ethics as we examine this critical issue.

showcases.title: People of the Book - American Jewish Literature

videos.title: Week 3-People of the Book - American Literature
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded August 4, 2022
Instructor: Dr. Joellyn Zollman
What modern stories do we, as American Jews, remember and tell about ourselves? From your bookshelves, the best-seller lists, and modern scholarship on Jewish literature and popular culture, Dr. Zollman has selected three titles that play an important role in shaping modern American Jewish memory and identity: Tevye the Dairyman/Fiddler on the Roof, The Diary of Anne Frank, and Exodus. Together, we’ll explore the creation, production, and reception of these books, and discuss what their popularity can tell us about American Jewish life.

showcases.title: Perfect Pitch: Music’s Role in Jewish History

videos.title: Week 4-Perfect Pitch: Music’s Role in Jewish History
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded March 2, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
The Jewish people have a rich history of music playing a vital role in our celebrations, prayers, the way we communicate with one another, and even our mourning rituals. In this 4-week course taught by Melton-favorite Rabbi Johnny Solomon, participants learn about song and music throughout different historical periods, in the Synagogue, in the Jewish homes, and in other Jewish communal settings. Throughout this series we ask significant questions such as: What makes Jewish music Jewish? What role does music play in times of joy and sorrow? How can we harness the power of music to bring greater joy and unity to the Jewish people and to the world? Through the exploration of our musical past, we will gain new insights into the shaping of Jewish culture today.

showcases.title: Praying as Human, Praying as a Jew

videos.title: Week 4 - Praying as Human, Praying as a Jew
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded May 24, 2022
Rabbi Dr. Amy Wallk
The Jewish people have used prayer throughout the annals of time, turning toward God for gratitude and petitions. These prayers of deep introspection embody hopes, desires, and yearnings for oneself, one’s community and for humanity as a whole. Delve into Jewish prayer and explore the tension between praying for oneself and praying for the universal.

showcases.title: Purim 5786 - March 2026

videos.title: Visions of Esther: Painted Portraits of Faith and Fate
videos.description:
Recorded March 11, 2025
Instructor: Evgenia Kempinski
Throughout history, artists from different cultures have offered unique perspectives on Queen Esther, Mordechai, King Ahasuerus, and Haman, shaping our understanding of their roles and the broader meaning of the Purim story.  

This fascinating session will examine how Italian, French, and Dutch artistic traditions have depicted the Book of Esther, with a special focus on 17th-century Dutch art, where Esther emerged as a symbol of independence during the Netherlands' struggle against Spanish rule. Artists like Rembrandt and his contemporaries infused the story with themes of resilience, courage, and identity, reflecting the social and political realities of their time.  

Through virtual visits to renowned institutions such as the Hermitage Museum in St. Petersburg, the Pushkin Museum in Moscow, the Louvre in Paris, and the Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam, we will analyze celebrated and controversial paintings. Discussions will focus on Esther's evolving imagery—from young and innocent to mature and decisive—as well as the complex characterizations of Ahasuerus, Mordechai, and Haman.  

This session is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

videos.title: Tipsy Turvy: The Obligation to Get Drunk on Purim
videos.description:
Recorded March 20, 2024 
Instructor: Rabbi Ben Kramer 
Beginning with the story of Noah, Jewish tradition repeatedly warns us about the dangers of imbibing too much wine. But on the holiday of Purim there seems to be an obligation to get drunk! In this special holiday class with acclaimed Melton teacher Rabbi Ben Kramer, we will study a variety of classical rabbinic texts to better understand the nature of this unusual obligation and the meaning behind it. We will also explore how rabbis throughout the generations have tried to reconcile this obligation with the generally negative attitude toward drunkenness in Jewish tradition.

videos.title: Getting (Un)Comfortable with Megillat Esther
videos.description:
Recorded March 14, 2022
Instructor: Rabbi Daniel Reifman, PhD
The book of Esther is one of the best known narratives in the Bible, the core of one of the most popular holidays on the Jewish calendar.  Yet a closer look at this familiar story reveals some unsettling elements, among them the Persian culture that pervades the book and the uncomfortable position that the Jewish characters occupy within it.  What does Esther suggest about Jewish peoplehood in the Diaspora and how does this reflect on Jewish identity in our own time?

videos.title: The Book of Esther: "Biblical Leadership, Dual Loyalties and the Divided Self"
videos.description: Dr. Erica Brown leads a discussion on The Book of Esther: "Biblical Leadership, Dual Loyalties and the Divided Self". Recorded 27 Feb 2020.

showcases.title: Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks z’l: A Spiritual Biography

videos.title: Week 4-Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks z'l- A Spiritual Biography
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded November 8, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
If you know a scholar well enough, you see how the ideas they share are windows to their soul. In this ground-breaking course, Rabbi Johnny Solomon, a dedicated student of Rabbi Lord Sacks z’l, will share a range of teachings of Rabbi Sacks on themes relating to identity, self-esteem, failure, and joy. While doing so, we will gain a knowledge and understanding of the great man who was the Chief Rabbi of England but whose teachings impacted contemporary Jews from around the world.

showcases.title: Religious Pluralism - Threat or Opportunity

videos.title: Week 4-Religious Pluralism - Threat or Opportunity
videos.description: The fact that diverse religious opinions and positions stand side by side with one another in our world is inescapable. Religious pluralism is a feature of living in a multicultural world. As a result, it has become challenging for any one position to claim absolute authority and truth. How does Judaism deal with this modern dilemma? In this course, we’ll look at the various problems that religious pluralism raises for 20th century Jewish thinkers, against the backdrop of Jewish history and theology. Among the figures we’ll be considering will be Rabbi Joseph B. Soloveitchik, Rabbi Abraham Joshua Heschel, Rabbi Eugene Borowitz, Rabbi David Hartman, Rabbi Yitz Greenberg, and Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks.

showcases.title: Restless Soul: Mordecai Kaplan and the Reinvention of American Judaism

videos.title: Week 3-Restless Soul: Mordecai Kaplan and the Reinvention of American Judaism
videos.description:
Recorded February 26, 2026
Instructors: Rabbi Ed Feinstein and Professor Jenna Weissman Joselit
Rabbi Mordecai Kaplan was admired by many, challenged by others, and never stopped trying to reinvent what it meant to live a Jewish life in the modern world. In this special three-part Melton+ series presented in cooperation with Valley Beth Shalom, Rabbi Ed Feinstein sits down with historian Jenna Weissman Joselit, author of the forthcoming Jewish Lives biography Mordecai M. Kaplan: Restless Soul, for a candid and compelling exploration of Kaplan’s life and legacy. Founder of the Reconstructionist movement, fierce critic of inherited dogma, and tireless advocate for a Judaism that could speak to modern life, Kaplan reshaped American Jewish identity and ignited ongoing debate about faith, community, and change.

Together, they’ll explore:

Part 1: Kaplan’s World – An introduction to the early 20th-century American Jewish landscape and the tensions that gave rise to Kaplan’s bold critique.
Part 2: Upheaval – A close and personal look at how Kaplan transformed not only American Jewish life but also himself.
Part 3: In His Own Words – A deep dive into Kaplan’s writings, including some of his earliest and most overlooked works.
Jenna Weissman Joselit, Charles E. Smith Professor of Judaic Studies and Professor of History at George Washington University, is known for her engaging, accessible, and original approach to Jewish cultural history. Her book draws on Kaplan’s private diaries and personal correspondence to paint an intimate portrait of a restless, brilliant, and sometimes frustrating thinker. With warmth, insight, and a healthy dose of humor, Rabbi Ed Feinstein—a Melton+ favorite—guides the conversation and helps bring Kaplan’s voice into dialogue with the Jewish present.
