showcases.title: Return and Renew: High Holiday Teachings of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks

videos.title: Week 3-Return and Renew: High Holiday Teachings of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks
videos.description:
Recorded August 25, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
The High Holidays ask us to confront life’s biggest questions: What does it mean to be alive? What responsibilities do we carry into a new year? And how do we repair what’s been broken, both within and between us? In this three-part course, Rav Johnny Solomon guides us through the wisdom of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks, offering a deeply personal and profoundly Jewish meditation on what it means to reflect, return, and begin again. Drawing from the former Chief Rabbi’s commentary in the Koren Machzor and other writings, Rav Johnny will explore three major themes from Sacks's High Holiday teachings: 
“The Gift and Responsibilities of Life” - What does it mean to be given life, and what are we called to do with it?
“Why Life Is Not Easy, and Why That Isn’t a Bad Thing” - How do struggle and challenge shape us, and why are they essential to spiritual growth?
“The Blessing to Forgive and to Be Forgiven” - What role does forgiveness play in renewal, and how can we approach it with honesty and courage?

showcases.title: Sacred Stones: The Journey of Jewish Nationhood through Israel's Ancient Sites showcase

videos.title: Week 4-Sacred Stones:The Journey of Jewish Nationhood through Israel's Ancient Sites
videos.description:
Recorded November 20, 2024
Instructor: Daniella Gefen
This captivating series delves into the formation of Jewish national and religious identity by exploring the creation of holy sites and the intricate relationship between faith, people, ritual, memory, and salvation. Through the lens of groundbreaking archaeological discoveries in Israel, spanning from the era of King David to the 6th century A.C., participants journey through ancient history, uncovering the roots and evolution of a people and their enduring legacy. This class is generously sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

showcases.title: Script & Scripture: Torah & Shakespeare’s Comedy of Errors

videos.title: Week 4-Script & Scripture: Torah & Shakespeare’s Comedy of Errors
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded December 18, 2023
Instructor: Cantor Matt Austerklein
In this expansion of Cantor Austerklein’s hit Melton class, “The Bard & the Bible”, we will be taking a deep dive into the shared themes from the Torah and William Shakespeare’s classic “Comedy of Errors”. Join Cantor Austerklein for a fun and exciting exploration of the religious imaginations of the rabbis and the bard of Stratford-on-Avon. This four-part series discusses marriage and magic, exploring surprising ideas in both traditions that will resonate today. In the words of Shakespeare himself, “The love of heaven makes one heavenly.”

showcases.title: Seder Secrets: Unlocking the Story Within the HaggadahNew showcase

videos.title: Week 2- Seder Secrets- Unlocking the Hidden Story in the Haggadah
videos.description:
Recorded April 8, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Wallk
Each year at the Passover Seder, we follow 14 steps, recite familiar words, and retell the Exodus story—but beneath the rituals lie hidden meanings and powerful messages. How do these steps truly connect to the deeper themes of freedom, redemption, and hope? How can they spark meaningful conversations around our tables?

In this engaging session with Rabbi Amy Wallk, we will unlock the secrets of the Maggid section, exploring the key lessons of the Exodus and their relevance today. We’ll also dive into the mysteries of Eliyahu HaNavi, the call of "Next Year in Jerusalem," and the deeper messages woven into the structure of the Seder itself. Whether you lead your family’s discussion or simply want to bring fresh insights to the table, this class will help you uncover new layers of meaning and make your Passover experience more engaging and memorable.

showcases.title: Sephardic Jewry: Between Two Worlds

videos.title: Week 2-Sephardic Jewry: Between Two Worlds
videos.description:
Recorded January 22, 2025
Instructor: Avi Ben-Hur
Step into the rich and complex world of Sephardic Jewry on the Iberian Peninsula (what is now modern Spain and Portugal) at a time when Jewish life flourished amid two dominant civilizations. From groundbreaking poetry and philosophy to contributions that shaped Spanish society, Sephardic Jews navigated the dynamic intersection of Muslim and Christian cultures with resilience and brilliance. How did they preserve their unique identity while navigating these two powerful cultures? What conditions enabled their political and cultural golden age, and what forces brought it to its abrupt and tragic end? Join Melton Travel Guide and Educator Avi Ben-Hur for this engaging two-session journey to uncover the incredible legacy of Sephardic Jewry. Together, we’ll explore how their achievements and experiences continue to inspire and influence Jewish traditions and identity today. This program is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

showcases.title: Shalom Y’all - October 2023

videos.title: Week 3-Shalom Y'all: Jewish History and Identity in the American South
videos.description:
Week 3
Recorded October 25, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Joellyn Zollman
This three-part class introduces students to the deep roots and regional features of Southern Jewish life, with a focus on the two communities that we will travel to in November: Savannah and Charleston. Our learning adventure will start in the classroom, where we all take a deep dive into three key areas of Southern Jewish life: settlement, Civil War and Civil Rights. We invite you to continue the journey on the cobblestone streets of Charleston and Savannah from November 5-10, 2023.

showcases.title: Shavuot as Metamorphosis

videos.title: Week 2-Shavuot as Metamorphosis
videos.description:
Recorded June 10, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon 
The holiday of Shavuot celebrates two very different occurrences: it’s both a celebration of the harvest and a celebration of when we received the Ten Commandments. In this two-part series with acclaimed Melton teacher Rabbi Johnny Solomon, we will explore the metamorphosis of Shavuot from being a harvest festival to becoming the festival celebrating the receiving of the Torah. We will also reflect on the personal and spiritual metamorphosis of Ruth, whose remarkable Biblical story is read on Shavuot. Join us for an enlightening exploration of one of Judaism’s most joyful holidays.

This series is sponsored by Randi Brenowitz in honor of the fabulous men and women on the International Melton Board of Directors.

showcases.title: Shtetl Fabulous

videos.title: Week 4-Shtetl Fabulous
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded November 9, 2023
Instructor: Dr. Rachel Seelig
The shtetl (Yiddish for “small town”) is one of the most recognized symbols of Jewish life in Eastern Europe before the Holocaust. But what exactly was it? Since the mid-nineteenth century, Yiddish writers have mythologized, mocked, and memorialized the shtetl. In this four-session course, we will explore literary representations of the shtetl ranging from the sublime to the ridiculous. How has the shtetl been portrayed, and why does it still have such a strong hold on the Jewish imagination? showcases.title: Soul Searching: Jewish Views on the Afterlife

videos.title: Week 4-Soul Searching- Jewish Views on the Afterlife
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded August 28, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
In Judaism we use the word “soul” and the phrase “the world to come,” but do we really know what they mean? We won’t truly know about the afterlife until our moment arrives, leaving us with many more questions than answers. How does Judaism view the idea of the “soul”? What happens to the soul after we die? Do we have one soul during our lifetime or do we have a chance to develop our souls after life ends? Do Jews believe in reincarnation or people communicating with us from beyond the grave? What does Judaism think of mediums? Join expert Melton teacher Rabbi Michele Faudem for this meaningful, four-part exploration of Jewish views on all these topics related to the afterlife.

showcases.title: Speaking Volumes: Complaints, Community, and the Power of Words
Spiritually Preparing for the High Holidays in the Wake of October 7th

videos.title: Week 3-Spiritually Preparing for the High Holidays in the Wake of October 7th
videos.description:
Recorded September 19, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Amy Wallk 
The Jewish High Holidays are designed to both inspire and humble us. They are times for profound introspection and an opportunity for meaningful personal change. But this year each of us as Jews have more on our minds than our personal growth. In the wake of all that happened on October 7 and all that has followed, how do we spiritually prepare ourselves for the High Holidays in a different way? What are the themes and ideas that can help us to make meaning after this incredibly painful year? In this new course from High Holiday liturgical expert Rabbi Amy Wallk, we will talk about how our liturgy and traditions give us the tools to look towards the future, holding space for the pain of yesterday while dreaming for a better tomorrow.

showcases.title: Technology Ethics

videos.title: Week 4-Technology Ethics
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded July 31, 2023
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
With TikTok and ChatGPT in our daily headlines, it feels as though technology is inescapable. The fact is that technology is a major part of all our lives and it seems like it will be even more so in the future.  With this reality, it is more important than ever to seriously consider the ethical issues technology brings to the forefront. In this four-part course taught by expert Melton teacher Rabbi Johnny Solomon, we will examine a number of these ethical dilemmas. We will explore the moral issues that exist from sharing our lives and interacting with others on social media. We’ll also examine a range of ethical issues emerging from the use of Artificial Intelligence in travel, healthcare, and by the military. We don’t only highlight the problems, though, as we also consider the positive ways in which communities are enriched by these technologies. Join us for this timely course exploring some of our most pressing contemporary ethical issues.

showcases.title: The American Presidents and Israel

videos.title: Week 4-The American Presidents and Israel
videos.description:
Recorded February 3, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski
American presidents are often labeled as pro- or anti-Israel, but what does that really mean? How are these judgments formed, and what influences the policies and decisions of the U.S. presidency regarding Israel? Over four compelling sessions, Rabbi Uri Pilichowski, a Zionism educator and expert on Israel and U.S.-Israel relations, will guide you through the complex, evolving relationship between American presidents and the State of Israel. Session One: Foundations of a Relationship (Lincoln to Truman) Explore the early years of American engagement with the land of Israel, tracing the ideological and political roots that would shape future policy. From Lincoln’s vision of a homeland for Jews to Truman’s pivotal role in recognizing the State of Israel, this session examines the seeds of the U.S.-Israel relationship.
Session Two: From Cold War to Camp David (Eisenhower to Reagan) Delve into the era of post-World War II geopolitics and the Cold War, examining how presidents from Eisenhower to Reagan navigated U.S. interests in the Middle East. Topics include Kennedy's arms sales, Johnson's lack of support during the Six-Day War, Nixon’s support in the Yom Kippur War, Carter's historic Camp David Accords, and Reagan's criticism of Israel's attack on Iraq.
Session Three: Strategic Partnerships and Challenges (Bush 41 to Obama) Analyze the growing strategic alliance between the U.S. and Israel, focusing on critical moments such as the Gulf War, the Oslo Accords, and the Iran deal. This session explores how domestic and international factors influenced the policies of Presidents Bush (41), Clinton, Bush (43), and Obama.
Session Four: Recent Policies and Defining a Pro-Israel Presidency (Trump and Biden)
Examine the most recent administrations and their impact on U.S.-Israel relations, from Trump’s recognition of Jerusalem as Israel’s capital to Biden’s support and pressure during the 2023-2024 war. This session concludes with a discussion of the factors that define a "pro-Israel" president and the evolving criteria for this designation. Join us to uncover the history, politics, and personal convictions that have defined this enduring alliance. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

showcases.title: The Bible's #MeToo Moments

videos.title: Week 4-The Bible's #MeToo Moments
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded November 13, 2023
Instructor: Sivan Rotholtz
The Hebrew Bible is no stranger to rape, murder, victim-blaming, and some of the earliest recorded #MeToo moments in human history. Each week during this four-part series we'll pair the story of biblical women who suffered at the hands of violent patriarchal misogyny with midrash and poetry that adds depth and nuance to our understanding. We will reflect on what each of these women has to teach us, adding our own voices to an age-old tradition of wrestling with difficult Jewish texts. Join feminist Torah scholar and soon-to-be-Rabbi Sivan Rotholz as we consider, reconsider, and take a deep dive into a selection of #MeToo tales from biblical literature.

showcases.title: The Burden and Blessing of Exile

videos.title: Week 4-The Burden and Blessing of Exile
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded April 24, 2023
Instructor: Lynne Lieberman
Since the beginning, the Jewish people have been persecuted and forced to wander from land to land. Hinted at in the book of Daniel is the idea of there being Arba Galuyot, or Four Exiles. Our first exile to Egypt was self-imposed due to famine. Later, conquerors of Israel destroyed the Temple in Jerusalem and sent us away. Then as Christianity spread, growing antisemitism forced Jews to be on the move. This was followed by the Crusades, expulsions, pogroms, and the Holocaust which forced further exiles. We will explore each of these “four exiles” with a focus on both how the experiences have shaped the Jewish people as well as the Jewish people’s influence on the societies where they made their new homes. Join expert Melton educator, Lynne Lieberman, on this four-part exploration of Jewish migration across land and time to shed light on how our global journey has had profound implications for our Jewish self-identity and the Jewish influences that can be felt in the communities where we’ve lived.

Join expert Melton educator, Lynne Lieberman, on this four-part exploration of Jewish migration across land and time to shed light on how our global journey has had profound implications for our Jewish self-identity and the Jewish influences that can be felt in the communities where we’ve lived.

videos.title: Week 1-The Burden and Blessing of Exile
videos.description:
Week 1
Recorded April 3, 2023
Instructor: Lynne Lieberman
Since the beginning, the Jewish people have been persecuted and forced to wander from land to land. Hinted at in the book of Daniel is the idea of there being Arba Galuyot, or Four Exiles. Our first exile to Egypt was self-imposed due to famine. Later, conquerors of Israel destroyed the Temple in Jerusalem and sent us away. Then as Christianity spread, growing antisemitism forced Jews to be on the move. This was followed by the Crusades, expulsions, pogroms, and the Holocaust which forced further exiles. We will explore each of these “four exiles” with a focus on both how the experiences have shaped the Jewish people as well as the Jewish people’s influence on the societies where they made their new homes. Join expert Melton educator, Lynne Lieberman, on this four-part exploration of Jewish migration across land and time to shed light on how our global journey has had profound implications for our Jewish self-identity and the Jewish influences that can be felt in the communities where we’ve lived.

showcases.title: The Complex Drama of Forgiveness: Stories of Struggle, Power, and Repair showcase

videos.title: Week 2-The Complex Drama of forgiveness: Stories of Struggle, Power, and Repair
videos.description:
Recorded September 2, 2025
Instructor: Joe Septimus
Some of Judaism’s most powerful teachings are hidden in the heart of human interaction, as told through Talmudic stories. In this pre-high holiday class, Joe Septimus delves into two profound rabbinic narratives that explore the complex emotional terrain of personal flaws, offensive behavior, resentment, apology, and the hope for repair. At the intersection of power, judgment, social norms, and personal limits, these stories reveal our rabbis’ deep awareness of human vulnerability. They show how Jewish law alone can’t address every emotional or ethical challenge, and how stories helped the sages wrestle with human failure, conflict, and real-life struggles. Through layered discussion and thoughtful interpretation, we’ll explore what these narratives say about the human cost of misunderstanding, the challenge of forgiveness, and the courage it takes to move forward. The class presents a meaningful opportunity to embark on your own season of introspection, with honesty, compassion, and a commitment to change.

showcases.title: The Empowered Caregiver: Chesed and Kavod in Dementia Care

videos.title: Week 2-The Empowered Caregiver: Chesed and Kavod in Dementia Care
videos.description:
Recorded May 7, 2026
Instructors: Jane Michaels Grauman, LCSW and Yael Wyte, MSW
Caring for someone living with dementia can change the rhythms of daily life, and it can also change the way you speak, listen, and respond to the people you love most. This two-part Empowered Caregiver series, offered in partnership with the Alzheimer’s Association, brings practical caregiving tools into a Melton context shaped by the values that so often carry caregivers through: kavod (dignity), chesed (lovingkindness), and the steady work of showing up, again and again.
 
Led by Janie Michaels Grauman, LCSW, an Alzheimer’s Association Community Educator, these sessions offer clear guidance, usable strategies, and space for real questions. Alzheimer's Association Program & Education Manager Yael Wyte will help frame each class so the skills don’t just feel clinical but are connected to meaning, resilience, and relationships.

Class 1: Communicating Effectively
Learn how dementia affects communication and explore concrete tips for communicating with greater clarity and less stress, whether you’re speaking with family and friends or advocating with health care professionals.
Class 2: Responding to Dementia-Related Behaviors
Understand common behavior changes as a form of communication, learn non-medical approaches to responding, and recognize when it may be time to seek additional support.

showcases.title: The Greatest Israeli Stories Never Told

videos.title: Week 4-The Greatest Israeli Stories Never Told
videos.description:
Recorded May 1, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Uri Pilichowski
History is often shaped by the stories we know—but what about the ones we don’t? In this fascinating four-part series, rabbi and raconteur Uri Pilichowski unearths the little-known yet remarkable moments from Israeli history that shaped the nation. From the time of the Temples to modern military feats, these stories reveal the resilience, ingenuity, and sheer audacity that define Israel’s past and present. 

Pre-State - Ancient Legacies and the Fight for Independence: Long before the establishment of the State of Israel, Jewish history in the land was shaped by legendary figures, pivotal battles, and cultural shifts that continue to influence Israeli identity today. Why do so many Jewish people bear the Greek name Alex, Alexander, or Alexa, while names like Theos and Damian are almost nonexistent? And how did the 1929 riots, remembered as the Hebron riots, actually begin in Jerusalem? This session traces the hidden narratives of Jewish perseverance and rebellion from Temple times through the struggle against the British Mandate. 

The Start of Israel - Founders, Fighters, and Fortunes: The early years of the Jewish state were a whirlwind of impossible victories, bold leadership, and strategic gambles that defined the country’s character. How did a group of Ultra-Orthodox Yeshiva students- who don’t typically serve in the IDF- manage to stop the Jordanian army from conquering Jerusalem in 1948? And what happened to the billions of dollars in German reparations that disappeared under Ben-Gurion’s watch? This session uncovers the untold stories of the founders who laid the groundwork for the Israel we know today.

The Ground Settles - From Survival to Success: As Israel transitioned from a struggling new state to an emerging powerhouse, it faced moments of both unexpected challenge and triumph. Which future Israeli Prime Minister was wounded as a soldier before rising to power? And how did Menachem Begin accidentally dismantle an entire political system—all because he misunderstood Israeli slang? This session brings to light the quirks, courage, and key turning points that helped shape Israel’s evolving national identity. 

Modern Times - Innovation, Espionage, and Global Impact: Israel’s modern history is a tapestry of daring operations, diplomatic showdowns, and astonishing breakthroughs that continue to surprise the world. How did Israel pull off top-secret missions involving stolen documents, exploding beepers, and high-stakes intelligence? And which leader wound up competing with President Biden over the best Golda Meir story? This final session brings Israel’s most recent, mind-boggling tales to life, proving that the country’s history is still being written in ways both unexpected and extraordinary.

showcases.title: The Human Body in Jewish Law

videos.title: Week 4-The Human Body in Jewish Law
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded February 29, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Danny Schiff
Tattooing, body-piercing, cosmetic surgery, cremation, embalming, hair cutting, hair covering, immersing in water…In most contemporary societies these issues are matters of personal preference rather than religious law. Jewish law, however, has significant, thought-provoking things to say about how we treat the human body and what we do to it. Join world-renowned educator Rabbi Danny Schiff as we explore how Jewish law suggests we should treat our bodies.

showcases.title: The Master Storyteller: Isaac Bashevis Singer

videos.title: Week 4-The Master Storyteller: Isaac Bashevis Singer
videos.description:
Recorded March 25, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Shana Rosenblatt Mauer 
Isaac Bashevis Singer is one of the most important writers of the 20th century as a voice that crossed countless boundaries in the Jewish world, in addition to being a Nobel laureate. Raised in Torah-observant communities of rural Poland and Warsaw, Singer wrote with a religious spirit coupled with a secular zeal. He was a European and a native Yiddish speaker who took the American literary scene by storm and ensured that English reading audiences would have access to his work. While he did not directly fall victim to the Holocaust, Singer was still all too aware of the impending doom for the Jews of Europe having lost both his mother and brother to the Nazi terror. In this moving four-part program from Dr. Shana Mauer, teacher of the hit Melton International program “Emma Lazarus: Beyond the Statue of Liberty”, we will get a sense of this paradoxical writer who had an unparalleled imagination and an unprecedented storytelling voice.

showcases.title: The Mavens: the Pulpit, the Pen, and the Podcast

videos.title: Week 3-The Mavens: the Pulpit, the Pen, and the Podcast
videos.description:
Recorded September 17, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Steven Chervin
What does it take to become a voice people trust? For over a century, a surprising number of Jewish writers, thinkers, and therapists have taken on the public role of advice-givers, responding to questions about family, identity, sex, culture, and ethics. In this three-session course, Dr. Steven Chervin will explore the Jewish personalities behind some of America’s most iconic advice columns and counseling platforms, and how their responses reflected and shaped the cultural and moral concerns of their time.

The Bintel Brief (Sept. 3) - In the early 20th century, waves of Eastern European Jewish immigrants arrived in New York’s Lower East Side, bringing with them disorienting questions about identity, modernity, and belonging. The Bintel Brief, a Yiddish advice column in The Forverts, became a trusted outlet for navigating the conflicts of immigrant life: work and family, tradition and change, American ambition and Jewish values. We’ll read and discuss the letters that captured a generation’s struggle to find its footing in a new world.
Dueling Sisters Ann Landers and Dear Abby (Sept. 10) - Jewish advice went mainstream in the mid-20th century, thanks to twin sisters from Sioux City, Iowa—Esther Pauline and Pauline Esther Friedman—better known as Ann Landers and Dear Abby. Their syndicated columns reached tens of millions of readers across backgrounds and beliefs, shaping American conversations about everything from etiquette to ethics, marriage to morality. We’ll explore their rise to fame, their lasting cultural impact, and the fascinating rivalry that defined their careers.
Kosher Sex and Modern Intimacy (Sept. 17) - In more recent decades, a new generation of Jewish advice-givers emerged to tackle deeply personal—and often taboo—subjects. Holocaust survivor and media icon Dr. Ruth Westheimer, Orthodox provocateur Rabbi Shmuley Boteach, and psychotherapist Esther Perel each opened up conversations about sex, love, and emotional connection. Drawing on Jewish tradition and modern psychology, they redefined the public conversation around intimacy, trust, and identity in a changing world.
Taught by Dr. Steven Chervin, a veteran Melton educator and insightful interpreter of Jewish culture and history, this class combines humor, history, and real-life dilemmas. Whether you grew up reading these columns or are encountering them for the first time, you’ll leave with a deeper understanding of how advice dispensed from podiums, pulpits, and print shapes Jewish identity, values, and voice.

Recorded September 3, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Steven Chervin
What does it take to become a voice people trust? For over a century, a surprising number of Jewish writers, thinkers, and therapists have taken on the public role of advice-givers, responding to questions about family, identity, sex, culture, and ethics. In this three-session course, Dr. Steven Chervin will explore the Jewish personalities behind some of America’s most iconic advice columns and counseling platforms, and how their responses reflected and shaped the cultural and moral concerns of their time.

The Bintel Brief (Sept. 3) - In the early 20th century, waves of Eastern European Jewish immigrants arrived in New York’s Lower East Side, bringing with them disorienting questions about identity, modernity, and belonging. The Bintel Brief, a Yiddish advice column in The Forverts, became a trusted outlet for navigating the conflicts of immigrant life: work and family, tradition and change, American ambition and Jewish values. We’ll read and discuss the letters that captured a generation’s struggle to find its footing in a new world.

Dueling Sisters Ann Landers and Dear Abby (Sept. 10) - Jewish advice went mainstream in the mid-20th century, thanks to twin sisters from Sioux City, Iowa—Esther Pauline and Pauline Esther Friedman—better known as Ann Landers and Dear Abby. Their syndicated columns reached tens of millions of readers across backgrounds and beliefs, shaping American conversations about everything from etiquette to ethics, marriage to morality. We’ll explore their rise to fame, their lasting cultural impact, and the fascinating rivalry that defined their careers.

Kosher Sex and Modern Intimacy (Sept. 17) - In more recent decades, a new generation of Jewish advice-givers emerged to tackle deeply personal—and often taboo—subjects. Holocaust survivor and media icon Dr. Ruth Westheimer, Orthodox provocateur Rabbi Shmuley Boteach, and psychotherapist Esther Perel each opened up conversations about sex, love, and emotional connection. Drawing on Jewish tradition and modern psychology, they redefined the public conversation around intimacy, trust, and identity in a changing world.

Taught by Dr. Steven Chervin, a veteran Melton educator and insightful interpreter of Jewish culture and history, this class combines humor, history, and real-life dilemmas. Whether you grew up reading these columns or are encountering them for the first time, you’ll leave with a deeper understanding of how advice dispensed from podiums, pulpits, and print shapes Jewish identity, values, and voice.

showcases.title: The Music and Teachings of Our Exodus

videos.title: Week 4-The Music and Teachings of Our Exodus
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded March 30, 2023
Instructors: Rabbi Amy Wallk and Cantor David Lipp
Passover is one of the most observed Jewish holidays around the world centered around one of the greatest stories ever told, the Exodus from Egypt. We retell this story using the Haggadah, which even translates to “The Telling”. So, what exactly is the story we are supposed to tell on the eve of Passover? How are we meant to go about telling it? How do the tunes and the songs that accompany the telling contribute to the story? And what are the many different ways the Haggadah helps us tell this foundational story? Join Rabbi Amy Wallk and Cantor David Lipp as they share some of their favorite teachings and music that you can bring into your own Passover seder this year!

showcases.title: The Next Chapter - Between the Lines

videos.title: Week 6-The Next Chapter - Between the Lines
videos.description:
Recorded June 5, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi John Carrier
thought and practice for over 3,000 years.
But how do we connect to words written so long ago? What do they reveal today? And how do they inspire action? In this course, we will examine the many ways that we bring the words of our texts into our daily lives. We’ll look at the hints and clues in the letters of sacred Jewish texts: the Torah, the Prophets, the Talmud (no previous Hebrew knowledge required). We’ll exam structure and authorship. We’ll discuss the commentary of yesterday. We’ll share where we stand today.

Lesson 1 - A Learned People: Connecting through Jewish Text
Lesson 2 - Depths of Learning: The Roots of Jewish Texts
Lesson 3 - Who Wrote the Torah: Why Does It Matter?
Lesson 4 - From Text to Context: The Evolution of Jewish Practice
Lesson 5 - Open Your Hand and Your Heart: Tracing Jewish Values from Text to Life
Lesson 6 - Mussar and Middot: Ethics, Character Traits, and Being a Good Jew

showcases.title: The Next Chapter - Highs and Lows: Our Communal Joys and Sorrows
The Next Chapter - It's a Date: Marking Time the Jewish Way

videos.title: Week 6-The Next Chapter - It's a Date: Marking Time the Jewish Way
videos.description:
Recorded September 17, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi John Carrier
Whether you look forward to the Passover Seder all year or avoid it like the plague, add “It’s a Date” to your calendars. Gain new insight into days that mark profound moments in history, and others that mark when to harvest the grain — and pop open the wine. In this unit, we will explore how the Jewish calendar works. We will explain and discuss our holidays and holiday rituals. Rabbi John Carrier, instructor, has taught for the Melton School since 2015 and regards its curriculum as “the best next step” for people who develop a passion for Jewish learning during their conversion process.
Table of Contents
Lesson 1 - Marking the Moons: The Jewish Calendar
Lesson 2 - Awe-some Holy Days: New Year, New Beginning
Lesson 3 - Mistakes and Mindfulness: A Day of Forgiveness
Lesson 4 - Uniting in Joy: Sukkot, Shemini Atzeret, Simchat Torah
Lesson 5 - Retelling and Reconnecting: The Pesach Seder Up-Close
Lesson 6 - Harvesting the Field and Soul: Shavuot through the Ages

showcases.title: The Path of Moral Wisdom: A Journey Through the Book of Proverbs

videos.title: Week 3-The Path of Moral Wisdom: A Journey Through the Book of Proverbs
videos.description:
Recorded May 21, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
three-session journey through the Book of Proverbs (Mishlei)—King Solomon’s biblical masterpiece, rich with ethical insight and spiritual depth. Together, we explore how this ancient collection of moral teachings speaks to the everyday challenges of modern life. Our map through the text is provided by Rabbi Samson Raphael Hirsch, the 19th-century thinker who overlayed philosophical clarity and thematic structure on Mishlei. Rather than reading verse by verse, Hirsch invites us to explore key topics that run through the book. This series focuses on six of them:
Speech – the ethics and impact of our words
Parenting – raising children with wisdom and values
Money – navigating wealth, poverty, and dignity
Friendship – the nature of real relationships
Anger – the costs of losing control
Pride and Humility – the balance of self-worth and modesty
With Rav Johnny’s gentle, wise, and heartfelt teaching style, we’ll uncover practical insight and personal meaning. His thoughtful presence, combined with Hirsch’s moral clarity, opens a path for learners to reflect, connect, and rediscover Mishlei as a living guide for ethical Jewish living.

showcases.title: The Prince and the Prophet: Machiavelli, Moses, and the Journey to Freedom

videos.title: Week 3-The Prince and the Prophet- Machiavelli, Moses, and  the Journey to Freedom
videos.description:
Recorded March 4, 2025
Instructor: Dr. Rivka Amado
Machiavelli, often seen as an amoral political thinker, found unexpected inspiration in the biblical story of Exodus. His vision of republican self-governance and the transformation of passive subjects into active citizens echoes Moses’ leadership and the Israelites’ journey from bondage to freedom. Join Dr. Rivka Amado—an expert in applied ethics and an advocate for cultural preservation—for a fascinating exploration of how these two worlds intersect, revealing timeless lessons on leadership, autonomy, and collective responsibility.

Session 1: Discover Machiavelli’s biography and the historical context shaping his revolutionary ideas.
Session 2: Explore Machiavelli’s concepts of “Virtù” and spiritual transformation through key excerpts from The Prince and Discourses.
Session 3: Examine Moses’ leadership challenges and how they parallel Machiavelli’s vision of creating a republican political community.
This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

showcases.title: The Sinai Revolution: The Inner Workings of Jewish Texts with Rabbi Danny Schiff

videos.title: Week 4-The Sinai Revolution: The Inner Workings of Jewish Texts
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded May 18, 2022
Rabbi Dr. Danny Schiff
What was revealed at Sinai and how do other canons of Jewish literature fit in to our story? Join Rabbi Schiff in the weeks leading up to Shavuot as he explores the structure of Jewish texts, beginning with Torah and continuing through contemporary times.  The role of Torah, Tanakh, and Talmud, will be examined to gain a deeper understanding of Jewish law and lore.  As Shavuot approaches, we will take an up close look at the moment of revelation and the texts that flowed from that wondrous encounter.

showcases.title: The Temple Mount: The Pain of the Past and the Politics of the Present

videos.title: Week 4-The Temple Mount: The Pain of the Past and the Politics of the Present
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded August 9, 2022
Rav Johnny Solomon, Instructor
Central to Jewish history is our relationship with Jerusalem. Central to Jewish worship is our focus on Jerusalem. Central to Tisha Ba’av is the twice destroyed Temple in Jerusalem. And central to the future of the State of Israel are the challenges and opportunities that exist in the modern city of Jerusalem. Explore the events that took place in Jerusalem leading to the destruction of both Temples, as well as the dramas that are occurring today, including the debates concerning access to, and worship on, the Temple Mount.

showcases.title: Theodor Herzl: Dreaming a Jewish Future

videos.title: Week 3-Theodor Herzl: Dreaming a Jewish Future
videos.description:
Recorded February 16, 2026
Instructor: Professor Derek Penslar
Theodor Herzl wasn’t the first to imagine a Jewish homeland, but his vision, charisma, and political acumen turned that dream into a global movement. In this special Melton+ course, produced in partnership with Keren Kayemeth LeIsrael-Jewish National Fund in Israel as well as Jewish National Fund-USA, and the World Zionist Village - The World’s First Global Campus for the Greatest Conversation on Zionism!, renowned historian Professor Derek Penslar, author of Theodor Herzl: The Charismatic Leader, guides us through Herzl’s personal journey, public leadership, and prophetic imagination.

The course marks the 130th anniversary of Herzl’s Der Judenstaat, the publication that transformed a private alarm into a public program and helped launch a modern political movement. This anniversary offers a timely opportunity to revisit Herzl’s ideas with fresh eyes and consider how his bold propositions continue to echo in Jewish life. Over three sessions, we’ll trace how Herzl became the unlikely father of modern political Zionism and how his deeds and writings continue to shape conversations about Jewish peoplehood and the State of Israel today.

Session 1 - Herzl’s Path to Zionism: What led an assimilated Jewish journalist to become an impassioned Zionist?
Session 2 - Herzl as a Zionist Leader: Through Herzl’s words and actions, we explore his leadership style, the movement he created, and the challenges he faced.
Session 3 - Herzl’s Vision of the Jewish Future: We conclude with Altneuland, Herzl’s utopian novel, asking how his imagined future speaks to today’s realities—and dreams yet unfulfilled.
Whether you’re new to Herzl or returning to his legacy with fresh eyes, this class offers an opportunity to explore the roots of Zionism with one of the foremost scholars of modern Jewish history.

showcases.title: Thought Process: Jewish Law and Mental Health

videos.title: Week 4-Thought Process: Jewish Law and Mental Health
videos.description:
Week 4
Recorded January 30, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
Every decade or so, societal shifts emerging from economic development, technological innovation, and medical research force rabbis to clarify Torah values and to issue Jewish legal guidelines on new matters that need to be addressed. Recently, one of the main topics is mental health and Jewish law. In this four-part series taught by expert Melton educator Rabbi Johnny Solomon, we will explore some of the questions being asked today, as well as the responses being provided. We will begin with a session on methodological considerations to see how Jewish law is determined in response to questions of mental health. This will then be followed by three sessions examining the way religious observance is challenging for individuals dealing with depression, anxiety, OCD, and eating disorders. We hope you can join us for this important course offered during a time when many are struggling with mental health challenges.

showcases.title: To Heal a Fractured World: The Vision of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks

videos.title: Week 2-To Heal a Fractured World: The Vision of Rabbi Jonathan Sacks
videos.description:
Recorded November 25, 2025
Instructor: Rabbanit Sarah Sharman Moser
What made Rabbi Lord Jonathan Sacks one of the most beloved and influential Jewish thinkers of our time? From his iconic yellow tie to his profound moral clarity, Rabbi Sacks brought Judaism into global conversations about ethics, faith, and the future of society. Taught by Rabbanit Sarah Sharman Moser, a Sacks Scholar, translator, and close colleague of Rabbi Sacks, this course offers a rare insider’s view of his life and teachings.

In the first session, The Man Behind the Yellow Tie, we will explore Rabbi Sacks’ journey from a philosophy student to Chief Rabbi of the UK, uncovering the foundational ideas that shaped his worldview. We will also look at his many writings, which speak both inward to the Jewish people and outward to the wider world. The second session, To Heal a Fractured World, will focus on one of Rabbi Sacks’ most influential works, published twenty years ago yet still as relevant as ever. Together, we will consider his vision of moral responsibility, the role of religion in contemporary society, and his framing of Judaism as a tradition of protest, justice, and ethical action.

This course is offered in celebration of the Rabbi Sacks Global Day of Learning 2025, an annual moment when communities worldwide come together to honour his memory through study and reflection. At a time when our world is marked by moral confusion, political violence, deep divisions, and disagreement as a national pastime, Rabbi Sacks’s wisdom feels more urgent than ever. His voice of clarity, hope, and moral courage continues to offer a path forward, reminding us that Judaism, and indeed humanity, is called to heal rather than fracture, to build rather than break.

showcases.title: Touchpoints of Faith: Finding Judaism in Everyday Life

videos.title: Week 3-Touchpoints of Faith: Finding Judaism in Everyday Life
videos.description:
Recorded February 20, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Amy S. Wallk
How can Jewish rituals add meaning and connection to our lives? Join Rabbi Amy Wallk for this engaging three-part series exploring the big ideas behind the everyday ritual traditions of Jewish life. From the significance of a mezuzah to the nuances of keeping kosher, and the beauty of celebrating Shabbat at home, this class will focus on the “why” rather than the “how to. Together, we will explore the values and principles that underlie these practices, offering a welcoming space for learners of all backgrounds. Come with curiosity and leave with inspiration to embrace the rituals that resonate with you, regardless of your level of observance. 

Session 1: Discover the meaning behind the mezuzah and explore where and why to hang it in your home. 
Session 2: Learn about the various approaches to keeping kosher and how dietary laws can enrich daily life. 
Session 3: Dive into Shabbat rituals and traditions, creating a meaningful celebration in your home.

This series is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

showcases.title: Trailblazing Jewish Women: Conversos, Spies, Farmers, and Fighters showcase

videos.title: Week 3-Trailblazing Jewish Women: Conversos, Spies, Farmers, and Fighters
videos.description:
Recorded April 7, 2025
Instructor: Daniella Gefen
Throughout history, remarkable Jewish women have defied societal norms, shattered glass ceilings, and left indelible marks on their communities and beyond. In this three-part Melton+ course, esteemed educator Daniella Gefen will guide us through the lives and legacies of four extraordinary figures, each a beacon of resilience and leadership in her era: 

Part 1: My heart is in the West, but I am in the East- The stories of two women–one at the beginning of the Ottoman era in Israel, and one at the end–and the role they both played in the relief and safety of the Jews in Israel. From the catastrophe of the Spanish expulsion to the turn of a new promise in the early 20th century, we will encounter two very different women, their struggles and achievements, and the road they paved their people

Part 2: We can do it! - Through the story of the founding mother of women's work in Israel, we will explore the ideology, dreams, and achievements of the Second Aliyah—the wave of Socialist immigration that aspired to build a just and renewed Israel. This movement sought to forge a new kind of Jew: a farmer, deeply connected to the land, cultivating both its soil and its future.

Part 3: Still Orthodox - A glimpse into a contemporary pioneer and trailblazer who is still leading change for the women of Israel and her community. We will explore the fastest growing society in Israel, the ultra-Orthodox Haredis, and investigate their attitudes towards the state, service and women in the public sphere. This course is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

showcases.title: Unmasking Esther: Power, Providence, and Palace Intrigue

videos.title: Week 2-Unmasking Esther: Power, Providence, and Palace Intrigue
videos.description:
Recorded March 10, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Michele Faudem
The Book of Esther is more than a tale of palace intrigue and dramatic reversals—it is a story of identity, resilience, and hidden meaning. As we prepare for Purim, this class takes a fresh look at the Megillah, exploring key moments from different chapters through the lens of the original text, classical commentaries, and modern insights. Join Rabbi Michele Faudem, a beloved Melton+ teacher, for an engaging and thought-provoking journey into the characters of Esther, Mordechai, Ahasuerus, and Haman. Through an in-depth study of their motivations, struggles, and transformations, we uncover timeless lessons on leadership, courage, and the complexities of fate. Whether you’re revisiting the Megillah or encountering it in a new way, this class will enrich your understanding of Purim and provide new insights to bring to your holiday celebrations.
This series is sponsored by the Goldsmith Family.

showcases.title: Untangling the Toughest of Talmudic Tales

videos.title: Week 4-Untangling the Toughest of Talmudic Tales
videos.description:
Recorded December 3, 2024
Instructor: Rabbi Johnny Solomon
The Talmud records numerous stories. Some of these stories seem to provide insights that are easily applicable to our lives today, while others require more "creativity" to understand and find relevant. The more challenging stories often describe unusual events which seem impossible to imagine having actually occurred or contain cryptic teachings which make little sense if taken literally. In this four-part course with expert Melton teacher Rabbi Johnny Solomon, we will study four of these types of Talmudic stories and then consider the different approaches which have been suggested to decode and find the underlying meaning in these fascinating tales and teachings. Through studying these stories and learning how to understand them, we will gain new skills and perspectives critical to learning Talmud. This course is generously sponsored by The Goldsmith Family.

In this four-part course with expert Melton teacher Rabbi Johnny Solomon, we will study four of these types of Talmudic stories and then consider the different approaches which have been suggested to decode and find the underlying meaning in these fascinating tales and teachings. Through studying these stories and learning how to understand them, we will gain new skills and perspectives critical to learning Talmud. This course is generously sponsored by The Goldsmith Family.

showcases.title: Voices from the Tenements

videos.title: Week 4-Voices From the Tenements
videos.description:
Recorded July 22, 2024
Instructor: Dr. Shana Rosenblatt Mauer 
Some of our most vivid, engaging descriptions of the hardscrabble life of turn-of-the-20th century Jewish immigrants come from portraits created by writers of fiction. Describing the sights and sounds of their immigrant neighborhoods, along with their poverty, religious conflicts, anti-Semitism, generational divides, and culture shock, these authors created an animated, impassioned body of work that depicts the challenges of facing a foreign culture, a new language, and an unfamiliar way of life. Moreover, they dramatized how these trials specifically impacted Eastern European Jews, who were jolted by the realities of this new country, but also determined to make it their own. In this follow-up to her hit course, “The Master Storyteller: Isaac Bashevis Singer,” Dr. Shana Mauer will focus on the writings of the famed triumvirate of immigrant writers Abraham Cahan, Mary Antin, and Anzia Yezierska, whose work has practically come to define early 20th century American Jewish literature. Join us for this insightful look at the life, times, and words of some of the tenements’ most prolific writers.

showcases.title: What Do We Do Now: Jewish Responses to Communal Catastrophe

videos.title: Week 3-What Do We Do Now: Jewish Responses to Communal Catastrophe
videos.description:
Recorded August 7, 2024
Instructor: Harman Avery Grossman 
In the wake of October 7, many of us are struggling to find ways to cope and move forward. Over thousands of years, the Jewish people have had far too many opportunities to develop responses to communal tragedy. In this insightful new course from expert Melton teacher Harman Grossman, we will examine texts – from the Bible through modernity – reflecting different strategies to deal with collective catastrophe embedded in our tradition. From there, we will consider which, if any, of these traditional responses might help us today. Join us for this meaningful exploration of how we handle the hardest moments the world throws at our community.

showcases.title: What the Sages Said: A Chain of Wisdom from Pirkei Avot

videos.title: Week 3-What the Sages Said: A Chain of Wisdom from Pirkei Avot
videos.description:
Recorded May 20, 2025
Instructor: Rabbi Neil Fleischmann
Take a closer look at Pirkei Avot (Ethics of Our Fathers) with Rabbi Neil Fleischmann in this fresh and thought-provoking exploration of its first chapter. Often quoted but rarely studied in sequence, this chapter traces the chronological chain of Jewish leadership—from Moses at Sinai through the rabbis of the Mishnah—revealing a carefully constructed lineage of learning and law. Rabbi Fleischmann returns to Melton+ after his acclaimed course on Jewish allegory, bringing his signature blend of wit, warmth, and wide-ranging sources. With insight and curiosity, he invites learners to rediscover these ancient voices and the enduring values they passed down, generation to generation.

showcases.title: Where Can I Find Redemption: Past or Future

videos.title: Week 2-Where Shall I Find Redemption: Past or Future?
videos.description:
Recorded July 3, 2025
Instructor: Joe Septimus
In this two-part series, beloved Melton+ teacher Joe Septimus will introduce us to the very first piece of Aggadah in the Talmud: a haunting and beautiful tale of the first-century sage Rabbi Yossi, a ruined Jerusalem, and an unexpected encounter with the Prophet Elijah, offering essential wisdom for the Jewish future. Aggadah—the storytelling tradition within the Talmud—includes legends, parables, theological reflections, and moral teachings. If the Talmud is a work of law, why are fantastical stories included at all? What do these poetic interludes reveal about the inner world of the Rabbis, and what might they reveal about ours? As we encounter this ancient story of ruins and revelation, we may also uncover timeless wisdom about what it means to rebuild, both personally and collectively.

Through lively discussion and close reading, Joe will bring the text to life, blending profound scholarship with open-hearted curiosity. Together, we will explore what Aggadah is, why the Rabbis chose to open a vast legal text with this surreal narrative, and how its themes—destruction and resilience, prayer and presence, divine grief and human response—still speak to us today.

showcases.title: With Dignity and Compassion: Jewish Wisdom for the End of Life

videos.title: Week 1-With Dignity and Compassion: Jewish Wisdom for the End of Life
videos.description:
Recorded May 26, 2026
Instructors: David Zinner, Rick Light, Rabbi Stuart Kelman, and Dan Fendel of The Jewish Association for Death Education 
What does Jewish tradition teach us about how to approach the end of life with dignity, compassion, and care? Across centuries, Jewish texts and practices have created a framework that honors both the individual and the community, offering guidance in moments that can feel uncertain or overwhelming.

In partnership with the Jewish Association for Death Education, this three-part course invites learners into a thoughtful exploration of Jewish end-of-life practices. Through close reading of classical texts and liturgy, we will uncover how Jewish tradition responds to the emotional, spiritual, and communal needs that arise at the end of life, and how these practices continue to shape Jewish life today.

In this three-part series, we will explore:

Session 1: Jewish Traditions at the End of Life
An introduction to the core values and historical development of Jewish end-of-life practices, from Tanach through contemporary expressions. We will explore key texts and moments, including the role of the Chevrah Kadisha and the foundations of communal care.
Session 2: The Power of Taharah Liturgy
A close reading of the prayers recited during taharah, the ritual preparation of the body. We will ask what these words do, what needs they respond to, and how their structure and language reflect deeper spiritual and emotional truths.
Session 3: Jewish End-of-Life Practices in Moed Katan
A study of foundational rabbinic teachings on burial and mourning. Through selected texts, we will explore how Jewish law understands grief, ritual, and community, and what the connection between mourning and festival life reveals about Jewish tradition. 
This course is generously sponsored by the Poretzky family.
